                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          YUKHÍTI KÓY                                                               
                                                                                    
          A REFERENCE GRAMMAR   OF THE ATAKAPA LANGUAGE                             
                                                                                    
          WITH TRANSLATED AND ANALYZED TEXTS AND VOCABULARY                         
                                                                                    
          COMPILED BY GEOFFREY KIMBALL                                              
                                                                                    
          FROM THE FIELD NOTES OF ALBERT SAMUEL GATSCHET, PROVIDED BY KIŠYUC AND TOTTOKŠ

               This book was made possible by a legacy from the late Dr. Stephen Lee Pearce, Esq.,
                                                                                    
          without whose support this work could not have been accomplished. I thank theDr… two
                                                                                    
          anonymous reviewers whose comments and suggestions greatly improved the content of the
                                                                                    
          book; I also thank Heather Stauffer and Matthew Bokovoy of the University of Nebraska Press
                                                                                    
          for their expert assistance in shepherding this project to its successful completion. I should like

          PREFACE                                                                   
                                                                                    
            At the time when Europeans first entered what is now the Southeastern United States, people
                                                                                    
          speaking related languages occupied the coastal strip from Vermillion Bay in Louisiana
                                                                                    
          westward to Galveston Bay in Texas, living in small bands of seasonally migrating hunter-
                                                                                    
          gatherers. The people in the west, the Orkokisak, were known to the Spanish as the Orcoquizac1,
                                                                                    
          while those living in the middle of their range, the Yukhíti, and those living to the east, the
                                                                                    
          Hiyékiti, were given the name hattak ápa by the Choctaw, and this was adopted in English as
                                                                                    
          Atakapa. The Choctaw name means ‘eaters of human beings;’ however, all acts of cannibalism
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          attributed to them seem to be anecdotal as opposed to observational.2 The people who spoke
                                                                                    
          what we may call Atakapan languages were very much isolated from European settlement and as
                                                                                    
          a rule did not come into conflict with Europeans. Being isolated, their languages were little
                                                                                    
          noted. In 1721 Jean Bérenger captured nine Indians at Galveston Bay, and recorded 45 words of
                                                                                    
          their language; this is the only material known for the Orkokisak, or the Western Atakapa. In
                                                                                    
          1802 Martin Duralde collected a vocabulary of 287 words in Franklin, Louisiana, near
                                                                                    
          Vermillion Bay; this is the only documentation for Hiyékiti, or Eastern Atakapa. The late
                                                                                    
          eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries saw a drastic fall in the Atakapa population, by the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          middle of the nineteenth century many speakers of Orkokisak and Hiyékiti had amalgamated
                                                                                    
          with the Yukhíti living along the Calcasieu River in Louisiana. The population continued to fall;
                                                                                    
          by 1850, there were only two villages left, Túl Núŋ and Ló, on the east and west sides of Lake
                                                                                    
          Prien on the Calcasieu River. In January, 1885, Albert Samuel Gatschet came to Lake Charles,
                                                                                    
          Louisiana, and found and worked with the two most knowledgeable speakers of the language
                                                                                    
          there in Túl Núŋ, the last surviving village. At the time, he found the community to be
                                                                                    
          dispersing, with speakers of the language moving away to Texas and Oklahoma. Although he
                                                                                    
                                         11                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          published a plea for more people to work on the language, no one else, aside from John R.
                                                                                    
          Swanton, ever did. Thus, Gatschet’s field notes are the only source for deriving a grammar of
                                                                                    
          the language.                                                             
                                                                                    
            John R. Swanton wrote a grammatical sketch of Atakapa (1929) based on Gatschet’s notes,
                                                                                    
          and then in 1932 published under Gatschet’s name and his own the texts that Gatschet had
                                                                                    
          gathered, and then a dictionary under the entries of which he included the majority of the words
                                                                                    
          and sentences that Gatschet had collected.                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            Atakapa is the only American Indian language of the Southeast on which no serious work has
                                                                                    
          been done in over eighty years. The grammar is essentially unknown, the phonology is unclear,
                                                                                    
          and the vocabulary is difficult to use. As Swanton’s publication is the only easily accessible
                                                                                    
          source for Atakapa, almost all examples are cited in the form in which they occur therein.
                                                                                    
          However, Gatschet’s original field notes (1885a; 1885b; and 1885c) have been used in
                                                                                    
          preference to this material, and when appropriate, his original recordings are used.
                                                                                    
            Despite the fact that Atakapa has not been used as a spoken language for at least three
                                                                                    
          quarters of a century, interest in it still exists, as shown by the publications of people such as
                                                                                    
          Singleton (2000a; 2000b) who have Atakapa as a heritage language, and the existence of a
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Facebook pages dedicated to the language. Thus a reference grammar is of more than just
                                                                                    
          academic interest.                                                        
                                                                                    
          <Insert Illustration 1 after this page>                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         12                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          INTRODUCTION.                                                             
                                                                                    
          The Atakapa language is attested almost entirely through the work of Albert S. Gatschet, which
                                                                                    
          he did in January of 1885 with two speakers of the language, Kišyuc (‘Will-o’-the-wisp’), also
                                                                                    
          known as Yoyot, and her cousin Tottokš (‘Round [Eyes]’), whose English names were Louison
                                                                                    
          Huntington3 (born ca. 1830, died ca. 1905) and Delilah Moss4 (born ca. 1831, died ca. 1920).
                                                                                    
          Gatschet (1885d) indicates that they were the oldest and best-informed Atakapa speakers of his
                                                                                    
          day. He also made the comment: “The remnants of the old Atakapa are reduced to a few women
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          who in 1888 lived a few miles south of Lake Charles R. R. station” (1903), indicating that they
                                                                                    
          resided in the village of Túl Núŋ. Gatschet himself searched tirelessly for speakers of American
                                                                                    
          Indian languages, literally traveling across the continent, often working in primitive conditions.
                                                                                    
          In Louisiana alone between 1885 and 1886, in addition to Atakapa he documented Tunica,
                                                                                    
          Chitimacha, Biloxi, and two dialects of Choctaw.                          
                                                                                    
            John R. Swanton collected a small amount of material from Teet Verdine (1854-1925)5 in
                                                                                    
          1907 and Armojean Reon (1873-1925)6, in 1908, but by that time, speakers of the language seem
                                                                                    
          to have been reticent to talk to strangers about it.7                     
                                                                                    
            A little linguistic information exists for the Atakapa living in Texas (the Orkokisak) at the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          westernmost part of their range, and rather more for the Atakapa at the easternmost part of their
                                                                                    
          range (called by Swanton the Eastern Dialect, and by Yukhíti-speakers Hiyékiti). Both groups
                                                                                    
          had sufficiently different phonological forms of words, as compared to the better-attested
                                                                                    
          Yukhíti (Central Atakapa) of the lakes at the mouth of the Calcasieu River in Louisiana, to
                                                                                    
          indicate that their languages were more than just dialects of each other.8
                                                                                    
          Orkokisak oc        tiniƛ     iš        oƛ        keš                     
                                                                                    
          Yukhíti   úc        itiyílš   kakáw9    wó·l      te·š                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         13                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Hiyékiti  ict       teŋi·ƛešt nak       wil       taheš                   
                                                                                    
                    ‘nose’    ‘moon’    ‘sun’     ‘eye’     ‘hair’                  
                                                                                    
            This grammar is based on the assumption that Gatschet recorded what he heard to the best of
                                                                                    
          his ability, and that discrepancies between items recorded at different times should be
                                                                                    
          rationalized first by processes within the language itself before assuming mis-hearing on the part
                                                                                    
          of Gatschet. Because Swanton’s publication of Gatschet’s field notes is the only available
                                                                                    
          source for others to reference while following this grammar, the vast majority of the examples
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          and all of the texts are presented in the form found in the publication; references to the source of
                                                                                    
          these examples is provided in every case. Examples are taken from Gatschet’s field notes when
                                                                                    
          the published form is incomplete, segmented when the original is not, or mistranscribed.
                                                                                    
            All examples in the grammar are given a reference after the translation. For examples taken
                                                                                    
          from the dictionary part of the publication the reference is of the form (1932:44:1), where the
                                                                                    
          second number is the page number, and the third number is the column number. For examples
                                                                                    
          taken from the texts in the publication the reference is of the form (1932:14:71); the second
                                                                                    
          number is the page number, and the third number is the continuous line number assigned by
                                                                                    
          Swanton. Examples taken directly from Gatschet’s field notes have the form (1885b:17), where
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          the first number refers to the specific one of the three notebooks, and the second number to the
                                                                                    
          page number on which the example is found. The transcription of the Atakapa as found in the
                                                                                    
          publication is enclosed in square brackets; the transcription as found in Gatschet’s field notes is
                                                                                    
          given in curly brackets. English translation of the Atakapa is based on my understanding of the
                                                                                    
          material, and not necessarily on the glosses of Gatschet, or the translation found in Gatschet and
                                                                                    
          Swanton. For considerations of space, Atakapa forms in lists do not have the original
                                                                                    
          transcriptions, nor is justification provided in the cases where the accent I write differs from the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         14                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          accent published in Gatschet and Swanton. For the purposes of this grammar, the term Atakapa
                                                                                    
          will refer to the language spoken by Kišyuc and Tottokš; however, when it needs to be compared
                                                                                    
          with the two other Atakapan languages, Orkokisak and Hiyékiti, it will be called Yukhíti in order
                                                                                    
          to avoid the long-established, but inaccurate, geographical names for the languages.
                                                                                    
          Other Work on Atakapa. Prior to Gatschet’s fieldwork, the only publication of any Atakapa
                                                                                    
          linguistic material was the word list of Eastern Atakapa gathered by Martin Duralde in Albert
                                                                                    
          Gallatin’s 1836 publication. In 1929 Swanton published a grammatical sketch of the language
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          based on Gatschet’s field work. Although he had some sharp insights, such as realizing that
                                                                                    
          nouns and verbs are structurally identical, on the whole the work is handicapped by being pre-
                                                                                    
          phonemic, and being written before most modern grammatical and theoretical work had been
                                                                                    
          done. There has been no serious grammatical study of Atakapa since Swanton.
                                                                                    
            In his publication of 1932 Swanton re-transcribed all of Gatschet’s material, and inevitably as
                                                                                    
          part of the process of preparing the work for publication, errors, both typographical and of
                                                                                    
          interpretation crept in. For example, the two sentences, “Don’t pinch the cat; she might bite
                                                                                    
          you,” and “My cousin has come back from the Mississippi River,” were each split into two parts,
                                                                                    
          and the fragments placed in four different parts of the publication. In addition, the dictionary was
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          not arranged by words, but by roots that Swanton saw, or thought he saw, in the Atakapa
                                                                                    
          material. In some cases distinct and unrelated words are thrown together due to similarities in
                                                                                    
          phonology or semantics, as for example iwa·t ‘to move in the direction of the speaker,’ is listed
                                                                                    
          under the hypothetical root *o ‘to come,’ because Gatschet had glossed examples of this verb
                                                                                    
          with English ‘come.’                                                      
                                                                                    
            Swanton’s work was used as a source by Morris Swadesh for his publications comparing
                                                                                    
          Atakapa and Chitimacha (1946b and 1947), and again served as a source for Karl-Heinz
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         15                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Gursky’s publication (1969) comparing Atakapa, Chitimacha, and Tunica. Neither author did
                                                                                    
          any further grammatical work on the Atakapa material. A recent publication on Atakapa is
                                                                                    
          Kaufman (2014), which is a retranscription and examination of the first Atakapa text in Gatschet
                                                                                    
          and Swanton; this last is included in Kaufman (2019b). Kaufman has also reorganized the
                                                                                    
          vocabulary section of Gatschet and Swanton as a learner’s dictionary (2019a).
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes. There are three field notebooks that Gatschet used for his field work.
                                                                                    
          The first one I call 1885a (as it is first in the archival box at the National Anthropological
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Archives) is a disbound copy of John Wesley Powell’s Introduction to the Study of Indian
                                                                                    
          Languages (1880).                                                         
                                                                                    
          Gatschet filled in the book’s schedules at times from his principal field notebook, at others he
                                                                                    
          elicited items directly from the book and did not enter them in his principal notebook. The
                                                                                    
          second notebook (1885b) is Gatschet’s principal field notebook. Pages 3 to 115 contain linguistic
                                                                                    
          material provided by Kišyuc, written in black ink. Starting on page 115 the material was
                                                                                    
          provided by Tottokš, and is written in red ink. As Gatschet worked with Tottokš, he went over
                                                                                    
          all of the material of Kišyuc, and made annotations and emendations in red ink. The material
                                                                                    
          from Tottokš up to page 148 is written in red ink, after which, seemingly having finished going
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          over the earlier material, he switched back to black ink, which he used to the end of the notebook
                                                                                    
          on page 175. In addition to the Atakapa field notes, pages 24 and 25 contain a vocabulary of
                                                                                    
          Mobilian written in pencil, and pages 177-78 have comparisons between Atakapa and
                                                                                    
          Chitimacha, and 179-80 a short index. The third notebook (1885c), which is unpaginated,
                                                                                    
          consists of ten pages of verb paradigms and short sentences, many of which do not occur in his
                                                                                    
          main notebook. It is written partly in ink and partly in pencil. This notebook does not seem to
                                                                                    
          have been used by Swanton.                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         16                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Notes                                                                     
                                                                                    
          1. Swanton (1932:1) re-named them the Akokisa, apparently after John Sibley’s 1805 spelling of
                                                                                    
          the name as Accokesaw. The name can be reconstructed as Orkokisak, ‘persimmon gathering
                                                                                    
          people,’ Yukhíti ol ‘persimmon’ kokiš ‘take something from someone,’ and iša·k ‘people.’ This
                                                                                    
          is similar to the name of the southernmost band of the Caddo, the na šitˀuš ‘pawpaw people.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          (Chafe, nd:72)                                                            
                                                                                    
          2. The lack of attested acts of cannibalism is noted in Post (1962:25-26). In the traditional
                                                                                    
          literature of the Chitimacha, cannibal Indians are the Karankawa, who lived southwest of the
                                                                                    
          Orkokisak (Swadesh, 1950:A.37), in that of the Koasati, the cannibals are the Tonkawa, who
                                                                                    
          lived to the northwest of the Orkokisak (Kimball, 2010:230-33). It may well be that hattak ápa
                                                                                    
          was simply an ethnic slur, given by a technologically more advanced people, cf. the Nahuatl
                                                                                    
          name for a less-advanced ethnic group in Mexico, cuitlateca, or ‘shit-people.’
                                                                                    
          3. She took her surname from that of her son-in-law or husband, Ambrose Huntington. Both
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s and Swanton’s notes on the interrelationships of the Atakapa speakers are elliptical
                                                                                    
          and confusing, and US Census records miss them for decades at a time.     
                                                                                    
          4. Swanton also gives her name as Delia Morse (1911:362) and Delilah Morse (1929), but in his
                                                                                    
          1907 notebook he calls her Delilah Verdine. In Gatschet’s field notes her English name is given
                                                                                    
          as Dalila or Delila Moss. In official records her name is even more varied, appearing as Dalila
                                                                                    
          Clifton, Dolly Clifton, Della Moss, and Dolly Moss.                       
                                                                                    
          5. Teet (in official records Tete or Tate) was a nickname. His actual name was Rosemond
                                                                                    
          Verdine; he seems to have been the brother of Tottokš.                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         17                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          6. Armojean Reon’s official name was Onezime Reon. He seems to have used both names
                                                                                    
          throughout his life. He likely was a grandson of Kišyuc.                  
                                                                                    
          7. Swanton’s quote is instructive: “This material taken May 8 [1907] from an Attacapa named
                                                                                    
          Teet Verdine at Sulphur Station. Tried him and his sister, Eliza Verdine, on a few words but
                                                                                    
          with rather poor success. They know many words and expressions, but do not recognize
                                                                                    
          quickly.” (Swanton, 1907 NAA MS4201:189:13). Swanton apparently also did some field work
                                                                                    
          with Tottokš, as occasional comments in the publication indicate. However, the field notes of his
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          work with her, and with Armojean Reon, seem not to have survived.         
                                                                                    
          8. Hiyékiti forms are retranscribed from Manuscript 1627, National Anthropological Archives,
                                                                                    
          Smithsonian Institution, pp. 195-201; Orkokisak forms are retranscribed from de Villiers du
                                                                                    
          Terrage (1919:435-36); see Appendices II and III.                         
                                                                                    
           Although Yukhíti and Hiyékiti were closely related languages, they were not completely
                                                                                    
          mutually intelligible. The following comment is recorded by Gatschet: “Hiyékiti, the easterners,
                                                                                    
          au du soleil levant, name of the tribe from which Fanchonette [came]. L(ouison) understood
                                                                                    
          almost all she said, but could not talk it.” (1885b:53)                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          9. There is a Yukhíti synonym that is cognate to the Hiyékiti form, lák ‘sun.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         18                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1. PHONOLOGY                                                              
                                                                                    
          Orthography. Reconstructing the phonology from the record is difficult. Table I shows the
                                                                                    
          symbols that Gatschet used in his field notes. Although Swanton provides a table of phonetic
                                                                                    
          signs (1932, 6-7) the actual phonemic equivalence is not clear. Because Gatschet’s field notes
                                                                                    
          had been recorded in a then-old-fashioned way, Swanton tacitly updated the material to a newer
                                                                                    
          phonetic system, which itself today is long-outdated, as it pre-phonemic.1 Among the more
                                                                                    
          problematic symbols used are the orthographic hyphen and the orthographic apostrophe.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Orthographic hyphen. The hyphen is used throughout the dictionary; this is based on
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s usage in his field notes. The temptation is to read it as a hiatus, and thus a glottal
                                                                                    
          stop, as Swadesh 1946b:115) suggested, however, close examination of orthographic variation
                                                                                    
          shows that the hyphen is frequently used to indicate a syllable-ending w or y (for example [ka-u]
                                                                                    
          ‘die,’ [kawat] ‘died,’ [coko-i] ‘chief’ [cokoyat] ‘was chief,’ [te-u] ‘upstream,’ [tewike] ‘from
                                                                                    
          upstream’). A hyphen also is used between identical vowels instead of h: [ha-an] hahan ‘not
                                                                                    
          having,’ [hatse-ec] haceheš ‘bad;’ and [tso-ots] cohoc ‘corn.’ Furthermore, a hyphen is
                                                                                    
          occasionally used to mark a morpheme boundary [puns-mickectsĕl] punš-miškeš-cel ‘we blow it
                                                                                    
          around in a circle.’ Inspection of Gatschet’s field notes shows that he used a hyphen when he
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          had to break a word at the end of a line, which hyphens Swanton published without deleting. In
                                                                                    
          addition, Gatschet used an equals sign (=) when he recognized a morpheme boundary.
                                                                                    
          Orthographic apostrophe. Swanton describes orthographic apostrophe as a pause, which one
                                                                                    
          might assume to mean a glottal stop. However, when one observes its usage in the publication
                                                                                    
          (which follows Gatschet’s usage in his field notes), the apostrophe usually occurs before n, and
                                                                                    
          in fact indicates that the n is syllabic ([ˈntol a] n̥.tól á ‘Are you well?’). There is only one word
                                                                                    
          where the apostrophe looks as if it indicates a glottal stop, [ˈnˈox] ‘red face paint’ (also written
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         19                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          [ˈnˈo] and [ĕnˈox]), which might seem to represent the pronunciation *n̥Ɂoh. However, this
                                                                                    
          word with a glottal stop would be unique, so another interpretation must be found. Initial h in
                                                                                    
          Atakapa seems to have been very weakly articulated, and was often not written by Gatschet. It
                                                                                    
          seems most likely then that the second apostrophe represents h, and the word is retranscribed as
                                                                                    
          n̥hóh /n̥.hóh/.                                                           
                                                                                    
          Atakapa phonemes. The following are the phonemes of Atakapa:              
                                                                                    
          Stops:         p    t    c(ʦ) k    (Ɂ)                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Fricatives          ƛ(tł) š        h                                      
                                                                                    
          Resonant            l                                                     
                                                                                    
          Nasals         m    n         ŋ                                           
                                                                                    
          Semivowels     w    y                                                     
                                                                                    
          Vowels              i, i·     u                                           
                                                                                    
                              e, e·     o, o·                                       
                                                                                    
                                   a, a·                                            
                                                                                    
                              (m̥ )     n̥                                          
                                                                                    
          <Insert Table I and Table II near here>                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Consonants. The stops seem to have been fortis and aspirated; very rarely are they written with
                                                                                    
          the symbols for the corresponding voiced consonants. In final position they seem to have been
                                                                                    
          unreleased. The glottal stop is a marginal phoneme; it needs to be postulated only for purposes
                                                                                    
          of reduplication. Word-initial vowels may have an initial glottal stop; if so, it is non-distinctive.
                                                                                    
            All the fricatives have positional variants. The phoneme ƛ has the phonetic form of a
                                                                                    
          voiceless lateral affricate, ƛ (tł) in syllable initial position (/ƛemƛem/ ‘torn (pl)’) and the phonetic
                                                                                    
          form of a lateral fricative, ɬ in medial or syllable-final position (/teɬk/ ‘flower’ /aɬiš/ ‘to lose’). It
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         20                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          does not occur regularly in word-final position; comparative evidence suggests that word-final ƛ
                                                                                    
          has fallen together with l (wo·l ‘eye,’ Orkokisak o·λ [audle]). The very few examples of ƛ in
                                                                                    
          word-final position suggest that it was pronounced as a voiced lateral affricate λ (dʫ), [ōˊdl] /ó·λ/
                                                                                    
          ó·l ‘nearby.’ It would appear that historically this phoneme was a lateral fricative, ł, in all
                                                                                    
          positions, which became affricated in initial and final position. Because of the consistently
                                                                                    
          different pronunciations of this phoneme, it will be written both as ƛ and ł in this grammar.
                                                                                    
            The phoneme š has the phonetic form s when it forms a cluster with t or n as an initial or final
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          member, or forms a cluster with itself. (/yukits/ ‘as for us,’ /nomšsim/ ‘nothing but children’)
                                                                                    
          The phoneme h has the phonetic form x as the initial member of a consonant cluster (/haxšat/
                                                                                    
          ‘she did not have.’)                                                      
                                                                                    
            The nasal consonant ŋ occurs only in root-final position (é·ŋ ‘name’), however it can appear
                                                                                    
          in syllable-initial position before a suffix (é·ŋip ‘by name.’)           
                                                                                    
            The semi-vowel w is devoiced in syllable-final position before a stop consonant (áwp /aw̥ p/
                                                                                    
          ‘right here,’ written [aˊxp ~ aˊvp]), when devoiced before other consonants, w is written [f], as in
                                                                                    
          [etwiˊfc] /hətwíφš/ hatwíwš ‘he thinks.’ At times in syllable-final position or syllable-medial
                                                                                    
          position w is a voiced bilabial fricative, β, and is written [v] as in [ivivhaˊhět] /hiβíβháhət/
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          hiwíwháhat ‘he did not believe me.’ The liquid l is devoiced before the sibilant š (polpólštit
                                                                                    
          /polpól̥štit/ ‘they are galloping.’                                       
                                                                                    
          Geminate consonants. As a rule, Gatschet did not write geminate consonants. There are only a
                                                                                    
          few examples of a geminate consonant written in monomorphemic words, such as haccewwán2
                                                                                    
          ‘to forget’ (ww) and ekúnnak ‘just now.’ All other geminate consonants are the result of
                                                                                    
          assimilations across morpheme boundaries. Even though most geminate consonants were not
                                                                                    
          written, they almost certainly were pronounced.3                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         21                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Vowels. Atakapa vowels occur both short and long. In Gatschet’s manuscript and Swanton’s
                                                                                    
          publication, long vowels are marked with a macron, but all were not consistently written, eg,
                                                                                    
          ha·k ‘he, ergative’ (from /haʰik/) is written hak, while ha·š ‘as for him’ (from /haʰiš/) is written
                                                                                    
          hāc. If the majority of the recordings of a word have a long vowel, then the vowel here is written
                                                                                    
          long; a few long vowels have certainly been missed. Vowel clusters occur only across
                                                                                    
          morpheme boundaries; between the vowels occurs an ephemeral transitional sound, the quality
                                                                                    
          of which depends on the initial vowel. Thus, after a, a weak h is heard, after e and i, y, and after
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          o and u, w, for example huo ‘I hear,’ phonetically /huʷo/.                
                                                                                    
            Atakapa has five short vowels, but only four long vowels. The vowel u does not have a long
                                                                                    
          counterpart. This is due to historical reasons. Wherever comparative evidence from the eastern
                                                                                    
          dialect is available, every example of Yukhíti (Central4 Atakapa) u has a cognate in Hiyékiti
                                                                                    
          (Eastern Atakapa) with a different vowel. Note the following:             
                                                                                    
          Yukhíti        Hiyékiti                                                   
                                                                                    
          hu             hiw            ‘to see’                                    
                                                                                    
          ku             kiw            ‘egg’                                       
                                                                                    
          ilu            aliw           ‘be hot’                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wulkol         wilkol         ‘raccoon’                                   
                                                                                    
          nikuc          nikict         ‘breast; nipple’                            
                                                                                    
          nešmuŋ         nišmi·n        ‘pine tree’                                 
                                                                                    
          tanuk          hanik          ‘one’                                       
                                                                                    
          tuc            tec            ‘leg’                                       
                                                                                    
          Orkokisak mostly agrees with Yukhíti in having u (O. tus ‘leg,’ Y. túc; H. tec), but occasionally
                                                                                    
          differs from both (O. oc ‘nose,’ Y. úc, H. ict] This evidence suggests that the vowel u is an
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         22                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          innovation in Yukhíti, after the conditions under which produced long vowels in an earlier state
                                                                                    
          of the language no longer were operative.                                 
                                                                                    
          Vowel length. Vowel length carries a low functional load in Atakapa. Long vowels are almost
                                                                                    
          exclusively restricted to final, accented syllables of words, and words with long vowels are of
                                                                                    
          low frequency. Nonetheless, Gatschet’s consultants insisted on the difference, and provided
                                                                                    
          several minimal pairs.5                                                   
                                                                                    
          Long Vowel                    Short Vowel                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          á·m            ‘to cook’      ám             ‘to drink’                   
                                                                                    
          cí·ci6         ‘tobacco pipe’ cíci           ‘baby’                       
                                                                                    
          é·ŋ            ‘name’         éŋ             ‘grease; fat’                
                                                                                    
          í·t            ‘face’         ít             ‘dung’                       
                                                                                    
          ká·k           ‘crow’         kák            ‘forest’                     
                                                                                    
          lá·k           ‘be strong’    lák            ‘sun’                        
                                                                                    
          ná·l           ‘be shallow’   nál            ‘to hunt’                    
                                                                                    
          né·            ‘land’         né             ‘to be ripe’                 
                                                                                    
          ó·             ‘rope’         ó              ‘at the edge of’             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          pá·c           ‘to squeeze’   pác            ‘to whip’                    
                                                                                    
          šé·š           ‘babies’       šéš            ‘mulberry; fig’              
                                                                                    
          Note that homonyms exist in words with both short and long vowels, for example káwš ‘to comb
                                                                                    
          something,’ káwš ‘to prick something;’ náw ‘shoe,’ náw ‘hair, fur, feathers,’ ní·t ‘five,’ ní·t
                                                                                    
          ‘thorn; honeylocust tree.’                                                
                                                                                    
          Syllabic nasals. Syllabic n is relatively common, occurring initially, medially, and finally.
                                                                                    
          Gatschet transcribed it as {‘n}, {ën}, {ěn}, and {n}. The occurrence is of this phoneme is most
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         23                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          common in morphemes, such as n̥- ‘second-person singular stative subject/active object,’=n̥
                                                                                    
          ‘noun connector,’ and –n̥t- ‘continuative.’ In monomorphemic words, syllabic n̥ occurs only
                                                                                    
          word-initially, usually before t, such as n̥tán ‘to be either/or;’ and n̥túl ‘river mouth, and word-
                                                                                    
          medially, išn̥cé·t ‘woman’s brother.’ In one monomorphemic word it occurs medially with the
                                                                                    
          only example of syllabic m̥ : {tó-u’ntáv’m} town̥táwm̥ hiy ‘sometimes’/tow.n̥.taβ.m̥ /.
                                                                                    
          Accent. Determining the nature of Atakapa accent is difficult, but necessary. First, the presence
                                                                                    
          or absence of accent distinguishes several words, elements, and constructions. Second, the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          phonetic feature of vowel weakening is dependent on the accent. Vowel weakening accounts for
                                                                                    
          much of the variation in the phonetically written forms of Atakapa words. The fact that phonetic
                                                                                    
          weakening of unaccented vowels takes place suggests that stress is a component of Atakapa
                                                                                    
          accent. An accent is written on most words in the corpus, even monosyllabic words, which
                                                                                    
          suggests that pitch is also a significant component of the accent. Examples such as [tsaˊxtaˊ]
                                                                                    
          ‘Choctaw’ where both syllables of a monomorphemic word bear an accent also suggest that
                                                                                    
          Atakapa accent also had a pitch component. Gatschet’s writing of the accent is fairly consistent,
                                                                                    
          but not always so. At times he underdifferentiates, not writing an accent when one should have
                                                                                    
          been written. Nonetheless, there are certain consistencies in cases where accent is not written,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          suggesting that, at least in the case of noun phrases, accent was a property of the phrase, not the
                                                                                    
          word. Gatschet more often overdifferentiates, and writes an accent where one did not exist.
                                                                                    
          There are two morphemes that are habitually accented when other evidence suggests that they
                                                                                    
          were unaccented: the directional case suffix -ot, and the enclitic marking coordination of verb
                                                                                    
          phrases, =ya. In most cases, Gatschet treats -ot as if it were a postposition, but unlike true
                                                                                    
          postpositions, which are nouns, it cannot be verbalized, nor can it take other nominal cases.
                                                                                    
          Occasionally he does write it as part of the noun, for example [tuˊlut] ‘to the lake,’ showing the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         24                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          typical post-accentual vowel weakening, rather than the more frequent [tuˊl oˊt]. The enclitic
                                                                                    
          =ya he treats as if it were equivalent to German und or English ‘and’ and writes it as a separate,
                                                                                    
          accented word whenever he recognizes it. However, in the cases that he does not recognize it, he
                                                                                    
          writes it, unaccented, as part of the previous word:7                     
                                                                                    
               [icak taˊ-ic oˊkiăn yukitnē mon ialpeˊhiulĕt]                        
                                                                                    
          1)   iša·k táyš ókya yukit né· mo·n yálpéhiulat
