               iša·k     tayš      ok=ya     yukit ne· mo·n                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               person    strange   come=and  our  land all                          
                                                                                    
               yal-peh-i-ul-at                                                      
                                                                                    
               take-finish-CAUS-3PLS-PRET                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘Strange people have come and completely taken all of our land.’ (1932:88:1)
                                                                                    
          At times, instead of, or in addition to, an accent Gatschet writes a long vowel, even if the vowel
                                                                                    
          is not organically long. This strongly suggests that he was hearing pitch, rather than stress. An
                                                                                    
          example is [ok hūˊya] ‘he came to see him, and…’ where a long u is written, even though long u
                                                                                    
          is not a phoneme of Atakapa. An additional complication is that when Swanton published
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s material, he occasionally added accents that were not in the field notes, and
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          occasionally omitted some that were.                                      
                                                                                    
           Accent distinguishes several elements, for example há ‘he,’ versus ha ‘his;’ ók ‘to come,’ ok-
                                                                                    
          ‘come and do.’ It also has syntactic use in distinguishing verb phrases from adjectival phrases:
                                                                                    
          išá·k káw ‘a man is dead,’ and iša·k káw ‘a dead man.’ There is at least one case where accent
                                                                                    
          placement distinguishes words: ití ‘night’ versus íti ‘front.’ Therefore, even though determining
                                                                                    
          accent can be problematic, it is necessary for the proper understanding of the language.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         25                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Pitch levels. Assuming that Atakapa accent has a pitch component, one can postulate two
                                                                                    
          primary pitch levels, high, which Gatschet marked with an accent, and mid, which remains
                                                                                    
          unmarked. In addition, there are traces of a low pitch accent. The low accent can be identified
                                                                                    
          when Gatschet does not write an accent on the syllable on which it should occur, but rather
                                                                                    
          writes accents on the surrounding syllables, which are usually never accented. The following are
                                                                                    
          a few identified examples. Examples 4-7 suggest that low pitch indicates that a noun is
                                                                                    
          verbalized.                                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [uctiˊkit]                                                           
                                                                                    
          2)   ùštikit                                                              
                                                                                    
               ø-uš-tikit                                                           
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.so-FUT                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘It will be so.’ (1932:139:2)                                        
                                                                                    
               [hikuˊ wi nuˊk tik iˊka koˊ]                                         
                                                                                    
          3)   hikú wi núk tikhikàkóh                                               
                                                                                    
               hiku wi   nuk  tik-hi-ka-koh                                         
                                                                                    
               soup I    self go.and-1SSTATS-make-DESID                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I myself want to go and make soup.’ (1932:41:1)                     
                                                                                    
               [na ˊ-ic ĕncoˊkoi]                                                   
                                                                                    
          4)   náiš n̥šokòy                                                         
                                                                                    
               na-iš     n̥-šokòy                                                   
                                                                                    
               you(sg)-FOC 2STATS-be.chief                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘You are a chief.’ (1932:83:1)                                       
                                                                                    
               [hāc coˊko-i]                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         26                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          5)   há·š šokòy                                                           
                                                                                    
               ha-iš     ø-šokòy                                                    
                                                                                    
               he-foc    3STATS-be.chief                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘He is a chief.’ (1932:69:1)                                         
                                                                                    
               [hikīˊke iˊcak]                                                      
                                                                                    
          6)   hikí·ke išà·k                                                        
                                                                                    
               hiki-ike  ø-išà·k                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               west-EL   3STATS-be.person                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘He is a man from the west.’ (1932:40:2)                             
                                                                                    
               [Taˊkapo kic yukiˊti nuˊnkin kēˊt’ntat]                              
                                                                                    
          7)   tákapa kìš yukhíti núŋkin ké·tn̥ tat                                 
                                                                                    
               takapa    ø-kìš          yukhiti   nuŋ-kin   ke·t-n̥ ta-t            
                                                                                    
               Atakapa   3STATS-be.woman Indian   village-LOC dwell-CONTIN-PRET     
                                                                                    
               ‘She was an Atakapa woman, and she used to live in an Indian village.’ (1932:14:70-1)
                                                                                    
          In the Muskogean languages of the Southeast, for example Creek (Haas, 1940), pitch has an
                                                                                    
          aspectual function in the verbal system, and the probable cases of Atakapa low pitch accent also
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          occur on verbs, which suggests an aspectual function as well. It seems fairly clear that the low
                                                                                    
          pitch accent is frequent on nouns that are verbalized in equational sentences.
                                                                                    
          Verbal accentuation. The accent in verbs is absolutely regular, which suggests that accent is a
                                                                                    
          lexical feature of verb roots. For accentual purposes, the verb root is CV(·)C. Syllables
                                                                                    
          preceding the root are not considered for accentual purposes, nor are the derivational suffixes -i
                                                                                    
          and -iš. Roots beginning or ending in a vowel are treated as if there were an initial or final
                                                                                    
          consonant, which historically was a glottal stop, which seems no longer to have been phonemic
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         27                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          in the language when it was recorded. The various modal suffixes, even if they are of the form
                                                                                    
          CVC, are never accented.                                                  
                                                                                    
               [viˊvulat]

               ‘They believed it.’ (1932:18:121)

          accent.

               šoká·k-háŋš-ti-n                                                     
                                                                                    
               ceremonial.dance-FINISH-PST.PROG-WHEN                                
                                                                                    
               ‘When they had finished the ceremonial dance…’ (1932:18:124-25)      
                                                                                    
          Nominal accentuation. In contrast to verbal accentuation, which is regular and predictable,
                                                                                    
          accentuation of nouns and pronouns is much more complex. Accent seems to be a feature of the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          noun phrase, and is not lexically determined. Thus, a noun root is accented when followed by
                                                                                    
          case suffixes, but is unaccented if followed by an adjective in a noun phrase; in that case, the
                                                                                    
          adjectival root is accented. Pronouns acting as subjects of verbs are accented; singular pronouns
                                                                                    
          acting as possessors are not. All this is made more complicated as occasionally Gatschet seems
                                                                                    
          to have written the accent he expected to hear, not the one that actually was spoken. There seem
                                                                                    
          also to be other, less definable, conditioning factors for nominal accentuation.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         30                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            The accentuation of root nouns in isolation depends on the shape and weight of the nominal
                                                                                    
          root. Atakapa root nouns, as a general rule, occur in the following syllable shapes: CV(·)C,
                                                                                    
          CVCV(·)C, CVCCV(·)C, CVCC, and CVCV. Light syllables are of the form CV; heavy
                                                                                    
          syllables are of the form CVC or CV·. Nouns are accented on the final heavy syllable of a root.
                                                                                    
          The few nouns of the form CVCV are accented on the final light syllable. Note that long vowels
                                                                                    
          do not occur in initial syllables of monomorphemic polysyllabic nouns. Nouns of the form V,
                                                                                    
          CV, and VC seem to have lost an initial or final glottal stop, which is written in the following
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          examples to illustrate the original syllable shape of the words in which it occurred.
                                                                                    
          CV(·)C roots                                                              
                                                                                    
          kíš       ‘woman’                                                         
                                                                                    
          téy       ‘vine’                                                          
                                                                                    
          má·l      ‘thigh’                                                         
                                                                                    
          ká·k      ‘raven’                                                         
                                                                                    
          né·       ‘land’                                                          
                                                                                    
          ó·        rope < *ʔo·                                                     
                                                                                    
          á         ‘this’ <*ʔáʔ                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          áy        ‘marsh’ <*ʔáy                                                   
                                                                                    
          CVCV(·)C root                                                             
                                                                                    
          canúk     ‘horse’                                                         
                                                                                    
          hiláy     ‘wife’                                                          
                                                                                    
          hišán     ‘mother-in-law’                                                 
                                                                                    
          kicá·k    ‘whiskey’                                                       
                                                                                    
          ité·t     ‘father’ <*ʔité·t                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         31                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ehéw      ‘sweetgum tree’ <*ʔehéw                                         
                                                                                    
          CVCCVC                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wolšél    ‘great horned owl’                                              
                                                                                    
          kamcíš    ‘dragonfly’                                                     
                                                                                    
          kulšá     ‘grandfather’ <*kulšáʔ                                          
                                                                                    
          CVCC                                                                      
                                                                                    
          cíšt      ‘pipe’                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          láns      ‘deer’                                                          
                                                                                    
          náhc      ‘turtle’                                                        
                                                                                    
          íck       ‘chest and stomach’ <*ʔick                                      
                                                                                    
          ášk       ‘shadow’ <*ʔášk                                                 
                                                                                    
          CVCV                                                                      
                                                                                    
          nilá      ‘grandmother’                                                   
                                                                                    
          hipá      ‘husband’                                                       
                                                                                    
          kató      ‘lip’                                                           
                                                                                    
          hikí      ‘west’                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          inó       ‘side’ <*ʔinó                                                   
                                                                                    
          iné       ‘wax myrtle’ <*ʔiné                                             
                                                                                    
          A number of kin terms have unusual root shapes; however, their accentuation is regular:
                                                                                    
          iškišíl   ‘daughter’                                                      
                                                                                    
          iškišé·t  ‘sister of a man’                                               
                                                                                    
          išn̥cé·t  ‘brother of a woman’                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         32                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Unexpected accentuation. The recorded accentuation of the following nouns does not follow
                                                                                    
          the regular patterns. They all are hapax legomena, and all have the accent occurring on the
                                                                                    
          initial syllable.                                                         
                                                                                    
          tópišt    ‘oak’                                                           
                                                                                    
          tókicit   ‘grasshopper’                                                   
                                                                                    
          témokš    ‘brant’                                                         
                                                                                    
          témom     ‘ashes’                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          cíkip     ‘little blue heron’                                             
                                                                                    
          máke·c    ‘lizard’                                                        
                                                                                    
          It is possible that this pattern is not one in which the initial syllable is accented, but that it is
                                                                                    
          higher in pitch than the final syllable, which would then have the low pitch accent, and the
                                                                                    
          elicited form would be a verbalization, thus makè·c ‘it is a lizard,’ topìšt ‘it is an oak,’ and so
                                                                                    
          forth (c.f. example 4, above), given that there is no formal distinction in Atakapa between
                                                                                    
          nominal and verbal roots.                                                 
                                                                                    
          Phonetic processes. There are four phonetic processes that affect the orthography of the lexical
                                                                                    
          items in Gatschet’s field notes and as published in Gatschet and Swanton: vowel weakening,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          aspiration, preaspiration, and h-stopping. These are sub-phonemic processes which account for
                                                                                    
          the majority of the attested variation in the corpus.                     
                                                                                    
          Vowel Weakening. The short vowels a, e, i, and u in syllables that fall before or after an
                                                                                    
          accented vowel can be weakened to schwa; the vowel o is weakened to ʌ. This is indicated in the
                                                                                    
          corpus by variation in the phonetic transcription of a weakened vowel, as in the following
                                                                                    
          examples.                                                                 
                                                                                    
          Post-accentual weakening                                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         33                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Taˊkapo icāˊk hatwiˊvat]                                            
                                                                                    
               /tákapǝ išá·k hatwíβat]                                              
                                                                                    
          19)  tákapa išá·k hatwíwat                                                
                                                                                    
               takapa    iša·k     hat-wiw-at                                       
                                                                                    
               Atakapa   people    REFLEX-believe-PRET                              
                                                                                    
               ‘The Atakapa people believed it.’ (1932:17:112-13)                   
                                                                                    
               [tuˊl taˊyut iyeˊ kaˊc]                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               /túl táyʌt iyékaš/                                                   
                                                                                    
          20)  túl táyot iyékaš                                                     
                                                                                    
               tul  tay-ot    ø-iye-kaš

          Pre-accentual weakening                                                   
                                                                                    
               [cokcukyiˊkc yaˊ cukwaˊk ‘n āˊl pai-oˊk yaˊ]                         
                                                                                    
               /šokšəkyíkšya šʌkwákn a·l payókya …/
