               ‘She sells, and brings back bread and meat, and…’ (1932:15:75-76)    
                                                                                    
          Both pre-and post-accentual weakening                                     
                                                                                    
               [Tawatweˊnăt Utsutaˊt ut.]                                           
                                                                                    
               /tawwatwénat ʌcʌttá·tʌt/                                             
                                                                                    
          22)  tawwatwénat ocottá·tot                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         34                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               taw-hat-wen-ø-at    oc-ot-ta·t-ot                                    
                                                                                    
               stand-REFLEX-pray-3SS-PRET above-DIR-stand-DIR                       
                                                                                    
               ‘They stood and prayed to God (She-who-stands-above).’ (1932:9:14)   
                                                                                    
          Aspiration. All the stop consonants apparently were fortis and aspirated; in taking dictation,
                                                                                    
          Gatschet occasionally recorded the non-phonemic aspiration of t and k as if they were phonetic
                                                                                    
          parts of the word, for example antú ‘owl’ recorded as [ant’huˊ], itáŋ ‘where,’ recorded as
                                                                                    
          [it’haˊñ], and eykún ‘now,’ recorded as [ik’huˊn]. There are, however, a few words where /kh/
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          occurs at an old /k-k/ morpheme boundary, such as ikhawc ‘to drown,’ *hik- ‘action in water,’
                                                                                    
          káw ‘to die,’ and yukhíti ‘Indian; Atakapa,’ cf. Hiyékiti yok ‘we’ and –kiti ‘*tribe,’ the latter
                                                                                    
          found in hiyé-kiti ‘Eastern Atakapa,’ lit. ‘Sunrise Tribe,’ iyé-c ‘to rise, of the sun.’
                                                                                    
          Pre-aspiration. A notable feature of the texts dictated by Tottokš is the occasional strong pre-
                                                                                    
          aspiration of nasal n, and stops, especially k, after the vowels a and o, which does not occur in
                                                                                    
          the speech of Kišyuc.                                                     
                                                                                    
               [cox̥koˊ-i] [coˊx̥ kiti] (1932:9:10)                                 
                                                                                    
          T:   /šoxkóy/  /šoxkìti/                                                  
                                                                                    
               [cokoˊ-i] [cokiˊti] (1932:9:10)                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          K:   /šokóy/8  /šokíti/                                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘chief’   ‘headman’                                                  
                                                                                    
          The preaspiration is so strong that occasionally the stop was not recorded.
                                                                                    
               [coxtoˊl]                                                            
                                                                                    
               /šok-tól/                                                            
                                                                                    
               thing-good                                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘good luck’ (1932:13:52)                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         35                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Kaufman (2014:75) suggests that orthographic /xk/ represents the consonant cluster /kk/. While
                                                                                    
          this is a plausible suggestion, given that the orthography /xC/ sometimes does indicate a /kC/
                                                                                    
          cluster, as in /cox̥ mōˊn/ for /šokmó·n/ ‘property,’ elsewhere, where /kk/ clusters occur across
                                                                                    
          morpheme boundaries, they are written simply as /k/ or carefully as /k’k/:
                                                                                    
               [cakaˊu koˊ-i]                                                       
                                                                                    
          23)  šakkáw kóy                                                           
                                                                                    
               šak-kaw        koy                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               PL-be.dead     language                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘The language of the dead, i.e. white people.’ (1932:68:2)           
                                                                                    
               [ta-ucok’koˊ-ikit]                                                   
                                                                                    
          24)  tawšokkóykit                                                         
                                                                                    
               taw-šok-ø-koy-kit                                                    
                                                                                    
               STAND-STH-3STATS-say-PROG                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘He is standing and saying things, i.e. he is preaching.’ (1932:69:1)
                                                                                    
          This feature seems to be the result of the extremely slow speech required to dictate texts, and the
                                                                                    
          attempt to articulate the words clearly for Gatschet.                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          H-stopping. Just as preaspiration is a feature of the speech of Tottokš; h-stopping is a feature of
                                                                                    
          the speech of Kišyuc. H-stopping causes intervocalic h to surface as k, for example [yaˊko] ‘I eat
                                                                                    
          it’ for yáho. H-stopping is much rarer in texts than in individual elicited items, so it seems that it
                                                                                    
          is an artifact of unnaturally slow speech on the part of Kišyuc.          
                                                                                    
          Lack of orthographic h. At times, initial, medial, and final h are not written in the field notes.
                                                                                    
          Initial h is not written primarily before a, i, and o, thus [at-] for hat-, ‘reflexive,’ [i-] for hi- first-
                                                                                    
          person singular object/stative subject, and [ok-] for hok- ‘reciprocal. Medial h is usually
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         36                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          represented by a hyphen; only variations in spelling show that the h is present, [o-inaˊka] /
                                                                                    
          [ahenaˊka] ahinákka ‘how.’ Final h often can be recovered only when a word ending in h is
                                                                                    
          followed by suffixes, [yukiˊt iccukiáˊ-ikoˊ] / [hin cokiaˊx koˊxca] yukít iššokyáhkóh ‘we want to
                                                                                    
          eat’/ nšokyáhkóhš á ‘do you want to eat?’                                 
                                                                                    
          Variable initial h. At other times a vowel-initial word is recorded with an initial h; primarily
                                                                                    
          words beginning in i, but also beginning in o. It is possible that some of these words do have an
                                                                                    
          initial h, only in that most of the recordings lack it. Two words that are recorded with initial h
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          which almost certainly lack it are ité·t ‘father,’ and oké·t ‘mother.’ Many kin terms begin with h,
                                                                                    
          such as hišán ‘mother-in-law,’ hišile·t ‘younger sister of a woman,’ hiláy ‘wife,’ hipá ‘husband,’
                                                                                    
          and hiyó·l ‘son;’ thus the occasional initial h is based on the pattern of the other kin terms.9
                                                                                    
          The status of orthographic x. The possibility of there being two glottal fricatives, h and x
                                                                                    
          must be explored. Swanton’s characters [x] and [x̥] are his retranscriptions of Gatschet’s {q},
                                                                                    
          {’h} and {χ}. The sequence Gatschet writes {’h} however, is only retranscribed as [x] before a
                                                                                    
          consonant, it is left [’h] after a vowel. There are very few examples of [x] in the corpus. It never
                                                                                    
          occurs word initially, or medially between two vowels. It occurs occasionally as a careful
                                                                                    
          articulation of word-final h, and as the pronunciation of h before a stop consonant, nasal, or
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          fricative, and in the phonetic process of pre-aspiration. There are no words that are differentiated
                                                                                    
          by the contrast of h and x (compare vowel length). Thus, we can deduce that there is only one
                                                                                    
          glottal fricative, h.                                                     
                                                                                    
          Active phonological processes. There are two primary phonological processes operating in
                                                                                    
          Atakapa. The first is consonant assimilation and the second is vowel coalescence.
                                                                                    
          Consonant assimilation. When a consonant cluster forms (or historically formed) across a
                                                                                    
          morpheme boundary, the two consonants can assimilate. The double consonant thus formed is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         37                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          almost never shown in writing, but variations in orthography show its presence. Note that none
                                                                                    
          of these assimilations are obligatory.                                    
                                                                                    
          šh > šš   iš-hat-cíškiš-at >  iššaccíškišat  ‘we were glad’               
                                                                                    
          nh > nn   tan-héw        >    tannéw         ‘others’                     
                                                                                    
          wh > ww   taw-hat-wén-at >    tawwatwénat    ‘they stood and prayed’      
                                                                                    
          yh > yy   kuy-hat-kól    >    kuyyatkól      ‘sausage’                    
                                                                                    
          ln > ll   líl-nak-líl    >    líllaklíl      ‘very soft’                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ln̥ > nn  pay-ók-cel-n̥  >    payókcenn      ‘when we return’             
                                                                                    
          ŋm > mm   máŋ-maŋ        >    mámmaŋ         ‘long (pl)’                  
                                                                                    
          ŋn > nn   nuŋ-núl        >    nunnúl         ‘dwell in a village’         
                                                                                    
          pk > kk   kóp-kop        >    kókkop         ‘white (pl)’                 
                                                                                    
          tk > kk   šokatkéŋiš     >    šokakkéŋiš     ‘blackberry’                 
                                                                                    
          tc > cc   hi-hat-cewwán-at >  hihaccewwánat  ‘I forgot it’                
                                                                                    
          kp > pp   nak-pémst á    >    nappémst á     ‘are you (pl) tired?’        
                                                                                    
          kt > tt   hiyik-tá·to    >    hiyittá·to     ‘I stand up’                 
                                                                                    
          kc > cc   pakcalšópš     >    paccalšópš;    ‘squirrel’                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          kƛ > ƛƛ   ƛáw-nak-ƛáw    >    ƛáwnaƛƛáw      ‘very light’                 
                                                                                    
          ky >kk    šok-šak-yíkš-kit >  šokšakkíkškit  ‘he is selling’              
                                                                                    
          Vowel coalescence. When a cluster of two vowels is formed by any morphological process, the
                                                                                    
          two vowels may coalesce into a single long vowel. In addition, sequences of VhV may
                                                                                    
          optionally coalesce into a long vowel.                                    
                                                                                    
          há-iš > há·š        ‘he/she (focus)’                                      
                                                                                    
          wí-ik > wí·k        ‘I (ergative)’                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         38                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          né·-ip > né·p       ‘in the land’                                         
                                                                                    
          né-ot > ne·t        ‘downwards’                                           
                                                                                    
          póhip > pó·p        ‘by means of smoke’                                   
                                                                                    
          háhan > há·n        ‘not existing’                                        
                                                                                    
          wéhet > wé·t        ‘me (directional)’                                    
                                                                                    
          hilóhišne > hiló·šne ‘to help me’                                         
                                                                                    
          Vowel coalescence explains certain cases of vowel length in Central Atakapa, for example ká·k
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ‘crow; raven’ Eastern kahak.                                              
                                                                                    
          Allophonic variation. The semi-vowel w can have the voiced bilabial fricative allophone /β/ in
                                                                                    
          initial or medial position; this is written [v] in Gatschet’s notes and the publication; in syllable
                                                                                    
          final position, it has the allophone /φ/, which is written [f]. The nasal consonants n and ŋ are
                                                                                    
          neutralized before k; both nk and ŋk are written [nk]. The nasals m, n, and ŋ are often weakened
                                                                                    
          to vowel nasalization word-finally; this has resulted in variation in citation forms for a few
                                                                                    
          words, such as šokóm / šokón / šokóŋ ‘cow.’                               
                                                                                    
          Initial syllable syncope. Very rarely, polysyllabic words beginning with the syllable šVk- will
                                                                                    
          be syncoped to š.                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Full form      Syncoped form       Meaning                                
                                                                                    
          šaktálko       štálko              ‘Skin-desirer’                         
                                                                                    
          šoknáyke       šnáyke              ‘Duck-owner’                           
                                                                                    
          This syncope is probably the origin of the words škóp ‘dipper,’ and škalškáš ‘to be crazy,’ as
                                                                                    
          initial consonant clusters do not otherwise occur in native Atakapa words.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Notes                                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         39                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1 . Morris Swadesh phonemicized the Atakapa forms from the 1932 publication for his paper on
                                                                                    
          comparing Atakapa to Chitimacha (1946b:115). His phonemicization is very similar to that used
                                                                                    
          here, except that he ignored accent, vowel length, and gemination. Karl-Heinz Gursky (1969:86)
                                                                                    
          used Swadesh’s system, again ignoring accent, vowel length, and gemination, with two
                                                                                    
          additional modifications: he decided to replace ŋ with the cluster nk, and treated all u vowels as
                                                                                    
          if they were o. Neither Swadesh nor Gursky referred to Gatschet’s original field notes. Mary R.
                                                                                    
          Haas (1958:241) developed another phonemicization with the proviso that there were long
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          vowels which she did not write. Pamela Munro used Haas’s system (1994:215) in quoting
                                                                                    
          Atakapa forms in her comparative study of Yuki-Gulf.                      
                                                                                    
          2 . The initial geminate consonant comes from the assimilation of the prefix hat- to the initial
                                                                                    
          consonant of cewwán)                                                      
                                                                                    
          3 . On very rare occasions Gatschet wrote these geminate consonants, for example on page 18 of
                                                                                    
          his field notes (1885b) he wrote {mámang} for the plural of máŋ ‘long,’ and later corrected it to
                                                                                    
          {mámmang} mámmaŋ, the assimilated form of máŋmaŋ.                         
                                                                                    
          4 . Swanton calls the language Gatschet recorded Western Atakapa. However, since there is a
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          recorded Atakapan language to the west, Orkokisak (Swanton’s Akokisa), one can with better
                                                                                    
          justification call Yukhíti Central Atakapa.                               
                                                                                    
          5 . This begins on the very first page of the field notes, where Kišyuc insists on the distinction
                                                                                    
          between šakyól ‘male’ and išá·k yó·l ‘ill-tempered man.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:3).
                                                                                    
          6 . This word seems to be an allegro form of the compound cít ‘tobacco,’ and cišt ‘pipe.’ Given
                                                                                    
          that Gatschet rarely writes geminate consonants, this form may in fact be cícci, without a long
                                                                                    
          vowel at all.                                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         40                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          7 There are examples in Gatschet’s field notes where he scratches out affixed, unstressed =ya,
                                                                                    
          and replaces it with a stressed independent word yá.                      
                                                                                    
          8 . Swanton (1932:69) derives šokóy from šok ‘thing’ and kóy ‘speak,’ thus ‘sayer of things,’
                                                                                    
          and this etymology has been followed by later writers. However the word is never written
                                                                                    
          *[cok’ko-i] but only as [coko-i] or [cox̥ ko-i], the last form showing the preaspiration of k that
                                                                                    
          occurs in the writing of the true compound šok-íti ‘headman’ [cox̥ kiti]. The syllable canon of
                                                                                    
          šokóy, CVCVC, fits that of a monomorphemic word in Atakapa, and in all adjoining languages the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          word for ‘chief’ is monomorphemic.                                        
                                                                                    
          9 . Kin terms show other traces of being reshaped after each other. Many kin-terms end in the
                                                                                    
          sequence -e·t, so in addition to ‘father,’ ‘mother,’ and ‘younger sister of a woman,’ we have
                                                                                    
          kelé·t ‘aunt,’ isn̥cé·t ‘brother of a woman,’ išiwé·t ‘elder sister of a woman,’ wé·t ‘sister/female
                                                                                    
          cousin.’ n̥cé·t ‘brother of a man, (?)’ and iškišé·t ‘sister of a man.’   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         41                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          2. THE VERB AND VERBAL MORPHOLOGY                                         
                                                                                    
           The Atakapa verb has some unique features in comparison with the verbal systems of other
                                                                                    
          Southeastern languages. The verb in the aorist mode is inflected in the first person singular, and
                                                                                    
          in the first, second, and third persons plural by suffixes; in the second- and third-person singular
                                                                                    
          the verb is unmarked, and inflected using independent pronouns. There is a second set of person
                                                                                    
          markers for all persons but the third-person singular which are prefixes, and which indicate
                                                                                    
          direct and indirect objects. These same object prefixes are used to indicate the subjects of verbs
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          of state or emotion, stative verbs, such as occur in all attested Southeastern languages, also
                                                                                    
          termed a split-s system. What is unique about the Atakapa verbal system is that the stative
                                                                                    
          prefixes are used to mark the subjects of active verbs when the verb is inflected for the preterite,
                                                                                    
          dubitative, definite future, progressive, and subordinate modes.          
                                                                                    
          The aorist mode. This mode is used to describe actions in the present, as well as the past and
                                                                                    
          the future if there is no emphasis on the temporal nature of the action. The following is a chart
                                                                                    
          of the inflectional forms for the aorist, with the inflection of the verb ám ‘to drink’ (1932:25:2):
                                                                                    
          1s        ROOT-o    ám-o      ‘I drink it’                                
                                                                                    
          2s   na   ROOT-ø    na ám     ‘you (sg) drink it’                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          3s   ha   ROOT-ø    ha ám     ‘he/she drinks it’                          
                                                                                    
          1pl       ROOT-cel  ám-cel    ‘we drink it’                               
                                                                                    
          2pl       ROOT-tem  ám-tem    ‘you (pl) drink it’                         
                                                                                    
          3pl       ROOT-ul   *ám-ul    ‘they drink it’1                            
                                                                                    
          2simp     ROOT-ø    ám        ‘drink it!’    (1932:26:1)                  
                                                                                    
          1plimp    ROOT-lu   ám-lu     ‘let us drink it!’                          
                                                                                    
          2plimp    ROOT-to   ám-to     ‘drink it, you all!’                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         42                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          First person singular                                                     
                                                                                    
               [aˊñ-idsōˊn otōˊlco ‘nkēˊtne yukiˊt aˊnkin]                          
                                                                                    
          25)  aŋicón itólšo n̥ké·tne yukít áŋkin                                   
                                                                                    
               aŋicon    itolš-o   n̥-ke·t-ne          yukit aŋ-kin                 
                                                                                    
               room      prepare-1SS 2SSTATS-stay-GER  our  house-LOC               
                                                                                    
               ‘I have prepared a room for you to stay in at our house.’ (1932:12:42-43)
                                                                                    
          Second person singular:                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [naˊ teˊm ǎˊm]                                                       
                                                                                    
          26)  ná tém ám                                                            
                                                                                    
               na        tem       am-ø                                             
                                                                                    
               you(sg)   yesterday drink-2/3SS                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘You drank yesterday.’ (1932:83:1)                                   
                                                                                    
          Third person singular:                                                    
                                                                                    
               [haˊ yaˊx]                                                           
                                                                                    
          27)  há yáh                                                               
                                                                                    
               ha   yáh-ø                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               s/he eat-2/3SS                                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘He eats it.’ (1932:32:2)

               [Yukiˊt noˊmcsĕm tuhtsĕl yukiˊt nuk]                                 
                                                                                    
          28)  yukít nómšsim túhcel yukít nuk                                       
                                                                                    
               yukit nomš-šim      tuh-cel        yukit nuk                         
                                                                                    
               we   child-NOTHING.BUT stay(pl)-1PLS we alone                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         43                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘It was nothing but us children; we stayed there all by ourselves.’ (1932:17:107-08)
                                                                                    
          Second person plural:                                                     
                                                                                    
               [naˊkīt oˊktěm makeˊne]                                              
                                                                                    
          29)  nakìt óktem makene                                                   
                                                                                    
               nakit     ok-tem    make-ne                                          
                                                                                    
               you(pl)   come-2PLS trade-GER                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘You all come in order to trade.’ (1932:79:1)                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Third person plural:                                                      
                                                                                    
               [icāk heˊ-u kiˊwilc ipcoˊx̥ kuˊl]                                    
                                                                                    
          30)  iša·khéw kíwilš ipšókul                                              
                                                                                    
               iša·k-hew kiwilš    ipšok-ul                                         
                                                                                    
               person-PL white.person be.doctor-3PLS                                
                                                                                    
               ‘The men are European doctors.’ (1932:67:2)                          
                                                                                    
          Hortative and Imperative. There are special forms only for the first person plural hortative (-
                                                                                    
          lo) and the second person plural imperative (-to). The second person singular is usually the bare
                                                                                    
          stem of the verb, although there is an occasional suffix -n̥ , which is found more frequently with
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          negative imperatives (see the section below on negation). First person singular and third person
                                                                                    
          imperatives are handled using a double-verb construction with the verb nawš ‘to let; permit.’ The
                                                                                    
          following are examples:                                                   
                                                                                    
               [Oˊk, ichuˊ]                                                         
                                                                                    
          31)  ók išhú                                                              
                                                                                    
               ok-ø      iš-hu-ø                                                    
                                                                                    
               come-2SIMP 1PLO-see-2SIMP                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         44                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Come! See us!’ (1932:14:68)                                         
                                                                                    
               [cakwaˊntsěn]                                                        
                                                                                    
          32)  šakwáncn̥                                                            
                                                                                    
               šak-wanc-n̥                                                          
                                                                                    
               3PLO-say-2SIMP                                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘Tell it to them!’ (1932:143:1)                                      
                                                                                    
               [mon hoˊktiˊwe puˊmlo]                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          33)  mo·n hoktiwé·púmlo                                                   
                                                                                    
               mo·n hok-tiwe·-pum-lo                                                
                                                                                    
               all  RECIP-be.together-dance-1PLIMP                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘Let us all dance together!” (1932:107:1)                            
                                                                                    
               [yukiˊt tiuoˊ-ilo]                                                   
                                                                                    
          34)  yukít tiwóylo                                                        
                                                                                    
               yukit tiw-oy-lo                                                      
                                                                                    
               we   GO(PL)-sleep-1PLIMP                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘Let us go and sleep!’ (1932:96:1)                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [yukiˊt aˊñ ot tūˊkto]                                               
                                                                                    
          35)  yukít áŋot túkto                                                     
                                                                                    
               yukit aŋ-ot    tuk-to                                                
                                                                                    
               our  house-DIR bring-2PLIMP                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘Bring it to our house, you all!’ (1932:136:2)                       
                                                                                    
               [hoktewē to]                                                         
                                                                                    
          36)  hoktiwé·to                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         45                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hok-tiwé·-to                                                         
                                                                                    
               RECIP-be.together-2PLIMP                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘Be together with one another, you all!’ (1932:133:2)                
                                                                                    
          Object/Patient prefixes. There are distinct prefixes used to reference pronoun objects on the
                                                                                    
          verb. Depending on the semantics of the verb they will refer to direct objects or
                                                                                    
          dative/benefactive objects. If there is a chance of ambiguity, the dative/benefactive object is
                                                                                    
          cross-referenced by a noun phrase in the directional case. In addition, these prefixes are used to
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          mark the subjects of stative verbs in the aorist mode, and the subjects of all verbs in the other
                                                                                    
          modes. This reflects the basic split-s nature of Atakapa grammar (after Dixon, 1994:71-72).
                                                                                    
          1s   hi-            1pl  iš-                                              
                                                                                    
          2s`  n̥-            2pl  nak-                                             
                                                                                    
          3s   ø-             3pl  šak- ~ ø-                                        
                                                                                    
          The following illustrates the inflectional prefixes for stative verbs in the aorist which are also
                                                                                    
          used for subject and object prefixes in all other modes. The example is lák (sg) and lákla·k (pl)
                                                                                    
          ‘to be strong’ (1932:75:1):                                               
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT   wí hila·k      ‘I am strong’                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          2s        n̥-ROOT   ná n̥lá·k      ‘you are strong’                       
                                                                                    
          3s        ø-ROOT    há lá·k        ‘he is strong’                         
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT   yukít išlákla·k ‘we are strong’                       
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT  nakít naklákla·k ‘you (pl) are strong’                
                                                                                    
          3pl       ø-ROOT 2  hakít lákla·k  ‘they are strong’                      
                                                                                    
          The examples below show these prefixes in their use as objects of transitive verbs.
                                                                                    
               [hiˊmic kauˊkau]                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         46                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          37)  himìš kawkàw                                                         
                                                                                    
               hi-miš-ø            kawkaw                                           
                                                                                    
               1SOBJ-give-2SIMP    water                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘Give me water!” (1932:80:2)                                         
                                                                                    
               [‘n huˊ-u ha]                                                        
                                                                                    
          38)  n̥húoháh                                                             
                                                                                    
               n̥-hu-o-hah                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               2SOBJ-see-1SS-NEG                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I do not see you.’ (1932:34:1)                                      
                                                                                    
               [icpaˊts haˊhat]                                                     
                                                                                    
          39)  išpácháhat                                                           
                                                                                    
               iš-ø-pac-hah-at                                                      
                                                                                    
               1PLOBJ-3STATS-beat-NEG-PRET                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘He did not beat us.’ (1932:16:103)                                  
                                                                                    
               [haˊ naˊk tsuˊmĕt]                                                   
                                                                                    
          40)  há nakcúmat                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ha   nak-ø-cum-at                                                    
                                                                                    
               he   2PLOBJ-3STATS-pinch-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘He pinched you all.’ (1932:123:1)                                   
                                                                                    
          Preterite mode. The preterite mode marker is the suffix -at. It is notable that this mode is split
                                                                                    
          so that first and second person subjects are inflected by means of the stative/object prefixes,
                                                                                    
          while third-person subjects use the active suffixes. The primary meaning of the preterite mode is
                                                                                    
          that the action of the verb is completed, so it is frequently equivalent to the past tense. However,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         47                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          it is also resultative, so if the present state of the subject is the result of a completed action, the
                                                                                    
          preterite can also refer to a present tense. A paradigm is not provided in the publication,
                                                                                    
          although a paradigm which only includes the second and third persons singular and plural is
                                                                                    
          found in Gatschet’s manuscripts (1885c:3).                                
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT-at          *hi-ám-at      ‘I drank’                    
                                                                                    
          2s        n̥-ROOT-at          n̥-ám-at       ‘you drank’                  
                                                                                    
          3s        ROOT-ø-at           ám-ø-at        ‘he/she drank’               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT-at          *iš-ám-at      ‘we drank’                   
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT-at         nak-ám-at      ‘you all drank’              
                                                                                    
          3pl       ø-ROOT-ul-at        ám-ul-at       ‘they drank’                 
                                                                                    
          Examples of preterite forms of verbs are given below.                     
                                                                                    
          First person singular                                                     
                                                                                    
               [wi hikaˊwĕt]                                                        
                                                                                    
          41)  wí hikáwat                                                           
                                                                                    
               wi   hi-kaw-at                                                       
                                                                                    
               I    1SSTATS-be.dead/drunk-PRET                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I was drunk.’ Or, with resultative meaning, ‘I am drunk.’ (1932:60:2)
                                                                                    
               [hilaˊwet]                                                           
                                                                                    
          42)  hiláwat                                                              
                                                                                    
               hi-law-at                                                            
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-be.burned- PRET                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘I got burned.’ (1932:40:1)                                          
                                                                                    
          Second person singular

               wi   ika·t n̥-pak-at                                                 
                                                                                    
               my   foot 2SSTATS-step.on- PRET                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘You stepped on my foot.’ (1932:20:143)

               [nak cakaˊxlecat]                                                    
                                                                                    
          47)  nakšakáɬišat                                                         
                                                                                    
               nak-šak-aƛiš-at
