               ‘You all lost them’ (1932:13:58)                                     
                                                                                    
          Third person plural                                                       
                                                                                    
               [hakiˊt teˊm ăˊmulĕt]                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          48)  hakít tém ámulat                                                     
                                                                                    
               hakit tem      am-ul-at                                              
                                                                                    
               they yesterday drink-3PLS-PRET                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘They drank yesterday.’ (1932:129:1)                                 
                                                                                    
          Resultative                                                               
                                                                                    
               [yaˊ ka-ucnăˊ paˊl’at]                                               
                                                                                    
          49)  yá káwšne páłat                                                      
                                                                                    
               ya   kawšne    ø-paƛ-at                                              
                                                                                    
               that comb      3STATS-be.broken-PRET                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘That comb is broken.’ (1932:102:2)                                  
                                                                                    
          Dubitative mode. This mode like the preterite mode, is split, with first and second person
                                                                                    
          subjects being marked by the stative/object prefixes, and third person subjects are marked by the
                                                                                    
          active suffixes. This mode indicates that an action may take place, but does not make a
                                                                                    
          judgement on its probability. The dubitative is usually translated as a future in Gatschet and
                                                                                    
          Swanton, but it is often qualified with some element of doubt, and this, with the examples,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         50                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          suggests its meaning. The dubitative is marked by the suffix -ehe. A full paradigm using the
                                                                                    
          verb am ‘to drink’ is found in the publication (1932:26:1).               
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT-ehe         hi-ám-ehe      ‘I might drink it’           
                                                                                    
          2s        n̥-ROOT-ehe         n̥-ám-ehe      ‘you might drink it’         
                                                                                    
          3s        ROOT-ø-ehe          ám-ø-ehe       ‘he/she might drink it’      
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT-ehe         iš-ám-ehe      ‘we might drink it’          
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT-ehe        nak-ám-ehe     ‘you all might drink it’     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          3pl       ROOT-ul-ehe         ám-ul-ehe      ‘they might drink it’        
                                                                                    
          The following are further examples:                                       
                                                                                    
               [Yíl mōn taˊ-i ō hoˊktiwē icwāˊñĕhĕ]                                 
                                                                                    
          50)  yíl mo·n táy o hoktiwé·išwáŋehe                                      
                                                                                    
               yil  mo·n tay  o         hok-tiwe·-iš-waŋ-ehe                        
                                                                                    
               day  every river along   RECIP-be.together-1PLSTATS-walk-DUB 3       
                                                                                    
               ‘We might walk along the river together every day.’ (1932:13:45)     
                                                                                    
               [ha hilaˊ-i aˊv oˊt’ĕhĕ]

          Future mode. This mode, marked by the suffix -tikit, is used to indicate an action that will
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          definitely occur in the future. In this mode, all subjects are indicated by the stative/object
                                                                                    
          prefixes. Gatschet’s glosses do not distinguish clearly between the dubitative and the future;
                                                                                    
          however, in the one text example (54 below) the distinction becomes clear:
                                                                                    
               {iyā́ng tī́via ná-u o’ho’h ishyā́mcne áyip kunä́ ka’hlṓpc tsäma kúts shoχmōn
                                                                                    
          54)  iyáŋ tíwya náwohóh išyá·mšne áyip konán kathópš cimakúc šokmó·n      
                                                                                    
               ish yā́mshĕhĕ kidsónkshkin i shakwāktikit4}                          
                                                                                    
               išyá·mšehe kicóŋškin iššakwáktikit                                   
                                                                                    
               iyaŋ tiw=ya    nawohoh   iš-ya·mš-ne         ay-ip     konan         
                                                                                    
               there go=and   chinquapin 1PLSTATS-gather-GER swamp-LOC potato       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kathopš   cimakuc   šok-mo·n  iš-ya·mš-ehe        kicoŋš-kin         
                                                                                    
               waterlily crawfish  thing-all 1PLSTATS-gather-DUB fire-LOC           
                                                                                    
               iš-šak-wak-tikit                                                     
                                                                                    
               1PLSTATS-PL.OBJ-roast-FUT                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘Us going there and gathering chinquapins; we may gather everything, duck
                                                                                    
               potatoes, waterlilies, and crawfish. We will roast them in the fire.’ (Gatschet,
                                                                                    
               1885b:154)                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         52                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          In this example, the speaker is telling the listener the things that they may gather when they go
                                                                                    
          out together, but whatever they have gathered they will definitely roast.5 A further semantic
                                                                                    
          difference between the future and the dubitative is noted by Gatschet (1885c:[10]), in that while
                                                                                    
          an interrogative can be formed from a future, it is not permitted to be formed on a dubitative. In
                                                                                    
          this mode, the third person plural form is formally identical to the third person singular form, and
                                                                                    
          only context distinguishes the two. An almost complete paradigm of the future is given in the
                                                                                    
          publication (1932:25:2).                                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT-tikit       *hi-ám-tikit6  ‘I shall drink it’           
                                                                                    
          2s        n̥-ROOT-tikit       n-ám-tikit     ‘you will drink it’          
                                                                                    
          3s        ø-ROOT-tikit        ø-ám-tikit     ‘he/she will drink it’       
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT-tikit       iš-ám-tikit    ‘we shall drink it’          
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT-tikit      nak-ám-tikit   ‘you all will drink it’      
                                                                                    
          3pl       ø-ROOT-tikit        ø-ám-tikit     ‘they will drink it’         
                                                                                    
          The following are further examples of the future:                         
                                                                                    
               [haˊl hiwaˊntikit haˊ]                                               
                                                                                    
          55)  hál hiwáŋtikithah                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hal  hi-waŋ-tikit-hah                                                
                                                                                    
               again 1SSTATS-walk-FUT-NEG                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘I shall not walk again.’ (1932:141:1)                               
                                                                                    
               [‘nkaˊ-utikit]                                                       
                                                                                    
          56)  n̥káwtikit                                                           
                                                                                    
               n̥-kaw-tikit                                                         
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-die-FUT                                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         53                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘You will die.’ (1932:60:2)                                          
                                                                                    
               [uctiˊkit]                                                           
                                                                                    
          57)  ùštikit                                                              
                                                                                    
               ø-uš-tikit                                                           
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.so- FUT                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘It will be so.’ (1932:139:2)                                        
                                                                                    
          Progressive mode. This mode is uniquely marked by the suffix -kit for singular subject and -tit
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          for plural subject. It indicates that the action of the verb to which it is suffixed is ongoing at the
                                                                                    
          time of speaking. The paradigm with the verb ám is found only in Gatschet’s field notes
                                                                                    
          (1883c:10), not in the publication.                                       
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT-kit    hi-ám-kit           ‘I am drinking it’           
                                                                                    
          2s        n̥-ROOT-kit    n̥-ám-kit           ‘you are drinking it’        
                                                                                    
          3s        ø-ROOT-kit     ø-ám-kit            ‘he/she is drinking it’      
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT-tit    iš-ám-tit           ‘we are drinking it’         
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT-tit   nak-ám-tit          ‘you all are drinking it’    
                                                                                    
          3pl       ø-ROOT-tit     ø-ám-tit or šak-ám-tit ‘they are drinking it’    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          First person singular:                                                    
                                                                                    
               [himakauˊkit]                                                        
                                                                                    
          58)  himakáwkit                                                           
                                                                                    
               hi-makaw-kit                                                         
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-fall(sg)-PROG(SG)                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘I am falling.’ (1932:78:1)                                          
                                                                                    
               [wic aˊ cokihuˊkit haˊx̥ c]                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         54                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          59)  wí·š á šokhihúkitháhš                                                
                                                                                    
               wi-iš a   šok-hi-hu-kit-hahš                                         
                                                                                    
               I-foc this sth-1SSTATS-see-PROG(SG)-NEG                              
                                                                                    
               ‘I don’t see anything.’ (1932:34:2)                                  
                                                                                    
          Second person singular:                                                   
                                                                                    
               [nkaˊkit]                                                            
                                                                                    
          60)  n̥kákit                                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               n̥-ka-kit                                                            
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-do-PROG(SG)                                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘You are doing it.’ (1932:55:2)                                      
                                                                                    
          Third person singular:                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Tsīs wāñ-haihaiˊckit]

               ciš  ø-waŋ-hayhayš-kit                                               
                                                                                    
               baby 3STATS-go.about-cry-PROG(SG)

               {kaukáu ishámtit á}

          Third person plural:                                                      
                                                                                    
               [naˊ-u taˊ-u-walwaˊlctit]

               naw       taw-ø-walwalš-tit                                          
                                                                                    
               feather STAND-3STATS-wave-PROG(PL)                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘The feathers are waving.’ (1932:131:1)                              
                                                                                    
               {shakshṓsh shóksho wáng-shólktit}                                   
                                                                                    
          66)  šakšó·š šakšò wáŋšólktit                                             
                                                                                    
               šakšo·š   šak-šo    waŋ-ø-šolk-tit                                   
                                                                                    
               bird      PL-seed   go.about-3STATS-pick.up.many-PROG(PL)            
                                                                                    
               ‘The birds are going about picking up the seeds.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:90)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         56                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Subordinate mode. The subordinate mode is the form of verbs in clauses that provide
                                                                                    
          additional information about the action of the verb in the main clause. The principal suffixes of
                                                                                    
          the subordinate mode are -en, ‘if; when’ -kin, ‘when,’ and -ip ‘because.’ The time of the verb in
                                                                                    
          the subordinate mode is dependent on the verb in the main clause; it is not marked in this mode.
                                                                                    
          Any first- or second-person subjects are marked on the verb with the prefixes that mark direct
                                                                                    
          objects in the aorist mode.                                               
                                                                                    
          The suffix -n̥ . This suffix indicates that if one action takes place, another will follow, or if an
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          action took place, another happened as a result. It is best translated by ‘if’ or ‘when.’
                                                                                    
          Occasionally syllabic –n̥ appears as –n after a vowel. The following paradigm from is Gatschet
                                                                                    
          (1885c:[6]).                                                              
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT-n̥     hi-ám-n̥       ‘if I drink it’                   
                                                                                    
          2s        n-ROOT-n̥      n-ám-n̥        ‘if you drink it’                 
                                                                                    
          3s        ø-ROOT-n̥      ø-ám-n̥        ‘if he/she drinks it’             
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT-n̥     iš-ám-n̥       ‘if we drink it’                  
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT-n̥    nak-ám-n̥      ‘if you all drink it’             
                                                                                    
          3pl       šak-ROOT-n̥    šak-ám-n̥      ‘if they drink it’                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Iˊcāk hilaˊ-i tanuˊk keˊat. icāk hilaˊ-i tsīk keˊ-ĕn hatseˊ-ec]     
                                                                                    
          67)  išà·k hiláy tanúk kéat. išá·k hiláy cí·k kén̥ hacehéš                
                                                                                    
               iša·k     hilay tanuk ø-ke-at.     iša·k     hilay ci·k              
                                                                                    
               person    wife one  3STATS-have-PRET person  wife two                
                                                                                    
               ø-ke-n̥        haceheš                                               
                                                                                    
               3STATS-have-IF be.bad                                                
                                                                                    
               ‘A man had only one wife. If a man has two wives, it is a bad thing.’(1932:10;15-16)

               [Icāˊk hokwaˊñc wāˊñĕn haˊ cok’-okināˊtsne mōn hoktiwēˊ imōˊculat]

               iša·k     hokwaŋš   ø-waŋ-n̥       ha   šok-hok-inac-ne

          action.

          ul and the first-person plural aorist suffix -cel; in the former case there is no recorded
                                                                                    
          assimilation, but in the latter case the sequence ln̥ becomes nn.

               [yukiˊt paiˊ ōˊktsen]                                                
                                                                                    
          77)  yukít payókcenn                                                      
                                                                                    
               yukit pay-ok-cel-n̥                                                  
                                                                                    
               we   BACK-come-1PLS-IF                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘when we return…’ (1932:158:1)                                       
                                                                                    
          The suffix -kin. This temporal affix indicates that the action of the verb to which it is suffixed
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          has to occur before the action of the verb of the main clause. It can be translated ‘when;’ more
                                                                                    
          literally ‘the time when.’ It is homophonous with the locative suffix -kin and may be formally
                                                                                    
          identical to it. A nearly complete paradigm occurs in Gatschet’s field notes (1885c:[6])
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ROOT-kin    hi-ám-kin      ‘when I drink it’                 
                                                                                    
          2s        n̥-ROOT-kin    n̥-ám-kin      ‘when you drink it’               
                                                                                    
          3s        ø-ROOT-kin     ø-ám-kin       ‘when he/she drinks it’           
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ROOT-kin    *iš-ám-kin8    ‘when we drink it’                
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ROOT-kin   nak-ám-kin     ‘when you all drink it’           
                                                                                    
          3pl       šak-ROOT-kin   šak-ám-kin     ‘when they drink it’              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Taˊkapa kic n icāˊk kaˊ-ukin īt utskaˊ-uculat okyuˊlik yaˊ okaˊ oˊtsi]
                                                                                    
          78)  tákapa kíšn̥ išá·k káwkin í·t uckáwšulat okyúlikya okká óci          
                                                                                    
               takapa    kiš=n̥    iša·k ø-kaw-kin          i·t                     
                                                                                    
               Atakapa   woman=and man  3STATS-die(sg)-WHEN face                    
                                                                                    
               uckawš-ul-at                                                         
                                                                                    
               cover-3PLS-PRET                                                      
                                                                                    
               okyul-ik=ya              okka oci                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         61                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               handkerchief-ERG.INSTR=and cloth on.top

               handkerchief and put a cloth on top.’ (1932:18:120)                  
                                                                                    
               [Hikaˊ-uckin kaˊt oˊts paˊkĕt]

               ‘When I was sitting and drinking, Jack fell down.’ (1932:62:2)       
                                                                                    
               [Wāˊñ-hokpēˊmkin iˊcāk cakōˊñne okcĕkiaˊlat]                         
                                                                                    
          81)  wáŋhokpé·mkin iša·kšakkóŋne okšakyálat                               
                                                                                    
               ø-waŋ-hok-pe·m-kin       iša·kšakkoŋne                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               3STATS-go.about-RECIP-shoot-WHEN sheriff                             
                                                                                    
               ok-šak-yal-at                                                        
                                                                                    
               COME.AND-3PLO-sieze(pl)-PRET                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘When they were going about shooting at each other, the sheriff came and
                                                                                    
               arrested them.’ (1932:20:147-48)                                     
                                                                                    
          The suffix -ip. This suffix indicates that the action of the main verb of the clause is a result of
                                                                                    
          the action of the verb to which it is suffixed. It can thus be translated ‘because.’ This suffix is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         62                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          homophonous to the inessive case suffix, and may be formally identical to it. The only examples
                                                                                    
          of its use were obtained through elicitation; none occur in the texts. In addition, Gatschet did not
                                                                                    
          elicit a paradigm, as he did for the other suffixes of the subordinate mode.
                                                                                    
               [hicokēcip hioˊk aˊxcat]                                             
                                                                                    
          82)  hišoké·šip hiókháhšat                                                
                                                                                    
               hi-šoke·š-ip        hi-ok-hahš-at                                    
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-be.sick-CAUSE 1SSTATS-come-NEG-PRET                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Because I was sick, I did not come.’ (1932:38:1)                    
                                                                                    
               [Jack an laˊkin inhoˊlcilat hiweˊ-uka aˊmip]                         
                                                                                    
          83)  <Jack> aŋlá·kkin inhólšulat hiwéwka ámip                             
                                                                                    
               <Jack>    aŋ-la·k-kin    inholš-ul-at   hiwew-ka                     
                                                                                    
               PN        house-strong-LOC put.in-3PLS-PRET be.strong-ADV            
                                                                                    
               ø-am-ip                                                              
                                                                                    
               3STATS-drink-CAUSE                                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘They put Jack in jail because he was drinking hard.’ (1932:26:1)    
                                                                                    
          Gerund. The gerund is formed by suffixing -ne to the verb root. The gerund is a noun, and
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          supplies the verbal infinitive. A gerund can be formed from a compound verb, and from a
                                                                                    
          negated verb. In addition, the gerundial suffix derives new nouns from verbs. It was especially
                                                                                    
          productive in creating new terms for items of European technology, for which see the section on
                                                                                    
          nominalization. The gerund is used as the complement to verbs such as ka ‘to make’ kóhš ‘to
                                                                                    
          want,’ óŋš ‘not to want,’ hatpéh ‘to be ready to,’ and nám ‘to beg.’ The gerund also is used to
                                                                                    
          nominalize a verb phrase to create a relative clause equivalent (see the chapter below on syntax).
                                                                                    
          The gerund has the variant -n, which occurs after the vowel a.            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         63                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [kitsaˊk aˊmne toˊl haˊ]                                             
                                                                                    
          84)  kicá·k ámne tólháh                                                   
                                                                                    
               kica·k    am-ne     ø-tol-hah                                        
                                                                                    
               whiskey   drink-GER 3STATS-be.good-NEG                               
                                                                                    
               ‘It is not good to drink whiskey.’ (1932:135:1)                      
                                                                                    
               [puˊns’n kaˊ-u]                                                      
                                                                                    
          85)  púnsne káo                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               punš-ne   ka-o

               ‘I make him blow.’ (1932:107:2)

               nima-n    ø-kohš-hah-ul-at

          The gerund is also used to mean ‘for V-ing.’ In this usage its meaning is very close to that of the
                                                                                    
          participle.

          The verb háhš ‘not to have’ has an irregular gerund, há·n. This derives from the occasional form
                                                                                    
          háhan10 which does not use the verbalizing suffix –š. The gerund shows the regular shortening
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          of VhV to V·.                                                             
                                                                                    
               [Icāˊk icaˊt haˊn huˊ-ulat]

               person    head not.to.have(GER) see-3PLS-PRET

               someone   1SO-help-GER   not.to.have(GER)

           The verb ké, ‘to have,’ has an irregular gerund, ké·tne, which is homophonous with the gerund
                                                                                    
          of ké·t, ‘to sit.’ Tottokš insisted that the two forms were distinct. A regular form kéne also
                                                                                    
          occurs.                                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         66                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Participle. The participle is formed by suffixing -ta to the verb root. The participle is
                                                                                    
          uninflected for person of the subject, which is often understood through context alone. This
                                                                                    
          participle expresses purpose, and can be translated as ‘in order to,’ or ‘to be supposed to.’ This
                                                                                    
          is clearest when the participle depends on another verb.                  
                                                                                    
               [hakīt cakyīˊkcta oˊkulĕt]                                           
                                                                                    
          96)  hakít šakyíkšta ókulat                                               
                                                                                    
               hakit šak-yikš-ta   ok-ul-at                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               they 3PLO-buy-PCPL  come-3PLS-PRET                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘They came in order to buy things.’ (1932:155:2)                     
                                                                                    
               [cakikoˊmta oˊko]                                                    
                                                                                    
          97)  šakikómta óko                                                        
                                                                                    
               šak-ikom-ta    ok-o                                                  
                                                                                    
               3PLO-fish-PCPL come-1SS                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘I come for fishing.’ (1932:70:2)                                    
                                                                                    
          The participle can occur without a verb on which it depends or an expressed pronominal subject.
                                                                                    
          The construction is translated “one is to V” and if the subject is not expressed, it is then
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          understood from context.                                                  
                                                                                    
               [Yiˊl paˊxĕ ‘nyaˊ-uta ‘noˊkne]                                       
                                                                                    
          98)  yíl páhw n̥yáwta n̥ókne                                              
                                                                                    
               yil  pahw      n̥-yaw-ta           n̥-ok-ne                          
                                                                                    
               day  seven     2SOBJ-wait.for-PCPL 2SSTATS-come-GER                  
                                                                                    
               ‘I am to wait seven days for you to come.’ (1932:12:42)

          A negative form of the participle also occurs.                            
                                                                                    
               {maká-u háχshta}                                                     
                                                                                    
          101) makáwháhšta                                                          
                                                                                    
               makaw-hahš-ta                                                        
                                                                                    
               fall(sg)-NEG-PCPL                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘It is not to fall.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:32)                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Except for example 98, the participle occurs only in elicited material. In the texts purpose is
                                                                                    
          indicated by the gerund, as in example 90 passim above.                   
                                                                                    
          Stative verbs. A stative verb is a verb in which the affixes which inflect the verb for notational
                                                                                    
          subject are the same as the ones that mark the direct object of a transitive verb. This kind of
                                                                                    
          verbal inflection, a split-S system, is widespread among languages in the Southeast. In contrast
                                                                                    
          to the neighboring languages to the east, Chitimacha (Swadesh, 1946a:326), west, Tonkawa
                                                                                    
          (Hoijer 1933:70), and north, Caddo (Melnar, 2004:42-45) where stative verbal inflection is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         68                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          restricted, in Atakapa all verbs, except those in the aorist mode, are inflected as stative verbs. In
                                                                                    
          the aorist mode itself, there are a handful of verbs that are always inflected as stative verbs.
                                                                                    
          Aorist stative verbs primarily refer to emotional or physical states over which the subject has
                                                                                    
          little or no control.                                                     
                                                                                    
               [ayiˊl kaukauˊkit hēˊyilākc]                                         
                                                                                    
          102) ayíl kawkáwkit hiilákc                                               
                                                                                    
               a-yil     kawkaw-kit     hi-ilakc                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               this-day  rain-PROG(SG)  1SSTATS-be.bored/lazy                       
                                                                                    
               ‘It is raining today; I am bored.’ (1932:41:1)                       
                                                                                    
               [mpeˊtsta]                                                           
                                                                                    
          103) n̥péct á                                                             
                                                                                    
               n̥-pect        a                                                     
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-be.tired(sg) Q                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘Are you tired?’ (1932:105:1)                                        
                                                                                    
          The verbs ké ‘to have something,’ and héwš, ‘to have a lot of something,’ in the aorist are always
                                                                                    
          inflected as stative verbs.                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [kuˊtsnă(n) nakēˊa]                                                  
                                                                                    
          104) kúcnan nakké á                                                       
                                                                                    
               kucnan    nak-ke         a

          Full paradigms of the stative verbs ké ‘to have’ (1932:63:1) and óŋš ‘not to want’ (1932:98:1)
                                                                                    
          appear in the corpus. In the published paradigm, ké does not distinguish between third person
                                                                                    
          singular and plural subjects; however, examples such as 236 show the third-person plural subject
                                                                                    
          marker to be šak- as well.                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1s        hi-ké          hi-óŋš                                           
                                                                                    
          2s        n-ké           n-óŋš                                            
                                                                                    
          3s        ké             óŋš                                              
                                                                                    
          1pl       iš-ké          iš-óŋš                                           
                                                                                    
          2pl       nak-ké         nak-óŋš                                          
                                                                                    
          3pl       ké             šak-óŋš                                          
                                                                                    
          Negation. The suffix -háh/-háhš negates the action of the verb. Its location in the verb complex
                                                                                    
          is somewhat free. It always follows the affixes of the future and progressive modes, and usually
                                                                                    
          directly follows the root in the aorist mode. Occasionally it precedes the third-person plural
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          suffix -ul, although doing so seems to make no difference in meaning. The negative suffix
                                                                                    
          always precedes the gerund suffix -ne and the preterite mode suffix -at.  
                                                                                    
               [haˊl hiwaˊntikit haˊ]                                               
                                                                                    
          106) hál hiwáŋtikitháh                                                    
                                                                                    
               hal  hi-waŋ-tikit-hah                                                
                                                                                    
               again 1SSTATS-walk-FUT-NEG                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘I shall not walk again.’ (1932:141:1)                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         70                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [itiyiˊlc ihukit’ha]                                                 
                                                                                    
          107) itiyílš hihúkitháh                                                   
                                                                                    
               itiyilš hi-hu-kit-hah                                                
                                                                                    
               moon 1SSTATS-see-PROG(SG)-NEG                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘I do not see the moon.’ (1932:156:1)                                
                                                                                    
               [Taneˊ-u cakinaˊ-uc koˊxcaˊhulat]

          An interesting feature of Atakapa is the existence of suppletive negation. Three common verbs,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ké ‘to have,’ kóh, ‘to want’ and úhc ‘to be able’ have suppletive negatives: háhš ‘not to have,’
                                                                                    
          óŋš ‘not to want; not to like,’ and áwš ‘not to be able.’ All three verbs are inflected as statives in
                                                                                    
          the aorist mode. In the following three pairs of examples, the first of the pair illustrates the
                                                                                    
          positive verb; the second illustrates the suppletive negative verb.       
                                                                                    
               [Ickiciˊl nīt keˊat]                                                 
                                                                                    
          110) iškišíl ní·t kéat

               daughter  five 3STATS-have-PRET                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘He had five daughters.’ (1932:16:97)                                
                                                                                    
               [wi kauˊkau ihāˊc]

               wi   kawkaw    hi-hahš                                               
                                                                                    
               I    water     1SSTATS-not.have                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘I do not have water.’ (1932:58:1)                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Caktaˊlko Yuk’hiˊti icaˊk kaˊ-u haˊtalkoˊat]

               šak-tal-koh     yukhiti  iša·k kaw ha   tal  ø-koh-at
