               ‘They wanted to kill him and were not able to.’ (1932:91:2)          
                                                                                    
          While there is no alternative form of negation for ké, there are occasional examples of normal
                                                                                    
          negation of kóh. For the verb úhc, the suppletive negative is used for the meaning ‘not to be able
                                                                                    
          to do,’ while the normal negative means ‘not to know.’                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [coˊk co heˊ-ux̥ tsaˊ]                                               
                                                                                    
          116) šókšo hiùhccáh                                                       
                                                                                    
               šokšo     hi-uhc-hah                                                 
                                                                                    
               anything  1SSTATS-know-NEG                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘I don’t know anything.’ (1932:138:2)                                
                                                                                    
          A negative imperative is formed by suffixing -háhš or -háhš-n̥ to the verb.
                                                                                    
               {shiwán tsúm háχsh ntsá-ĕhĕ}                                         
                                                                                    
          117) šiwán cúmháhš n̥cáwehe                                               
                                                                                    
               šiwan cum-hahš-ø    n̥-ø-caw-ehe                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               cat  pinch-NEG-2SIMP 2SOBJ-3STATS-bite-DUB                           
                                                                                    
               ‘Don’t pinch the cat, or she might bite you.’ (Gatschet 1885b:41)    
                                                                                    
               {hál wáng háχshn ól ishtiwē ṓk}                                     
                                                                                    
          118) hál wáŋháhšn̥ ó·l ištiwé·ók                                          
                                                                                    
               hal       waŋ-hahš-n̥    o·l  iš-tiwe·-ok-ø                          
                                                                                    
               behind    go.about-NEG-2SIMP near 1PLSTATS-be.with-come-2SIMP        
                                                                                    
               ‘Do not walk behind; come near with us!’ (Gatschet 1885b:49)         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         73                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [iˊuc haˊxcin]                                                       
                                                                                    
          119) íwšháhšn̥                                                            
                                                                                    
               iwš-hahš-n̥                                                          
                                                                                    
               be.angry                                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘Don’t be angry!’ (1932:54:2)                                        
                                                                                    
          Aspect suffixes. These are composed of -hán ‘almost’ -háŋš ‘completive’ -kóh/-kóhš
                                                                                    
          ‘desiderative’ -n̥t- ‘continuative,’ -n̥st ‘customarily,’ –ins/-inso ‘habitually.’ -tán + negative
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ‘before.’ and -tíhi- ‘past progressive.’ All of these suffixes, with the exception of -tán and -tíhi,
                                                                                    
          co-occur with the preterite suffix -at. Given the widespread Atakapa construction of two verbs
                                                                                    
          with the same subject and whose action is occurring at the same time being compounded without
                                                                                    
          additional morphology, these suffixes are clearly, sometimes obviously, independent verbs that
                                                                                    
          have been attracted to the verb stem.                                     
                                                                                    
          Almost. The suffix -hán is attached directly to the verb root of the preterite mode. It is derived
                                                                                    
          from the verb hán ‘to fail to do something.’ The third-person plural suffix -ul and the preterite
                                                                                    
          suffix -at follow it. It indicates that the action of the verb almost, but not quite, took place.
                                                                                    
               [Haˊ hilaˊ-i cok’hēˊc yaˊ kaˊ-u haˊnĕt]                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          120) ha hiláy šoké·šya káwhánat                                           
                                                                                    
               ha   hilay šoke·š=ya ø-kaw-han-at                                    
                                                                                    
               his  wife be.sick=and 3STATS-die(sg)-ALMOST                          
                                                                                    
               ‘His wife was sick and almost died.’ (1932:13:54-55)                 
                                                                                    
               [wi cocoˊlc hinteˊhi hăˊnulet]                                       
                                                                                    
          121) wi šošólšhin̥téhihánulat                                             
                                                                                    
               wi   šošolš-hi-n̥tehi-han-ul-at                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         74                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               I    scare(sg)-1SOBJ-frighten-ALMOST-3PLS-PRET                       
                                                                                    
               ‘They scared and almost frightened me.’ (1932:93:2)                  
                                                                                    
          Completion. This suffix -háŋš also is attached directly to the verb root. Although rare, it is
                                                                                    
          found in the aorist, subordinate and progressive modes. It is related to the independent verb háŋ
                                                                                    
          ‘to fail.’                                                                
                                                                                    
               [ekuˊn (aiˊkun) hatlacxaˊñco]

          124) hokinácháŋškit                                                       
                                                                                    
               hok-ø-inac-haŋš-kit                                                  
                                                                                    
               RECIP-3STATS-fight-COMPL-PROG

          Desiderative. This is formed by suffixing -koh, which is the verb ‘to want,’ and inflecting the
                                                                                    
          entire compound with the stative subject prefixes. The desiderative seems to be used to express
                                                                                    
          a generalized or habitual desire, and is only fully distinctive in the first and second persons. The
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         75                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          following example shows the morphological distinction between a double-verb construction with
                                                                                    
          the verb ‘to want’ and the desiderative.                                  
                                                                                    
               [yukiˊt cokiaˊ ickoˊ]                                                
                                                                                    
          125a) yukít šokyáh iškóh                                                  
                                                                                    
               yukit šok-yah  iš-koh                                                
                                                                                    
               we   STH-eat   1PLSTATS-want                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘We want something to eat.’ (1932:152:2)                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [yukiˊt iccukiáˊ-ikoˊ]                                               
                                                                                    
          125b) yukít iššokyáhkóh                                                   
                                                                                    
               yukit iš-šok-yah-koh                                                 
                                                                                    
               we   1PLSTATS-STH-eat-DESID                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘We want to eat something.’ (1932:152:2)                             
                                                                                    
          Despite the existence of the construction using the independent verb kóh ‘to want,’ the
                                                                                    
          desiderative is the primary method used in the texts and the elicited examples to express the
                                                                                    
          notion of wanting to do something. In the texts the form of the desiderative is usually -koh,
                                                                                    
          while in the elicited examples, it is occasionally -kohš, which is the form of the verb ‘to want’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          used with nominal objects.                                                
                                                                                    
               [Pa-i aˊkc tiˊc koˊhat]

          Another difference between the desiderative and the independent verb koh, is that while the
                                                                                    
          negative of koh is usually óŋš ‘not to want’ the negative desiderative is formed by suffixing –
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          háhš to the positive.                                                     
                                                                                    
               {hin shokiáχ kóχsh hā́χsh <a>}

               ‘Don’t you want to eat?’ (Gatschet, 1885b:64)                        
                                                                                    
          Continuative. This aspect is marked by the suffix -n̥t-, which never occurs in word-final
                                                                                    
          position. The suffix is bisyllabic, with n̥ being the syllabic nasal. Use of this suffix indicates that
                                                                                    
          the action keeps on going on without a set beginning or ending. Examples are fund in both the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          aorist and preterite modes.                                               
                                                                                    
               [kuˊlke hokteweˊ toˊxntsȏl]                                          
                                                                                    
          133) kúlke hoktiwé·tóhn̥ccel                                              
                                                                                    
               kúlke     hok-tiwe·-toh-n̥t-cel                                      
                                                                                    
               always    RECIP-be.with-live(pl)-CONTIN-1PLS                         
                                                                                    
               ‘We have always lived with one another.’ (1932:133:2)                
                                                                                    
               [to (h)okwaˊlcntsĕl]

               there 3STATS-dwell-CONTIN-PRET we-FOC lake other.side

          Future Continuative. The continuative suffix -n̥t- is part of a very commonly elicited but
                                                                                    
          difficult to interpret construction. In about half of the examples, the construction consists of -n̥t-
                                                                                    
          plus -o·; in the other half of the examples it consists of the string -ki-n̥t-o·. The long o, as it is
                                                                                    
          never accented, loses its length due to the process of phonetic weakening. The construction is
                                                                                    
          translated in Gatschet and Swanton as a present, present progressive, or future. There are no
                                                                                    
          examples with plural subjects, and the vast majority of examples have first person singular
                                                                                    
          subjects. All examples, including the non-first person ones, terminate in -o, so this cannot be the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         79                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          first-person singular suffix of the aorist, but must be the phrase-terminal marker -o· (see
                                                                                    
          discussion below). The difference between -nt-o· and -ki-nt-o· is not clear, although verbal
                                                                                    
          semantics seems to play a part: for example the verbs tá·t ‘to stand,’ and ké·t ‘to sit,’ are only
                                                                                    
          found with -nt-o·, as are the verbs láš ‘to shave someone,’ and wálš ‘to fan someone.’ The suffix
                                                                                    
          string -ki-nt-o· is found exclusively with yáh ‘to eat,’ and wáŋ ‘to go about.’ The verbs yúlš ‘to
                                                                                    
          write’ and píš ‘to twist,’ occur with both. The majority of the examples of this construction lack
                                                                                    
          an overt noun or pronoun subject, so the subject must be determined from context. The
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          examples of this construction that occur in sentences suggest that its semantics are those of a
                                                                                    
          present or future continuative. Example 137a is glossed with example 137b, showing that the
                                                                                    
          meaning is close to that of the aorist mode.                              
                                                                                    
               [wic māˊñut waˊnkinto]

          Customary. This suffix, -n̥št, which surfaces phonetically as -n̥st, seems semantically very
                                                                                    
          similar to the Habitual, Continuative, and the Past Progressive, with the latter of which it
                                                                                    
          sometimes co-occurs, as in example 143. Note that overt marking of the subject of those verbs
                                                                                    
          which use the customary affix is blocked, and the subject must be inferred from context.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         81

          Habitual. This suffix was explained by Gatschet only in his field notes (1885c:[7]); its meaning
                                                                                    
          is not discussed in the publication. It has two forms –inš and –inšo; (and variants –ins and –inso,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          with the customary depalatalization of š after n) and seems closely related to the customary
                                                                                    
          suffix –n̥st. The optional final –o seems to be the same phrase-terminal marker that is found in
                                                                                    
          the continuative construction –nt-o / -ki-nt-o above. The few paradigmatic examples suggest
                                                                                    
          that this suffix is part of the subordinate mode. The following are examples:
                                                                                    
               [Moˊyu[m] kimāt tso-oˊts konăˊn olōˊl hihulat yaˊ-ins(o)]            
                                                                                    
          146) móyu kimá·t còhoc konán ólo·l híhulat yáhins (K) / yáhinso (T)       
                                                                                    
               moyu      kima·t    cohoc     konan ol,ol    hiš-ul-at               
                                                                                    
               pumpkin   beans     maize     potato REDUP,sweet plant-3PLS-PRET     
                                                                                    
               ø-yah-ins-o·                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               3S-eat-HABIT-PHR.TERM                                                
                                                                                    
               ‘They plant and habitually eat pumpkins, beans, maize, and sweet potatoes.’ (1932:9:4-5)
                                                                                    
               {yukít ish yá-insho}

          149) wí hišokóyins ilú ní·t

               I    1SSTATS-be.chief.-HABIT year five                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I have been a chief for five years.’ (1932:69:2)                    
                                                                                    
          Before. The aspectual element -tán is derived from the verb tán ‘to be still; to be yet.’ This
                                                                                    
          element is unique in that it must be suffixed to a verb in the negative. The expression of the
                                                                                    
          concept of ‘before’ with a negative construction is widespread in the Southeast, found in Koasati,
                                                                                    
          Natchez, and Chitimacha.11                                                
                                                                                    
               [Tik’huˊ koˊhat kaˊ-u hāˊxc taˊn, tiˊc aˊ-ucat]                      
                                                                                    
          150) tikhúkóhat káwháhštán tíš áwšat                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tik-ø-hu-koh-at          ø-kaw-hahš-tan tiš                          
                                                                                    
               GO.AND-3STATS-see-DESID-PRET 3STATS-die-NEG-YET go                   
                                                                                    
               ø-awš-at

          Past Progressive. The rare affix -tíhi- (unaccented form -ti-) occurs only in texts, and is never
                                                                                    
          word-final. This seems to be the equivalent of the progressive mode, only with the action taking
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          place in the past. This suffix also does not co-occur with subject affixes; the subject has to be
                                                                                    
          understood from context.                                                  
                                                                                    
               [Yuk’hiˊti icāk wāci a nēp nūn nultiˊhinst]

          The phrase-terminal marker. The suffix -o· is called a phrase-terminal marker more from its
                                                                                    
          position than from any discernable semantics. The meaning of this suffix which occurs
                                                                                    
          occasionally in texts and elicited sentences, is difficult to pin down. It is most commonly found
                                                                                    
          in the construction with the continuative, as discussed above. This suffix, unlike the first person
                                                                                    
          singular subject suffix of the aorist, -o, never is written [u] even though it is never accented.
                                                                                    
          This suggests that this suffix has an underlying long vowel, and thus is actually -o·. Although
                                                                                    
          there is no definite meaning that can be assigned to this suffix, as it always occurs on the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          terminal verb of a sentence, it is here termed a phrase-terminal marker.  
                                                                                    
               [Hatiuˊlco ‘n‘oˊhik]                                                 
                                                                                    
          157) hatyúlšo n̥hóhik                                                     
                                                                                    
               hat-yulš-ø-o·       n̥hoh-ik

          Verbal Prefixes. In contrast to verbal suffixes, which are not rigidly bound in order after the
                                                                                    
          verb root, verbal prefixes have a fixed order:                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                        R           
                                                                                    
          Directional – Incorp. – Indefinite – Second – First – Third person – Reflexive / – O
                                                                                    
          Locational Noun  Object   Person  Person Plural Object Reciprocal O       
                                                                                    
                                                                        T           
                                                                                    
          Verbal Object Prefixes. In the position closest to the root are the prefixes hat- reflexive, and
                                                                                    
          hok- reciprocal.                                                          
                                                                                    
          Reflexive. The reflexive prefix indicates that the action of the verb is being done to its subject.
                                                                                    
               [wi uˊts hatsaˊkco]                                                  
                                                                                    
          161) wi úc haccákšo

          Third person plural object. The prefix marking a third-person plural object occurs in the next
                                                                                    
          leftward position. This very common prefix indicates that the object of the verb is a plural noun.
                                                                                    
          It is probably derived from iša·k ‘person,’ although it is freely used to refer to animals and
                                                                                    
          things. Very frequently, even if a nominal adjunct is expressed, the third person plural object
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         91                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          prefix is used. Note that in example 174, even though the plural verb already indicates that the
                                                                                    
          object is plural, the prefix šak- is still used.                          
                                                                                    
               [ciwaˊn nohaˊmc cakcocoˊmcat]

          The third person plural object marker is also used as a third person plural stative subject marker.
                                                                                    
          This usage seems to have arisen by analogy to the object pronouns of the other persons, which
                                                                                    
          are used as stative subject markers.

          First person prefixes. These are the prefix hi- which indicates a first person singular direct or
                                                                                    
          indirect object of a transitive verb, or a first-person singular subject in the modes other than the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          aorist, and the prefix iš-, which indicates a first-person plural.

          Indefinite object. The next prefix, šok-, indicates that there is an object to the action of the verb,
                                                                                    
          but does not specify what it might be. It is probably derived from the independent word šók
                                                                                    
          ‘thing.’

          Incorporated nouns. There are only a few examples of incorporated nouns. They are only
                                                                                    
          noticeable when they occur after the directional and locational verbal prefixes, rather than
                                                                                    
          before.                                                                   
                                                                                    
          Examples 189a and 189b show a nominal object that is not incorporated; examples 190a and
                                                                                    
          190b show one that is.                                                    
                                                                                    
               [wi nēˊc kōˊtso]                                                     
                                                                                    
          189a) wí néš kóco                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wi   neš  koc-o

          The verb šil ‘to sew something’ incorporates the noun šok ‘thing’ to produce the intransitive verb
                                                                                    
          šokšíl ‘to be sewing.’ This is illustrated in examples 191a and 191b.     
                                                                                    
               [wi keˊ-u cukciˊkinto]                                               
                                                                                    
          191a) wí kewšokšílkinto                                                   
                                                                                    
               wi   kew-šok=šil-ki-nt-o·                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               I    SIT-thing=sew-CF-CONTIN-PHR.TERM                                
                                                                                    
               ‘I am going to sit and sew.’ (1932:112:1)                            
                                                                                    
               [wi keˊ-u cukcokciˊkinto]                                            
                                                                                    
          191b) wí kewšokšokšílkinto                                                
                                                                                    
               wi   kew-šok-šok=šil-ki-nt-o·                                        
                                                                                    
               I    SIT-thing-STH=sew-CF-CONTIN-PHR.TERM                            
                                                                                    
               ‘I am going to sit and sew things.’ (1932:112:1)                     
                                                                                    
            The noun šók is incorporated in a construction that detransitivizes a verb and creates an
                                                                                    
          antipassive. In some cases, such as šokyáh ‘to eat,’ it has become so tightly bound to the verb
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          root that it no longer separates, and has to be treated as part of the root, as in example 181 above.
                                                                                    
          Locational and directional verbal prefixes. These, the outermost set of prefixes, give further
                                                                                    
          information about the way an action is being performed. These are kew- ‘action performed
                                                                                    
          sitting,’ taw- ‘action performed standing (singular or plural),’ cow- ‘action performed standing
                                                                                    
          (plural),’ tik- ‘motion going towards an action,’ ok- ‘motion coming for an action,’ pay- ‘motion
                                                                                    
          back,’ and the rarer hiyik- ‘motion upwards,’ and ik- ‘action done with an instrument.’ There are
                                                                                    
          traces of a further prefix hik-, ‘action in water,’ for which see the discussion of Line 10 of Text 9.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         97                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Action performed sitting. This prefix, kew- is derived from the singular verb ke·t, ‘to sit; to
                                                                                    
          remain.’ It indicates that the action is performed while the subject is in a seated position. It can
                                                                                    
          be metaphorically extended to inanimate subjects which are conceived to be in a sitting position.
                                                                                    
          It is only found in elicited examples.                                    
                                                                                    
               [neˊc hidsoˊm ke-u cakuˊdskit]

          Action performed standing. The prefix for this notion, taw- is derived from ta·t ‘to stand’ by
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          the same process that derived kew- from ke·t. It indicates that an action is done standing. It is
                                                                                    
          also metaphorically extended to the actions of inanimate subjects that take place without moving
                                                                                    
          from a location. This prefix can be used with both singular and plural subjects, in contrast with
                                                                                    
          the prefix cow- which is used with plural subjects only.                  
                                                                                    
               [Tawatweˊnăt Utsutaˊt ut.]

          Motion towards. The prefix tik- which is related to both forms of the verb ‘to go,’ (tíš, singular
                                                                                    
          and tíw, plural) indicates that the subject is in motion for the purpose of doing an action. Purpose
                                                                                    
          without motion is handled by the gerund or the participle, as mentioned above.

          Action performed with an instrument. This also extremely rare prefix is of the form ik-; it is

          Adjectives. All adjectives are stative verbs. Because adjectives are verbs, they are accented,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          and they always follow the noun, as the normal Atakapa sentence word order is Subject (Object)
                                                                                    
          Verb.

          The adjective mo·n ‘all’ has two peculiarities. First, the form mo·n is never accented, unlike all
                                                                                    
          other adjectives. Second, it has the accented form mó·ni which is used only to refer to animate
                                                                                    
          nouns.                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Yiˊl mōn taˊ-i ō hoˊktiwē icwāˊñĕhĕ]

               he   all(animate) 2PLO-3STATS-give-PRET

          Adverbs. There are two kinds of adverbs, non-adjectival adverbs and adjectival adverbs. Non-
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          adjectival adverbs primarily of words relating to time, and a few other miscellaneous notions.
                                                                                    
          These adverbs can occur any place in the sentence. The following are the principal examples:
                                                                                    
               atkáki    ‘since; ago’                                               
                                                                                    
               ayíl      ‘today’                                                    
                                                                                    
               cátn̥     ‘afterwards’                                               
                                                                                    
               eykún     ‘now’                                                      
                                                                                    
               háhuwi·t  ‘previously’                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         107                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hokwitólip ‘at the same time’                                        
                                                                                    
               ilú tanúkkin ‘last year’                                             
                                                                                    
               ináha     ‘sort of’                                                  
                                                                                    
               ipúc      ‘only’

               yíln̥     ‘tomorrow’                                                 
                                                                                    
               [Iluˊ woc-peˊ haˊl imatoˊl (a)tkaki kaˊwat iluˊ wocpeˊ latsīˊk hinaˊka wăˊciñ]
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          226) ilú wošpé hálhimatól atkáki káwat ilú wošpé laccí·k hinákka wášiŋ

          Several of these adverbs are derived or polymorphemic, such as ití ‘at night,’ from the noun ití
                                                                                    
          ‘night,’ or ayíl ‘today’ from á yíl ‘this day,’ or yíln̥ ‘tomorrow,’ c.f. yíl ‘be day,’ or totayháhš ‘in
                                                                                    
          vain’ -háhš ‘negative.’ A non-adjectival adverb can also be used as a noun by placing it in
                                                                                    
          subject relation to an intransitive verb.                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         109                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               {kúltan ok-tíshat okwángsh hā́ngshat}                                
                                                                                    
          231) kultàn oktíšat, hokwáŋš háŋšat                                       
                                                                                    
               kultan    ok-ø-tiš-at         hokwaŋš   ø-haŋš-at                    
                                                                                    
               long.time COME-3STATS-go-PRET war       3STATS-be.ended-PRET         
                                                                                    
               ‘A long time has passed, the (Civil) War is over.’ (Gatschet 1885b:168)
                                                                                    
            Adjectival adverbs are formed from an adjective root by means of the suffix -ka, which is
                                                                                    
          probably derived from the verb ká ‘to make; to do.’ These adverbs must precede the verbs they
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          modify directly.                                                          
                                                                                    
               [toˊlka heyiˊkiulat]

               tol-ka    hi-yiki-ul-at

               [hiweˊ-uka kaukauˊkit]

          Comparison of Adjectives. Comparison of adjectives using a standard marker and standard of
                                                                                    
          comparison is only found in elicited sentences.15 In Southeastern languages, comparison of
                                                                                    
          adjectives using a standard of comparison seems not to have been done aboriginally, and only
                                                                                    
          arose as calques from European languages (see Kimball, 1989, for how comparison is handled in
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          natural texts in Koasati). With that caveat, comparative constructions are formed in Atakapa
                                                                                    
          using the verb iyé ‘to be more’ compounded with the adjectival verb in the comparison, with the
                                                                                    
          standard of comparison is in the directional case. When compounded with iyé, the adjectival verb
                                                                                    
          loses its accent.                                                         
                                                                                    
               [tuˊl taˊyut iyeˊ kaˊc]                                              
                                                                                    
          237) túl táyot iyékaš                                                     
                                                                                    
               tul  tay-ot    ø-iye-kaš-ø                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         111                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               lake river-DIR 3STATS-be.more-be.deep                                
                                                                                    
               ‘The lake is deeper than the river.’ (1932:125:1)                    
                                                                                    
               [yaˊ tsiˊk oˊt cakiyeˊ lāk]                                          
                                                                                    
          238) yá cí·kot šakiyéla·k                                                 
                                                                                    
               ya        ci·k-ot   šak-ø-iye-la·k                                   
                                                                                    
               that.person two-DIR 3PLO-3STATS-more-be.strong                       
                                                                                    
               ‘That person is the stronger of the two.’ (1932:154:1)               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          This construction is derived from a natural one in which the noun in the directional case, the
                                                                                    
          standard of comparison, does not occur.                                   
                                                                                    
               [yukiˊti nuˊnkin kēˊt’ntat Yuk’hiˊti Tuˊl toˊlpot inoˊ Tuˊl Teˊ-u iyeˊ edson]
                                                                                    
          239) yukhíti núŋkin ké·tntat yukhíti túl tólpot inó túl téw iyéhicon      
                                                                                    
               yukhiti nuŋ-kin ø-ke·t-nt-at       yukhiti   tul  tolpot ino         
                                                                                    
               Indian village-LOC 3STATS-dwell-CONTIN-PRET Indian lake east side    
                                                                                    
               tul  tew       ø-iye-hicon                                           
                                                                                    
               lake upstream  3STATS-be.more-be.small                               
                                                                                    
               ‘She used to live in an Indian village on the east side of Lake Prien (Indian Lake);
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Lake Charles (Upstream Lake) is smaller.’ (1932:71-72)               
                                                                                    
          Statements of equality use the adverb ahinákka to indicate the comparison.
                                                                                    
               {kiwílsh yukíti shakónshûl wī́sh akíta hináka hé-u hiatú-iv}         
                                                                                    
          240) kiwílš yukhíti šakóŋšul wí·š hakít akhinákka héwhihatwíw             
                                                                                    
               kiwilš    yukhiti   šak-oŋš-ul     wi-iš hakit akhinakka             
                                                                                    
               white.person Indian 3PLO-not.like-3PLS I-FOC they how                
                                                                                    
               hew-hi-hat-wiw                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         112                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               be.much-1SSTATS-REFLEX-believe                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘The white people do not like the Indians, but I think as much of myself as they do.’
                                                                                    
               (Gatschet, 1885b:80)                                                 
                                                                                    
          The equivalent of a superlative is formed using the verb iyáhi ‘to be ahead of’ in a set of two
                                                                                    
          clauses.                                                                  
                                                                                    
               [yaˊ kic cokciˊ-u taneˊ-ucaˊk iyaˊ-i]                                
                                                                                    
          241) yá kíš šokšíw tannéw šakiyáhi                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ya   kiš       šokšiw    tan-hew   šak-ø-iyahi                       
                                                                                    
               that woman     be.stingy other-PL  3PLO-3STATS-be.ahead.of           
                                                                                    
               ‘That woman is the stingiest one of all the others,’ lit. ‘That woman is stingy; she is
                                                                                    
               ahead of the other people.’ (1932:112:2)                             
                                                                                    
          A similar construction is found in a natural text.                        
                                                                                    
               [cokoˊ-i taˊnuk mōn cokiyaˊ-i <ōˊtsi> taneˊ-uts]                     
                                                                                    
          242) šokóy tanùk mo·n šakiyáhi tannéw [óci]                               
                                                                                    
               šokoy tanuk mo·n šak-ø-iyahi       tan-hew   oci                     
                                                                                    
               chief one all  3PLO-3STATS-be.ahead of other-PL above                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘One chief was ahead in regard to all things above the others.’ (1932:9:10-11)
                                                                                    
          A second comparative construction compounds the adjective with the verb íti, ‘to be in front.’ It
                                                                                    
          is given as an exact equivalent of iyé plus an adjective. It cannot express a standard of
                                                                                    
          comparison; if that is required, the construction with iyé and the directional case is needed, as in
                                                                                    
          example 245.                                                              
                                                                                    
               [yaˊ hidsoˊn iti]                                                    
                                                                                    
          243) yá hicóníti                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         113                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ya        ø-hicon-íti                                                
                                                                                    
               that.one  3STATS-be.small-be.in.front                                
                                                                                    
               ‘That person is smaller.’ (1932:42:2)                                
                                                                                    
               {ā́sh lā́k tsík íti}                                                 
                                                                                    
          244) á·š lá·k cí·k íti                                                    
                                                                                    
               a-iš      ø-la·k         ci·k ø-íti                                  
                                                                                    
               this.one-foc 3STATS-be.strong two 3STATS-be.in.front                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘This person is the stronger of the two.’ (Gatschet 1885b:81)        
                                                                                    
               [tsanuˊkc meˊts iˊti cuˊlut iyeˊmets]                                
                                                                                    
          245) canúkš mécíti šúlot iyémec                                           
                                                                                    
               canuk-š   ø=mec-íti           šul-ot    ø-iye-mec

          This construction is derived from a natural one.                          
                                                                                    
               [hiōl waˊcin iˊti haˊ ēˊñ Toussaint]

          adjectival.                                                               
                                                                                    
               [tsanuˊk cuˊl oˊt iyeˊ lāˊk patnaˊ-u]                                
                                                                                    
          247) canúk šúlot iyéla·kpatnáw

               horse dog-DIR  3STATS-be.more-be.strong-run
