          Verbal Derivational Processes. Verbs can be derived by root verbalization, derivational
                                                                                    
          suffixes, and several means of root pluralization.                        
                                                                                    
          Root verbalization. There is no formal distinction in Atakapa between nominal, postpositional,
                                                                                    
          and verbal roots. The language lacks a copula, nor are there any traces of the former existence of
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          one. A root noun may be converted into a verb by changing the pitch of the accented syllable of
                                                                                    
          the noun in the aorist mode, or adding verbal suffixes in any mode, in which case standard
                                                                                    
          accentuation rules apply.                                                 
                                                                                    
               [kiˊc]              [Taˊkapo kic]                                    
                                                                                    
          248) kíš                 tákapa kìš                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘woman’             takapa    ø-kìš                                  
                                                                                    
                                   Atakapa   3STATS-be.woman                        
                                                                                    
                                   ‘She was an Atakapa woman.’ (1932:14:70-71)      
                                                                                    
               [aˊlc]              [aˊlckit]                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          249) álš                 álškit                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘ice’               ø-alš-kit                                        
                                                                                    
                                   3STATS-ice-PROG(SG)                              
                                                                                    
                                   ‘it is freezing weather’ (1932:25:1)             
                                                                                    
               [haˊ-u]             [haˊ-ukit]                                       
                                                                                    
          250) háw                 háwkit                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘snow’              ø-haw-kit                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         115                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                   3STATS-snow- PROG(SG)                            
                                                                                    
                                   ‘it is snowing’ (1932:37:1)                      
                                                                                    
               [icāˊk]             [wi aˊp i-icaˊkět]                               
                                                                                    
          251) išá·k               wí áp hiišá·kat                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘person’            wi   ap   hi-iša·k-at                            
                                                                                    
                                   I    here 1SSTATS-person-PRET                    
                                                                                    
                                   ‘I was born here.’ (1932:144:2)                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [cokoˊ-i]           [haˊkit cokoˊ-i-yulĕhĕ]

          Postpositions can also be changed into verbs by adding verbal suffixes.   
                                                                                    
               [oˊ]                [aˊñ taˊ-i oˊat]

          Verbs derived from nouns have modal forms identical to those of root verbs, as shown by the
                                                                                    
          following four examples derived from the noun ipšók ‘doctor.’             
                                                                                    
               [ipcoˊx̥ kuˊl]                                                       
                                                                                    
          256) ipšókul                                                              
                                                                                    
               ipšok-ul                                                             
                                                                                    
               doctor-3PLS                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘They are doctors.’ (1932:49:2)                                      
                                                                                    
               [wic hi-ipcoˊkat]

               wi-iš hi-ipšók-at                                                    
                                                                                    
               I-foc 1SSTATS-doctor-PRET

               [ipcoˊkěnhăn]

          258) ipšókehe                                                             
                                                                                    
               ø-ipšok-ehe                                                          
                                                                                    
               3STATS-doctor-DUB

               [ipcotikit]

               3STATS-doctor-FUT

          This easy verbalization of words of various classes is probably the reason why Atakapa lacks a
                                                                                    
          copula, or a verb ‘to be’. Compare the Southeastern language Koasati, which possesses a copula,
                                                                                    
          with Atakapa, which lacks one:                                            
                                                                                    
          KOASATI:                                                                  
                                                                                    
          260a) mikkó              mikkók cíl                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘chief’             mikkó-k   ci-li-DEL                              
                                                                                    
                                   chief-NOM 2STATS-be-PHR.TERM                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                   ‘You are a chief.’                               
                                                                                    
          ATAKAPA:                                                                  
                                                                                    
               [cokoˊ-i]           [ĕˊncoˊkoi]                                      
                                                                                    
          260b) šokóy ‘chief’      n̥šokòy                                          
                                                                                    
                                   n̥-šokoy                                         
                                                                                    
                                   2STATS-be.chief                                  
                                                                                    
                                   ‘You are a chief.’ (1932:69:1)                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         118                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The following are further examples of noun verbalization in what are copular sentences. In
                                                                                    
          examples 262 and 263 nouns in locative cases are verbalized.              
                                                                                    
               [yaˊ cuˊl haˊkit kiñ]                                                
                                                                                    
          261) yá šúl hakìt kìŋ                                                     
                                                                                    
               ya   šul  hakit ø-kìŋ                                                
                                                                                    
               that dog  their 3STATS-be.pet                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘That dog is their pet.’ (1932:117:1)                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [wi anˊ nē-tsaˊx̥ kip]                                               
                                                                                    
          262) wi aŋ ne·cákip                                                       
                                                                                    
               wi   áŋ   ø-ne·-cak-ip                                               
                                                                                    
               my   house 3STATS-land-dry-INESS.be                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘My house is on dry land.’ (1932:28:2)                               
                                                                                    
               [yuk’hiˊti cakimōc-neˊkin haˊ]                                       
                                                                                    
          263) yukhíti šakhimo·šné·kinháh                                           
                                                                                    
               yukhiti   ø-šakhimo·šne·-kin-hah                                     
                                                                                    
               Indian    3STATS-cemetery-LOC.be-NEG                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘She is not in the Indian cemetery.’ (1932:15:90)                    
                                                                                    
          Verbal derivational suffixes. While nouns can become verbs without morphological change,
                                                                                    
          there are a handful of suffixes that can derive verbs from other verbs and from nouns. The
                                                                                    
          productive derivational suffixes are the causative –(i)š, the transitivizer -i, and -šim, ‘to be
                                                                                    
          nothing but N.’ There is one non-productive suffix, -c, which changes the valency of a verb in
                                                                                    
          an unpredictable fashion.                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         119                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The causative. The causative suffix, –(i)š, is added to an intransitive verb that describes a state
                                                                                    
          to indicate the action of the intransitive verb is caused by the subject. The form -iš is used when
                                                                                    
          suffixing -š would form an impermissible consonant cluster.               
                                                                                    
               [atsiˊl kuˊts]                                                       
                                                                                    
          264a) haccíl kúc                                                          
                                                                                    
               haccil ø-kuc                                                         
                                                                                    
               rust 3STATS-be.red                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘The rust is red’ (1932:74:1)                                        
                                                                                    
               [hatkuˊdsico]                                                        
                                                                                    
          264b) hatkúcišo                                                           
                                                                                    
               hat-kuc-iš-o                                                         
                                                                                    
               REFLEX-be.red-CAUS-1SS                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I paint myself red.’ (1932:74:1)                                    
                                                                                    
               [wi tsanuˊk mēˊl]                                                    
                                                                                    
          265a) wi canúk mé·l                                                       
                                                                                    
               wi   canuk     ø-me·l                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               my   horse     3STATS-be.black                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘My horse is black.’ (1932:80:1)                                     
                                                                                    
               [wi mēˊlco]                                                          
                                                                                    
          265b) wí mé·lšo                                                           
                                                                                    
               wi   me·l-š-o                                                        
                                                                                    
               I    be.black-CAUS-1SS                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I blacken it.’ (1932:80:1)                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         120                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          This suffix is not freely used with other intransitive verbs, although there are a few examples
                                                                                    
          such as waŋiš, ‘to drive something,’ from wáŋ ‘to go about,’ and hitáwš ‘to let something out,’
                                                                                    
          from hitáh ‘to come out.’16                                               
                                                                                    
               [cakwinēˊ cokwaˊñic yaˊcoktuˊkulet]                                  
                                                                                    
          266) šakwiné šakwaŋišya šaktúkulat                                        
                                                                                    
               šak-wine  šak-wáŋ-iš=ya            šak-tuk-ul-at                     
                                                                                    
               3PLO-find 3PLO-go.about-CAUS=and   3pl-bring-3pls-pret               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘They find them, drive them, and bring them.’ (1932:13:60)           
                                                                                    
          Rather than using this suffix, the causative of most intransitive verbs and of almost all transitive
                                                                                    
          verbs is expressed with a double verb construction with ka ‘to make,’ or nawš ‘to let.’
                                                                                    
               [kidcoˊmc npuˊnsĕn laˊ-u ka]                                         
                                                                                    
          267) kicóŋš n̥púnsen láw ká                                               
                                                                                    
               kicoŋš    n̥-puns-en     law  ka-ø                                   
                                                                                    
               fire      2SSTATS-blow.on-IF burn make-2/3S                          
                                                                                    
               ‘If you blow on a fire, it makes it burn.’ (1932:67:1)               
                                                                                    
               [wi makaˊu naˊuco]                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          268) wí makáw náwšo                                                       
                                                                                    
               wi   makáw     náwš-o                                                
                                                                                    
               I    fall(sg)  let-1SS                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I make it fall.’ (1932:78:2)                                        
                                                                                    
          There are traces of the former use of the suffix -iš /-š with some transitive verbs, for example the
                                                                                    
          noun ‘cup; dipper’ ámšne is a nominalization of the verb *amš ‘to cause to drink something,’ and
                                                                                    
          in the compound noun šokwán šakúciš, ‘interpreter’ šakúciš comes from the verb *úciš ‘to cause
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         121                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          to know something.’ There is also a rare use of this suffix with transitive verbs to form a kind of
                                                                                    
          applicative:                                                              
                                                                                    
               haccewwán ‘to forget something’ haccewwán-iš ‘to forget something belonging to
                                                                                    
                                                  someone’                          
                                                                                    
               kóc ‘to cut something’   kóc-iš ‘to cut a piece out of something’    
                                                                                    
               ƛóp ‘to stick something in’ ƛóp-iš ‘to stick something into something’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            Quite frequently the suffix -š seems simply to indicate that a word is a verb, and seems to add
                                                                                    
          nothing semantically to the root meaning. The vast majority of verbs that have a root ending in -
                                                                                    
          š are transitive; however, there are any number of intransitive verbs that end in -š, such as tíš ‘to
                                                                                    
          go,’ pólš, ‘to float,’ and ƛákš, ‘to be dirty.’ There are also many transitive verbs that do not have
                                                                                    
          the suffix -š, such as nám ‘to beg someone,’ ká ‘to do something,’ and yáh ‘to eat something.’
                                                                                    
            This suffix is optional on the verb ‘to want’ kóh/kóhš (although it is obligatory on oŋš ‘not to
                                                                                    
          want something’). It is also optional on the negative affix -háh, which then appears as -háhš.
                                                                                    
          There is apparently no meaning difference between the forms.              
                                                                                    
               [hinimaˊha]                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          269a) hinimáháh                                                           
                                                                                    
               hi-nima-ø-hah                                                        
                                                                                    
               1SO-kill-2SIMP-NEG                                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘Don’t kill me!’ (1932:91:2)                                         
                                                                                    
               [hinimaˊhaxc]                                                        
                                                                                    
          269b) hinimáháhš                                                          
                                                                                    
               hi-nima-ø-hah-š                                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         122                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               1SO-kill-2SIMP-NEG-š                                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘Don’t kill me!’ (1932:91:2)                                         
                                                                                    
          Uniquely, the negative affix with -š is used as an independent verb meaning ‘not to have
                                                                                    
          something,’ and is the negative of ké ‘to have something.’                
                                                                                    
               [Ickiciˊl nīt keˊat. Hiōˊl hāˊxcat]

          The transitivizer. The suffix -i derives transitive verbs from intransitives. There are a number
                                                                                    
          of such transitives in -i which lack a corresponding intransitive.        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Intransitive        Transitive                                            
                                                                                    
          cák  ‘to be dry’    cáki      ‘to dry something’                          
                                                                                    
          láw  ‘to burn’      láwi      ‘to burn something’17                       
                                                                                    
          líl  ‘to be soft’   líli      ‘to soften something                        
                                                                                    
          páƛ  ‘to break’     páƛi      ‘to break something’18                      
                                                                                    
          péh  ‘to finish’    péhi      ‘to finish something’                       
                                                                                    
          tóh  ‘to sit (pl)’  tóhi      ‘to get in a vehicle’                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         123                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               -              íŋi       ‘to look for something’                     
                                                                                    
               -              iyáhi     ‘to be ahead of someone’                    
                                                                                    
               -              kíni      ‘to sharpen something’                      
                                                                                    
               -              lóhi      ‘to help someone                            
                                                                                    
               -              n̥téhi    ‘to frighten something’                     
                                                                                    
               -              óŋi       ‘to thread something’                       
                                                                                    
               -              péni      ‘to cure someone’                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               -              wáhi      ‘to order someone’                          
                                                                                    
               -              yíki      ‘to pay someone’                            
                                                                                    
          There is an example of this transitivizing suffix being added to a transitive verb, making it
                                                                                    
          ditransitive: ámi ‘to give someone something to drink.’ The following example is found in
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes; it was not seen by Swanton.                       
                                                                                    
               {na há ‘n ámeat}                                                     
                                                                                    
          272) ná há n̥ ámiat                                                       
                                                                                    
               ná        há   n̥-ámi-at                                             
                                                                                    
               you(sg)   s/he 2SSTATS-give.drink-PRET                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘You gave him something to drink.’ (Gatschet, 1885c[8])              
                                                                                    
          The suffix -šim ‘to be nothing but N’. Suffixed to a noun, this suffix indicates that there is
                                                                                    
          nothing but the noun involved in the action. Suffixed to a body-part noun, however, it indicates
                                                                                    
          that the body part is bare or exposed. This semantically unusual suffix is not unusual in the
                                                                                    
          context of Southeastern languages. Verbalizing suffixes with identical semantics have been
                                                                                    
          recorded in Koasati (Kimball 1991:169-172) and Natchez (Kimball 2005:428-429). The š of the
                                                                                    
          suffix surfaces as s when the suffix is attached to a noun ending in a stop or š.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         124                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [ha icāˊt ha uˊc mōn naˊ-ucĕm]                                       
                                                                                    
          273) ha išá·t ha úš mo·n náwšim                                           
                                                                                    
               ha   iša·t ha  uš   mo·n ø-naw-šim                                   
                                                                                    
               his  head his  body all  3STATS-hair-NOTHING.BUT                     
                                                                                    
               ‘His head and his whole body were nothing but hair.’ (1932:11:33-34) 
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊt noˊmcsĕm tuhtsĕl yukiˊt nuk]                                 
                                                                                    
          274) yukít nómšsim túhcel yukít nuk                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               yukit ø-nomš-šim         tuh-cel        yukit nuk                    
                                                                                    
               we   3STATS-child-NOTHING.BUT stay(pl)-1PLS we alone                 
                                                                                    
               ‘It was nothing but us children; we stayed there all by ourselves.’ (1932:17:107-08)
                                                                                    
               [cokiōˊl noˊmc hakīˊt cukiōlc-hatkeˊ mōn luˊcĕm taˊlcĕm]             
                                                                                    
          275) šakyólnómš hakít šakyólšokhatké mó·n lúšim tálšim

          With numbers, the suffix -šim has an additional meaning: ‘N apiece,’ tanúkšim ‘one apiece,’
                                                                                    
          cí·kšim ‘two apiece,’ and so forth.                                       
                                                                                    
          The valence-changing suffix –c. There is a non-productive suffix –c that changes the valency
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          of a verb. The following are the only examples noted:19                   
                                                                                    
          ikháwš    ‘to drown something’ ikháw-c  ‘to drown (intransitive)’         
                                                                                    
          wán       ‘to say something’  wán-c     ‘to say something to someone’     
                                                                                    
          taŋkóhi   ‘to jump into something’ kóhi-c ‘to sink; set (of the sun)      
                                                                                    
          Detransitivizing prefixes. There are two ways of making a transitive verb intransitive, the first
                                                                                    
          using the reciprocal prefix hat-, the other is incorporating the noun cók ‘thing’ as a dummy
                                                                                    
          object. Each of these formations can be seen as a kind of antipassive (c.f. Dixon, 1994:146-152).
                                                                                    
          In the case of intransitive verbs derived with the prefix hat-, the subject of the verbal action is
                                                                                    
          treated as if it were doing the action to itself.                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               {nésh hatsā́tskit}                                                   
                                                                                    
          278) néš haccá·ckit                                                       
                                                                                    
               neš  hat-ca·c-kit                                                    
                                                                                    
               wood REFLEX-crack-PROG(SG)                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘The wood is cracking.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:131)                        
                                                                                    
               [kaukauˊ hatpaˊ-u]                                                   
                                                                                    
          279) kawkáw hatpáw                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         126                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kawkaw    hat-paw                                                    
                                                                                    
               water     REFLEX-swing                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘The water eddies.’ (1932:103:2)                                     
                                                                                    
            The incorporation of the noun cók as a dummy object derives an intransitive verb from one
                                                                                    
          that requires a direct object. Usually, the incorporated noun is seperable, as in example 276b;
                                                                                    
          however, in a few derived intransitives it is fused to the root; most notably šokyáh ‘to eat,’
                                                                                    
          derived from yáh ‘to eat something.’ In this case, the incorporated noun loses its accent.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [cakioˊl ckaliˊt haihaiˊc nāˊkco]                                    
                                                                                    
          280a) šakyólškalít hayháyš nákšo                                          
                                                                                    
               šakyol-iškalit hayhayš nakš-o                                        
                                                                                    
               man-child weep      hear-1ss                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘I hear a boy weeping.’                                              
                                                                                    
               [coˊknaˊkc haˊxc]                                                    
                                                                                    
          280b) šóknákšháhš                                                         
                                                                                    
               šok=nakš-hahš                                                        
                                                                                    
               thing=hear-NEG                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘He does not hear.’ (1932:85:1)                                      
                                                                                    
               [coˊkuxts (h)aˊxc]                                                   
                                                                                    
          281a) šókúhcháhš                                                          
                                                                                    
               šok=uhc-hahš

               ‘He is ignorant.’ (1932:138:2)                                       
                                                                                    
               [coˊk-heˊ-ux̥ ts]

            In the following textual example of this kind of antipassive, the narrator is not interested in
                                                                                    
          what things her cousin is sewing, rather she is focusing on the fact that her cousin is a hard
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          worker.                                                                   
                                                                                    
               [cokaˊ-hiweˊ-u cithaˊñ liˊl[i] yaˊ cokcukyiˊkc yaˊ cukwaˊk ‘n āˊl pai-oˊk yaˊ
                                                                                    
          283) šokká hiwéw šityáŋ líliya šokšakyíkšya šokwákn̥ a·l payókya

               šokwak=n̥ a·l       pay-ok-ø=ya    pil-kin

               ø-tiht-šok=šil-kit                                                   
                                                                                    
               3STATS-lie(sg)-thing=sew-PROG                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘She works hard; she softens green Spanish moss and she sells it, and brings back bread
                                                                                    
               and meat, and she keeps on lying on the bed sewing.’                 
                                                                                    
          Verbal derivation by prefixation. Occasionally new verbs are derived by prefixation, the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          semantics of which are not related to the semantics of the individual prefixes, as in the following:
                                                                                    
               yikš      ‘to pay something’                                         
                                                                                    
               šakyíkš   ‘to buy something’ šak- ‘third person plural object’       
                                                                                    
               šokšakyíkš ‘to sell something’ šok- ‘indefinite object’20            
                                                                                    
          Verb pluralization. There are three kinds of verb pluralization in Atakapa: Reduplication,
                                                                                    
          Plurals in -m, and Suppletion.                                            
                                                                                    
          Reduplication. Reduplication is an active verbal process in Atakapa grammar. There are two
                                                                                    
          kinds of reduplication: simple reduplication and reduplication with infixation.
                                                                                    
          Simple reduplication. This form of reduplication replicates the initial CVC of the verb root.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Vowel-initial roots are treated as if they have an initial glottal stop. If the verb root has a long
                                                                                    
          vowel, the reduplicated syllable has a short vowel. The semantics of the verb root determine the
                                                                                    
          meaning of reduplication. In stative verbs, reduplication indicates plurality of subject. In active
                                                                                    
          verbs reduplication indicates either repetition of an action or plurality of object. In reduplication
                                                                                    
          of stative verbs, the accent shifts to the reduplicated element; in the reduplication of active verbs,
                                                                                    
          the accent remains on the verb root.                                      
                                                                                    
               [nēˊc līˊl]                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         129                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          284a) néš líl                                                             
                                                                                    
               neš  ø-lil                                                           
                                                                                    
               wood 3STATS-be.soft                                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘The piece of wood is soft.’ (1932:76:2)                             
                                                                                    
               [nēˊc lilil]                                                         
                                                                                    
          284b) néš líllil

               wood 3STATS-REDUP,be.soft                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘The pieces of wood are soft.’ (1932:76:2)                           
                                                                                    
               [taˊntstal tleˊm]

               [wi nēˊc tamtaˊmco]

               wi   neš  tam,tam-š-o                                                
                                                                                    
               I    wood REDUP,split-EXT-1SS                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘I split a block of wood in many places.’                            
                                                                                    
               ‘I split many blocks of wood.’ (1932:126:1)                          
                                                                                    
               [wi caˊkiko]

               ‘I wound someone in various places.’ (1932:111:1)                    
                                                                                    
               {hú yá ishāk}

               hu-ø      ya   iša·k

          A variety of this kind of reduplication replicates the third-person plural object prefix, as in the
                                                                                    
          following example:                                                        
                                                                                    
               [taˊ-ucokcokwaˊnkit]                                                 
                                                                                    
          292) tawšokšokwánkit                                                      
                                                                                    
               taw-,šok,šok-ø-wan-kit                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               STAND-,REDUP,STH-3STATS-speak-PROG                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘He is standing and saying something repeatedly, i.e. he is preaching.’ (1932:123:2)
                                                                                    
          Very rarely, the entire verb root is reduplicated.                        
                                                                                    
               [coˊyuan]                                                            
                                                                                    
          293a) šóywan                                                              
                                                                                    
               ø-šoywan                                                             
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.mischevious                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         133                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘He is mischevious.’ (1932:116:2)                                    
                                                                                    
               [coyuancoyuaˊn]                                                      
                                                                                    
          293b) šóywanšóywan                                                        
                                                                                    
               ø-šoywan,šoywan                                                      
                                                                                    
               3STATS-REDUP,be.mischevious                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘They are mischevious.’ (1932:116:2)                                 
                                                                                    
          Reduplication with infixation. Although examples of this kind of reduplication are not
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          numerous, it still seems to have been an active process. The only examples in active verbs are
                                                                                    
          found in one of the texts, and not through elicitation. It requires the reduplication of the verb
                                                                                    
          root, and the infixation of an element between the reduplicated parts. For stative verbs element is
                                                                                    
          universally -nak-, ‘intensivizer’, while for active verbs the regular verbal prefixes hok-
                                                                                    
          ‘reciprocal object’ and šak- ‘plural or indefinite object’ occur. The meaning of this reduplication
                                                                                    
          is an increase in the intensity of the state or increase in intensity or repetition of an action. In
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes such forms are written as one or two (rarely three) words; however, this
                                                                                    
          reduplication is written in the publication with each syllable as an independent word. The
                                                                                    
          assimilation of the ln cluster in example 290b and the kƛ cluster in 291b shows that the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          construction is a single word.                                            
                                                                                    
               [kuˊts]                                                              
                                                                                    
          294a) kuc                                                                 
                                                                                    
               ø-kuc                                                                
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.red                                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘It is red.’ (1932:74:1)                                             
                                                                                    
               [kuˊts nak kuˊts]                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         134                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          294b) kúcnakkúc                                                           
                                                                                    
               ø-kuc,nak,kuc                                                        
                                                                                    
               3STATS-REDUP,INTENS,be.red                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘It is dark red. (1932:74:1)                                         
                                                                                    
               [liˊl]

          There are several examples of this form of reduplication that reduplicate the entire word rather
