          300b) talkópnaktalkóp                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ø-talkop,nak,talkop                                                  
                                                                                    
               3STATS-REDUP,INTENS,be.blue                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘It is dark blue.’ (1932:125:2)                                      
                                                                                    
          Plurals in -m. Pluralizing a verb root with the suffix -m is not a productive process in Atakapa.
                                                                                    
          This form of pluralization takes place by replacing the last consonant of the verb root with m. If
                                                                                    
          the verb is intransitive, the plural in –m refers to the subject of the verb; if the verb is transitive, it
                                                                                    
          refers to the object of the verb. It is of interest that the neighboring language Chitimacha uses a
                                                                                    
          suffix -ma to pluralize verbs, and that this suffix also replaces the final consonant of the verb
                                                                                    
          (Hieber, 2019a:321-22; Swadesh, 1946b:117). Chitimacha pluralization in -ma also has the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          same semantic effect of pluralizing the subject of an intransitive verb, and the object of a
                                                                                    
          transitive verb, thus treating S (intransitive subject) in the same way as O (transitive object),
                                                                                    
          another indication of the ergativity of Atakapa grammar. The following list is exhaustive.
                                                                                    
          Singular  Plural    Gloss                                                 
                                                                                    
          aŋkát-š   aŋkám-š   ‘to play’                                             
                                                                                    
          hacehé-š  hacemé-š  ‘to be bad’                                           
                                                                                    
          himá·k-š  himá·m-š  ‘to mix together’                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         137                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          imíl-š    imím-š    ‘to hate’                                             
                                                                                    
          išó·l     išó·m     ‘to be small (animate)’                               
                                                                                    
          *káw-š    *kám-š    ‘to cause to put something in.’21                     
                                                                                    
          pák       pám       ‘to step on’                                          
                                                                                    
          pét-št    pém-št    ‘to be tired’                                         
                                                                                    
          púh       púm       ‘to jump’                                             
                                                                                    
          šošól-š   šošóm-š   ‘to scare’                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          tekáw-š   tekám-š   ‘to be a branch’                                      
                                                                                    
          Suppletion. Only a few verbs show plural by suppletion. Like other languages of the Southeast
                                                                                    
          (Chitimacha, Swadesh, 1946a:318; Koasati, Kimball, 1991:322-24; Chickasaw and Creek,
                                                                                    
          Fitzgerald, 2016:691-93; Yuchi, Wagner, 1934:352-53), it is verbs of motion and position that
                                                                                    
          show this kind of pluralization.                                          
                                                                                    
          Singular  Plural    Gloss                                                 
                                                                                    
          kaw       píhk      ‘to die’                                              
                                                                                    
          ké·t      núl/tóh   ‘to sit; to dwell’                                    
                                                                                    
          makáw     wiló      ‘to fall’                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          mók       náw       ‘to arrive’                                           
                                                                                    
          níhuwe    nók/nókiš ‘to lie down’                                         
                                                                                    
          tá·t      có·t      ‘to stand’                                            
                                                                                    
          tíht      yóht      ‘to be lying down’                                    
                                                                                    
          tíš       tíw       ‘to go’                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Notes                                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         138                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1. This form is not attested in the corpus, but in inferred from such forms as hatwíwul ‘they
                                                                                    
          believe it of themselves.’                                                
                                                                                    
          2. Stative verbs without a plural form mark their subjects with the prefix šak-.
                                                                                    
          3. This is a compound verb; the construction is discussed in the section on verbal conjunction
                                                                                    
          and disjunction in the chapter on syntax.                                 
                                                                                    
          4. The initial i does not occur in the publication, but in Gatschet’s field notes. There it is
                                                                                    
          scratched out, done erroneously as he was reviewing the text with his consultant.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          5. The original dictation of this text had a dubitative form with -ehe, but a note below it says that
                                                                                    
          the form with -tikit is better.                                           
                                                                                    
          6. This form is lacking in the publication, but is reconstructed based on forms like hikétikit, ‘I
                                                                                    
          shall have.’                                                              
                                                                                    
          7. The word náhka written {naqka} and {náχka} in the field notes, is not listed under any root in
                                                                                    
          the publication. The published translation of this sentence is “if you are a smart woman, you will
                                                                                    
          not go there (or would not go there).’ although in the field notes (1885b:109), Gatschet scratched
                                                                                    
          out ‘there.’ in red. There is no negative verb, so by deduction nahka must be negative, and
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          because its location is that of an adverb, the meaning ‘must not’ is postulated. In the example
                                                                                    
          following, it and the verb are translated ‘will not tell you what,’ even though the pronoun is in
                                                                                    
          the ergative case and a focus form, so it must again be a negative, and the same meaning is
                                                                                    
          postulated.                                                               
                                                                                    
          8. The form given in the paradigm lacks the prefix iš-, clearly a slip in recording, cf. iš-icyuc-kin
                                                                                    
          ‘when we grew up.’                                                        
                                                                                    
          9. This form is questioned in the publication, probably because the usual negative for kóhš ‘to
                                                                                    
          want’ is óŋš ‘not to want.’                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         139                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          10. An example is šúl háhan ‘not having a dog’ [cuˊl haˊ-an] (1932:116:2).
                                                                                    
          11. The following are examples of this construction in the aforementioned languages:
                                                                                    
          Chitimacha:                                                               
                                                                                    
          11a) tewe·š kap kˀušti kˀan ki kap kˀetnaɁa                               
                                                                                    
               tewe·š    kap  kˀušt-i   kˀan ki   kap  kˀet-inaɁa                   
                                                                                    
               however   PVB  eat-GER   be.not in PVB  kill(pl)-AOR(2/3PLS)         
                                                                                    
               However, before it ate her, they killed it. (Swadesh, 1950: A.32.c.4)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Koasati:                                                                  
                                                                                    
          11b) hasáykohá:lon ƚóykalą́                                               
                                                                                    
               hasáy-ko-há:lo-n    ƚóyka-l-á-V̨                                     
                                                                                    
               rain.hard-NEG-SEQ-SW return(sg)-1SS-INTENT-PHR:TERM                  
                                                                                    
               Before it rains, I will return. (Kimball, 1994:239)                  
                                                                                    
          Natchez:                                                                  
                                                                                    
          11c) wita Ɂišahkuša·t a·miNt                                              
                                                                                    
               wit-a     Ɂi-šahkuš-a·t  a·-miNt                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               day-ART   PRT-arrive-NEG be(aor)-MOD                                 
                                                                                    
               Before the day arrived… (Kimball, 2005:417)                          
                                                                                    
          12. Here the translation in the publication expresses a first-person subject. The Atakapa marks
                                                                                    
          no subject except a tacit third person subject. Either the published translation is in error, or the
                                                                                    
          subject, like that of the participle, is understood from context.         
                                                                                    
          13. Gatschet made the following comment about this example: “A son-in-law of L(ouison) was
                                                                                    
          treated this way by the Americans here, because he had married an Indian, whom they claimed to
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         140                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          be a negress. (about 1880). He was an Englishman.” This apparently refers to Ambrose
                                                                                    
          Huntington.                                                               
                                                                                    
          14. Note the lack of nominal accent in the adjectival phrases iša·k šoké·š ‘sick person,’ and šok
                                                                                    
          hacehéš hiwéw ‘something very bad.’                                       
                                                                                    
          15. In Gatschet’s field notes, the examples of comparative sentences are full of scratch-outs,
                                                                                    
          insertions, and word order changes, suggesting that such sentences were unnatural for his
                                                                                    
          consultants.                                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          16. The final h of this verb comes from the devoiced allophone of w.      
                                                                                    
          17. Compare example 262 above, where the causative of láw ‘to burn’ is láw ká, not láwi.
                                                                                    
          18. The verb ‘to break’ has the form páł intervocalically, and pál syllable-finally. This is an
                                                                                    
          example of word-final ƛ becoming l. There are also examples of this word with intervocalic l,
                                                                                    
          (pálo / páło ‘I break’) as the root was becoming regularized.             
                                                                                    
          19. A further example of this suffix may occur in iyé-c ‘to rise, of the sun,” c.f. hiyé-kiti ‘Sunrise
                                                                                    
          People; Eastern Atakapa.                                                  
                                                                                    
          20. Both the verbs šakyíkš ‘to buy something’ (also šakí·kš ) and šokšakyíkš (also šokšakí·kš) ‘to
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          sell something’ lack third-person plural object agreement, so number must be marked in the
                                                                                    
          nominal adjuct.                                                           
                                                                                    
               {shokón tanúk shakī́ksho}

                                         142                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          3. NOUNS AND NOMINAL MORPHOLOGY                                           
                                                                                    
           Nouns in Atakapa are not distinguished from verbs in form, but only in function. Nouns have
                                                                                    
          two tacit features that are reflected in grammatical agreement, animacy and mass status.
                                                                                    
          Animacy. Atakapa has two adjectives that have different forms for use with animate and
                                                                                    
          inanimate nouns: mo·n (inanimate) / mó·ni (animate) ‘all,’ and hicón (inanimate) / išó·l (sg) /
                                                                                    
          išó·m ~ išó·mš (pl) (animate) ‘small.’ The use of these adjectives indicate the classes of nouns
                                                                                    
          considered animate. The words išó·l (sg) / išó·m ~ išó·mš (pl) are used to indicate small animate
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          creatures, and as such are used to describe the young of animals. Parts of the human body are
                                                                                    
          also shown by these words to be animate, as well as trees and plants. In addition, these
                                                                                    
          adjectives also are used with the words táy ‘river,’ tík ‘arrow,’ tú ‘river cane,’ kocnán ‘metal,’
                                                                                    
          and ók ‘cloth.’ This recalls the inclusion of culturally important items in the class of animates in
                                                                                    
          Algonquian languages.                                                     
                                                                                    
               šúl       ‘dog’          šú·l išó·l     ‘puppy’                      
                                                                                    
               šiwán     ‘cat’          šiwán išó·l    ‘kitten’                     
                                                                                    
               šokóŋ     ‘cow’          šokóŋ išo·l    ‘calf’                       
                                                                                    
               nohámš    ‘chicken’      nohámš išó·m   ‘chicks’                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wóš       ‘hand’         wošišóˑl       ‘little finger’              
                                                                                    
               hiká·t    ‘foot’         hiká·t išó·m   ‘little toes’                
                                                                                    
               néš       ‘tree’         néš išó·m      ‘bushes’                     
                                                                                    
               táy       ‘river’        táy išo·l      ‘bayou’                      
                                                                                    
               tík       ‘arrow’        tík išó·m      ‘bullets’                    
                                                                                    
               tú        ‘river cane’   tú išó·mš      ‘arrow shafts’               
                                                                                    
               kocnán    ‘metal’        kucnán išó·l   ‘iron nail’                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         143                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ók        ‘cloth’        ók išo·m       ‘thread’                     
                                                                                    
          The word néš ‘tree’ is inanimate when it means ‘wood,’ as shown by its use with the verb hicón
                                                                                    
          ‘to be small (inanimate): néš hicón ‘stick.’                              
                                                                                    
          Mass status. Certain nouns in Atakapa are considered mass nouns, and as such always require
                                                                                    
          a plural adjective. Examples are té·š ‘hair of the head;’ compare kíš, ‘woman,’ and šít ‘Spanish
                                                                                    
          moss,’ compare šokón ‘cow.’                                               
                                                                                    
               té·š mókmok ‘short hair’                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kíš mók   ‘a short woman’     kéš mókmok     ‘short women’           
                                                                                    
               šít mélme·l ‘black (dry) Spanish moss’                               
                                                                                    
               šokónmé·l ‘buffalo (black cow)’ šokonmélme·l ‘buffaloes’             
                                                                                    
          Because of the scarcity of data, it is not always possible to determine the animacy or mass status
                                                                                    
          of a particular noun.                                                     
                                                                                    
          Ordering of nominal morphology. Noun affix ordering is much simpler than verb affix
                                                                                    
          ordering. There is a single prefix and there are only three suffixes. The following is the template
                                                                                    
          for the noun phrase:                                                      
                                                                                    
          plural(šak) - ROOT - plural(hew) - case - focus                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The two plural affixes are mutually exclusive; if one is used, the other does not occur.
                                                                                    
          Combinations of affixes are extremely rare in the data. The plural -héw has been found only in
                                                                                    
          the absolutive case, never with the ergative or any of the locative cases. The only case marker
                                                                                    
          that the focus form has been found with is the ergative. All this seems to be the result of
                                                                                    
          inadequate data.                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         144                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Nominal cases. The five mutually exclusive overt case markers are the only elements unique to
                                                                                    
          nouns. Case markers are to be distinguished from postpositions by the impossibility of them
                                                                                    
          being independent verbs, unlike true postpositions. These suffixes are the following:
                                                                                    
          Ergative       -ik                                                        
                                                                                    
          Absolutive     -ø                                                         
                                                                                    
          Directional    -ot                                                        
                                                                                    
          Locative       -kin                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Elative        -ike                                                       
                                                                                    
          Inessive       -ip                                                        
                                                                                    
          Ergative case. This case is polysemous. It marks the nominal or pronominal agent/subject of a
                                                                                    
          transitive verb, but only if the direct object is human and animate. It also marks an instrument, if
                                                                                    
          the noun refers to an object with which an action is done. Finally, it marks causes, nouns which
                                                                                    
          are the reasons for an action occurring. See the chapter on syntax for a further discussion of
                                                                                    
          ergativity.                                                               
                                                                                    
            The existence of the ergative case was not recognized by Gatschet (nor by Swanton) because
                                                                                    
          it did not follow the pattern of nominative-accusative marking familiar from the Classical
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          languages of Europe. The common presence of the absolutive state in Atakapa clearly led him to
                                                                                    
          consider noun phrases in the language to be analytic, and in his field notes he removed or
                                                                                    
          questioned a number of genuine forms because of their synthetic nature. Ergative forms of
                                                                                    
          pronouns are especially rare in the data because Gatschet often wrote down columns of pronouns
                                                                                    
          before asking for the verbs, and rarely changed what he had previously written when his
                                                                                    
          consultants said something different.                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         145                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            Because the ergative case marks an agent, it is not used on the subjects of verbs whose
                                                                                    
          subjects are not considered agents of the verbal activity. The first two examples show that non-
                                                                                    
          human nouns as subjects of transitive verbs do not take the ergative when the objects are non-
                                                                                    
          human, but rather are in the absolutive state.                            
                                                                                    
               [ciwaˊn anpāˊkc koˊnkit]                                             
                                                                                    
          301) šiwán anpákš kóŋkit                                                  
                                                                                    
               šiwan-ø   anpakš-ø  ø-koŋ-kit                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               cat-ABS   mouse-ABS 3STATS-seize-PROG(SG)                            
                                                                                    
               ‘The cat is catching a mouse.’ (1932:112)                            
                                                                                    
               [loˊñloñc neˊc kōˊñět]                                               
                                                                                    
          302) lóŋloŋš néš kóŋat                                                    
                                                                                    
               loŋloŋš-ø neš-ø     ø-koŋ-at                                         
                                                                                    
               thunder-ABS tree-ABS 3STATS-seize-PRET                               
                                                                                    
               ‘Lightning struck a tree.’ (1932:71)                                 
                                                                                    
          In example 303, the ergative case occurs directly on a noun, in 304 and 305 its occurrence on the
                                                                                    
          following adjective shows that the noun-adjective complex is considered to be a semantic unit
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          for the purposes of case marking.                                         
                                                                                    
               [Taˊkapo icāˊk hatwiˊvat, caˊ-ik cukteˊ-i hatse-eˊc miˊculat]        
                                                                                    
          303) tákapa išá·k hatwíwat šáik šoktéy hacehéš míšulat                    
                                                                                    
               takapa    išak      hat-wiw-at     ša-ik               šoktey        
                                                                                    
               Atakapa   people    REFLEX-believe-PRET someone-ERG.AGNT             
                                                                                    
               medicine                                                             
                                                                                    
               haceheš   ø-miš-ul-at                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         146                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               bad(sg)   3OBJ-give-3PLS-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘The Atakapa people believed that some people had given them bad medicine.’
                                                                                    
               (1932:17:112-13)                                                     
                                                                                    
               [Hilaˊyi taˊxnik paˊmat]                                             
                                                                                    
          304) hiláy tánik pámat                                                    
                                                                                    
               hilay tan-ik        ø-pam-ø-at                                       
                                                                                    
               wife other-ERG.AGNT 3SOBJ-beat-3SS-PRET                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Another wife beat him.’ (1932:10:17)                                
                                                                                    
               {ishlá-ush tátik nimá-at}                                            
                                                                                    
          305) hišláwš tá·tik nimáat                                                
                                                                                    
               hišlawš   ta·t-ik        ø-nima-ø-at                                 
                                                                                    
               fever     yellow-ERG.AGNT 3SOBJ-kill-3SS-PRET                        
                                                                                    
               ‘Yellow fever killed her.’ (Gatschet,1885b:163)                      
                                                                                    
               [wi k cakiˊnu]                                                       
                                                                                    
          306) wí·k šakínu                                                          
                                                                                    
               wi-ik     šak-in-o                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               I-ERG.AGNT 3PLOBJ-ask-1SS                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘I ask them.’ (1932:47:2)                                            
                                                                                    
               [kucmeˊlik nimaˊat]                                                  
                                                                                    
          307) kušmé·lik nimáat                                                     
                                                                                    
               kušme·l-ik          ø-nima-ø-at                                      
                                                                                    
               black.person-ERG.AGNT 3SOBJ-kill-3SS-PRET                            
                                                                                    
               ‘A black person killed him.’ (1932:91:2)1                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         147                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               {wi hitét hikipámĕt}                                                 
                                                                                    
          308) wi hité·tik hipámat                                                  
                                                                                    
               wi hite·t-ik        hi-pam-ø-at                                      
                                                                                    
               my father-ERG.AGNT  1SOBJ-beat-3SS-PRET                              
                                                                                    
               ‘My father beat me.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:55)                            
                                                                                    
               {odsé hikitsáwet}                                                    
                                                                                    
          309) océhik hicáwat                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               oceh-ik        hi-caw-ø-at                                           
                                                                                    
               snake-ERG.AGNT 1SOBJ-bite-3SS-PRET                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘A snake bit me.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:56)                               
                                                                                    
               {shāk hiwálshat}                                                     
                                                                                    
          310) šá·k hiwálšat                                                        
                                                                                    
               ša-ik          hi-walš-ø-at                                          
                                                                                    
               someone-ERG.AGNT 1SOBJ-fan-3SS-PRET                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘Someone fanned me.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:32)                            
                                                                                    
          The ergative case is also used to indicate instruments, nouns with which actions are done. The
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          instrumental verbal prefix ik- (see above) seems to be a parallel development.
                                                                                    
               [wi caknoˊmc puˊckin wāˊñ aˊñkāˊmctit coˊktokcik]                    
                                                                                    
          322) wi šaknómš púškin waŋaŋkámštit šóktokšik                             
                                                                                    
               wi   šak-nomš puškin ø-waŋ-aŋkamš-tit             šoktokš-ik         
                                                                                    
               my   PL-child outside 3STATS-go.about-play(pl)-PROGRESS(PL) ball-ERG.INSTR
                                                                                    
               ‘My children are going about outside playing with a ball.’ (1932:13:63)
                                                                                    
               [wi nec paˊ-icik taˊmco]                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         148                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          323) wí néš páyšik támšo                                                  
                                                                                    
               wi   néš       páyš-ik   tám-š-o                                     
                                                                                    
               I    wood      axe-ERG.INSTR split-CAUS-1SS                          
                                                                                    
               ‘I split a block of wood with an axe.’ (1932:126:1)                  
                                                                                    
          The ergative case also expresses causes and sources:                      
                                                                                    
               [īclaˊ-uc tāˊtik kaˊwat]                                             
                                                                                    
          313) hišláwš tá·tik káwat                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hišlawš   ta·t-ik        ø-kaw-at                                    
                                                                                    
               fever     yellow-ERG.INSTR 3SSTATS-die-PRET                          
                                                                                    
               ‘She died of yellow fever. (1932:15:88)                              
                                                                                    
               [eluˊik hicok’hēˊcat]                                                
                                                                                    
          314) ilúik hišoké·šat                                                     
                                                                                    
               ilu-ik         hi-šoke·š-at                                          
                                                                                    
               heat-ERG.INSTR 1SSTATS-be.sick-PRET                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘I became sick from the heat.’ (1932:47:2)                           
                                                                                    
               {wá-ush hikiláwĕt}                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          315) wáwšik hiláwat                                                       
                                                                                    
               wawš-ik        hi-law-at                                             
                                                                                    
               steam-ERG.INSTR 1SSTATS-burn-PRET                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I got burned by the steam.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:55)2                   
                                                                                    
               [kutsnăˊn tuˊik kaˊ]                                                 
                                                                                    
          316) kucnán túik ká                                                       
                                                                                    
               kucnan    tu-ik          ka                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         149                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               knife     cane-ERG.INSTR made                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘knife made of cane’ (1932:18:128)                                   
                                                                                    
               [luˊ itka aˊmcne]                                                    
                                                                                    
          317) lúik ká ámšne                                                        
                                                                                    
               lu-ik          ka   am-š-ne                                          
                                                                                    
               clay-ERG.INSTR made drink-CAUS-GER                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘drinking vessel made of clay’ (1928:77:2)                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Absolutive. This is unmarked, and it is the basic state of an Atakapa noun. It is somewhat
                                                                                    
          misleading to treat it as a case, for unlike a language like Natchez, which has a marked
                                                                                    
          absolutive case, there is no case marking for the absolutive in Atakapa. A zero marker is used in
                                                                                    
          the examples below, but it is purely formal, and does not imply the former existence of an overt
                                                                                    
          form. The absolutive is the form of noun used as the subject of an intransitive verb, the subject
                                                                                    
          of a transitive stative verb, the object of a transitive verb, either active or stative, and as a citation
                                                                                    
          form.                                                                     
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊti Icaˊt toˊkc coxkoˊ-i waˊci kaˊwĕt]                          
                                                                                    
          318) yukhíti iša·ttókš šokóy wáši káwat                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               yukhiti   iša·ttókš šokoy waši-ø   ø-kawat                           
                                                                                    
               Indian    PN        chief old-ABS  3STATS-die-PRET                   
                                                                                    
               ‘The Indian, Iša·ttokš, the old chief, died.’ (1932:13:54)           
                                                                                    
          319) čákta yukhíti iša·thatyíncne hinákka šakkéat                         
                                                                                    
               čakta3    yukhiti   iša·thatyincne-ø hinakka šak-ke-at               
                                                                                    
               Choctaw   Indian    crown-ABS      like      3PLSTATS-have-PRET      
                                                                                    
               ‘The Choctaw Indians had crowns in the same way.’ (1932:19:136-37)   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         150                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Icāk cokcaktsăñc konˊuˊhts]                                         
                                                                                    
          320) iša·k šokšakcáŋš kóŋ úhc                                             
                                                                                    
               iša·k     šok-šak-caŋš-ø koŋ  ø-uhc                                  
                                                                                    
               person    STH-3PLO-steal -ABS sieze 3PLSTATS-be.able                 
                                                                                    
               ‘They can catch the thief.’ (1932:13:61)                             
                                                                                    
               [Iˊcak capiˊxk caklaˊwiulhahat]                                      
                                                                                    
          321) iša·k šappíhk šakláwiulháhat                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               iša·k     šak-pihk-ø     šak-law-i-ul-hah-at                         
                                                                                    
               person    PL-die(pl)-ABS 3PLOBJ-burn-CAUS-3PLS-NEG-PRET              
                                                                                    
               ‘They did not burn the dead.’ (1932:19:140)                          
                                                                                    
          Note that, as in the Southeastern language Natchez (Kimball, 2005:443), if the first verb in a
                                                                                    
          series of verbs is intransitive, then the subject noun is in the absolutive case. The following is a
                                                                                    
          textual example.                                                          
                                                                                    
               [Yuk’hiˊti ipcōˊk oˊk yaˊ peˊneat]                                   
                                                                                    
          322) yukhíti ipšók ókya péniat                                            
                                                                                    
               yukhiti   ipšok-ø   ok=ya     ø-peni-ø-at                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Indian    doctor-ABS come=and 3OBJ-cure-3SS-PRET                     
                                                                                    
               The Indian doctor came and cured her. (1932:55-56)                   
                                                                                    
          Directional case. The directional case is marked by the suffix -ot. Frequently it is written -ut,
                                                                                    
          the latter a weakened form occurring after an accented syllable. Most examples in the texts and
                                                                                    
          dictionary are written separately from the noun. This is based on Gatschet’s field notes, for after
                                                                                    
          he decided that the case marker was a postposition, he often went back and separated it from the
                                                                                    
          noun it was written together with, and added an accent. However, -ot is not a postposition, as
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         151                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          unlike true postpositions it never occurs verbalized. The basic meaning of the directional case is
                                                                                    
          action happening to or towards the noun to which it is attached. In terms of grammar, it has both
                                                                                    
          dative and locative functions.                                            
                                                                                    
               [Iyuˊtskin hac okwaˊñc oˊt tiˊcat yaˊ kaˊwat]                        
                                                                                    
          323) hiyúckin há·š hokwáŋšot tíšatya káwat                                
                                                                                    
               hi-yuc-kin          ha-iš     hokwaŋš-ot ø-tiš-at=ya                 
                                                                                    
               1SSTAS-be.grown-WHEN he-FOC   war-DIR   3STATS-go-PRET=and           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ø-kaw-at                                                             
                                                                                    
               3STATS-die-PRET                                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘When I was grown, he went to the war and died.’ (1932:16-17:104-05) 
                                                                                    
               [Haˊlkin tsīˊpcat Yuk’hiˊti Tuˊl oˊt]                                
                                                                                    
          324) hálkin cípšat yukhíti túlot                                          
                                                                                    
               halkin    cipš-ø-at      yukhiti   tul-ot                            
                                                                                    
               afterwards move-3SS-PRET Indian    lake-DIR                          
                                                                                    
               ‘Afterwards he moved to Lake Prien (Indian Lake).’ (1932:16:95)      
                                                                                    
          The directional case is also used to indicate or disambiguate indirect objects. In the following
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          example, the verb išmišat can mean both ‘he gave it to us’ or 'we gave it to him,’ as the object
                                                                                    
          prefixes in the preterite mode also serve as subject prefixes. The directional case then
                                                                                    
          disambiguates the meaning.                                                
                                                                                    
               [ha yuˊkit ut icmiˊcat]                                              
                                                                                    
          325) há yukítot išmíšat                                                   
                                                                                    
               ha   yukit-ot  iš-miš-ø-at                                           
                                                                                    
               he   we-DIR    1PLO-give-3SS-PRET                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         152                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘He gave it to us.’ (1932:80:2)                                      
                                                                                    
          The directional case sometimes has a meaning ‘in regard to; pertaining to; belonging to,’ as in
                                                                                    
          the following textual examples.                                           
                                                                                    
               [Loˊ yukiˊti haˊl cox̥ koˊyit yuk’hit cakicaˊk ut]                   
                                                                                    
          326) ló yukhíti halšokóyat yukhitšakiša·kot                               
                                                                                    
               lo   yukhiti   ø-hal-šokoy-at      yukhit-šak-iša·k-ot               
                                                                                    
               PN   Indian    3STATS-be.last-be.chief-PRET Indian-PL-person-DIR     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Lo was the last Indian chief pertaining to the Indian people.’ (1932:10:28)
                                                                                    
               [Wāˊñ-pum yaˊ kic tanuˊk taˊxn ut ikāt paˊkĕt]                       
                                                                                    
          327) wáŋpúmya kíš tanúk tánot iká·t pákat                                 
                                                                                    
               waŋ-pum=ya     kiš       tanuk tan-ot                                
                                                                                    
               go.about-dance=and woman one  another-DIR foot                       
                                                                                    
               hika·t ø-pak-ø-at                                                    
                                                                                    
               3OBJ-step.on-3SS-PRET
