               ‘They were going about dancing, and one woman stepped on the foot belonging to
                                                                                    
               another.’ (1932:20:141-142)                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The first-person singular pronoun wi has the unique directional form wéhet, which also takes the
                                                                                    
          form wé·t. The following are the only attested examples:                  
                                                                                    
               [yaˊ icāk weˊhet ivivhaˊhĕt]                                         
                                                                                    
          328) ya išá·k wéhet hiwíwháhat                                            
                                                                                    
               ya   išak      wehet     hi-wiw-ø-hah-at                             
                                                                                    
               that person    I(DIR)    1SO-believe-3SS-NEG-PRET                    
                                                                                    
               ‘That person did not believe me.’ (1932:150:1)                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         153                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [wēt hihaˊyuĕt]                                                      
                                                                                    
          329) wé·t hiháyuat                                                        
                                                                                    
               wehet     hi-hayu-ø-at                                               
                                                                                    
               I(DIR)    1SO-laugh.at-3SS-PRET                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘He laughed at me.’ (1932:37:2)                                      
                                                                                    
               [weˊhet himiˊcat]                                                    
                                                                                    
          330) wéhet himíšat                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wehet     hi-miš-ø-at                                                
                                                                                    
               I(DIR)    1SO-give-3SS-PRET                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘He gave it to me.’ (1932:80:2)                                      
                                                                                    
          All three of these example show the directional case of the pronoun disambiguating a verb, as
                                                                                    
          hiwíwháhat can mean both ‘I did not believe him’ and ‘he did not believe me,’ hiháyuat can
                                                                                    
          mean ‘I laughed at him,’ and ‘he laughed at me,’ and himíšat can mean ‘I gave it to him,’ and ‘he
                                                                                    
          gave it to me.’ The expected directional case form wíot also occurs.      
                                                                                    
          Locative case. This case is marked by the suffix -kin. It Indicates that an action is taking place
                                                                                    
          in a static location, with little or no movement. It can also express temporal location, as in
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          example 333.                                                              
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊt nuˊnkin puˊm ichāˊxc a itiyiˊlc]                             
                                                                                    
          331) yukít núŋkin púm išháhš a itiyílš                                    
                                                                                    
               yukit nuŋ-kin  pum       iš-hahš        a    itiyilš                 
                                                                                    
               our  village-LOC dance   1PLSTATS-not.have this month                
                                                                                    
               ‘We are not having a dance in our village this month.’ (1932:13:53)  
                                                                                    
               [Viˊvulat iˊcak capiˊx̥ k taneˊ-u okcak’huˊ-ulat. Aˊnkin tiˊx̥ kin ok-huˊ-ulat]
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         154                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          332) wíwulat iša·k šappíhk tannéw okšakhúulat áŋkin tíhkin okhúulat       
                                                                                    
               wiw-ul-at      iša·k     šak-pihk  tan-hew                           
                                                                                    
               believe-3PLS-PRET person PL-die(pl) other-PL                         
                                                                                    
               ok-šak-hu-ul-at          aŋ-kin    ø-tiht-kin                        
                                                                                    
               COME.AND-3PLO-see-3PLS-PRET house-LOC 3STATS-lie-WHEN                
                                                                                    
               ok-hu-ul-at                                                          
                                                                                    
               COME.AND-See-3PLS-PRET                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘They believed that other dead people came to see them; they came to see
                                                                                    
               him while he was lying in the house.’ (1932:18:121-21)               
                                                                                    
               [haˊl yiˊlkin imōˊculat]                                             
                                                                                    
          333) hál yílkin himó·šulat                                                
                                                                                    
               hal  yil-kin   himo·š-ul-at                                          
                                                                                    
               next day-LOC   bury-3PLS-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘On the next day, they buried him/her.’ (1932:18:124)                
                                                                                    
          It is notable that the locative suffix -kin is homophonous with, if not identical to, the suffix -kin,
                                                                                    
          ‘while, when,’ one of the markers of the subordinate mode. While a case suffix cannot be
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          verbalized on its own, it is possible for a noun in the locative case to be verbalized, as in the
                                                                                    
          following example.                                                        
                                                                                    
               [Haˊ nuˊk iyāˊñ imōˊcti(xt) yuk’hiˊti cakimōc-neˊkin haˊ]            
                                                                                    
          334) há nuk iyáŋ himó·štíht yukhíti šakhimo·šné·kinháh                    
                                                                                    
               ha   nuk  iyaŋ ø-himo·š-tiht       yukhiti                           
                                                                                    
               she  alone there 3STATS-be.buried-lie(sg) Indian                     
                                                                                    
               šakhimo·šne·-kin-hah                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         155                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.cemetery-LOC-NEG                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘She alone lies buried there. She is not in the Indian cemetery.’ (1932:15:89-90)
                                                                                    
          Elative case. This case, marked with the suffix -ike, indicates that something is coming from the
                                                                                    
          noun to which it is suffixed. The first example is the only one from a natural text. Note that the
                                                                                    
          sequence ii in example 338 collapses to long i.                           
                                                                                    
               [kaukaˊ-u aˊm’n aˊnike ntaˊhĕntat]                                   
                                                                                    
          335) kawkáw ámn̥ ánike n̥ táhn̥ tat                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kawkaw    ø-am-n̥        an-ike    ø-n̥tah-n̥t-at                    
                                                                                    
               water     3STATS-drink-WHEN ear-EL 3STATS-come.out-CONTIN-PRET       
                                                                                    
               ‘When he drank water, it kept on coming out of his ears. (1932:10:19-20)
                                                                                    
               [yā icak akipai-iˊke ōt]                                             
                                                                                    
          336) ya išá·k akipáyike ók                                                
                                                                                    
               ya   iša·k     akipay-ike ok-ø                                       
                                                                                    
               that person    south-EL  come-3SS                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘That man comes from the south.’ (1932:24:1)                         
                                                                                    
               {wi ipăn payóket tá-i hétsike}                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          337) wi ipán payókat táy hécike                                           
                                                                                    
               wi   ipan      pay-ok-ø-at         tay  hec-ike                      
                                                                                    
               my   male.cousin BACK-come-3SS-PRET river big-EL                     
                                                                                    
               ‘My cousin has come back from the Mississippi River.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:48)
                                                                                    
               [hikīˊke iˊcak]                                                      
                                                                                    
          338) hikí·ke išà·k                                                        
                                                                                    
               hiki-ike  ø-išà·k                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         156                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               west-EL   3STATS-be.person                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘He is a man from the west.’ (1932: 40:2)                            
                                                                                    
          Inessive case. This case is marked with the suffix -ip. The inessive case indicates that
                                                                                    
          something is within an area, rather than being located statically.        
                                                                                    
               [nec hicoˊmckin yuk’hiˊti tulip lōˊx̥kin iwāˊt’n hūˊnst]             
                                                                                    
          339) neš hišómškin yukhíti túlip lókkin iwá·tn húnst                      
                                                                                    
               neš  hišomš-kin yukhiti  tul-ip    lok-kin                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tree small-LOC Indian    lake-INESS prairie-LOC                      
                                                                                    
               ø-iwa·t-n           hu-nšt                                           
                                                                                    
               3STATS-move.hither-WHEN see-CUSTOM                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘They customarily see it (the Skin-Desirer) in the brush around Lake Prien (Indian
                                                                                    
               Lake), when it moves towards them in the prairie.’ (1932:11:35)      
                                                                                    
               [nē mak oˊtsip tiˊcta wi itēt teweˊta]                               
                                                                                    
          340) ne·mak ocip tišta wi ite·t tiwe·ta                                   
                                                                                    
               ne·mak    oc-ip     tiš-ta    wi   ite·t     tiwe·-ta                
                                                                                    
               mountain  on-INESS  go(sg)-PCPL my father    be.with-PCPL            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I am to go into the mountains in order to be with my father.’ (1932:78:1)
                                                                                    
          The inessive case has a secondary meaning of ‘by means of; using.’ It is a kind of instrumental,
                                                                                    
          but only used with nouns that cannot be physically handled.               
                                                                                    
               [icak heˊ-u hakit eˊñip cakwaˊ-uco]                                  
                                                                                    
          341) iša·k héw hakit é·ŋip šakwáhušo                                      
                                                                                    
               iša·k     hew  hakit e·ŋ-ip   šak-wahuš-o                            
                                                                                    
               person    many their name-INESS 3PLO-call-1SS                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         157                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I call many people by their names.’ (1932:31:1)                     
                                                                                    
               [haˊ icāˊk luˊkin tiktāt temaˊkip]                                   
                                                                                    
          342) á išá·k lúkin tiktá·t temákip                                        
                                                                                    
               a    iša·k     lu-kin    tik-ta·t-ø     temak-ip                     
                                                                                    
               this person    mud-LOC   go.and-stand-3SS knee-INESS                 
                                                                                    
               ‘This man goes and stands up to his knees in mud.’ (1932:129:2)      
                                                                                    
               [tsanuˊkip iteˊ-u-iciˊko taˊ-i kipaˊxci]                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          343) canúkip itéwišího táy kipáhši                                        
                                                                                    
               canuk-ip  itew-iših-o    tay  kipahši                                
                                                                                    
               horse-INESS ride-ford-1SS river across                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I ford across the river riding on a horse.’ (1932:119:1)            
                                                                                    
               [tuˊpoˊ-ipwaˊñ / tuˊpoˊpwān]                                         
                                                                                    
          344) tupóhipwáŋ / tupó·pwáŋ                                               
                                                                                    
               tu-poh-ip-waŋ                                                        
                                                                                    
               boat-smoke-INESS-go.about                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘steamboat’ (1932:105:2)                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The inessive case has a unique meaning when used on numerals, where it means ‘N times.’
                                                                                    
               [Itiˊ tanuˊkip yiˊl tūt nēn ōˊl paˊi moˊhat]                         
                                                                                    
          345) ití tanúkip yíl tútnan ó·l paymóhat                                  
                                                                                    
               ití  tanuk-ip  yil  tutnan    o·l  pay-moh-ø-at                      
                                                                                    
               night one-INESS day before    near BACK-return-3SS-PRET              
                                                                                    
               ‘Once at night, just before daybreak, she returned back.’ (1932:17:108-09)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         158                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The inessive case is also used on the initial noun of certain compounds. In this usage, the noun
                                                                                    
          in the inessive case sets a limit on the location of the occurrence of the noun to which the
                                                                                    
          compound refers.                                                          
                                                                                    
               [aˊyip teˊxlk taˊlkop]                                               
                                                                                    
          346) áyip téɬk talkòp                                                     
                                                                                    
               ay-ip          teƛk      talkop                                      
                                                                                    
               swamp-INESS    flower    blue                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘marsh lily,’ lit. ‘the blue flower found within the swamp.’ (1932:23:1)
                                                                                    
               [kaˊkip hiyeˊn]                                                      
                                                                                    
          347) kákip hiyén                                                          
                                                                                    
               kak-ip hiyen                                                         
                                                                                    
               forest-INESS hog                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘opossum,’ lit. ‘the hog that is found within the forest.’ (1932:56:2)
                                                                                    
               [nûñ-uˊcip cokoˊ-i]                                                  
                                                                                    
          348) nuŋúšip šokóy                                                        
                                                                                    
               nuŋuš-ip  šokoy                                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               city-INESS chief                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘judge,’ lit. ‘the chief that is found within the city.’ (1932:60:2) 
                                                                                    
          The inessive case suffix has a homophonous suffix -ip used in the subordinate mode of verbs
                                                                                    
          with the meaning ‘because.’                                               
                                                                                    
          Noun pluralization. Plural is primarily a verbal category in Atakapa, rather than a nominal one,
                                                                                    
          as in the following example in which the plurality of the subject and the object is marked entirely
                                                                                    
          on the verb.                                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         159                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               {kiwílsh yukíti shakónshûl}                                          
                                                                                    
          349) kiwilš yukhiti šakoŋšul                                              
                                                                                    
               kiwilš         yukhiti   šak-oŋš-ul                                  
                                                                                    
               white.person   Indian    3PLO-not.like-3PLS                          
                                                                                    
               ‘White people do not like the Indians.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:80)         
                                                                                    
          Even then, if the context is clear, plurality of a noun is simply assumed, as in the following
                                                                                    
          textual example:                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Tchaˊkta yuk’hiti icāˊt atyiˊnsne hinaˊka cakeˊat]                  
                                                                                    
          350) čákta yukhíti iša·thatyíncne hinákka šakkéat                         
                                                                                    
               čakta     yukhiti   iša·t-hat-yinc-ne        hinakka                 
                                                                                    
               Choctaw   Indian    head-REFLEX-wrap.around-GER like                 
                                                                                    
               šak-ke-at                                                            
                                                                                    
               3PLSTATS-have-PRET                                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘The Choctaw Indians (plural marked on verb) had crowns (plural assumed) in the same
                                                                                    
               way.’ (1932:19:136-37)                                               
                                                                                    
          Nonetheless, there is a limited degree of noun pluralization in Atakapa. This consists of four
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          kinds: prefixed pluralization, suffixed pluralization, adjectival pluralization, and irregular
                                                                                    
          pluralization.                                                            
                                                                                    
          Prefixed pluralization. A seemingly productive way to form a plural in Atakapa is to prefix
                                                                                    
          šak- to the noun. This prefix is homophonous with, and probably identical to, the third-person
                                                                                    
          plural object prefix šak-. The following list is exhaustive.4             
                                                                                    
          Singular                 Plural                                           
                                                                                    
          ipšók     doctor         šakipšók       doctors                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         160                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          išá·k     person         šakišá·k       people; relatives                 
                                                                                    
          kíŋ       pet            šakkíŋ         pets                              
                                                                                    
          lúš       fish scale     šaklúš         fish scales                       
                                                                                    
          níl       grandchild     šakníl         grandchildren                     
                                                                                    
          noknáw    wing feather   šaknoknáw      wing feathers                     
                                                                                    
          nómš      child          šaknómš        children                          
                                                                                    
          šó        seed           šakšó          seeds                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          šokóy     chief          šakšokóy       chiefs                            
                                                                                    
          The few attested examples suggest that a plural noun requires a plural verb.
                                                                                    
               [ha cakicāˊk tsīˊpsulat hiweˊvc tanuˊk maˊ-i]                        
                                                                                    
          351) ha šakišá·k cípšulat hiwéwš tanúk máy                                
                                                                                    
               ha   šak-iša·k cipš-ul-at     hiwewš    tanuk may                    
                                                                                    
               his  PL-person move-3PLS-PRET measure   one  away                    
                                                                                    
               ‘His relatives moved a mile away.’ (1932:19:138-39)                  
                                                                                    
          Suffixed pluralization. There is only one suffix that marks the plural, -héw, derived from the
                                                                                    
          independent verb héw, ‘to be many.’ Many of the examples collected by Gatschet are
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ambiguous; that is, they can be understood either as plurals or as multiple verb constructions
                                                                                    
          when the pluralized noun is the subject of the sentence and directly precedes the verb, as in the
                                                                                    
          two analyses of the following example.                                    
                                                                                    
               [otseˊ heˊ-u ciˊutiutit]                                             
                                                                                    
          352a) océhhéw šíwtiwtit                                                   
                                                                                    
               océh-héw  ø-šíw-tiw-tit                                              
                                                                                    
               snake-PL  3STATS-crawl-go(pl)-PROG(PL)                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         161                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘The snakes are going crawling.’                                     
                                                                                    
          352b) océh héwšíwtiwtit                                                   
                                                                                    
               océh      ø-héw-šíw-tiw-tit                                          
                                                                                    
               snake     3STATS-be.many-crawl-go(pl)-PROG(PL)                       
                                                                                    
               ‘Many snakes are going crawling.’ (1932:99:1)                        
                                                                                    
          If the noun used with -héw is the object of a transitive verb, it is a plural, and not a multiple verb
                                                                                    
          construction, as if -héw were a verb, it would have a different subject, and multiple verb
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          constructions require the same subject.                                   
                                                                                    
               [neˊc heˊu caktaˊmo]                                                 
                                                                                    
          353) néšhéw šaktámo                                                       
                                                                                    
               néš-héw   šak-tám-o                                                  
                                                                                    
               tree-PL   3PLO-climb-1SS                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘I climb trees.’ (1932:39:2)                                         
                                                                                    
               [wi cokiuˊlc heˊ-u keˊ-u huˊnto]                                     
                                                                                    
          354) wi šokyúlšhéw kéwhún̥to                                              
                                                                                    
               wi   šokyulš-hew kew-hu-n̥t-o·                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               I    book-PL   SIT-read-CONTIN-PHR.TERM                              
                                                                                    
               ‘I am going to sit and read books.’ (1932:39:2)                      
                                                                                    
          The suffix -héw pluralizes a noun-adjective sequence when the adjective does not have a plural
                                                                                    
          form of its own.                                                          
                                                                                    
               [kakauˊ hidsoˊm heˊ-u cak’huˊo]                                      
                                                                                    
          355) kakáw hicónhéw šakhúo                                                
                                                                                    
               kakaw hicon-hew šak-hu-o                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         162                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               sun  small-PL  3PLO-see-1SS                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘I see the stars.’ (1932:42:2)                                       
                                                                                    
          In the texts, there is an unambiguous example of this suffix; it is used as the plural of tán ‘some;
                                                                                    
          other,’ and the cluster /nh/ assimilates to /nn/ and the accent shifts to the final syllable, showing
                                                                                    
          that this indeed is a plural suffix.                                      
                                                                                    
               [Taneˊ-u tuˊlkin paˊk-cakpaˊkicat.]                                  
                                                                                    
          356) tannéw túlkin pákšakpákišat                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tan-hew   tul-kin   pak,šak,pak-iš-at                                
                                                                                    
               some-PL   lake-loc  REDUP,3PLOBJ,throw.in-CAUS-PRET                  
                                                                                    
               ‘Some of them they threw again and again into the lake.’ (1932:20:146)
                                                                                    
          Another example is the interrogative pronoun šinnéw ‘who (pl);’ however the singular root *šin-
                                                                                    
          is not attested.                                                          
                                                                                    
          Adjectival pluralization. Several nouns in Atakapa are formed of a compound between a noun
                                                                                    
          and an adjective. Pluralization of these nouns is done by pluralizing the adjectival part of the
                                                                                    
          compound. This is another productive method of noun pluralization.        
                                                                                    
               Singular                 Plural                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kušmé·l 5 ‘black person’ kušmélme·l     mé·l ‘be black’              
                                                                                    
               kiškóp    ‘old woman’    keškópkop 6    kóp  ‘be white’              
                                                                                    
               iša·kkúc 7 ‘Indian’      iša·kkúckuc    kúc  ‘be red’                
                                                                                    
               noktewmé·l ‘goose’       noktewmélme·l  mé·l ‘be black’              
                                                                                    
               šokommé·l ‘bison’        šokommélme·l   mé·l ‘be black’              
                                                                                    
               iša·kkáw 8 ‘white person’ iša·kšappíhk  káw / šappíhk ‘be dead’      
                                                                                    
          A noun pluralized in this fashion also requires a plural verb.            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         163                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Samtiˊ hitiˊ kuˊcmelmēl puˊm kaˊ-ulat]                              
                                                                                    
          357) samtí ití kúšmélme·l púm káulat                                      
                                                                                    
               samti     ití  kuš,mel,me·l   pum  ka-ul-at                          
                                                                                    
               Saturday  night black.person,PL, dance make-3PLS-PRET                
                                                                                    
               ‘On Saturday night, the black people held a dance.’ (1932:20:141)    
                                                                                    
          Irregular pluralization. There are only four nouns in Atakapa that form plurals in this way, kíš
                                                                                    
          ‘woman’, pl. kéš 9, kíš iškalít ‘girl,’ pl. kešéš, cíš ‘infant,’ pl. šé·š, and katpák ‘trout,’ pl. katpá.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          These plural nouns requires a plural adjective and a plural verb.         
                                                                                    
               [keˊc caksokeˊc yoˊxt wi caˊk’hinai]                                 
                                                                                    
          358) kéš šakšoké·šyóht wí šakhinày                                        
                                                                                    
               keš       šak-šoke·š-yoht     wi   šak-hi-nay                        
                                                                                    
               women     3PLSTATS-sick-lie(pl) I  3PLO-1STATS-think                 
                                                                                    
               ‘I think many women are lying sick.’ (1932:65:2)                     
                                                                                    
           There is a single noun which forms its plural with an irregular prefix: wáš ‘leaf,’ plural
                                                                                    
          téwaš. This plural noun also requires a plural verb.                      
                                                                                    
               {téwash wilótit}                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          359) téwaš wilótit                                                        
                                                                                    
               té-waš    ø-wiló-tit                                                 
                                                                                    
               PL-leaf   3STATS-fall(pl)-PROG(PL)                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘The leaves are falling.’ (Gatschet, 1885a:223)                      
                                                                                    
          Nouns plural in form but singular in meaning. There are a handful of Atakapa nouns that
                                                                                    
          appear plural in form, using adjectival pluralization, the meanings of which are singular.
                                                                                    
               noktewmélme·l  ‘goose’   mélme·l   ‘black (pl)’                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         164                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tépukkúckuc    ‘plum’    kúckuc    ‘red (pl)’                        
                                                                                    
               yáwlákla·k     ‘drum fish’ láklá·k ‘hard (pl)’                       
                                                                                    
          All three of these nouns were originally descriptive phrases: noktéw mélme·l ‘its shoulders are
                                                                                    
          black;’ tépuk kúckuc ‘they are red peaches,’ and yáw á·l lákla·k ‘hard-fleshed fish,’ in the latter
                                                                                    
          case, pluralization of the adjective occurred because á·l ‘flesh’ is a mass noun. No plural forms
                                                                                    
          of these nouns have been noted.                                           
                                                                                    
          Noun possession. Possession is marked by placing the possessor noun or pronoun before the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          possessed noun.                                                           
                                                                                    
               [Iˊcak kaˊw aˊñ laˊwiulat]                                           
                                                                                    
          360) išà·k káw áŋ láwiulat                                                
                                                                                    
               iša·k     kaw  aŋ   law-i-ul-at.                                     
                                                                                    
               person    dead house burn-CAUS-3PLS-PRET                             
                                                                                    
               ‘They burned the dead person’s house. (1932:19:138)                  
                                                                                    
          If the possessor is a singular pronoun, the pronoun is unaccented.        
                                                                                    
               [ha icāˊt ha uˊc mōn naˊ-ucĕm]                                       
                                                                                    
          361) ha išá·t ha úš mo·n náwšim                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ha   iša·t ha  uš   mo·n ø-naw-šim                                   
                                                                                    
               his  head his  body all  3STATS-hair-NOTHING.BUT                     
                                                                                    
               ‘His head and his whole body were nothing but hair.’ (1932: 11:33-34)
                                                                                    
               {shók na komhópshkin nkéa? laklā́ksh nīt}                            
                                                                                    
          362) šók na komhópškin n̥ké á? laklá·kš ní·t                              
                                                                                    
               šok       na   komhopš-kin n̥-ke        a    lakla·kš  ni·t          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               something your pocket-LOC 2SSTATS-have  Q    dollar    five          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         165                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘What do you have in your pocket? Five dollars.’ (Gatschet 1885b:79) 
                                                                                    
               [wi kulcaˊ Cuˊkuhui yuk’hiˊti cox̥ koˊyat]                           
                                                                                    
          363) wi kulšá šúkuhuy yukhíti šokóyat                                     
                                                                                    
               wi   kulša     šukuhuy   yukhiti   ø-šokoy-at                        
                                                                                    
               my   grandfather PN      Indian    3STATS-be.chief-PRET              
                                                                                    
               ‘My grandfather, Šukuhuy, was an Indian chief.’ (1932:16:93)         
                                                                                    
          The use of a resumptive pronoun for third person possession is very common, even though the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          apposition construction in example 360 seems perfectly adequate.          
                                                                                    
               [Caktaˊlko Yuk’hiˊti icaˊk kaˊ-u haˊtalkoˊat hinaˊk cakioˊñculĕt]    
                                                                                    
          364) šaktálko yukhíti išá·k káw ha tàl kóat hinák šakyóŋšulat             
                                                                                    
               šak-tal-ko     yukhiti   iša·k kaw ha   tal  ø-ko-at                 
                                                                                    
               PL-skin-wanter Indian    person dead his skin 3STATS-want-PRET       
                                                                                    
               hinak     šak-yoŋš-ul-at                                             
                                                                                    
               be.like   3PLO-call-3PLO-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘The Skin-desirer wanted the skin of a dead Indian; for this reason they named it so.’
                                                                                    
               (1932:11:32)                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [icak heˊ-u hakit eˊñip cakwaˊ-uco]                                  
                                                                                    
          365) iša·k héw hakit é·ŋip šakwáhušo                                      
                                                                                    
               iša·k     hew  hakit e·ŋ-ip   šak-wahuš-o                            
                                                                                    
               person    many their name-INESS 3PLO-call-1SS                        
                                                                                    
               ‘I call many people by their names.’ (1932:31:1)                     
                                                                                    
          The possessive phrase forms a semantic unit and can be the subject or the object of a verb.
                                                                                    
               [wīc wi itēˊt iyaniaˊn haˊxcat]                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         166                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          366) wí·š wi ité·t hiyanyánháhšat                                         
                                                                                    
               wi-iš wi  ite·t hi-yanyan-hahš-at                                    
                                                                                    
               I-FOC my  father 1SSTATS-fear-NEG-PRET                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I did not fear my father.’ (1932:16:103)                            
                                                                                    
          The possessor noun must be in the absolutive state and not have the focus marker -iš. Note the
                                                                                    
          following example, where the pronoun in the focus form is the subject of the verb ‘not to have,’
                                                                                    
          and is not the possessor of the noun ‘husband.’                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [hāc ipaˊ hāˊxcĕt (h)iuˊxts atkaˊki]                                 
                                                                                    
          367) há·š hipá háhšat hiúhc atkáki                                        
                                                                                    
               ha-iš     hipa      ø-hahš-at           hi-uhc     atkaki            
                                                                                    
               she-FOC   husband   3STATS-not.have-PRET 1SSTATS-know since          
                                                                                    
               ‘She did not have a husband from the time I knew her.’ (Not *her husband did
                                                                                    
               not exist…) (1932:14:73-74)                                          
                                                                                    
          A noun phrase can act as the possessor of another noun.                   
                                                                                    
               [kéˊc ‘n cakióˊl téc mōñ cakmañmáˊñĕt]                               
                                                                                    
          368) kéšn̥ šakyól té·š mo·n šakmaŋmáŋat                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               keš=n̥         šakyol    te·š mo·n šak-maŋ,maŋ-at                    
                                                                                    
               woman(pl)=and  man       hair all  3PLSTATS-REDUP, be.long-PRET      
                                                                                    
               ‘Women’s and men’s hair was all long.’ (1932:10:23)                  
                                                                                    
          In this example the third-person plural stative prefix acts as a marker of indirect object.
                                                                                    
          The noun té·š is a mass noun and requires plural verb agreement, so the prefix refers back to the
                                                                                    
          nouns acting as possessors. The verb can be translated literally as ‘it was long to/for them.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         167                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            Complex possessive phrases also occur. In the following example the word hakít ‘they’ is the
                                                                                    
          possessor of nómš, ‘child’ which is the possessor of the word išá·t ‘head.’
                                                                                    
               {Tsaxtá shakīt nómsh ishát shakpalpálishat}                          
                                                                                    
          369) cahtá·š hakít nómš išá·t šakpalpálišat                               
                                                                                    
               cahta-iš  hakit nomš     iša·t     šak-,pal,pal-iš-ø-at              
                                                                                    
               Choctaw-FOC their child  head      3PLO-,REDUP,be.flat-CAUS-3SS-PRET 
                                                                                    
               ‘As for the Choctaw, they flattened the various heads of their children.’ (Gatschet,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               1885b:147)                                                           
                                                                                    
          Pronouns. Atakapa pronouns distinguish three persons and two numbers. They occur in
                                                                                    
          absolutive, ergative (marked by the suffix -ik)10, and focus forms (marked by the suffix -iš),
                                                                                    
          although not all ergative forms are attested, and only two of the ergative and focused forms is
                                                                                    
          found in the corpus.                                                      
                                                                                    
                              Absolutive Ergative Focus     Ergative/Focus          
                                                                                    
          1st person singular wí        wí·k      wíiš / wí·š (lacking)             
                                                                                    
          2nd person singular ná        ná·k      náiš / ná·š ná·kš                 
                                                                                    
          3rd person singular há        há·k      haiš / há·š (lacking)             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1st person plural   yukít     yukitik   yukítiš / yukíts (lacking)        
                                                                                    
          2nd person plural   nakít     (lacking) nakitiš     (lacking)             
                                                                                    
          3rd person plural   hakít     (lacking) hakitiš / hakíts (lacking)        
                                                                                    
          Pronouns are preposed to nouns to mark possession, preposed to verbs to mark subject. Subject
                                                                                    
          pronouns are very frequent in the elicited material in the dictionary, but are much less frequent in
