          the texts, where they always seem to have a certain emphasis.             
                                                                                    
               [wīc wi itēˊt iyaniaˊn haˊxcat]                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         168

          378) yokón šakyókat hakítot                                               
                                                                                    
               yokon     šak-yok-ø-at        hakit-ot                               
                                                                                    
               song      3PLO-sing-3SS-PRET  they-DIR                               
                                                                                    
               ‘She sang songs to them.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:162-63)                   
                                                                                    
          Deictics. There are two deictics, á ‘this; these,’ and yá ‘that; those;’ a third deictic has but a
                                                                                    
          single occurrence: ináha ‘that; those far off.’ The deictics can also serve as pronouns, in which
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          case they mean ‘this person; this thing,’ and ‘that person; that thing.’ In their use as deictics,
                                                                                    
          they always precede the noun they modify.                                 
                                                                                    
               [Kic Tōt haˊ nīl Yoyoˊt haˊ ickiciˊl haˊhu īt kaˊwat aˊ nuˊnkin]     
                                                                                    
          379) kištó·t ha níl yoyót ha iškišíl háhuwi·t káwat á núŋkin              
                                                                                    
               kišto·t ha nil      yoyot ha  iškišil   hahuwi·t                     
                                                                                    
               PN     her grandchild PN her  daughter  previously                   
                                                                                    
               ø-kaw-at       a    nuŋ-kin                                          
                                                                                    
               3STATS-die-PRET this village-LOC                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘Kišto·t, her grandchild, Yoyot’s daughter, had died previously in this town.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               (1932:15:90-91)                                                      
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊt nuˊnkin puˊm ichāˊxc a itiyiˊlc]

          384) yá káwšne pałat                                                      
                                                                                    
               ya   kawšne    paƛ-ø-at

               [inaˊha noˊmc]                                                       
                                                                                    
          386) ináha nómš

          The deictics are related to the nouns ap ‘here,’ awp ‘ right here,’ and yap / yaŋ ‘there,’ The
                                                                                    
          adverb ináha ‘sort of; somewhat,” is homophonous with, and may be identical to the deictic
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ináha.                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Postpositions. Postpositions are relational nouns that indicate the location of the nouns they
                                                                                    
          modify. Postpositions can take nominal cases, and like other nouns can be verbalized freely. As
                                                                                    
          their name indicates, postpositions follow the nouns they modify.         
                                                                                    
          The following is a list of some of them:                                  
                                                                                    
               cikátip   ‘between’                                                  
                                                                                    
               hiki ino  ‘on the west side of’                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         173                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               huy       ‘under’                                                    
                                                                                    
               inahi     ‘beyond’                                                   
                                                                                    
               iti       ‘in front of’                                              
                                                                                    
               maynoy    ‘on the other side of’                                     
                                                                                    
               o         ‘at the edge of; near’                                     
                                                                                    
               oc        ‘right on’                                                 
                                                                                    
               oci       ‘over’                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ó·l       ‘near’                                                     
                                                                                    
               tólpot inó ‘on the east side of’                                     
                                                                                    
          Note that cikátip ‘between’ is placed after the second noun.              
                                                                                    
               [wi aˊñ wañnē taˊ-i tsikaˊdep tāt(o)]                                
                                                                                    
          387) wi áŋ wáŋne táy cikátip tá·to

               my   house     road      river between  stand-3SS-PHR.TERM           
                                                                                    
               ‘My house stands between the road and the river.’ (1932:28:2)

          Numerals. There are three kinds of numerals in Atakapa, cardinal, adverbial, and distributive.11
                                                                                    
          Numerals are nouns, and can take case suffixes; the inessive case suffix -ip derives the adverbial
                                                                                    
          numerals from the cardinal numerals. In addition to the regular numerals, the quantifiers héw
                                                                                    
          ‘many’ and hatnáh ‘so many’ pattern as numerals.                          
                                                                                    
          Cardinal                      Adverbial                                   
                                                                                    
          héw            many           héwip               many times              
                                                                                    
          hatnáh         so many        hatnáhip            how many times?         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          tanúk          one            tanúkip             once                    
                                                                                    
          cí·k           two            cí·kip              twice                   
                                                                                    
          lá·t           three          lá·tip              thrice                  
                                                                                    
          himatól        four           himatólip           four times              
                                                                                    
          ní·t           five           ní·tip              five times              
                                                                                    
          laccí·k        six            laccí·kip           six times               
                                                                                    
          páhw           seven          páhwip              seven times             
                                                                                    
          himatólcí·k    eight          himatólcí·kip       eight times             
                                                                                    
          wóšišólhá·n    nine           wóšišólhá·nip       nine times              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wošpé          ten            wošpéip             ten times               
                                                                                    
          wospé hál tanúk eleven        wospéip hál tanúkip eleven times            
                                                                                    
          wospé hál cí·k twelve         wospéip hál cí·kip  twelve times            
                                                                                    
          wošpécí·k      twenty         wošpécí·kip         twenty times            
                                                                                    
          wošpéní·t      fifty          wošpéní·tip         fifty times             
                                                                                    
          hiyén pón      hundred        hiyén pónip         a hundred times         
                                                                                    
          hiyén pón cakóp thousand      hiyén pón cakópip   a thousand times        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         176                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [yiˊl lāt himatoˊl-u tatiˊhin-tāt]                                   
                                                                                    
          393) yil la·t himatólot tà·ttihin̥tat                                     
                                                                                    
               yil  la·t himatol-ot ta·t-tihi-n̥t-at                                
                                                                                    
               day  three four-DIR stand(sg)-CONTIN-PRET                            
                                                                                    
               ‘He kept on living for three to four days.’ (1932:10:18-19)          
                                                                                    
               [Icyutyuˊts ya tanuˊkip co haˊ icpaˊts haˊhat]                       
                                                                                    
          394) išyucyucya tanúkip šoháh išpácháhat                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               iš-,yuc,yuc=ya      tanúk-ip  šo-háh    iš-pác-ø-háh-at              
                                                                                    
               1PLO-,REDUP,raise=and one-INESS ever-NEG 1PLO-whip-3SS-NEG-PRET      
                                                                                    
               ‘He raised us and never whipped us even once.’ (1932:16:102-03)      
                                                                                    
               [tsoˊ-ots heˊwip hilc]                                               
                                                                                    
          395) cohòc héwip hílš                                                     
                                                                                    
               cohoc hew-ip   hilš-ø                                                
                                                                                    
               corn many-INESS sift-3SS                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘He sifts the corn many times.’ (1932:39:2)                          
                                                                                    
          Only the numbers one through five and seven are monomorphemic. Six, laccí·k is from la·t cí·k
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ‘two threes,’ eight, himatólcí·k is from himatól cí·k ‘two fours,’ and nine, wóšišólhá·n is from
                                                                                    
          wóšišó·l há·n ‘the little finger is lacking.’                             
                                                                                    
            The distributive numerals are derived with the suffix -šim, which makes them into verbs.
                                                                                    
          The following list is exhaustive; there are no examples of their use in sentences.
                                                                                    
          Distributive                                                              
                                                                                    
          tanúkšim       one apiece                                                 
                                                                                    
          cí·kšim        two apiece                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         177                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          lá·tsim        three apiece                                               
                                                                                    
          himatólšim     four apiece                                                
                                                                                    
          ní·tsim        five apiece                                                
                                                                                    
          laccí·ksim     six apiece                                                 
                                                                                    
          páhwsim        seven apiece                                               
                                                                                    
          wošpéšim       ten apiece                                                 
                                                                                    
          Kišyuc and Tottokš explicitly stated that Atakapa lacks ordinal numerals (first, second, etc.).12
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The closest Atakapa comes to placing nouns in order is using the words í·t ‘face; first’ kimátip
                                                                                    
          ‘in the middle,’ and hál ‘second; back; last.’                            
                                                                                    
               [woˊc īt]                                                            
                                                                                    
          396) wóš í·t                                                              
                                                                                    
               woš       i·t                                                        
                                                                                    
               hand/finger face                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘first finger (index finger)’ (1932:151:1)                           
                                                                                    
               [wi hipaˊ haˊl]                                                      
                                                                                    
          397) wi hipá hál                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wi   hipa      hal                                                   
                                                                                    
               my   husband   back                                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘my second husband; my last husband.’ (1932:35:2)                    
                                                                                    
          There is one additional numeral, šukí·ŋ ‘one-half.’ No adverbial or distributive form is attested.
                                                                                    
               [kuiyatkoˊl lāt haˊl cukīˊñ]                                         
                                                                                    
          398) kuyyatkól lá·t hál šukí·ŋ                                            
                                                                                    
               kuyyatkol la·t hal  šuki·ŋ                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         178                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               sausage   three more half                                            
                                                                                    
               ‘three-and-a-half sausages.’ (1932:116:2)                            
                                                                                    
               {hatnáχkan mḗtsá? hikát nī́t yahaˊl cokī́ng, wí(sh)}                
                                                                                    
          399) hatnáhka n̥méc á? hiká·t ní·tya hál šukí·ŋ, wí·š                     
                                                                                    
               hatnah-ka n̥-mec    a    hika·t ni·t=ya hal  šuki·ŋ    wi-iš         
                                                                                    
               so.much-ADV 2STATS-be.tall Q foot be.five=and more be.half I-FOC     
                                                                                    
               ‘How tall are you? As for me, I am five and a half feet.’ (Gatschet 1885b:83)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Noun formation. There are two primary methods of noun formation, derivation and
                                                                                    
          compounding. Nouns are derived from verbs by the suffixes -ne and -nán. Words formed by
                                                                                    
          means of the gerund suffix -ne retain the accent of the verbal root; those formed with -nán
                                                                                    
          usually shift the accent to the suffix. Originally, it seems that nouns derived with -ne focused on
                                                                                    
          the result of the action, for example í·t hathúne ‘mirror,’ from í·t hat-hú ‘to see one’s face.’ The
                                                                                    
          suffix -nán, on the other hand focused on a noun that does the action, for example kocnán
                                                                                    
          ‘knife,’ from kóc ‘to cut,’ literally ‘cutter,’ and formed agentive nouns. However, the suffix -nán
                                                                                    
          is now restricted to a very few words, and often have an alternative form in -ne, and there are
                                                                                    
          quite a few agentive nouns formed with the suffix -ne. Many derived nouns are nominalizations
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          of verb phrases, not simply root verbs.                                   
                                                                                    
          Nominalization of verb root:                                              
                                                                                    
          himó·šne       ‘grave’        himó·š         ‘he/she buries him’          
                                                                                    
          wáŋne          ‘road’         wáŋ            ‘he/she goes about’          
                                                                                    
          káwšne         ‘comb’         káwš           ‘he/she combs it’            
                                                                                    
          kimíŋne        ‘windpipe’     kimíŋ          ‘he/she breathes’            
                                                                                    
          neˑšilšnán     ‘broom’        neˑšílš        ‘he/she sweeps’              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         179                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          nakšnán        ‘violin’       nákš           ‘he/she plays music’         
                                                                                    
          Nominalization of a verb phrase                                           
                                                                                    
          iša·kšakkóŋne  ‘sheriff’      išá·k šakkóŋ   ‘he seizes people’           
                                                                                    
          katnáw hatlášne ‘razor’       katnáw hatláš  ‘he shaves his beard’        
                                                                                    
          kapí líline    ‘coffee grinder’ kapí líli    ‘he/she grinds coffee’       
                                                                                    
          ne·šilšnán     ‘broom’        né· šílš       ‘he/she sweeps the ground’   
                                                                                    
          šokšilnán      ‘sewing machine’ šokšíl       ‘he/she sews’                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          A nominalized verb can in turn form part of a further nominalization      
                                                                                    
          kucnán kininán ‘whetstone’    kucnán kíni    ‘he/she sharpens knives      
                                                                                    
          Agentive nouns are productively formed with the suffix -ne, as above. However, a more archaic
                                                                                    
          way of forming agentive nouns was to convert the plain verb root into a noun.
                                                                                    
          wakpúh         ‘to jump about; hail’ wakpùh  ‘grasshopper’                
                                                                                    
          šokyáh         ‘to eat’       šokyáh         ‘eater’                      
                                                                                    
          šaktál kóh     ‘he wants skins’ šaktalkóh    ‘the Skin-Desirer’           
                                                                                    
          Compounding is the other major way to form new nouns in Atakapa. The components can be
                                                                                    
          two (or more) nouns, or a noun and an adjective, or nouns and verb roots. 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          tík  ‘arrow’   išá·t     ‘head’    tikišá·t  ‘arrowhead’                  
                                                                                    
          tút  ‘cypress’ áy        ‘swamp’   tutáy     ‘cypress swamp’              
                                                                                    
          pé·m ‘gun’     mók       ‘short’   pe·mmók   ‘pistol’                     
                                                                                    
          míŋ  ‘bee’     pá        ‘nest’    míŋpá     ‘beehive’                    
                                                                                    
          néš ‘tree’ kíŋš ‘to saw’ támš ‘to split’ áŋ ‘house’ neškiŋštámšáŋ ‘sawmill’
                                                                                    
          A noun in the directional case can be the first member of a compound noun.
                                                                                    
          óc-ot ‘abovewards’ tá·t  ‘to stand’ ocottá·t ‘God’                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         180                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ók-ot ‘into cloth’ káwš  ‘to put in’ okotkáwš ‘shirt’                     
                                                                                    
          Occasionally the compound word has a meaning unrelated to the individual elements which
                                                                                    
          compose it.                                                               
                                                                                    
          šiwát ‘alligator’ te· ‘leaves’ néš ‘tree’ šiwatte· néš ‘prickly ash’      
                                                                                    
          hiláŋ ‘vulture’ teˑ ‘leaves’ néš ‘tree’ hiláŋteˑ néš ‘magnolia’           
                                                                                    
          hiláŋ ‘vulture’ wo·l ‘eye’              hilaŋwo·l ‘muscadine’             
                                                                                    
          hiláŋ ‘vulture’ išo·l ‘young’ téy ‘vine’ hilaŋišó·l téy ‘summer grapes’   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          There are a handful of words borrowed from other languages, mostly from French:
                                                                                    
          kapí      ‘coffee’       French    café                                   
                                                                                    
          skalé     ‘12½ cents’    French    escalin cf. Koasati skalí              
                                                                                    
          líla      ‘chinaberry’   French    lilas (des Antilles)                   
                                                                                    
          kúmpo     ‘okra’         French    gumbo < Mbundu ngombo                  
                                                                                    
          pákan     ‘pecan’        French    pacane                                 
                                                                                    
          ši·l      ‘sail of a ship’ English sail                                   
                                                                                    
          ánanuy    ‘bullfrog’     Natchez   ʔananaY c.f. Koasati hanonó            
                                                                                    
          kamcíš    ‘dragonfly’    Chitimacha kumčˀit                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          cáyon     ‘Mexico’       Caddo     hispayun < Spanish Español             
                                                                                    
          áleman    ‘German’       French    Allemand                               
                                                                                    
          inkilíš   ‘American’     English   English                                
                                                                                    
          cahtá     ‘Choctaw’      Choctaw   čahta                                  
                                                                                    
          alpamú    ‘Alabama’      Alabama   alba:mo                                
                                                                                    
          nékitoš   ‘Natchitoches’ Caddo     na šitˀuš cf. Eng /ˈnækədıš/           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         181                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          There are certainly borrowings from other American Indian languages, but as the neighboring
                                                                                    
          languages are so poorly known it is difficult to determine what they might be. There is clear
                                                                                    
          Chitimacha influence on Hiyékiti; for example, Hiyékiti heysiŋ ‘ten’ is borrowed from
                                                                                    
          Chitimacha heyčˀi ‘ten,’ c.f. Yukhíti wošpé ‘ten.’ There are at least two loan translations from
                                                                                    
          another Indian language, kakók tím ‘garden,’ lit. ‘picket fence,’ Koasati holihtá caká:wa
                                                                                    
          ‘garden,’ lit. ‘picket fence,’ and wólhopšiškám ‘sifting basket with wide openings,’ wól-hopš-
                                                                                    
          iškám ‘eye-hole-big’ Koasati tiłcobá ‘sifting basket with wide holes,’ (it)tił(í)-cobá ‘eye-big.’13
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Notes                                                                     
                                                                                    
          1. This example, from Kišyuc, so struck Gatschet that he made the comment “kushmélik is here
                                                                                    
          subject” (1885b:66). He, thinking that word order was primary, elicited the phase kušmé·l nimá
                                                                                    
          from Tottokš, seemingly to see if the noun would be the subject, and her translation was ‘he
                                                                                    
          killed the black person.’ This is as close as Gatschet came to realizing the existence of the
                                                                                    
          ergative case.                                                            
                                                                                    
          2. This example almost looks like the transitive sentence, ‘The steam burned me,’ except that
                                                                                    
          the intransitive verb láw ‘to burn’ is used, not the transitive verb láwi ‘to burn something.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          3. This is the Atakapa pronunciation of the English ‘Choctaw.’ Elsewhere, the Atakapa
                                                                                    
          pronunciation of the name is given as cahtá.                              
                                                                                    
          4. A further example may be šak-tal ‘skins’ in the name of the monster šaktalkoh ‘the Skin-
                                                                                    
          Desirer.’                                                                 
                                                                                    
          5. The element kuš- is synchronically unanalyzable. It may be related to the word úš, ‘body.’
                                                                                    
          6.           7. This word is probably a calque on English. It is never used in the texts or example sentences,
                                                                                    
          where yukhíti is universally used in the meaning ‘Indian,’ and is glossed by the Louisiana French

          8. This word was said to be archaic, and reflects the idea that the pale skin color of Europeans
                                                                                    
          looked like the skin color of corpses; a comment by Kišyuc (1885b:91) indicates that the
                                                                                    
          Atakapa first thought that white people were dead people who had come back to life.

          Verdine:                                                                  
                                                                                    
               {kushmếl’n shakó ṓkêt}

               kušmé·l=n̥     šakkáw    ók-kit

          9. It may be of interest to note that the word for woman in the neighboring language Chitimacha,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          kiča, has an irregular plural kič.                                        
                                                                                    
          10. Ergative forms of the pronouns were frequently not written by Gatschet, as he seems to have
                                                                                    
          assumed that pronouns were indeclinable. At times, when he recorded an ergative case form, he
                                                                                    
          went back and edited it out, as in this example (1885b:71) {wi kshok mísho} wí·k šakmíšo, ‘I
                                                                                    
          give it to them,’ where he went back and scratched out the k with red ink. Gatschet also edited
                                                                                    
          out other ergative forms, for example (1885b:69) he wrote {shíwonikna tsúmĕt na kámshĕt}
                                                                                    
          šiwònik nakcúmat nakkámšat ‘The cat pinched you all and scratched you all,’ and he went back
                                                                                    
          and edited it until he had {shíwon nakshakkámšat} šiwòn nakšakkámšat, and glossed it ‘The cat
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         183                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          scratched ye,’ even though if read as a grammatical sentence it means, ‘You all scratched the
                                                                                    
          cats.’                                                                    
                                                                                    
          11. Gatschet (and Swanton in his turn) mistakenly thought that there was a set of multiplicative
                                                                                    
          numerals in -ip hipónso, eg. ‘seven-fold’ {pa’hwip hipónso} (1885a:101) páhwip hipónso.
                                                                                    
          However this is simply the literal translation of ‘I fold it seven times’ hipóns-o simply meaning
                                                                                    
          ‘I fold it.’                                                              
                                                                                    
          12. On page 36 of his field notes (1885b), the red ink indicating Tottokš as the source, Gatschet
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wrote: “hál tsík [‘second’] hal lāt [‘third’] etc. could be used; not real words.”
                                                                                    
          13. Gatschet’s notes say that this kind of basket came from Hickory Flat, which was the name of
                                                                                    
          the area between the Calcasieu River and Bayou Blue, which flows into the Mermentau River;
                                                                                    
          the 1880 US Census locates the Koasati Indians at Hickory Flat.
