                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         184                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          4. SYNTAX                                                                 
                                                                                    
          Ergativity. Ergative agreement in Atakapa is complex, and can be divided into two categories:
                                                                                    
          verbal agreement and nominal agreement. In the verb in the aorist mode, Atakapa has a split-s
                                                                                    
          system (Dixon, 1994:70-110). There is a set of Agent (A) pronominal suffixes (-o / -cel / -tem /
                                                                                    
          -ul which inflect the majority of verbs, be they transitive or intransitive, and there is a set of
                                                                                    
          Object (O) pronominal prefixes (hi-, n̥-, iš-, nak-, šak-) which usually indicate the direct or
                                                                                    
          indirect object of a verb. The O prefixes, however, in the aorist mark the subjects of certain
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          intransitive verbs the action of which is not under the control of the subject, such as makáw ‘fall’
                                                                                    
          and hiwálš ‘dream.’ The O prefixes also mark the subject of a handful of transitive verbs, the
                                                                                    
          most notable of which are ké ‘have something,’ kóh, ‘want something,’ and úhc ‘know
                                                                                    
          something,’ and their negative counterparts háhš, óŋš, and awš. The language also allows fluid-
                                                                                    
          S marking (see Dixon, 1994:78-83, for the concept).                       
                                                                                    
            The first split comes in the preterite and dubitative modes where first- and second-person
                                                                                    
          subjects of all verbs are marked with the O prefixes, while the third person subjects are inflected
                                                                                    
          with the A suffixes (this is only overt in the third-person plural, as the third-person singular is
                                                                                    
          unmarked). The second split occurs in the progressive, future, and dependent modes, in which
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          all verbs in all persons are inflected for subject with the O prefixes. This system creates
                                                                                    
          ambiguities which the speakers seemed willing to tolerate, for example himišat is segmented hi-
                                                                                    
          ø-miš-at and the morphemes can be understood as 1SSTATS-3SOBJ-give-PRET ‘I gave it to him,’
                                                                                    
          or hi-miš-ø-at 1SO-give-3SS-PRET ‘He gave it to me.’ These can be disambiguated with either
                                                                                    
          the subject pronoun being inflected for the ergative case, as in examples 400 or the object
                                                                                    
          pronoun being inflected with the directional case, as in example 401. However, the speakers
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         185                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          often produced the forms without disambiguating pronouns, some of which Gatschet often added
                                                                                    
          when reviewed the notes with them.                                        
                                                                                    
               [haˊk himiˊcat]                                                      
                                                                                    
          400) há·k himíšat                                                         
                                                                                    
               ha-ik          hi-miš-ø-at                                           
                                                                                    
               s/he-ERG.AGNT  1SO-give-3SS-PRET                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘He gave it to me.’ (1932:31:2)                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [weˊhet himiˊcat]                                                    
                                                                                    
          401) wéhet himíšat                                                        
                                                                                    
               wehet     hi-miš-ø-at                                                
                                                                                    
               I(DIR)    1SO-give-3SS-PRET                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘He gave it to me.’ (1932:80:2)                                      
                                                                                    
            The ergative case is highly restricted in Atakapa. Only the subjects of verbs with the
                                                                                    
          semantic roles of AFFECT and GIVING (Dixon, 1994:7) can be inflected with the ergative case. In
                                                                                    
          addition, noun phrases are marked with the ergative case only if two conditions are present: first,
                                                                                    
          the noun marked with the ergative case must be the agent of the action of a transitive verb, not
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          simply the subject of that verb, and second, the object of the action of the transitive verb must be
                                                                                    
          human and animate.1 Body parts, which are animate nouns in Atakapa, lack the feature of being
                                                                                    
          human, so subject of verbs referring to actions on human body parts are not marked with the
                                                                                    
          ergative case.                                                            
                                                                                    
          Fluid-S marking. Because of the broadness of the translations of the example sentences in the
                                                                                    
          dictionary proving the existence of fluid-s marking sometimes has to rely on morphological
                                                                                    
          evidence. Some Atakapa verbs can be inflected in the aorist either with active or stative subject
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         186                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          marking. Because of the semantics of the verbs involved, stative subject marking seems to
                                                                                    
          indicate accidental or non-volitional action on the part of the subject, while active subject
                                                                                    
          marking seems to indicate the volitional action of the subject.           
                                                                                    
               {tolhópshik hiatká-ushat}                                            
                                                                                    
          402a) tolhópšik hihatkáwšat                                               
                                                                                    
               tolhopš-ik     hi-hat-kawš-at                                        
                                                                                    
               needle-ERG.INSTR 1SSTATS-REFLEX-prick-PRET                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I accidentally pricked myself with a needle.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:63)2 
                                                                                    
               [toloˊpcik hatkaˊuco]                                                
                                                                                    
          402b) tolhópšik hatkáwšo                                                  
                                                                                    
               tolhopš-ik     hat-kawš-o                                            
                                                                                    
               needle-ERG.INSTR REFLEX-prick-1SS                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I pricked myself with a needle on purpose.’ (1932:61:2)             
                                                                                    
               [hiˊtix̥ t]                                                          
                                                                                    
          403a) hitìht                                                              
                                                                                    
               hi-tiht                                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-lie                                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘I am lying down.’ (1932:132:2)                                      
                                                                                    
               [wi koˊmep tiˊhito]                                                  
                                                                                    
          403b) wi kómip tíhto                                                      
                                                                                    
               wi   kom-ip    tiht-o                                                
                                                                                    
               my   belly-INESS lie-1SS                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘I lie on my belly.’ (1932:132:2)                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         187                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [haihaiˊco]                                                          
                                                                                    
          404a) hayháyšo                                                            
                                                                                    
               hayhayš-o                                                            
                                                                                    
               weep-1SS                                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘I wept’                                                             
                                                                                    
               [hihaihaiˊc]                                                         
                                                                                    
          404b) hihayháyš                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hi-hayhayš                                                           
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-weep                                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘I burst into tears.’                                                
                                                                                    
          In the following example, the verb óŋš, which is usually inflected in the aorist as a stative verb,
                                                                                    
          as in 405a, is inflected in 405b as an active verb, indicating that in the opinion of the speaker the
                                                                                    
          action of not liking is more under the control of the subject, and in fact is making a moral
                                                                                    
          judgement about the subject. The nominal subject is not in the ergative case because the verb
                                                                                    
          does not fit the narrow semantic requirements for ergative marking.       
                                                                                    
               [hakīt cakoñc]                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          405a) hakít šakóŋš                                                        
                                                                                    
               hakit šak-oŋš                                                        
                                                                                    
               they 3PLSTATS-not.want                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘They do not want it / They do not like it.’ (1932:98:1)             
                                                                                    
               {kiwílsh yukíti shakónshûl}                                          
                                                                                    
          405b) kiwilš yukhíti šakoŋšul                                             
                                                                                    
               kiwilš         yukhiti   šak-oŋš-ul                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         188                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               white.people   Indian    3PLO-not.like-3PLS                          
                                                                                    
               ‘White people do not like the Indians.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:80)         
                                                                                    
          Word order. The basic word order of an Atakapa sentence is Subject Object Verb, whether or
                                                                                    
          not the subject is in the ergative case or is absolutive.                 
                                                                                    
               [caˊ-ik cukteˊ-i hatse-eˊc miˊculat]                                 
                                                                                    
          406) šáik šoktéy hacehéš míšulat                                          
                                                                                    
               ša-ik               šoktey    haceheš   ø-miš-ul-at                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               someone-ERG.AGNT    medicine  bad(sg)   3OBJ-give-3PLS-PRET          
                                                                                    
               ‘Some people gave them bad medicine.’ (1932:17:112-13)               
                                                                                    
               [wīc wi itēˊt iyaniaˊn haˊxcat]                                      
                                                                                    
          407) wí·š wi ité·t hiyanyánháhšat                                         
                                                                                    
               wi-iš wi  ite·t hi-yanyan-hahš-at                                    
                                                                                    
               I-FOC my  father 1SSTATS-fear-NEG-PRET                               
                                                                                    
               ‘I did not fear my father.’ (1932:16:103)                            
                                                                                    
               {tú hōpké kaukáu tá-u iníχkit}                                       
                                                                                    
          408) tú hóp ké kawkáw tawiníhkit                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tu   hop  ø-ke      kawkaw    taw-ø-inih-kit                         
                                                                                    
               boat hole 3SSTATS-have water  STAND-3STATS-come.in-PROG(SG)          
                                                                                    
               ‘The boat has a hole; water is coming in.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:165)     
                                                                                    
               [caˊkiol katnaˊu cak ́āˊx̥ c]                                        
                                                                                    
          409) šakyòl katnáw šakháhš                                                
                                                                                    
               šakyol    katnaw    šak-hahš                                         
                                                                                    
               man       beard     3PLSTATS-not.have                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         189                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Men do not have beards.’ (1932:10:24)                               
                                                                                    
          Moving the object to the front of the sentence gives it extra prominence. 
                                                                                    
               {Ló hilá-i yok’hití winé-ulat kā́shki}                               
                                                                                    
          410) ló hiláy yukhìti winéulat káškin.                                    
                                                                                    
               lo   hilay yukhiti  wine-ul-at     kaš-kin                           
                                                                                    
               PN   wife Indian    find-3PLS-PRET be.high.tide-WHEN                 
                                                                                    
               ‘The Indians found Lo’s wife at high tide.’ (Gatschet 1885b:98)      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [kakauˊ itaˊns utskaˊucat]                                           
                                                                                    
          411) kakáw itáns uckáwšat                                                 
                                                                                    
               kakaw itans    uckawš-ø-at                                           
                                                                                    
               sun  clouds    cover-3SS-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘Clouds covered the sun.’ (1932:53:2)                                
                                                                                    
          When adjectives are not used as verbs, they follow the nouns they modify. Quantifiers, such as
                                                                                    
          héw in example 412, also follow the nouns they modify.                    
                                                                                    
               [Cok heˊ-u cakwaˊnts uˊxtsăt]                                        
                                                                                    
          412) šok héw šakwánc úhcat                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               šok  hew  šak-wanc  ø-uhc-at                                         
                                                                                    
               Thing many 3PLO-tell 3STATS-be.able-PRET                             
                                                                                    
               ‘She was able to tell them many things.’ (1932:15:84)                
                                                                                    
               [Icāk cuk’hēˊc kaˊ-ukin vīˊwulat cok hatse-eˊc hiweˊ-u kaˊ-at]       
                                                                                    
          413) iša·k šoké·š káwkin wíwulat šok hacehéš hiwéw káat                   
                                                                                    
               iša·k šoke·š ø-kaw-kin   wiw-u-lat      šok  haceheš hiwew           
                                                                                    
               person sick 3STATS-die-WHEN believe-3PLS-PRET thing bad very         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         190                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ka-at                                                                
                                                                                    
               do-PRET                                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘After someone sick died, they believed that something very bad had done it.’
                                                                                    
               (1932:18:118-119)                                                    
                                                                                    
          414) [nē-cilcnăˊn iˊl toˊlka ciˊlcntat]                                   
                                                                                    
               ne·šilšnán íl tólka šílšn̥tat                                        
                                                                                    
               ne·šilšnan il  tolka šilš-n̥t-at                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               broom     new  well sweep-CONT-PRET                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘A new broom always sweeps well.’ (resultative preterite) (1932:112:1)
                                                                                    
          Temporal phrases occur either at the beginning of a sentence, or after the final verb of the
                                                                                    
          sentence.                                                                 
                                                                                    
               [Itiˊ tanuˊk okinuˊlulat haˊ aˊñkin]                                 
                                                                                    
          415) ití tanúk hokinúlulat ha áŋkin

               [cukaˊulat itiˊ maˊñ mōn]

               šoka·k-ul-at        ití  maŋ  mo·n                                   
                                                                                    
               ceremonial.dance-3PLS-PRET night long all                            
                                                                                    
               ‘They did ceremonial dancing all night long.’ (1932:17-18:114-15)    
                                                                                    
          Adverbs, both simple and derived, are usually placed directly before the verbs that they modify.
                                                                                    
               [hāc noˊmc tanuˊk ipuˊts ke]                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         191                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          417) há·š nómš tanúk ipúc ke                                              
                                                                                    
               ha-iš     nomš tanuk ipuc ø-ke                                       
                                                                                    
               s/he-FOC  child one only 3STATS-have                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘He has only one child.’ (1932:49:2)                                 
                                                                                    
               {néšh akilî́kš tólka láu háχsh}                                      
                                                                                    
          418) néš akilíkš tólka láwháhš                                            
                                                                                    
               neš  akilikš   tolka law-ø-hahš                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wood wet       well burn-3SS-NEG                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘Wet wood does not burn well.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:83)                  
                                                                                    
          Locative phrases in the locative, inessive, and elative cases usually occur before the main verb of
                                                                                    
          the sentence. Nominal objects occur before the locative phrase, but if there is an emphasis on the
                                                                                    
          locative, it may precede the object, as in examples 421 and 422.          
                                                                                    
          Locative:                                                                 
                                                                                    
               [neˊc palpaˊl aˊnkin nûˊn-kētntat]

          Elative:                                                                  
                                                                                    
               [kaukaˊ-u aˊm’n aˊnike ntaˊhĕntat]                                   
                                                                                    
          425) kawkáw ámn̥ ánike n̥táhn̥ tat

               ya   iša·k     akipay-ike ok-ø                                       
                                                                                    
               that person    south-EL  come-3SS                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘That man comes from the south.’ (1932:24:1)                         
                                                                                    
          In natural texts, nouns in the directional case follow the main verb in the sentence, as in
                                                                                    
          examples 427 and 428. In elicited examples, they can occur before; some of these examples
                                                                                    
          occur with the verb míš ‘to give’ and seem to disambiguate pronoun objects. Also, if the noun in
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          the directional case can be understood in a locative sense, then the form occurs before the verb,
                                                                                    
          as is usual with other locative expressions.                              
                                                                                    
               [Tawatweˊnăt Utsutaˊt ut. Cukaˊkulĕt Utsutaˊt ut.]

               ocotta·t-ot

               PN-DIR                                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘They stood and prayed to She-who-stands-above; they did a ceremonial dance to She-
                                                                                    
               who-stands-above. (1932:9-10:14-15)                                  
                                                                                    
               [Yuk’hiˊt itēˊt peˊl (Cyprien) icaˊk toˊkat yukiˊt noˊmc oˊt]        
                                                                                    
          428) yukít ité·tpél <Cyprien> iša·k tólat yukít nómšot

               ha-ot     ø-miš-ø-at                                                 
                                                                                    
               he-DIR    3SO-give-3SS-PRET                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘He gave it to him’ (1932:32:1)                                      
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊti mōˊne oˊk-inuˊlulat imōˊc oˊt tiˊune]                       
                                                                                    
          430) yukhíti mó·ni hokinúlulat himó·šot tíwne                             
                                                                                    
               yukhiti   mo·ni     hok-inul-ul-at      himo·š-ot tiw-ne             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Indian    all(human) RECIP-gather-3PLS-PRET burial-DIR go(pl)-GER    
                                                                                    
               ‘All the Indians gathered together to go to the burial.’ (1932:18:125-26)
                                                                                    
          Locative compounds. In addition to a verb accompanied by a noun in the locative case, a noun
                                                                                    
          of location can be compounded with a verb to indicate the action taking place in the location of
                                                                                    
          the noun. The four examples noted are nuŋké·t (sg) / nuŋnúl (pl) ‘to live in a village,’ (núŋ
                                                                                    
          ‘village;’ ké·t / núl ‘sit; dwell’), tukémš ‘to row in a boat.’ (tú ‘boat;’ kémš ‘to row’), and tuké·t
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         195                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ‘to sit in a boat.’ Compare examples 431 and 432, which illustrate the same notion expressed
                                                                                    
          first by a locative phrase, and second by a locative compound.            
                                                                                    
               [yukiˊti nuˊnkin kēˊt’ntat Yuk’hiˊti Tuˊl toˊlpot inoˊ]              
                                                                                    
          431) yukhíti núŋkin ké·tn̥tat yukhíti túl tólpot inó                      
                                                                                    
               yukhiti nuŋ-kin ke·t-n̥t-at   yukhiti   tul  tolpot ino              
                                                                                    
               Indian village-LOC dwell-CONTIN-PRET Indian lake east side           
                                                                                    
               ‘She used to live in an Indian village on the east side of Lake Prien (Indian Lake).’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               (1932:14:71)                                                         
                                                                                    
               [neˊc palpaˊl aˊnkin nûˊn-kētntat]                                   
                                                                                    
          432) néšpalpaláŋkin nuŋkè·tn̥tat                                          
                                                                                    
               neš-pal,pal-aŋ-kin       nuŋ-ke·t-n̥t-at                             
                                                                                    
               wood-,REDUP,flat-house-LOC village-dwell-CONTIN-PRET                 
                                                                                    
               ‘She lived in the village in a split-plank house.’ (1932:14:74)      
                                                                                    
               [Yuk’hiˊti icāk wāci a nēp nūn nultiˊhinst]                          
                                                                                    
          433) yukhíti iša·k wáši a né·p nuŋnúltíhin̥st                             
                                                                                    
               yukhiti   iša·k     waši a    ne·-ip                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Indian    people    old  this land-INESS                             
                                                                                    
               nuŋ-nul-tihi-n̥st                                                    
                                                                                    
               village-live- PST.PROG-CUSTOM                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘The old Indian people customarily used to live in villages in this land.’ (1932:9:1)
                                                                                    
               [ntaˊn tuˊ-kēˊmc icwāˊñĕhĕ]                                          
                                                                                    
          434) n̥tán tukémšišwáŋehe                                                 
                                                                                    
               n̥tan     tu-kemš-iš-waŋ-ehe

               be.or     boat-row-1PLSTATS-go.about-DUB                             
                                                                                    
               ‘…or we might go about rowing in a boat.’ (1932:13:46)               
                                                                                    
               [icāk tuˊkēt]                                                        
                                                                                    
          435) išá·k tuké·t                                                         
                                                                                    
               iša·k tu-ke·t-ø                                                      
                                                                                    
               man  boat-sit(sg)-3SS (1932:61:2)                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘A man is sitting in a boat.’                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Focus. Atakapa has a suffix -iš / -š, used with nouns, noun phrases, and pronouns, that marks
                                                                                    
          the change of the prominent topic of a discourse. It is extremely common in the example
                                                                                    
          sentences, probably because in elicitation, shifting between sentences was perceived by
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s consultants as a change in focus.                              
                                                                                    
               {há án nēp}                                                          
                                                                                    
          436) ha áŋ né·p                                                           
                                                                                    
               ha     aŋ      ø-ne·p                                                
                                                                                    
               his/her house  3STATS-be.low                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘His/her house is low.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:6)                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               {yukít ángsh nēp}                                                    
                                                                                    
          437) yukít áŋš né·p                                                       
                                                                                    
               yukit  aŋ-š         ø-ne·p                                           
                                                                                    
               our    house-FOC    3STATS-be.low                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Our house is low.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:6)                              
                                                                                    
               {ití hi-iwáleshat}                                                   
                                                                                    
          438) ití hihiwálšat                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         197                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ití  hi-hiwalš-at                                                    
                                                                                    
               night 1SSTATS-dream-PRET                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘I dreamed last night.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:10)                         
                                                                                    
               {a-ití-ish hiwáleshta}                                               
                                                                                    
          439) á itíiš hiwálšta                                                     
                                                                                    
               a    ití-iš    hiwalš-ta                                             
                                                                                    
               this night-FOC dream-PCPL                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I am to dream tonight.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:10)3                       
                                                                                    
          In the following natural text, Tottokš has been focusing on her grandfather and his household.
                                                                                    
          When she changes the topic to her mother, she uses the focus form of the pronoun há ‘he/she.’
                                                                                    
          wi kulša káwkinya há·š yilké                                              
                                                                                    
           túl maynóy cípšatya išicyúcišat                                          
                                                                                    
          When my grandfather died, then she got married,                           
                                                                                    
           moved to the other side of the lake, and raised us.                      
                                                                                    
          A little later in the same text, Tottokš is talking about her stepfather Cyprien. Then, switching
                                                                                    
          the topic to her own reactions to him, she uses the focus form of the pronoun wí, ‘I,’ then,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          switching the topic back to her stepfather, she uses the focus form of há again.
                                                                                    
          išyucyucya tanúkip šoháh išpácháhat                                       
                                                                                    
           wí·š wi ité·t hiyanyánháhšat                                             
                                                                                    
            paytólušat wíot                                                         
                                                                                    
          hiyúckin há·š okwáŋšot tíšatya káwat                                      
                                                                                    
          He raised us and never beat us even once.                                 
                                                                                    
           I was not afraid of my father.                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         198                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            He was truly generous to me.                                            
                                                                                    
          When I was grown, he went to war and died.                                
                                                                                    
          Another example can be found in Text 5, where Tottokš is talking about her cousin Kišmok/
                                                                                    
          Ponponne. She is talking about her cousin, then when she switches and talks about her own
                                                                                    
          family, she uses the focus form of the pronoun yukít ‘we,’ then when she switches the topic back
                                                                                    
          to her cousin, she uses the focus form of há, ‘he/she.’                   
                                                                                    
          iyáŋ ké·tntat yukíts túl maynóy išicyúckin                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          há·š hipá háhšat hiúhc atkákiya                                           
                                                                                    
           néšpalpaláŋkin núŋke·tntat                                               
                                                                                    
          There she lived while we grew up on the other side of the lake            
                                                                                    
           She did not have a husband from the time that I knew her,                
                                                                                    
            and she lived in the village in a split-plank house.                    
                                                                                    
          Focus forms are used to disambiguate subjects and objects of transitive stative verbs; the focus
                                                                                    
          form of a noun or pronoun indicates the subject. The ergative case is not used because the
                                                                                    
          subject is not considered to be in complete control of the action of the verb.
                                                                                    
               {hilḗmsh}                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          440) hilé·mš                                                              
                                                                                    
               hi-ø-le·mš          hi-ø-le·mš                                       
                                                                                    
               1SO-3STATS-love     1SSTATS-3SO-love                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘He loves me.’      ‘I love him.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:58)               
                                                                                    
               {wish há ilēmsh}                                                     
                                                                                    
          441) wí·š há hilé·mš                                                      
                                                                                    
               wi-iš ha  hi-ø-le·mš                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         199                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               I-FOC s/he 1SSTATS-3SO-love                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘I love him.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:58)                                   
                                                                                    
          Clausal complements. Verbs such as wán ‘to say,’ wíw ‘to believe,’ náy ‘to think,’ hú ‘to see,’
                                                                                    
          and ná·kš ‘to hear’ take independent clauses as complements. Verbs such as óŋš ‘not to want’
                                                                                    
          and nám ‘to beg’ require the verb of the complement clause to be in the form of a gerund; this
                                                                                    
          construction can be understood as a relative clause equivalent.           
                                                                                    
               [Atsiˊc-kic haˊ waˊnat]                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          442) “hihaccíškišháh,” wánat                                              
                                                                                    
               hi-hat-ciškiš-hah        wan-ø-at                                    
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-REFLEX-be.pleased-NEG say-3SS-PRET                           
                                                                                    
               ‘“I am not pleased,” she said.’ (1932:20:143)                        
                                                                                    
               [wic ivīˊv há coˊx̥ ’nwaˊnkit]                                       
                                                                                    
          443) wí·š hiwíwhah šokn̥wánkit                                            
                                                                                    
               wi·š hi-wiw-hah          šok-n̥ -wan-kit                             
                                                                                    
               I-FOC 1SSTAT-believe-NEG STH-2SSTATS-say-PROG                        
                                                                                    
               ‘I do not believe anything you are saying.’ (1932:34:2)              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [yiˊl toˊl tsaˊk hiˊnay]                                             
                                                                                    
          444) yíl tólcák hinày                                                     
                                                                                    
               yil  ø-tol-cak      hi-nay                                           
                                                                                    
               day  3S-be.good-be.dry 1SSTATS-think                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘I think the day will be good and dry.’                              
                                                                                    
               {shakiól shkalít haihaísh nā́ksho}                                   
                                                                                    
          445) šakyól škalít hayháyš ná·kšo
