               šakyol škalit hayhayš-ø na·kš-o                                      
                                                                                    
               male child weep-3SS hear-1SS                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘I hear a boy weeping.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:122)                        
                                                                                    
               [paknaˊ-u wan năn hioˊñc]                                            
                                                                                    
          446) paknáwwáŋne nhióŋš                                                   
                                                                                    
               paknaw-waŋ-ne  n-hi-oŋš                                              
                                                                                    
               run-go.about-GER 2SO-1SSTATS-not.to.want                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I don’t want you to go running about.’ (1932:101:2)                 
                                                                                    
               [Kēˊtne naˊmtsel tiˊc hāˊxcne]                                       
                                                                                    
          447) ké·tne námcel tišhahšne                                              
                                                                                    
               ke·t-ne   nam-cel   tiš-hahš-ne                                      
                                                                                    
               stay-GER  beg-1PLS  go(sg)-NEG-GER                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘We begged her to stay and not to go.’ (1932:17:110-111)             
                                                                                    
          Non-verbal copular sentences. In addition to copular sentences produced by the verbalization
                                                                                    
          of nouns as discussed above in Chapter 2, when two nouns are understood to be simple
                                                                                    
          equivalents, they can be placed in apposition to one another.             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [haˊ Yuk’hiˊti ēñ Konāˊc]                                            
                                                                                    
          448) ha yukhíti é·ŋ koná·š                                                
                                                                                    
               ha   yukhiti   e·ŋ  kona·š                                           
                                                                                    
               his  Indian    name PN                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘His Indian name is Kona·š.’ (1932:15:82-83)                         
                                                                                    
               [haˊ ca aˊ]                                                          
                                                                                    
          449) há ša á                                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         201                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ha   ša        a                                                     
                                                                                    
               he   someone   Q                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘Who is he?’ (1932:108:1)                                            
                                                                                    
          Conjunction and disjunction. Noun phrases and verb phrases are treated differently in Atakapa
                                                                                    
          in terms of conjunction. Noun phrases, when not joined by apposition alone, are conjoined with
                                                                                    
          the clitic =n̥, which is a syllabic nasal, while verb phrases are connected by the clitic =ya. Both
                                                                                    
          clitics are written by Gatschet and Swanton as separate words, because their equivalents in
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          English and German are separate words. However, there is evidence both in the publication and
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes that both elements were attached to an adjoining word, and that is how
                                                                                    
          they are treated here.                                                    
                                                                                    
          Nominal Conjunction and Disjunction. More than two nouns in sequence are joined by simple
                                                                                    
          apposition, as in the following example:                                  
                                                                                    
               [iyāˊñ tīˊv ia naˊ-u oxoˊx icyāˊmcne aˊyip kună kaxlōˊpc tsăma kuˊts coxmōˊn
                                                                                    
          450) iyáŋ tíwya náwohóh išyá·mšne áyip konán kathópš cimakúc šokmó·n      
                                                                                    
               icyāˊmcĕhĕ Kidsoˊnkckin (i)cakwāktikit]                              
                                                                                    
               išyá·mšehe kicóŋškin iššakwáktikit                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               iyaŋ tiw=ya    nawohoh   iš-ya·mš-ne         ay-ip     konan         
                                                                                    
               there go=and   chinquapin 2PLSTATS-gather-GER swamp-LOC potato       
                                                                                    
               kathopš   cimakuc   šok-mo·n  iš-ya·mš-ehe        kicoŋš-kin         
                                                                                    
               waterlily crawfish  thing-all 2PLSTATS-gather-DUB fire-LOC           
                                                                                    
               iš-šak-wak-tikit                                                     
                                                                                    
               1PLSTATS-3PL.OBJ-roast-FUT                                           
                                                                                    
               ‘Us going there and gathering chinquapins; we may gather everything, duck
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         202                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               potatoes, waterlilies, and crawfish. We shall roast them in the fire.’ (1932:13:47-49)
                                                                                    
          However, when two nouns alone are linked, the clitic =n̥ is used. Note that in the first example,
                                                                                    
          the clitic can mean ‘or’ as well as ‘and.’                                
                                                                                    
               [cikoˊm ‘n canuˊk haˊxcĕt]                                           
                                                                                    
          451) šikómn̥ canúk háhšat                                                 
                                                                                    
               šikom=n̥  canuk     ø-hahš-at                                        
                                                                                    
               cow=and   horse     3STATS-not.have-PRET                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘She did not have cows or horses.’ (1932:15:80)                      
                                                                                    
               [ickiciˊl ēñ Louison Yuk’hiˊti ēñ Yoyoˊt ‘n Kic Yuˊts]               
                                                                                    
          452) iškišíl é·ŋ <Louison> yukhíti é·ŋ yoyótn̥ kišyúc                     
                                                                                    
               iškišíl   é·ŋ  <Louison> yukhíti   é·ŋ  yoyót=n̥  kišyúc             
                                                                                    
               daughter  name PN        Indian    name PN=and    PN                 
                                                                                    
               ‘Her daughter’s name is Louison; her Indian names are Yoyót and Kišyúts.’
                                                                                    
               (1932:15:83-84)                                                      
                                                                                    
          There is one example of a phrase in which multiple nouns are linked with -n̥. This phrase was
                                                                                    
          provided as a translation of the word ‘world.’                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               {nē n’itans’n icāk’n mōni}                                           
                                                                                    
          453) né·n̥ itánšn̥ išá·kn̥ mó·ni                                          
                                                                                    
               ne·=n̥    itanš=n̥       iša·k=n̥  mo·ni                             
                                                                                    
               earth=and sky/cloud=and  person=and all(animate)                     
                                                                                    
               ‘The earth and sky and people and all living things.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:87)
                                                                                    
          Verbal Conjunction and Disjunction. Sentences that are joined are linked by the clitic =ya.
                                                                                    
          When the verbs in the conjoined sentences are co-referential, the verb in the first sentence does
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         203                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          not have subject, mode, or aspect marking; all the latter are found on the final verb of the
                                                                                    
          following sentence. If the sentences have different subjects, then each verb bears the appropriate
                                                                                    
          marking, as in example 457; if the verbs have no causal or temporal relationship, they also retain
                                                                                    
          the appropriate marking, as in example 458. This construction is used when the actions described
                                                                                    
          by the verbs occur in sequential order. In one place in the field notes (1885b:141), Gatschet
                                                                                    
          glosses =ya with hál ‘afterwards,’ confirming that the action of the second verb follows that of
                                                                                    
          the first.                                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Kaˊ-u oˊkin toˊkc ya imōˊculat]                                     
                                                                                    
          454) káw ókkin tókšya himó·šulat                                          
                                                                                    
               kaw       ok-kin    tokš=ya        himo·š-ul-at                      
                                                                                    
               dead.person cloth-LOC wrap=and     bury-3PLS-PRET                    
                                                                                    
               ‘They wrapped the dead person in cloth and buried him/her.’ (1932:18:126)
                                                                                    
               [Imōˊc mak māˊñ kaˊ ya hoˊxp naˊ-ulat coˊ itaˊxne]                   
                                                                                    
          455) himo·šmak máŋ káya hóp náwulat šó hitáhne                            
                                                                                    
               himo·š-mak maŋ ka=ya     hop  naw-ul-at      šo                      
                                                                                    
               grave-mound long make=and hole leave-3PLS-PRET soul                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hitah-ne                                                             
                                                                                    
               come-out-GER                                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘They made a long grave-mound, and left a hole for the soul to come out.’ (1932:19:139-
                                                                                    
               140)                                                                 
                                                                                    
               [icāˊk taˊ-ic oˊkian yukīt nēˊ moˊn ialpeˊhiulĕt]                    
                                                                                    
          456) iša·k táyš ókya yukít né· mo·n yalpéhiulat                           
                                                                                    
               iša·k     tayš      ok=ya     yukit ne· mo·n yal-pehi-ul-at          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         204                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               person    strange   come=and  our  land all  take-finish-3PLS-PRET   
                                                                                    
               ‘Strange people have come and completely taken all our land.’ (1932:125:1)
                                                                                    
               {wi kitsāk ámwangyě himakáwět}                                       
                                                                                    
          457) wí kicá·k ámwáŋya himakáwat                                          
                                                                                    
               wi   kica·k    am-waŋ=ya      hi-makaw-at                            
                                                                                    
               I    whiskey   drink-go.about=and 1SSTATS-fall-PRET                  
                                                                                    
               ‘I was going about drinking whiskey, and I fell down.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:86)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [īclaˊuc taˊtik kaˊwat yaˊ haˊ nuˊnkin imoˊculat]                    
                                                                                    
          458) hišláwš tá·tik káwatya ha núŋkin himó·šulat                          
                                                                                    
               hišlawš   ta·t-ik        ø-kaw-at=ya         ha   nuŋ-kin            
                                                                                    
               fever     yellow-ERG.INSTR 3SSTATS-die-PRET=and her village-LOC      
                                                                                    
               himo·š-ul-at                                                         
                                                                                    
               bury-3PLS-PRET                                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘She died of yellow fever, and they buried her in her village.’ (1932:15:88-89)
                                                                                    
               [Kic moˊkat yaˊ yoˊlĕt]                                              
                                                                                    
          459) kiš mókatya yó·lat                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kiš       ø-mok-at=ya         ø-yo·l-at                              
                                                                                    
               woman     3STATS-be.short-PRET=and 3STATS-be.ill.tempered-PRET       
                                                                                    
               ‘She was a short woman and an ill-tempered one.’ (1932:15:78-80)     
                                                                                    
          When alternatives are offered in a sequence of sentences, they are joined by the verb n̥ tán, ‘to be
                                                                                    
          either/or.’ If the alternatives are mutually exclusive, the form n̥tánat is used, which looks like a
                                                                                    
          preterite form, although the semantics are different from what one would expect from a preterite.
                                                                                    
               [Yiˊl mōn taˊ-i ō hoˊktiwē icwāˊñĕhĕ ‘ntaˊn neˊc-paˊl idsonˊkin toˊ(h)i-a
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         205                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          460) yíl mo·n táy ó hoktiwé·išwáŋehe n̥tán nešpalhicónkin tóhiya          
                                                                                    
               loˊx̥ kin tiˊu-sakioˊns icwāˊñĕhĕ ntaˊn tuˊ-kēˊmc icwāˊñĕhĕ]         
                                                                                    
               lókkin tiwšakn̥óns išwáŋehe n̥tán tukémšišwáŋehe                     
                                                                                    
               yil  mo·n tay  o     hok-tiwe·-iš-waŋ-ehe         n̥tan              
                                                                                    
               day  all  river beside RECIP-be.together-1PLSTATS-go.about-DUB be.or 
                                                                                    
               nešpalhicon-kin tohi=ya  lok-kin   tiw-šak-n̥onš                     
                                                                                    
               buggy-loc      get.in=and prairie-loc GO(PL)-3PLO-visit              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               iš-waŋ-ehe          n̥tan     tu-kemš-iš-waŋ-ehe                     
                                                                                    
               1PLSTATS-go.about-DUB be.or   boat-row-1PLSTATS-go.about-DUB         
                                                                                    
               ‘All day we might go about together beside the river, or get in a buggy and go
                                                                                    
               visit people on the prairie, or we might go about rowing in a boat.’ (1932:13:45-47)
                                                                                    
               [oˊk ‘nuˊxts aˊ’ntaˊnat ‘nhaˊh]                                      
                                                                                    
          461) ók n̥úhc á n̥tánat n̥háh                                             
                                                                                    
               ok   n̥-uhc         a    n̥tan-at  n̥-hah                            
                                                                                    
               come 2SSTATS-be.able Q   be.or-PRET 2SSTATS-be.not                   
                                                                                    
               ‘Are you able to come? Or are you not?’4 (1932:12:43)                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          If there is a stated contrast between the two sentences, the suffix -kšo is used. The only examples
                                                                                    
          of this suffix were obtained through elicitation.                         
                                                                                    
               [miˊlc kcoˊ yiˊlmāñ cokaˊx̥ kit]                                     
                                                                                    
          462) mílškšó yíl máŋ šokkákit                                             
                                                                                    
               ø-milš-kšo     yil  maŋ       ø-šok=ka-kit                           
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.blind-BUT day be.long 3STATS-thing=do-PROG(SG)             
                                                                                    
               ‘He is blind, but he is doing things all throughout the day.’ (1932:72:2)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         206                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [heˊ (k)co toˊlha]                                                   
                                                                                    
          463) hékšó tólháh                                                         
                                                                                    
               ø-he-kšo                      ø-tol-hah                              
                                                                                    
               3STATS-be.strong.tasting-BUT 3STATS-be.good-NEG                      
                                                                                    
               ‘It is strong-tasting, but not good.’ (1932:72:2)                    
                                                                                    
               [hicokēc kcoˊ yiˊlkit mōn tiˊco]                                     
                                                                                    
          464) hišoké·škšó yílkit mo·n tíšo                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hi-šoke·š-kšo  yil-kit        mo·n tiš-o                             
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-be.sick-BUT be.day-PROG(SG) all go-1SS                       
                                                                                    
               ‘I am sick, but I go out every day.’ (1932:156:1)                    
                                                                                    
               [icāk hiyaniaˊncoˊ tiwē tiˊco]                                       
                                                                                    
          465) išá·k hiyanyánkšó tiwé·tíšo                                          
                                                                                    
               iša·k     hi-yanyan-kšo  tiwé·-tiš-o                                 
                                                                                    
               person    1SSTATS-fear-BUT be.with-go-1SS                            
                                                                                    
               ‘I am afraid of the man, but I am going with him.’ (1932:29:1)       
                                                                                    
          If two verbs have the same subject and the actions are occurring at the same time, the first verb is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          compounded with the second, to which all suffixes are attached.           
                                                                                    
               [Wi okēˊt māñ ilāñ-wāˊñtat]                                          
                                                                                    
          466) wi oké·t máŋ iláŋwáŋn̥ tat                                           
                                                                                    
               wi   oke·t     maŋ       ilaŋ-waŋ-n̥t-at                             
                                                                                    
               my   mother    long.time mourn-go.about CONTIN-PRET                  
                                                                                    
               ‘My mother kept on going about mourning for a long time.’ (1932:17:105)
                                                                                    
               [ha ˊcok’-okināˊtsne mōn hoktiwēˊ imōˊculat]                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         207                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          467) ha šokhokinácne mó·n hoktiwé·himó·šulat                              
                                                                                    
               ha   šokhokinacne mo·n hok-tiwe·-himo·š-ul-at                        
                                                                                    
               his  weapons   all  RECIP-be.together-bury-3PLS-PRET                 
                                                                                    
               ‘They buried all his weapons together with him.’ (1932:19:129-30)    
                                                                                    
          Independent conjunctions. There are independent conjunctions, but they are rare in the corpus.
                                                                                    
          The two most notable are káy ‘and then,’ and tik ‘until.’                 
                                                                                    
               [Kaˊ-i aˊn kaˊt-paˊ-ic naˊ-ulat haˊ aˊnkin kaˊ-u atkaˊki tiˊk imōˊctin Neˊ taˊmkin yaˊ]
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          468) káy aŋkát páyš náwulat ha áŋkin káw atkáki tikhimó·štin né·támkinya  
                                                                                    
               kay       aŋkat payš naw-ul-at     ha   aŋ-kin                       
                                                                                    
               and.then  door open leave-3PL-PRET his  house-LOC                    
                                                                                    
               ø-kaw          atkaki    tik  himo·š-ti-n    ne·tam-kin=ya           
                                                                                    
               3STATS-die(sg) since     until bury-PSTPROG-WHEN grave-LOC=and       
                                                                                    
               ‘And then they left the door open in his/her house from the time of his death
                                                                                    
               until they buried him/her in a grave, and…. (1932:18:122-24)         
                                                                                    
               [iti māˊñ hiwāˊlc tik yiˊlat]                                        
                                                                                    
          469) ití máŋ hi·wálš tik yílat                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ití  maŋ       hi-hiwalš      tik  yil-at                            
                                                                                    
               night be.long  1SSTATS-dream  until be.day-PRET                      
                                                                                    
               ‘I dreamed all night until it was day.’ (1932:43:1)                  
                                                                                    
          Omitted inflection in coordinate contexts. When two coordinated verbs in the desiderative,
                                                                                    
          and the preterite and dubitative modes share the same subject and aspect, one verb alone has the
                                                                                    
          subject and aspectual affixes.                                            
                                                                                    
               [kitsāk kaˊ-u yaˊ waˊñ-cakpaˊmkoh yaˊ cak’heˊ-uc]                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         208

               kica·k    ø-kaw=ya       waŋ-šak-pam-koh=ya       šak-hewš           
                                                                                    
               whiskey   3STATS-die(sg)=and go.about-3PLOBJ-beat-DESID=and 3PLOBJ-curse

               (1932:15:79-80)

          However, in the case of non-third persons in the aorist mode, both verbs retain the subject affixes
                                                                                    
          if the verbs are transitive. If the verbs are intransitive, only the final verb of the sequence has the
                                                                                    
          subject affix.                                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Hiyeˊn teˊm nimaˊ-utsĕl yaˊ kuyak’hoˊ cakaˊtsel]

          If an action is done to a body part, the possessive pronoun is not used, but the possessor is
                                                                                    
          understood from the affixes to the verb. This is a form of object possessor raising, which has
                                                                                    
          been documented in other languages of the Southeast (Munro, 1984; Martin, 1993).
                                                                                    
               [uˊts hatluˊticu]

               [ēˊñ hiwaˊhulat]                                                     
                                                                                    
          481) é·ŋ hiwáhu·lat                                                       
                                                                                    
               e·ŋ  hi-wahu-ul-at                                                   
                                                                                    
               name 1SO-call-3PLS-PRET                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘They called my name.’ (1932:31:1)                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         211                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Relative clause equivalents. Atakapa, like other Southeastern languages (Chickasaw, Munro,
                                                                                    
          2005:143-44; Chitimacha, Swadesh, 1946a:334; Creek, Hardy, 2005:237-8; Koasati,
                                                                                    
          Kimball,1991:525-27: Natchez, Kimball, 2005:444-45; Tunica, Haas, 1940:93), lacks
                                                                                    
          relativizing morphology; in fact, there are few examples of relative clause equivalents in the
                                                                                    
          corpus. In Atakapa, verbs of mental activity, such as hearing, seeing, thinking, believing, or
                                                                                    
          saying are followed directly by a complement clause which is an independent sentence with no
                                                                                    
          morphological connection to the verb, as mentioned above in the discussion of clausal
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          complements.                                                              
                                                                                    
               [Viˊvulat iˊcak capiˊx̥ k taneˊ-u okcak’hu-ulat]                     
                                                                                    
          482) wíwulat išà·k šappíhk tannéw okšakhúulat                             
                                                                                    
               wiw-ul-at      iša·k     šak-pihk  tan-hew                           
                                                                                    
               believe-3PLS-PRET person PL-die(pl) other-PL                         
                                                                                    
               ok-šak-hu-ul-at                                                      
                                                                                    
               come-3PLO-see-3PLS-PRET                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘They believed that other dead people came to see them,’ lit. ‘They believed it; other
                                                                                    
               dead people came to see them.’ (1932:18:121-22)                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [ca cokeˊc tiˊxt wi hiˊnai]                                          
                                                                                    
          483) ša šoké·š tíht wí hinày                                              
                                                                                    
               ša        šoke·š    tiht      wi   hi-nay                            
                                                                                    
               someone   sick      lie(sg)   I    1SSTATS-think                     
                                                                                    
               ‘I think that someone is lying sick,’ lit. ‘I think it; someone is lying sick.’ (1932:38:1)
                                                                                    
          Other relative clause equivalents are formed by nominalizing a sentence using the gerund suffix
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         212                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          -ne, and with the appropriate case marker on the nominalized sentence, depending on whether
                                                                                    
          the sentence is the subject, the object, or in a locative relationship with the main clause. Only
                                                                                    
          relative clause equivalents in object and locative relationships are found in the data.
                                                                                    
               [wi hitēt hioˊknă oˊñc]                                              
                                                                                    
          484) wi ité·t hiókne óŋš                                                  
                                                                                    
               wi   ite·t hi-ok-ne      ø-oŋš                                       
                                                                                    
               my   father 1SSTATS-come-GER 3STATS-not.want                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘My father does not want me to come, lit. my father does not want it that I come.’
                                                                                    
               (1932:43:1)                                                          
                                                                                    
               [Yiˊl paˊxĕ ‘nyaˊ-uta ‘noˊkne]                                       
                                                                                    
          485) yíl páhw n̥yáwta n̥ókne                                              
                                                                                    
               yil  pahw      n̥-yaw-ta           n̥-ok-ne                          
                                                                                    
               day  seven     2SOBJ-wait.for-PCPL 2SSTATS-come-GER                  
                                                                                    
               ‘I am to wait seven days for you to come, lit. I am to wait for you seven days that you
                                                                                    
               come.’ (1932:12:42)                                                  
                                                                                    
          There is a single example of such a relative clause equivalent in a locative relationship:
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [nuˊnkin toˊhulat kakauˊ iyeˊtsnē ûˊt]                               
                                                                                    
          486) núŋkin tóhulat kakáw iyécneot                                        
                                                                                    
               nuŋ-kin   toh-ul-at      kakaw     iyec-ne-ot                        
                                                                                    
               village-LOC dwell-3PLS-PRET sun    rise-GER-DIR                      
                                                                                    
               ‘They lived in villages towards where the sun rises.’ (1932:10:31)   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         213                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Here the main clause is núŋkin tóhulat ‘they lived in villages.’ The dependent clause is kakáw
                                                                                    
          iyéc ‘the sun rises.’ The latter is nominalized with the gerund suffix -ne, and related to the main
                                                                                    
          clause with the directional case ending -ot, thus creating a locative relative clause equivalent.
                                                                                    
           There is one further probable example of a relative clause equivalent.   
                                                                                    
               [Icāk tsanuˊki iˊñi caknaˊ-uc iyaˊnĕk mōn ‘nkaˊnē]                   
                                                                                    
          487) icá·k canúkkin <šak>íŋi šaknáwš iyáŋik mò·n n̥ káne                  
                                                                                    
               ica·k     canuk-kin <šak>-iŋi      šak-nawš-ø                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               person    horse-LOC 3PLO-look.for  3PLO-let-2SIMP                    
                                                                                    
               iyaŋ-ik        mò·n      n̥-ka-ne                                    
                                                                                    
               there-ERG.AGNT be.all    2SS-do-GER                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘Let people on horses look <for them>! That there is all that you do.’ (1932:13:59-60)
                                                                                    
          This example is from a text, that based on several factors, one of which is the lack of the third
                                                                                    
          person plural object pronoun on the verb íŋi ‘to look for,’ seems to have been a direct translation
                                                                                    
          of a prepared English original. In the publication (1932:13-14) the phrase iyáŋik mò·n nkáne is
                                                                                    
          not even translated except in the interlinear format, where it appears as: “that is all for you to
                                                                                    
          do.” If this is not a case of the gerund expressing purpose, then the unique ergative form of the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          noun iyáŋ ‘there’ acts as a resumptive pronoun referring to the the previous sentence, the noun
                                                                                    
          mo·n is verbalized as ‘to be all,’ and n̥káne, as a noun in the absolutive state, is the relative clause
                                                                                    
          equivalent meaning ‘that you do.’                                         
                                                                                    
          Interrogative sentences. The particle á placed after the verb of a sentence indicates that it is a
                                                                                    
          question. There is also a less common final suffix -n (á) that occasionally indicates questions, as
                                                                                    
          in examples 489 and 496.                                                  
                                                                                    
               [‘ntoˊl aˊ]                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         214                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          488) n̥tól á                                                              
                                                                                    
               n̥-tol         a                                                     
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-be.good Q                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Are you well?’ (1932:23:1)                                          
                                                                                    
               [‘n hihu haˊxc aˊ]                                                   
                                                                                    
          489) n̥hihúháhš á                                                         
                                                                                    
               n̥-hi-hu-hahš       a                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-1SO-see-NEG Q                                                
                                                                                    
               ‘Do you not see me?’ (1932:34:1)                                     
                                                                                    
               [‘nhiiˊnat hatnaˊxka paˊ-ihiticnăna]                                 
                                                                                    
          490) n̥hiínat hatnáhka payhitìšnen á                                      
                                                                                    
               n̥-hi-in-at         hatnah-ka      pay-hi-tiš-ne-n                   
                                                                                    
               a                                                                    
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-1SO-ask-PRET so.much-ADV   BACK-1SSTATS-go(sg)-GER-Q         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Q                                                                    
               ‘You asked for me; how am I to go back?’ (1932:37:1)                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Interrogative words do not form a single class. The concept ‘who’ is handled using ša,
                                                                                    
          ‘someone,’ followed by the interrogative particle, and the concept ‘what’ is expressed by šok,
                                                                                    
          ‘something,’ followed by the interrogative particle. There is a rare pronoun šinnéw (šin- + -héw
                                                                                    
          pl.) which means ‘who? (pl).’                                             
                                                                                    
               [haˊ ca aˊ]                                                          
                                                                                    
          491) há ša á                                                              
                                                                                    
               ha   ša        a                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         215                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               he   someone   Q                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘Who is he?’ (1932:108:1)                                            
                                                                                    
               [caˊ aˊ nāˊc]                                                        
                                                                                    
          492) šá á ná·š                                                            
                                                                                    
               ša        a    na-iš                                                 
                                                                                    
               someone   Q    you-FOC                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘Who are you?’ (1932:108:1)                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [cok ‘nkaˊ a]                                                        
                                                                                    
          493) šók n̥ká á                                                           
                                                                                    
               šok       n̥-ka     a                                                
                                                                                    
               something 2SSTATS-do Q                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘What are you doing?’ (1932:56:1)                                    
                                                                                    
               {shók na komhópshkin nkéa}                                           
                                                                                    
          494) šók na komhópškin n̥ké á                                             
                                                                                    
               šok       na   komhopš-kin n̥-ke        a                            
                                                                                    
               something your pocket-LOC 2SSTATS-have  Q                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘What do you have in your pocket?’ (Gatschet 1885b:79)               
                                                                                    
               [hakiˊt cineˊv aˊ]                                                   
                                                                                    
          495) hakít šinnéw á                                                       
                                                                                    
               hakit     šin-hew   a                                                
                                                                                    
               they      who-PL    Q                                                
                                                                                    
               ‘Who are they?’ (1932:23:1)                                          
                                                                                    
               [cineˊ-u yaˊ cukcakiˊkc]                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         216                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          496) šinnéw yá šokšakyíkš                                                 
                                                                                    
               šin-hew   ya   šok-šak-yikš                                          
                                                                                    
               who-PL    that STH-3PLO-sell                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘Who are those merchants?’ (1932:155:2)                              
                                                                                    
          The concept of ‘where’ is usually handled by the noun itáŋ ‘place;’ note itáŋ mo·n ‘everywhere.’
                                                                                    
               [it’haˊñ ike ok’n]                                                   
                                                                                    
          497) itáŋike ókn̥                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               itaŋ-ike  ok-ø-n̥                                                    
                                                                                    
               place-EL  come-3SS-Q                                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘Where does he come from?’ (1932:53:2)                               
                                                                                    
               [cul itaˊñ aˊ]                                                       
                                                                                    
          498) šúl itáŋ á                                                           
                                                                                    
               šul  itaŋ a                                                          
                                                                                    
               dog  place Q                                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘Where is the dog?’ (1932:53:2)                                      
                                                                                    
               {ná ná-u itháng ná-i? áng katmókin náyu}                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          499) na náw itáŋ náy? áŋkatmókkin náyo                                    
                                                                                    
               na   naw  itaŋ nay? aŋkat-mok-kin  nay-o                             
                                                                                    
               you  shoe place put door-short-LOC put-1SS                           
                                                                                    
               ‘Where did you put the shoes? I put them in the window.’ (Gatschet, 1885c:86)
                                                                                    
           In addition to this form there is the sentence-final particle má which means ‘where is?’
                                                                                    
               [ciwoˊn ma]                                                          
                                                                                    
          500) šiwán má                                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         217                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               šiwan ma                                                             
                                                                                    
               cat  where.is                                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘Where is the cat?’ (1932:112:2)                                     
                                                                                    
               [naˊ noˊmc ma]                                                       
                                                                                    
          501) na nómš má                                                           
                                                                                    
               na   nomš ma                                                         
                                                                                    
               your child where.is                                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Where is your child?’ (1932:83:1)                                   
                                                                                    
           Of interest is the fact that this particle and the construction in which it is used was borrowed
                                                                                    
          into Koasati, a Muskogean language spoken upstream of the Atakapa on the Calcasieu River
                                                                                    
          (Kimball, 1994:132).                                                      
                                                                                    
            The adjective hatnáh ‘so much’ in an interrogative sentence means ‘how much?’; converted
