          into an adverb with the suffix -ka, hatnáhka, it means ‘how?’             
                                                                                    
               [laklaˊx̥ c hatnaˊ nkeˊa]                                            
                                                                                    
          502) laklákš hatnáh n̥ké á                                                
                                                                                    
               laklakš   hatnah    n̥-ke          a                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               money     so.much   2SSTATS-have   Q                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘How much money do you have?’ (1932:37:1)                            
                                                                                    
               [hatnaˊ hiyiˊkicta]                                                  
                                                                                    
          503) hatnáh hiyikištá                                                     
                                                                                    
               hatnah    hi-yikiš-ta                                                
                                                                                    
               so.much   1SO-pay-PCPL                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘How much am I to be paid?’ (1932:37:1)                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         218                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [hatnaˊka peˊl a]                                                    
                                                                                    
          504) hatnáhka pél á                                                       
                                                                                    
               hatnah-ka      ø-pel          a                                      
                                                                                    
               so.much-ADV    3STATS-be.far  Q                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘How far is it?’ (1932:37:1)                                         
                                                                                    
               [hatnaˊx̥ kan mēˊts a]                                               
                                                                                    
          505) hatnáhka n̥méc á                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               hatnah-ka      n̥-mec         a                                      
                                                                                    
               so.much-ADV    2STATS-be.tall Q                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘How tall are you?’ (1932:37:1)                                      
                                                                                    
          There is a further question word listed in the dictionary, šókay ‘why?’. The questioned sentence
                                                                                    
          needs to be followed by the question suffix –n, or the question particle á. There are only two
                                                                                    
          examples given.                                                           
                                                                                    
               [coˊkaya]                                                            
                                                                                    
          506) šókay á                                                              
                                                                                    
               šokay a                                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               why  Q                                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘Why?’ (1932:113:2)                                                  
                                                                                    
               [coˊka-i ‘n-oˊt haˊxcnan]                                            
                                                                                    
          507) šókay n̥ókháhšnen                                                    
                                                                                    
               šokay n̥-ok-hahš-ne-n                                                
                                                                                    
               why  2SSTATS-come-NEG-GER-Q                                          
                                                                                    
               ‘Why do you not come?’ (1932:113:2)                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         219                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Idiomatic expressions. Atakapa has a number of individual idiomatic expressions. Several are
                                                                                    
          built around the verb pair káw / píhk ‘to die.’ In these expressions the subject is always
                                                                                    
          expressed by a stative prefix.                                            
                                                                                    
               kicáˑk káw / kicáˑk píhk ‘to be drunk’                               
                                                                                    
               kicá·k amkáw             ‘to be drunk’                               
                                                                                    
               kawkáw káw / kawkáw píhk ‘to be thirsty’                             
                                                                                    
               óˑl káw                  ‘to be hungry’                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [kitsāk kaˊ-u yaˊ waˊñ-cakpaˊmkoh yaˊ cak’heˊ-uc]                    
                                                                                    
          508) kicá·k káwya wáŋšakpámkóhya šakhéwš                                  
                                                                                    
               kica·k    ø-kaw=ya            waŋ-šak-ø-pam-koh=ya                   
                                                                                    
               whiskey   3SSTATS-die(sg)=and go.about-3PLOBJ-3STATS-beat-DESID=and  
                                                                                    
               šak-hewš                                                             
                                                                                    
               3PLOBJ-curse                                                         
                                                                                    
               ‘She got drunk and wanted to go about beating people and cursing them.’
                                                                                    
               (1932:15:79-80)                                                      
                                                                                    
               [Kitsāˊk cakpiˊx̥ k yaˊ paˊm (h)okpaˊmiculat]                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          509) kicá·k šappíhkya pámhokpámišulat                                     
                                                                                    
               kica·k    šak-pihk-ya         pam,hok,pam-iš-ul-at                   
                                                                                    
               whiskey   3PLSTATS-die(pl)-and REDUP,RECIP,beat-CAUS-3PLS-PRET       
                                                                                    
               ‘They were drunk and they really beat each other up.’ (1932:20:145)  
                                                                                    
               {nûng-úship shokó-i kitsā́k ámkawět}                                 
                                                                                    
          510) núŋúšip šokóy kicá·k ámkawat                                         
                                                                                    
               nuŋuš-ip  šokoy kica·k   am-ø-kaw-at                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         220                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               city-INESS chief whiskey drink-3STATS-die-PRET                       
                                                                                    
               ‘The judge was drunk.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:86)                          
                                                                                    
               [kaukaˊ-u hikaˊ-u haˊ]                                               
                                                                                    
          511) kawkáw hikáwháh                                                      
                                                                                    
               kawkaw    hi-kaw-háh                                                 
                                                                                    
               water     1SSTATS-die(sg)-NEG                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘I am not thirsty.’ (1932:60:2)                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [kaukau na piˊx̥ ka]                                                 
                                                                                    
          512) kawkáw nappíhk á                                                     
                                                                                    
               kawkaw    nak-pihk       a                                           
                                                                                    
               water     2PLSSTATS-die(pl) Q                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘Are you all thirsty?’ (1932:82:2)                                   
                                                                                    
               [ōlhikaˊ-u haˊ]                                                      
                                                                                    
          513) ó·l hikáwháh                                                         
                                                                                    
               o·l  hi-kaw-hah                                                      
                                                                                    
               hunger    1SSTATS-die(sg)-NEG                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘I am not hungry.’ (1932:60:2)                                       
                                                                                    
          There are two idioms used to describe the behavior of lightning. Rather than using the usual
                                                                                    
          word for lightning, yimyílš, the word lóŋloŋš ‘thunder’ is used. The verb yimyílš, ‘for lightning
                                                                                    
          to occur,’ is only used of the flash. Lightning is said to fall to the ground, and not to strike, but
                                                                                    
          to seize an object:                                                       
                                                                                    
               [loˊñloñc (neˊkin) makauˊ]                                           
                                                                                    
          514) lóŋloŋš né·kin makáw                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         221                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               loŋloŋš   ne·-kin   ø-makaw                                          
                                                                                    
               thunder   ground-LOC 3STATS-fall                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘Lightning strikes the ground,’ lit. ‘Thunder falls to the ground.’ (1932:77:2)
                                                                                    
               [loˊñloñc anˊ koˊñět]                                                
                                                                                    
          515) lóŋloŋš áŋ kóŋat                                                     
                                                                                    
               loŋloŋš   aŋ   koŋ-ø-at                                              
                                                                                    
               thunder   house strike-3SS-PRET                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Lightning struck the house,’ lit. ‘Thunder seized the house.’ (1932:77:2)
                                                                                    
          The adverb tíwhc, which usually indicates that a weather phenomenon is taking place gradually
                                                                                    
          or gently, is used in three idioms, one with the verb wáŋ ‘to go about,’ the other two with the
                                                                                    
          verb ké·t ‘to sit.’                                                       
                                                                                    
               [tiuxts waˊnkit]                                                     
                                                                                    
          516) tíwhc wáŋkit                                                         
                                                                                    
               tiwhc ø-waŋ-kit                                                      
                                                                                    
               gently 3STATS-go.about-prog(sg)                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘He is behaving.’ (1932:142:1)                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [tiˊuxts kēt iaˊ-uc]                                                 
                                                                                    
          517) tíwhc ké·t hiáwš                                                     
                                                                                    
               tiwhc     ke·t hi-awš                                                
                                                                                    
               gently    sit  1SSTATS-be.unable                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘I am restless.’ (1932:34:2)                                         
                                                                                    
               [wi tiˊuxts keˊt’nto]                                                
                                                                                    
          518) wi tíwhc ké·tn̥to                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         222                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wi   tiwhc 5   ke·t-n̥t-o·                                           
                                                                                    
               I    gently    sit-CONTIN-PHR.TERM                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘I am going to sit quietly.’ (1932:62:2)                             
                                                                                    
          Two idioms are based on the reflexive form of the verb ‘to see’ hu. The first is based on the
                                                                                    
          simple reflexive.                                                         
                                                                                    
               [toˊlka at’huˊ]                                                      
                                                                                    
          519) tólka hathú                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tolka hat-hu-ø                                                       
                                                                                    
               well REFLEX-see-2SIMP                                                
                                                                                    
               ‘Be careful!’ (1932:135:1)                                           
                                                                                    
          The other idiom compounds the reflexive of hu with the verb wáŋ, ‘to go about.’
                                                                                    
               [toˊlka hat’huˊ waˊñ]                                                
                                                                                    
          520) tólka hathúwáŋ                                                       
                                                                                    
               tolka hat-hu-waŋ                                                     
                                                                                    
               well REFLEX-see-go.about                                             
                                                                                    
               ‘He takes care of himself.’ (1932:135:1)                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Diseases either ‘do’ a person (ká ) or ‘have’ a person (ké ).             
                                                                                    
               [cok’hēˊc oktanuˊk cokaˊat]                                          
                                                                                    
          521) šoké·š hoktanúk šakkáat                                              
                                                                                    
               šoke·š    hoktanuk  šak-ka-ø-at                                      
                                                                                    
               illness   different 3PLO-do-3S-pret                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘They had different illnesses, lit. Different illnesses did them.’ (1932:15:91-92)
                                                                                    
               [Haˊ hilaˊ-i cok’hēˊc yaˊ kaˊ-u haˊnĕt Koyiˊu hiweˊ-u keˊat]         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         223                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          522) ha hiláy šoké·šya káwhánat koyíw hiwéw kéat                          
                                                                                    
               ha   hilay šoke·š=ya ø-kaw-han-at       koyíw hiwéw                  
                                                                                    
               his  wife be.sick=and 3STATS-die(sg)-ALMOST cold strong              
                                                                                    
               ø-ke-at                                                              
                                                                                    
               3STATS-have-PRET                                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘His wife was sick and almost died. A strong cold had her.’ (1932:13:54-55)
                                                                                    
           To express the concept ‘some…others…’ Atakapa uses the construction tannéw…tannéw…
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Taneˊ-u tuˊlkin paˊk-cakpaˊkicat. Taneˊ-u kaukauˊkin puˊm-hik’huˊlculat]
                                                                                    
          523) tannéw túlkin pákšakpákišat; tannéw kawkáwkin púmhikihólšulat        
                                                                                    
               tan-hew   tul-kin   pak,šak,pak-iš-at        tan-hew                 
                                                                                    
               some-PL   lake-loc  REDUP,3PLOBJ,throw.in-CAUS-PRET some-PL          
                                                                                    
               kawkaw-kin pum-hik-holš-ul-at                                        
                                                                                    
               water-LOC jump-LOC-put.in-3PLS-PRET                                  
                                                                                    
               ‘Some of them they threw again and again into the lake; others went and jumped
                                                                                    
               into the water.’ (1932:20:146-47)                                    
                                                                                    
          The language has developed an idiom to express the concept of washing something for someone,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          using a double-verb construction with pác ‘to wash something,’ and míš ‘to give something to
                                                                                    
          someone.’                                                                 
                                                                                    
               {okotká-ush íshak’hé-u shakshopātshimíshinto}                        
                                                                                    
          524) okotkáwš išà·khéw šakšokpáchimíšn̥to                                 
                                                                                    
               okotkawš  iša·k-hew šak-šok-pac-hi-miš-n̥t-o·                        
                                                                                    
               shirt     person-PL 3PLO-STH-wash-1SSTATS-give-CONTIN-PHR.TERM       
                                                                                    
               ‘I am going to wash shirts for people.’ (Gatschet 1885b, 62)         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         224                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Temporal expressions show that the Atakapa had a spatial perception of time, with the past being
                                                                                    
          perceived as being in front of a person, and the future perceived as being behind. In other
                                                                                    
          languages with similar spatial orientation of time, the past is in front because it is something that
                                                                                    
          is known, i.e. seen, while the future is behind because it is unknown and cannot be seen.
                                                                                    
               [iˊti iluˊ]                                                          
                                                                                    
          525) íti ilú                                                              
                                                                                    
               íti       ilu                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               in.front  year                                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘last year’ (1932:52:2)                                              
                                                                                    
               [haˊl itiyiˊlc]                                                      
                                                                                    
          526) hál itiyílš                                                          
                                                                                    
               hal       itiyílš                                                    
                                                                                    
               in.back   month                                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘next month’ (1932:155:2)                                            
                                                                                    
          One expression is a loan translation from French, Il a une bonne tête.    
                                                                                    
               {ishát tól kē}                                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          527) icáˑt tól ké                                                         
                                                                                    
               icáˑt tól ø-ké                                                       
                                                                                    
               head good 3sstats-have                                               
                                                                                    
               ‘He is intelligent.’ French: (Gatschet 1885b:57)                     
                                                                                    
          There is also an expression that is a loan translation from English, using the verb páłi, which
                                                                                    
          usually means ‘to split; to break,’ in the sense ‘to break out of something.’
                                                                                    
               {ánlā́k pá‘li yantá-ulĕt}                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         225                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          528) áŋlá·k páłiya n̥táhulat                                              
                                                                                    
               áŋlá·k páłi=ya n̥táh-ul-at                                           
                                                                                    
               jail break=and escape-3PLS-PRET                                      
                                                                                    
               ‘They broke out of jail and escaped.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:105)          
                                                                                    
          Areal comparisons. The three Atakapan languages, Yukhíti, Hiyékiti, and Orkokisak, form a
                                                                                    
          small family that seems to be unrelated to any other language or group. There have been
                                                                                    
          attempts to relate Atakapa to Chitimacha, the language isolate bordering Hiyékiti to the east,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          from the time of Albert S. Gatschet (1885b:177-178). In 1919, John R. Swanton published a
                                                                                    
          comparison of Atakapa, Chitimacha, and Tunica in an attempt to show that they were related
                                                                                    
          languages. Morris Swadesh limited the comparison to Atakapa and Chitimacha (1946b; 1947),
                                                                                    
          while Heinz-Karl Gursky (1965; 1969) expanded the comparison to include Tunica. Mary R.
                                                                                    
          Haas included Atakapa in her Gulf hypothesis (1951; 1952; 1958); this was accepted by Pamela
                                                                                    
          Munro in her exploration of the possible relationship between Gulf and the Yukian languages
                                                                                    
          (1994). However, these proposals have been seriously questioned (Campbell, 1997:305-6;
                                                                                    
          Mithun, 2001:302; 2017:215).                                              
                                                                                    
               I have pointed out elsewhere that there are certain structural similarities to Chitimacha,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          including the facts that both languages are ergative-absolutive and Atakapa verb plurals in -m are
                                                                                    
          strikingly similar to Chitimacha verb plurals in -ma. There are also some vocabulary items that
                                                                                    
          show a great deal of similarity, as has long been noted, such as Atakapa né· ‘land, Chitimacha
                                                                                    
          neyˀ, Atakapa kíš (sg) 'woman,’ kéš (pl), Chitimacha kiča (sg) / kič (pl), Atakapa áŋ ‘house,’
                                                                                    
          Chitimacha hana. Nonetheless, such similarities are very rare, and there seem to be no regular
                                                                                    
          sound correspondences such as exist between the three Atakapan languages. 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         226                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               One linguistic parallel between Atakapa and Chitimacha that has not yet been discussed
                                                                                    
          is the use of the focus suffix. In both Atakapa and Chitimacha, the suffix has the form -š, and is
                                                                                    
          attached to nouns and pronouns. In Chitimacha the suffix -š can be used on a verbal form if
                                                                                    
          there is no nominal form in the sentence, as well as on sentence-initial conjunctions. Compare
                                                                                    
          the following Chitimacha example, which is from the beginning of the story, A Long-Nosed
                                                                                    
          Monster Tries to Eat a Woman (Swadesh, 1950:A.32.a).6                     
                                                                                    
          kunukˀu kičantˀa-š haksikˀam hi wa·čtiʔi                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wetk we haksikˀam kap ʔu·kičˀiwkˀ-š                                       
                                                                                    
          wetk-š ʔaštkankiš we haksikˀam-š kun ni čˀamtink ču·ši                    
                                                                                    
          wetk we kičantˀa-š kap ʔu·kičˀiwšin                                       
                                                                                    
          kun hus ʔaši-š ni čˀamtink hup hi čuyi                                    
                                                                                    
          It is said that an old woman married a young man.                         
                                                                                    
          And then, she was jealous of the young man.                               
                                                                                    
          And also, sometimes the young man, it is said, used to go to dances.      
                                                                                    
          And then, the old woman used to be jealous of him.                        
                                                                                    
          It is said that her husband went to where there was a dance.              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                Because the main topic of the story is the old woman, in the first sentence kičantˀa is
                                                                                    
          marked with the focus suffix. Although the noun for young man, haksikˀam, is mentioned again
                                                                                    
          in the second sentence, and made definite with the article we, the focus is still on the old woman,
                                                                                    
          and since there is no nominal adjunct in the sentence that refers to her, the focus suffix is added
                                                                                    
          to the verbal form kap ʔu·kičˀiwkˀ ‘she was jealous of him.’ In the next sentence, the initial
                                                                                    
          sentence adverb wetk is marked with the focus suffix to show that there is a change of focus
                                                                                    
          from the old woman, and the noun haksikˀam ‘young man’ has the focus suffix to show that he is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         227                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          now the center of attention. In the fourth sentence, the focus once again is on the actions of the
                                                                                    
          old woman, and since there is an overt nominal subject of the verb kap ʔu·kičˀiwšin ‘she used to
                                                                                    
          be jealous of him,’ that noun, kičantˀa, has the focus suffix. Finally, in the fifth sentence,
                                                                                    
          attention shifts again to the actions of the young man, so the word ʔaši ‘man, husband’ is marked
                                                                                    
          with the focus suffix.                                                    
                                                                                    
               It is interesting to note that the usage of the focus morpheme in Chitimacha is parallel to
                                                                                    
          that of the Atakapa focus morpheme, as well as having the same phonemic form. If an off-hand
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          remark by Swanton is correct (1907b:277-78), that the Atakapa and Chitimacha shared stories, it
                                                                                    
          suggests that there may have been a long period of Atakapa-Chitimacha bilingualism.
                                                                                    
               Caddo, the language spoken to the north of Atakapa, is structurally very different, being a
                                                                                    
          classic example of a polysynthetic language, as opposed to Atakapa, whose morphology is
                                                                                    
          primary agglutinative. The only striking vocabulary similarity is Yukhíti kawkáw, Orkokisak
                                                                                    
          koko, ‘water,’ Caddo kú:kuʔ. In the past it has been suggested that this word is a loan from
                                                                                    
          Atakapa into Caddo; however this is implausible, given that the Caddo were an advanced
                                                                                    
          Mississippian society, and the Atakapa were hunter-gatherers. The fact that there is another
                                                                                    
          word in the Atakapan languages, Hiyékiti ak ‘water,’ Yukhíti á·k ‘liquid,’ suggests that the loan
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          came the other way.                                                       
                                                                                    
               To the west, northwest, and southwest of the Atakapa in Texas were many peoples, but
                                                                                    
          for the most part the languages they spoke (such as Adai, Bidai, and Karankawa) are poorly
                                                                                    
          attested. The one well-described language that was spoken in eastern Texas is Tonkawa, and
                                                                                    
          there are some structural similarities between the two languages. Tonkawa has an infinitive
                                                                                    
          suffix -n, which also derives nouns from verbs, for example xana-n ‘to drink,’ (Hoijer 1933:99),
                                                                                    
          xa·lo-hanan ‘drinking cup’ (Hoijer, 1949:60); cf. Atakapa ám-ne ‘to drink,’ ámne ‘a drink.’ In
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         228                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Tonkawa there is a suffix -ʔen which is only used to conjoin nouns (Hoijer, 1933:117), and is
                                                                                    
          comparable to the Atakapa clitic =n, which also is only used to conjoin nouns.
                                                                                    
          TONKAWA                                                                   
                                                                                    
          528a) heykʷecan-ʔen heyxaxalˀan-ʔen heynenwan-ʔen                         
                                                                                    
               ring-and       earring-and    red.paint-and                          
                                                                                    
               ‘Rings and earrings and red paint, and…’ (Hoijer:1933:117)           
                                                                                    
          ATAKAPA                                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          528b) {nē n’itans’n icāk’n mōni}                                          
                                                                                    
               né·n̥ itánšn̥ išá·kn̥ mó·ni                                          
                                                                                    
               ne·=n̥    itanš=n̥       iša·k=n̥  mo·ni                             
                                                                                    
               earth=and sky/cloud=and  person=and all(animate)                     
                                                                                    
               ‘The earth and sky and people and all living things.’ (Gatschet, 1885b:87)
                                                                                    
          Both languages are fluid-s languages, as shown by the following examples: 
                                                                                    
          TONKAWA                                                                   
                                                                                    
          529a) mˀe·ycoʔs               529b) kemˀe·ycoʔ                            
                                                                                    
          `    mˀe·yc-o-ʔ-s                  ke-mˀe·yc-o-ʔ-ø                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               urinate-DECL-PRES-1S          1SSTATS-urinate-DECL-PRES-3S           
                                                                                    
               ‘I urinate.’                  ‘I urinate involuntarily.’             
                                                                                    
                                             (Hoijer, 1933:70)                      
                                                                                    
          ATAKAPA                                                                   
                                                                                    
          530a) [hatkaˊuco]             530b) {hiatká-ushat}                        
                                                                                    
               hatkáwšo                      hihatkáwšat                            
                                                                                    
               hat-kawš-o                    hi-hat-kawš-at                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         229                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               REFLEX-prick-1SS              1SSTATS-REFLEX-prick-PRET              
                                                                                    
               ‘I pricked myself on purpose.’ ‘I accidentally pricked myself.’      
                                                                                    
               (1932:61:2)                   (Gatschet, 1885b:63)                   
                                                                                    
          In compound verbs with a non-third-person object, the object person prefix occurs between the
                                                                                    
          two parts of the compound in both languages:                              
                                                                                    
          TONKAWA                                                                   
                                                                                    
          531) nackexispoʔ                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               nac-ke-xisp-o-ʔ-ø                                                    
                                                                                    
               bite-1SOBJ-cut.sinew DECL-PRES-3S                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘He bites me, cutting a sinew.’ (Hoijer, 1933:56)                    
                                                                                    
          ATAKAPA                                                                   
                                                                                    
               [wi cocoˊlc hinteˊhi hăˊnulet]                                       
                                                                                    
          532) wi šošólšhin̥téhihánulat                                             
                                                                                    
               wi   šošolš-hi-n̥tehi-han-ul-at                                      
                                                                                    
               I    scare(sg)-1SOBJ-frighten-ALMOST-3PLS-PRET                       
                                                                                    
               ‘They scared and almost frightened me.’ (1932:93:2)                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Despite these structural parallels, there is almost no comparable vocabulary between Atakapa
                                                                                    
          and Tonkawa. The most notable is Atakapa yáh ‘to eat,’ Tonkawa yaxa, but given the lack of
                                                                                    
          other lexical similarities, this resemblance is likely a chance one.      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Notes                                                                     
                                                                                    
          1 . There are two examples of the ergative case being used with transitive verbs where the direct
                                                                                    
          object is not understood to be a human being. These are examples 371 and 373 above. In both
                                                                                    
          cases the object is indefinite, yá·š ‘that’ in 371, and covert in the verb yílš ‘to write’ in 373. That
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         230                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          there is an object in 373 is shown by the fact that most of the examples of the verb ‘to write’ are
                                                                                    
          in the form sokyílš, with the incorporated noun šok making the verb intransitive (or antipassive).
                                                                                    
          Both of these examples are also unique because the A role is filled by a second person singular
                                                                                    
          pronoun. There might be different rules for the use of the ergative case with second person
                                                                                    
          agents, or they may reflect an earlier stage of the language when the ergative case was used with
                                                                                    
          a broader group of semantic types.                                        
                                                                                    
          2 . Gatschet’s original is preferred to the one found in the publication (1932:612) because
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Swanton did not include the gloss ‘accidentally,’ which is found in the field notes. In Swanton’s
                                                                                    
          defense, when Gatschet went over his field notes with Tottokš, he had made some bad mistakes
                                                                                    
          in segmentation at this point, and when he corrected these in red, he either underlined or crossed
                                                                                    
          out the word ‘accidentally,’ and Swanton took it as a deletion.           
                                                                                    
          3 . The second half of the phrase is a gapped construction, with n̥háh ‘you are not,’ replacing the
                                                                                    
          expected ók n̥ úhcháh á. ‘Are you not able to come?’                      
                                                                                    
          4 . As discussed above, the participle in –ta is unmarked for subject. A first-person singular
                                                                                    
          subject is assumed because example 439 follows 438 immediately in the field notes.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          5. The meaning of the adverb tíwhc is confirmed by a variation on this sentence which replaces
                                                                                    
          the adverb with the verb hateláŋš ‘to be still; be quiet.’                
                                                                                    
          6. Swadesh did not recognize the discourse functions of the suffix -š in Chitimacha, saying,
                                                                                    
          “(p)erhaps to be included here is a postposition -š which is quite common at the end of phrases,
                                                                                    
          but which seems to have no meaning at all.” (1946a:329).                  
                                                                                    
          <Insert Illustration 2 after this page, facing the first page of Chapter 5>
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         231                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          5. TEXTS                                                                  
                                                                                    
          Introduction. English literary style, in fact European literary style in general, consists of
                                                                                    
          sentences gathered together into paragraphs. We have tended to treat Native American texts as
                                                                                    
          though they were structured in the same way. Languages in the Southeastern United States,
                                                                                    
          however, have their own unique literary style (see Kimball, 2010 for Koasati stylistics, and
                                                                                    
          Kimball 2013 for Natchez stylistics). The first stylistic criterion is that a text is made up of short
                                                                                    
          phrases, which can be termed lines or verses. The second criterion is that an idea can be stated,
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          and then restated with an augmentation of meaning. A third criterion is that a long text can be
                                                                                    
          divided into scenes, which contain lines grouped semantically. A fourth criterion is the use of
                                                                                    
          the number four in grouping lines or concepts in a text. Using the Southeastern stylistic criteria
                                                                                    
          makes translating an Atakapa text much simpler.                           
                                                                                    
           The texts here and in the publication are given in the order that Gatschet took them down in his
                                                                                    
          field notes. The texts that Kišyuc told him are untitled, but he gave titles to most of the ones that
                                                                                    
          Tottokš told him. Examination of the field notes shows how Gatschet gathered them. First, he
                                                                                    
          took the text down in Atakapa by dictation. Second, he went back over the text with his
                                                                                    
          consultant, making interlinear glosses in French and English, and making corrections to the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Atakapa text. When Tottokš became his consultant, he went back over the texts dictated by
                                                                                    
          Kišyuc, and made corrections and emendations in red ink.                  
                                                                                    
           The unglossed texts and translations are presented in a format that reflects the literary nature of
                                                                                    
          Atakapa speech, especially in the pairing of ideas, and groupings in twos and fours. The
                                                                                    
          analyzed texts are done traditionally and the commentary is based on the line numbers of the
                                                                                    
          analysis rather than on any numbering of lines in the unglossed versions. 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         232                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Text 1. Cultural and historical topics. The text translated below is found on pages 97-100 of
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes. He gave it no title, but Swanton entitled it “The Western Atakapa.” It
                                                                                    
          seems to be the response to a request such as, “Tell me how your people lived, what their chiefs
                                                                                    
          were like, what was marriage like, and what was religion like.” The form of the text that was
                                                                                    
          published in Gatschet and Swanton (1932:9-10) is not exactly what Gatschet recorded in his field
                                                                                    
          notes. Gatschet had gone over the text as spoken by Kišyuc with Tottokš and made emendations
                                                                                    
          in red, and Swanton incorporated these emendations uncritically and wholesale in the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          publication, which resulted in making certain sentences unclear. In addition, Swanton translated
                                                                                    
          the text without careful consideration of Gatschet’s interlinear glosses. 
                                                                                    
          Scene I. How the Indians Lived.                                           
                                                                                    
          yukhíti iša·k wáši a né·p nuŋnultíhin̥st                                  
                                                                                    
           túl ó·ši nuŋnultíhin̥st                                                  
                                                                                    
          tepúk néš híhulat                                                         
                                                                                    
           šéš néš híhulat
