           nohámš áyip

          Scene II. Indian Chiefs and Religion                                      
                                                                                    
          yukhíti mo·n šokíti šakkéat                                               
                                                                                    
            šokóy tanùk mo·n šokiyáhi tannéw <óci>                                  
                                                                                    
          ló hál šokóyat yukhítšakišá·kut                                           
                                                                                    
           ló hiláy yukhìti winéulat káškin                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           hiyékiti šakyóŋšulat                                                     
                                                                                    
          kakáw hiyékiti iyáŋ nuŋnùltihín̥st                                        
                                                                                    
          tawwatwénat ocottá·tot                                                    
                                                                                    
           šoká·kulat ocottá·tot                                                    
                                                                                    
          šé·š púm                                                                  
                                                                                    
           wašwáši púm púmulat                                                      
                                                                                    
          The Indians all had headmen.                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         236                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          One chief was ahead in regard to things above all the others.             
                                                                                    
          Lo was the last chief pertaining to the Indian people.                    
                                                                                    
            The Indians found Lo’s wife at high tide.                               
                                                                                    
             They called them Sunrise-people.                                       
                                                                                    
             They customarily lived in villages towards the sunrise.                
                                                                                    
          They stood and prayed to She-who-stands-above;                            
                                                                                    
           they did a ceremonial dance to She-who-stands-above.                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          They danced the Infants’ Dance                                            
                                                                                    
           and the Old Men’s Dance.                                                 
                                                                                    
          Scene III. Indian Marriage                                                
                                                                                    
          išá·k hiláy tanúk kéat                                                    
                                                                                    
           išá·k hiláy cí·k kén̥ hacéheš                                            
                                                                                    
            palnàl hiláy wáši·k pámnimá·t                                           
                                                                                    
              hiláy tánik pámat                                                     
                                                                                    
               palnál hiláy wáši·kiš pámnima·t                                      
                                                                                    
               yil la·t himatólot tà·ttihin̥tat                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                 ha išá·t pámlíkš mo·n                                              
                                                                                    
                kawkáw ámn̥ ánike n̥táhn̥tat                                        
                                                                                    
          hakit hokišá·k hokyálul áwš                                               
                                                                                    
           hišn̥cé·t kišé·t wé·t hokyálul hinák                                     
                                                                                    
          A man had only one wife.                                                  
                                                                                    
           If a man has two wives, it is a bad thing.                               
                                                                                    
          Palnal’s older wife beat him to death.                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         237                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           Another wife beat him.                                                   
                                                                                    
            Palnal’s older wife beat him to death.                                  
                                                                                    
              He kept on living for three to four days.                             
                                                                                    
               His head was all beaten and crushed,                                 
                                                                                    
               and when he drank water, it kept on coming out of his ears.          
                                                                                    
          They are not able to take their cousins in marriage.                      
                                                                                    
           It would be as if a woman’s brother married a man’s sister or a woman’s sister.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Scene IV. Daily Life and Religion                                         
                                                                                    
          wóšiŋ ahinákat                                                            
                                                                                    
           kéšn̥ šakyól té·š mo·n šakmaŋmáŋat                                       
                                                                                    
           šakyò·l katnáw šakháhš                                                   
                                                                                    
          hatyúlšo n̥hóhik                                                          
                                                                                    
           šakhatkópšen hatmé·lšo                                                   
                                                                                    
           hakit išá·k káw[ot] hatmé·lšo                                            
                                                                                    
          púmuƛ náw hakìt išá·tip hatnáwn̥st hathícam                               
                                                                                    
           <púmuƛ> hakít šìŋšnanik                                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           ikpúmuƛ hakít nàkšnan                                                    
                                                                                    
          It was as if they were naked.                                             
                                                                                    
           Women’s and men’s hair was all long.                                     
                                                                                    
           Men do not have beards.                                                  
                                                                                    
          They mark themselves with red face paint.                                 
                                                                                    
           When they paint themselves white, they paint themselves black.           
                                                                                    
           They paint themselves black for their dead relative.                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         238                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          They dance, and they customarily put feathers together on their heads.    
                                                                                    
           They dance with their rattles.                                           
                                                                                    
           They dance with their musical instruments.

                                         247                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [hakīt icáˊk káˊ-u hatmēˊlco]                                        
                                                                                    
          34)  hakit išá·k káw[ot] hatmé·lšo                                        
                                                                                    
               hakit iša·k    kaw-ot    hat-me·l-š-o·                               
                                                                                    
               their person   dead-DIR  REFLEX-be.black-CAUS-PHR.TERM               
                                                                                    
               ‘They paint themselves black for their dead relative.’               
                                                                                    
               [puˊmudl naˊ-u haˊkit icaˊtip hatnaˊ-inst? hatiˊdsom]                
                                                                                    
          35)  púmuƛ náw hakìt išá·tip hatnáwšn̥st hathícam                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               pum-ul-h       naw       hakit iša·t-ip hat-nawš-n̥št                
                                                                                    
               dance-3PLS-TOP feather   they head-INESS REFLEX-put.in-CUSTOM        
                                                                                    
               hat-hicam                                                            
                                                                                    
               REFLEX-join.together                                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘They dance, and they customarily put feathers together on their heads.’
                                                                                    
               [<puˊmudl> hakiˊt ciñcnăˊni]                                         
                                                                                    
          36)  <púmuƛ> hakít šìŋšnanik                                              
                                                                                    
               pum-ul-h       hakit šiŋšnan-ik                                      
                                                                                    
               dance-3PLS-TOP their rattle-ERG.INSTR                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘They dance with their rattles.’                                     
                                                                                    
               [tikpuˊmudl hakiˊt naxcnăˊn]                                         
                                                                                    
          37)  ikpúmuƛ hakít nàkšnan                                                
                                                                                    
               ik-pum-ul-h         hakit nakšnan                                    
                                                                                    
               INSTR-dance-3PLS-TOP their musical.instruments                       
                                                                                    
               ‘They dance with their musical instruments.’                         
                                                                                    
          Addition by Tottokš:                                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         248                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Loˊ yukiˊti haˊl cox̥ koˊyit yuk’hit cakicaˊk ut]                   
                                                                                    
          14a) ló yukhíti halšokóyat yukhitšakišá·kot                               
                                                                                    
               lo   yukhiti   hal-šokoy-ø-at      yukhit-šak-iša·k-ot               
                                                                                    
               PN   Indian    be.last-be.chief-3SS-PRET Indian-PL-person-DIR        
                                                                                    
               ‘Lo was the last Indian chief pertaining to the Indian people.       
                                                                                    
               [Ló hilá-i yuk’híti winé-ulat]                                       
                                                                                    
          14b) ló hiláy yukhíti winéulat                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               lo   hilay yukhiti  wine-ul-at                                       
                                                                                    
               PN   wife Indian    find-3PLS-PRET                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘The Indians found Lo’s wife.’                                       
                                                                                    
               [Há cakicāk Hiyékiti cakióñculat]                                    
                                                                                    
          14c) ha šakišá·k hiyékiti šakyóŋšulat                                     
                                                                                    
               ha   šak-iša·k hiyekiti  šak-yoŋš-ul-at                              
                                                                                    
               her  PL-person sunrise.people 3PLO-call.by.name.-3PLS-PRET           
                                                                                    
               ‘They called her relatives the Sunrise People.’                      
                                                                                    
               [Kaukaú káckin winé-ulat]                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          14d) kawkáwkáškin winéulat                                                
                                                                                    
               kawkaw-kaš-kin      wine-ul-at                                       
                                                                                    
               water-be.high.-WHEN find-3PLS-PRET                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘They found her when the tide was high.’                             
                                                                                    
               [Hiyeˊkiti cakioˊñculat]                                             
                                                                                    
          14e) hiyékiti šakyóŋšulat                                                 
                                                                                    
               hiyekiti       šak-yoŋš-ul-at                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         249                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               sunrise.people 3PLO-call.by.name.-3PLS-PRET                          
                                                                                    
               ‘They called them the Sunrise People.’                               
                                                                                    
               [nuˊnkin toˊhulat kakauˊ iyeˊtsnē ûˊt]                               
                                                                                    
          14f) núŋkin tóhulat kakáw iyécneot                                        
                                                                                    
               nuŋ-kin   toh-ul-at      kakaw     iyec-ne-ot                        
                                                                                    
               village-LOC dwell-3PLS-PRET sun    rise-GER-DIR                      
                                                                                    
               ‘They lived in villages towards where the sun rises.’                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Commentary. Line 1. Difficulties with this text start at the very beginning. Swanton translated
                                                                                    
          á né·p as “here below (in this bend)’ and ‘below this place’ and relate it to the verb né·p ‘to be
                                                                                    
          low.’ However, the verb né·p only refers to lowness in elevation, not to position. Actually, the
                                                                                    
          text form né·p is the inessive case form of né· ‘land,’ < né·ip (cf. pó·p < póh-ip ‘by steam’) and
                                                                                    
          á is the deictic, not a locational noun, which in any case is áp. The clincher is that the interlinear
                                                                                    
          gloss Gatschet gives in his field notes for this phrase is “in this land.”
                                                                                    
          Line 3. From here on are foodstuffs mentioned in sets of four. They originally started in groups
                                                                                    
          of three, but when Gatschet went over the text with Kišyuc, she inserted items to make them
                                                                                    
          groups of four. The list of foodstuffs starts with planted trees. The next four are agricultural
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          plants. The next four are hunted animals and large birds, the next four are fish, the next groups
                                                                                    
          are smaller birds and mammals, the next group splits, with two going with the preceding group
                                                                                    
          of birds and mammals, and the other two going with the next two groups of gathered plant foods.
                                                                                    
          In total, there are eight groups of four foodstuffs mentioned.            
                                                                                    
          Only in this text does the verb híh ‘to plant’ occur. Elsewhere in Gatschet’s field notes the verb
                                                                                    
          ‘to plant’ is given as híš, but the writing in the text is absolutely clear. The variation provided by
                                                                                    
          Swanton, [hiculat] (híšulat), does not occur in Gatschet’s field notes.   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         250                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 5. In the Gatschet and Swanton publication [Yaˊ-ins(o)] is written for the final verb, as if it
                                                                                    
          were a sentence of its own. In Gatschet’s notes, {yá-ins} is written below the line with a carat
                                                                                    
          inserting it into the sentence above. Later, he went over the text with Tottokš and inserted the o
                                                                                    
          in parentheses, meaning that both forms were correct here. Intervocalic h’s are often not
                                                                                    
          recorded in Gatschet’s notes, but comparison with other forms of the verb ‘to eat’ show the root
                                                                                    
          is yah. The suffix is the habitual suffix –ins, and translated in combination with the previous verb
                                                                                    
          is ‘they planted them, and they habitually eat them.’                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 6. The bird name nohámš ayip ‘chicken within the swamp’is glossed dinde farouche,
                                                                                    
          which in French means ‘wild turkey,’ and Gatschet translated it ‘turkey.’ However, Jeffery
                                                                                    
          Darensbourg of the Atakpa-Ishak tribe pointed out that the turkey is not native to the
                                                                                    
          environment in which the Atakapa used to live, and that given the congruence with the Louisiana
                                                                                    
          French term for the gallinule, poule d’eau de marais ‘chicken of the water of the swamp,’ that
                                                                                    
          the Atakpa term actually refers to the gallinule.                         
                                                                                    
          Line 9. The word akipcó·k ‘anhinga’ is an archaic compound, a(·)k-ip có·k ‘blackbird found
                                                                                    
          within the water.’ The word á·k at present does not mean water, but ‘liquid,’ perhaps even ‘thick
                                                                                    
          liquid’ cf. miŋá·k ‘honey’ (míŋ ‘bee’) and néškolaˑk ‘molasses’ (néškol ‘sugar’). The word at the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          end of this line, konán áyip, ‘duck potato’ fits the pattern of inessive-case formed compound
                                                                                    
          nouns discussed above: ‘the potato that is found within the swamp.’       
                                                                                    
          Line 11. In the original dictation, Kišyuc repeated konán ólo·l, ‘sweet potatoes,’ which she had
                                                                                    
          already mentioned in line 5. When Gatschet went over the text with Tottokš, either she or he
                                                                                    
          noticed the duplication, and inserted kúlšwálš, ‘peanuts,’ in its place. This change is not noted in
                                                                                    
          the publication.                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         251                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 12. Here starts a section about the political organization of the Atakapa. The word šokíti,
                                                                                    
          which seems to be from šok ‘things’ and íti ‘in front of’ seems to mean ‘headmen’ as opposed to
                                                                                    
          the head chief mentioned next.                                            
                                                                                    
          Line 13. This line is difficult, not so much in its meaning, which is clear, but in the Atakapa,
                                                                                    
          which seems to contain an unnoticed correction by Kišyuc. It is written in the field notes:
                                                                                    
          {shokíti ó-i tánuk mōn shokiyá-i ṓ́tsi tané-uts}. For the first word Kišyuc said šokíti ‘headman,’
                                                                                    
          then changed it to šokóy ‘chief.’ Comparison with a gloss added by Gatschet and given by
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Tottokš, and not included anywhere in the publication7, shows that iyáhi is a ditransitive stative
                                                                                    
          verb which means ‘to be ahead of someone in regard to something.’8 The sequence ṓtsi tané-uts
                                                                                    
          is not clear, unless one surmises that Kišyuc said šakiyáhi óci…tannéw óci meaning to be
                                                                                    
          understood as having said šakiyáhi tannéw óci, as óci is a postposition, and should be following
                                                                                    
          the noun it governs, not preceding it as here. Gatschet’s gloss is chef une beaucoup haut au
                                                                                    
          dessus les autres tous (one chief much higher above all the others).      
                                                                                    
          Lines 14-17. The description of Lo and his wife was clearly too compressed for Tottokš when
                                                                                    
          the text was read to her, so she dictated the following lines to replace them, which are found on
                                                                                    
          page 147 of Gatschet’s field notes:                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ló yukhíti halšokóyat yukhitšakišá·kot Lo was the last chief pertaining to the Indians
                                                                                    
          ló hiláy yukhíti winéulat     The Indians found Lo’s wife                 
                                                                                    
          ha šakišá·k hiyékiti šakyóŋšulat They called her people the Sunrise people
                                                                                    
          kawkáwkáškin winéulat         They found her when the tide was high       
                                                                                    
          hiyékiti šakyóŋšulat          They called them Sunrise people             
                                                                                    
          núŋkin tóhulat kakáw iyécneot They lived in villages towards the sunrise. 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         252                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Here Tottokš makes more explicit the relationship between the ethnic name hiyékiti, which
                                                                                    
          Kišyuc treated as the word ‘sunrise,’ with the verb iyéc ‘to rise, of the sun.’ The story of Lo’s
                                                                                    
          wife Fanchonette9 is intriguing. She was found as a child by the Atakapa at high tide; does that
                                                                                    
          mean she was a passenger on a canoe which sank, and she swam to shore? On page 51 of
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes, Kišyuc links Fanchonette’s people to the dialect spoken in the east; two of
                                                                                    
          the words recalled, hápalšt ‘two’ and kám ‘fire’ are recorded in Duralde’s 1803 vocabulary as
                                                                                    
          happalst and cam (Gallatin, 1836:307-67); thus, the Hiyékiti are definitely the Eastern Atakapa.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 14. This is a difficult line, made more difficult by Swanton’s uncritical inclusion of some
                                                                                    
          of Gatschet’s emendations, and omission of others. Gatschet put numbers beneath the first three
                                                                                    
          words to show their proper order, but these were ignored. In the second half of the phrase it
                                                                                    
          seems that Kišyuc said (Gatschet 1885:98): {yuk’híts(i)shakishákput} phonetically
                                                                                    
          /yukhitšakiša·kput/. Gatschet inserted and questioned the i in parentheses10 on his first review of
                                                                                    
          the text with Kišyuc, then on his second review with Tottokš added in red an i between the k and
                                                                                    
          p, then inserted above them -kip út and later again wrote a slash in pencil between the -kip and
                                                                                    
          út, and put a question mark by the -kip. Kišyuc used the unusual compound yukhit-šakiša·k
                                                                                    
          ‘Indian people, Atakapa people,’ then started to put the phrase in the inessive case with -ip in its
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          secondary usage, then changed her mind and thought the directional case would be better, and
                                                                                    
          thus said -ot. This is clearly what Tottokš understood, for in her retelling of this line, she uses
                                                                                    
          yukhit-šakiša·kot putting the compound noun in the directional case. Thus, there is no
                                                                                    
          justification for the published form [cakicaˊkip ut].                     
                                                                                    
          Line 15. Under the gloss for winéulat, ‘they found her,’ Gatschet added ‘(as a child) and further
                                                                                    
          below added ({kā́shki} swelling of the sea), which he clearly intended to be the final word of the
                                                                                    
          sentence. This was missed by Swanton.                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         253                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Lines 18-19. This is a discussion of religion. Here the deity is called Ucutá·t, while in the
                                                                                    
          dictionary the name is given as Ocotá·t, the writing of the former showing pre- and post-
                                                                                    
          accentual weakening. The name breaks down into oc-ot, the directional case form of the
                                                                                    
          postposition oc ‘above,’ plus the verb stem ta·t ‘to stand (sg).’ This epithet suggests that Ocota·t
                                                                                    
          was the sun, and female, as the sun was so considered throughout the Southeastern area.
                                                                                    
           The dances called here šé·š púm and wašwáši púm ‘the infants’ dance’ and ‘the elders’ dance’
                                                                                    
          are given different names by Kišyuc on page 41 of Gatschet’s field notes where they are called
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          {shakwáshi shokāk} šakwáši šoká·k ‘the old men’s ceremonial dance’ and {shókak shókshuks}
                                                                                    
          šokà·k šókšukš ‘young people’s ceremonial dance.’ Kišyuc seems to have treated the terms púm
                                                                                    
          and šoká·k as being relatively equivalent to each other, whereas Tottokš insisted on the
                                                                                    
          distinction, with púm being a secular dance, and šoká·k being a ceremonial dance.
                                                                                    
          Lines 20-28. This is a discussion of marriage rules. The dictum that a man was to have one wife
                                                                                    
          is given, then the example of Palnal is given as to why having more than one wife was bad. The
                                                                                    
          discussion continues with the prohibition against cousins marrying.       
                                                                                    
          Lines 22-24. There are some strange features about these lines as presented. The transitive
                                                                                    
          verb pam ‘to beat something’ is used in all three lines, and the object of the verb is human, but
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          seemingly only in the second line is the subject noun in the ergative case. What we have here
                                                                                    
          seems to be a case of hypercorrection and missegmentation. In line 47, in the sequence /waši·k
                                                                                    
          pamnima·t/ Gatschet scratched out the k of the ergative suffix that he had originally written. In
                                                                                    
          line 49, the word waši·kiš was segmented into the two words waši ‘old’ and kiš ‘woman’ and
                                                                                    
          translated as ‘old woman.’ This is incorrect, not only because the adjective here is made to
                                                                                    
          precede the noun, which is against the rules of Atakapa grammar, but also the language has an
                                                                                    
          idiomatic expression for ‘old woman,’ kiš kop ‘white (-haired) woman.’ The word waši·kiš is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         254                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          segmented correctly as waši-ik-iš ‘old-ERG.AGNT-FOC,’ the focus form of the noun phrase
                                                                                    
          indicating that while another of his wives beat him, it was the older wife who beat him to death.
                                                                                    
          Line 25. There is a mishearing in this line, as well as a mistranslation. In the sequence yíl lá·t
                                                                                    
          himatólot tà·ttihin̥tat, the two sequences of double t’s are not written. So instead of himatólot
                                                                                    
          being understood as the directional case form of the numeral (Atakapa numerals being nouns)
                                                                                    
          himatól ‘four’ with vowel weakening, it was written [himatoˊl-u] and a unique and spurious
                                                                                    
          morpheme *-u ‘or’ was created. The verb tà·ttihin̥tat does not mean ‘lie on the ground.’ The root
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          is ta·t, which means ‘to stand (sg).’ Clearly, Palnal, with his head beaten in, was not able to
                                                                                    
          stand, so the verb here seems to mean ‘stayed,’ suggesting that he lived three or four days after
                                                                                    
          he was beaten, not that his body lay on the ground for that time, and the following line about the
                                                                                    
          water he drank running out his ears makes more sense, if he were still able to drink after the
                                                                                    
          beating. Thus, instead of the translation, “When Palnal’s older wife beat him to death his body
                                                                                    
          lay on the ground three or four days with the head mashed in. The water he had drunk ran out of
                                                                                    
          his ears,” we have:                                                       
                                                                                    
          “Palnal’s older wife beat him to death.                                   
                                                                                    
          He kept on living for three or four days;                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          his head was all beaten and mashed;                                       
                                                                                    
          the water he drank kept on running out of his ears.”                      
                                                                                    
          Furthermore, reading Text 8 about burial customs, we find that a dead person was buried the day
                                                                                    
          after death. It seems highly unlikely that Palnal’s dead body was undiscovered for three or four
                                                                                    
          days; in a small village society everyone must have known about what happened, and he would
                                                                                    
          have been buried immediately. The fact of his not dying was of importance, and why the amount
                                                                                    
          of time it lasted was remembered.                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         255                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Lines 27-28. These lines are very difficult, again because of Swanton tacitly including
                                                                                    
          emendations in the published text, and not following Gatschet’s word order notes. The word
                                                                                    
          hokiša·k is used to mean ‘cousins,’ according to Gatschet’s marginal note, to which Swanton did
                                                                                    
          not refer. Just as šakiša·k means ‘relatives on both sides of the family,’ hokiša·k means ‘cousins
                                                                                    
          on both sides of the family.’ The prefix hok- is the reciprocal prefix, so this word indicates
                                                                                    
          people with relationships with one another. Line 28 only makes sense if it is read as follows:
                                                                                    
          hišn̥cé·t wé·t [hokyálul] ahinák                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          kišé·t hokyálul hinák.                                                    
                                                                                    
          it is as if women’s brothers and women’s sisters marry each other         
                                                                                    
          it is as if they [men] married with men’s sisters                         
                                                                                    
          This is not at all what Gatschet intended. Line 28 was dictated by Kišyúc with no other verb but
                                                                                    
          ahinák ‘to be like; to be so:’ {hish’ntsēt wet ahinák kîshēt}. When Gatschet went back over the
                                                                                    
          text with Kišyuc, he added subscript numerals to show the correct word order: {hish’ntsēt kîshēt
                                                                                    
          wet ahinák}. Finally, he went over the text with Tottokš, added a macron to {wēt} and the
                                                                                    
          words {okiálul ināk} in red. Clearly Gatschet intended for {okiálul ināk} to replace {ahinák},
                                                                                    
          thus producing the clear and grammatical sentence: {hish’ntsēt kîshēt wet okiálul ināk} hišn̥cé·t
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          kišé·t wé·t hokyálul hinák ‘It would be as if a woman’s brother married a man’s sister or a
                                                                                    
          woman’s sister.’                                                          
                                                                                    
           The languages of the Southeast make elaborate distinctions in terms for brothers and sisters
                                                                                    
          based on sex and age of speaker. Here the term hišn̥ce·t which is glossed in the dictionary only
                                                                                    
          as ‘brother’ is paired with we·t ‘woman’s sister,’ and given that the narrator was a woman,
                                                                                    
          hišn̥ce·t must mean ‘woman’s brother.’ There is no term paired with kiše·t ‘man’s sister,’ but it is
                                                                                    
          tempting to see the term n̥ce·t, also glossed ‘brother’ as actually meaning ‘man’s brother.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         256                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Lines 32-37. These five lines are probably the most difficult of the text. The word order is
                                                                                    
          different from the usual, and there are rare forms and suffixes. Notable is the presence of the
                                                                                    
          suffix -o. This cannot be the first-person singular subject marker for the aorist, for the context
                                                                                    
          requires the subjects of the verbs to be third-person plural. Rather, this seems to be the phrase-
                                                                                    
          terminal marker -o·, as discussed above in Chapter 2.                     
                                                                                    
          Line 33. In the phrase šakhatkópšn̥ hatmé·lšo, šak- is being treated as a third-person plural
                                                                                    
          stative subject marker, and the suffix -n̥ indicates that the action of the first verb precedes and
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          accompanies the action of the second verb, thus the line can be translated ‘when they paint
                                                                                    
          themselves white, they paint themselves black.’ This implies when the Atakapa painted
                                                                                    
          themselves, they used the two colors at the same time.                    
                                                                                    
          Line 34. In the phrase hakít iša·k káw hatmé·lšo, the elements hakít iša·k káw clearly mean ‘their
                                                                                    
          dead person,’ but hatmé·lšo means ‘they paint themselves black.’ Now, the dead person is not
                                                                                    
          painting herself or himself, so the phrase must be a benefactive, and should have the directional
                                                                                    
          case suffix -ot/-ut. It is quite possible that this element was missed in taking down the dictation,
                                                                                    
          so it is restored to make the sense clear: ‘they painted themselves black for their dead relatives.’
                                                                                    
          Line 35. Fronting the verb seems to be done to give it prominence. The change of the phoneme
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          is reminiscent of how Natchez verbs that end in resonant consonants surface with voiceless
                                                                                    
          resonants as the result of the connective suffix -k devoicing them, and then being dropped
                                                                                    
          (Kimball, 2005:399). It is tempting to see ƛ here as an archaism, given that ƛ does not occur in
                                                                                    
          word-final position, and where ƛ occurs in final position in other dialects, l is found in this
                                                                                    
          dialect. However, there are other examples in this text of final or medial -ul, where there is no
                                                                                    
          change, which suggests the fronting of the verb to the beginning of the sentence and the change
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         257                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          of the final consonant are linked. I shall postulate here a suffix -h, ‘topicalizer,’ with the caveat
                                                                                    
          that this morpheme is by no means confirmed.                              
                                                                                    
           The word questioned in this line, [hatnaˊ-inst?], has a complex history. Gatschet first wrote
                                                                                    
          {hatnaˊ-inst}, then went back and overwrote the i with an sh. Then, when he went back over it
                                                                                    
          with Tottokš, she could not understand what he was saying, and so he added three question
                                                                                    
          marks in red over the word. It seems that Kišyuc had said náw hakít išá·tip hatináwšn̥st
                                                                                    
          hathícam and that Gatschet misheard the complex phoneme cluster wšn̥st. The meaning of the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          sentence is ‘They customarily put feathers together on their heads.’      
                                                                                    
           The final word of the line, written {hatídsom} and glossed ‘they placed,’ does not make any
                                                                                    
          sense. The word is listed in the dictionary with the meaning ‘little things placed on themselves’
                                                                                    
          This is a mistake, as the reflexive prefix hat- is never used with intransitive verbs. Elsewhere in
                                                                                    
          the dictionary is the verb hicám meaning ‘to join together,’ which is the proper transcription and
                                                                                    
          translation of the word used here.                                        
                                                                                    
          Lines 36-37. These lines are very difficult as well. When they were read to Tottokš, she seems
                                                                                    
          not to have understood them well, for she turned the verb into a locational phrase. The problem
                                                                                    
          here seems to be a combination of compression on the part of the speaker, and mishearing on the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          part of the transcriber. Line 36 makes sense if one repeats the verb púmuƛ from the previous
                                                                                    
          line, and realize that the sequence of words written {shingshnä́ni tikpúmudl} was pronounced
                                                                                    
          /šiŋšnánikikpúmuƛ/, that is šiŋšnán-ik, ik-púmuƛ, and that the sequence [kik] was misheard as
                                                                                    
          [tik]11 and mistaken for the prefix tik- ‘to go and do something.’ This slight change allows for a
                                                                                    
          clear translation of these two lines. Instead of “sounding a rattle at the dancing place” (1932:11)
                                                                                    
          we have, “They danced with their rattles; they danced with their musical instruments.” The
                                                                                    
          variant form {tikpū́mst} provided by Kišyuc for {tikpúmudl} (with the initial consonant wrongly
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         258                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          copied) is ikpúmn̥st, ik-pum-n̥st ‘they habitually dance with them.’12 The form for the last word
                                                                                    
          that Tottokš supplied is {tikpum nékin}, which seems to be /tikpúm né·kin/ ‘they go and dance
                                                                                    
          on the ground,’ and has no semantic connection with the following words /hakít nakšnán/ ‘their
                                                                                    
          musical instruments.’ The nakšnán is glossed in the dictionary as ‘a musical instrument like a
                                                                                    
          xylophone, from 2 to 3 feet long, used in accompanying dances.’ (1932:84) 
                                                                                    
          Text 2. The Skin-desirer. Found on page 100 of Gatschet’s field notes, this text is a bit of
                                                                                    
          Atakapa folklore. Gatschet did not give it a title, but underlined the first word, which was his
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          usual way further on in his field notes to indicate a title. Swanton used that word as the title and
                                                                                    
          gave it the heading “Caktaˊlko (Human-skin Desirer).” While the Skin-Desirer seems to be a
                                                                                    
          monster unique to the Atakapa, various kinds of unpleasant beings are found in the oral
                                                                                    
          literatures of the peoples of the Southeast, such as the Long-Nosed Monster of the Chitimacha
                                                                                    
          (neka šiš kˀamin), the Clawed Witch of the Tunica (tínikówi), or the Koasati Eater-of-the-Living
                                                                                    
          (okcayyaí:pa). There is a short discussion of this being on page 43 of Gatschet’s field notes; an
                                                                                    
          insertion when going over these notes with Tottokš led to the creation of the spurious variant
                                                                                    
          form [*icakctaˊlko] which occurs in the dictionary portion of the publication. Gatschet inserted
                                                                                    
          /sh/ between the elements {shak} and {tálko} to indicate the variant form štálkoh, which is
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          formed by the regular, if rare, rule of initial syllable syncope; the initial i is placed in
                                                                                    
          parentheses, as if Gatschet thought the etymology were from išá·k tál kóh ‘he desires a man’s
                                                                                    
          skin’ rather than the more likely šaktál kóh ‘he desires skins.’ This text is structured entirely with
                                                                                    
          one pair of lines and two extended couplets, and is the closest thing to an Atakapa traditional
                                                                                    
          narrative that has been preserved.                                        
                                                                                    
          šaktálkoh yukhíti iša·k káw ha tál kóhat                                  
                                                                                    
            hinák šakyóŋšulat.
