                                         259                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          išá·k išat hán húulat                                                     
                                                                                    
            póhol cá·tn̥ húulat                                                     
                                                                                    
             ha išá·t ha úš mo·n náwšim                                             
                                                                                    
          kiwílš hìkulat tówn̥táwm̥                                                 
                                                                                    
            néš išó·mškin yukhíti túlip lóhkin iwá·tn̥ hún̥st                       
                                                                                    
             yíl iwá·tn̥.                                                           
                                                                                    
          The Skin-desirer wanted the skin of a dead Indian;                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           for this reason they named it so.                                        
                                                                                    
          They saw it as not having a human head.                                   
                                                                                    
           Afterwards they saw that it was lowered.                                 
                                                                                    
            His head and his whole body were nothing but hair.                      
                                                                                    
          White people have caught sight of it sometimes.                           
                                                                                    
           They customarily see it in the brush around Lake Prien,                  
                                                                                    
            when it moves towards them in the prairie,                              
                                                                                    
            when it moves towards them by day.                                      
                                                                                    
               [Caktaˊlko Yuk’hiˊti icaˊk kaˊ-u haˊtalkoˊat hinaˊk cakioˊñculĕt]    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1)   šaktálkoh yukhíti išá·k káw ha tàl kóhat hinák šakyóŋšulat           
                                                                                    
               šak-tal-koh    yukhiti iša·k  kaw  ha   tal  ø-koh-at                
                                                                                    
               pl-skin-wanter Indian person  dead his  skin 3STATS-want-PRET        
                                                                                    
               hinak     šak-yoŋš-ul-at                                             
                                                                                    
               be.like   3PLO-call-3PLO-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘The Skin-desirer wanted the skin of a dead Indian; for this reason they named it so.’
                                                                                    
               [Icāˊk icaˊt haˊn huˊ-ulat]                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         260                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          2)   išá·k išá·t há·n húulat                                              
                                                                                    
               iša·k     iša·t ha·n          hu-ul-at                               
                                                                                    
               person    head not.to.have(GER) see-3PLS-PRET                        
                                                                                    
               ‘They saw it as not having a human head.’                            
                                                                                    
               [poˊ-ol tsāˊt’n huˊulat]                                             
                                                                                    
          3)   póhol cá·tn̥ húulat                                                  
                                                                                    
               pohol     ca·tn̥    hu-ul-at                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               lowered   afterwards see-3PLS-PRET                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘Afterwards they saw that it was lowered.’                           
                                                                                    
               [ha icāˊt ha uˊc mōn naˊ-ucĕm]                                       
                                                                                    
          4)   ha išá·t ha úš mo·n náwšim                                           
                                                                                    
               ha   iša·t ha  uš   mo·n ø-naw-šim                                   
                                                                                    
               his  head his  body all  3STATS-hair-NOTHING.BUT                     
                                                                                    
               ‘His head and his whole body were nothing but hair.’                 
                                                                                    
               [Kiwilc hikuˊlat toˊ’ntaˊvm]                                         
                                                                                    
          5)   kiwílš hìkulat tówn̥táwm̥                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               kiwilš         hik-ul-at           town̥tawm̥                        
                                                                                    
               white.people   catch.sight-3PLS-PRET sometimes                       
                                                                                    
               ‘White people have caught sight of it sometimes.’                    
                                                                                    
               [nec hicoˊmckin yuk’hiˊti tulip lōˊx̥ kin iwāˊt’n hūˊnst yiˊl iwāˊt’n]
                                                                                    
          6)   neš išó·mškin yukhíti túlip lókkin iwá·tn̥ hún̥st yíl iwá·tn̥        
                                                                                    
               neš  išo·mš-kin yukhiti  tul-ip    lok-kin                           
                                                                                    
               tree small-LOC Indian    lake-INESS prairie-LOC                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         261                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ø-iwa·t-n̥          hu-n̥št   yil       ø-iwa·t-n̥                   
                                                                                    
               3STATS-move.hither-WHEN see-CUSTOM by.day 3STATS-move.hither-WHEN    
                                                                                    
               ‘They customarily see it in the brush around Lake Prien (Indian Lake), when it
                                                                                    
               moves towards them in the prairie, when it moves towards them by day.’
                                                                                    
          Commentary.                                                               
                                                                                    
          Line 3. This text has more than its share of hapax legomena; both póhol ‘lowered’ and cá·tn,
                                                                                    
          ‘afterwards’ occur only here in the corpus.                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 6. This sentence has a nice contrast in the usage of the locative and inessive cases. The
                                                                                    
          Skin-desirer is in the brush and in the prairie, a specific location, and thus the locative case, but
                                                                                    
          only in the general vicinity of Lake Prien, thus the inessive case.       
                                                                                    
           The verb iwá·t is not listed as a separate entry in Swanton’s dictionary. Confusingly, the
                                                                                    
          example sentences are listed under o, apparently because it is glossed ‘come’ by Gatschet
                                                                                    
          (Swanton decided that the “root” of the verb ‘to come’ was o, even though it is always ók). The
                                                                                    
          example sentences, however, suggest that the meaning of iwá·t is ‘move in the direction of the
                                                                                    
          speaker.’ This makes the story of the Skin-Desirer even more creepy as it suggests that the
                                                                                    
          monster perceives the white people looking at it, and starts to move in their direction, apparently
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          hoping to get their skins. It should be remembered that white people used to be called ‘dead
                                                                                    
          people’ in Atakapa (išá·k šappíhk), presumably from the color of their skins, and the Skin-
                                                                                    
          Desirer wants the skin of a dead person.                                  
                                                                                    
          Text 3. Treatment of the heads of infants. This short ethnographic text is found on page 112 of
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s field notes; the emendations given by Tottokš are found on page 147. He gave it the
                                                                                    
          title “Children’s Ears and Faces.” It seems to have been a response to a question about whether
                                                                                    
          the Atakapa ever flattened the skulls of their infants, which seems to have been still a practice of
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         262                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          the Choctaw, who in 1885 lived further up the Calcasieu River. Earlier in Gatschet’s fieldnotes,
                                                                                    
          on page 94, there is a short discussion in English about the topic; this text is an elaboration of it.
                                                                                    
          This is the last text that was dictated by Kišyuc. Interestingly enough, exactly like Text 2, it is
                                                                                    
          composed of an initial pair of lines, followed by two extended couplets.  
                                                                                    
          yukhíti·š nómš iša·tkin páltitháh.                                        
                                                                                    
           cáhta ahinák iša·tkin šappalšatháh                                       
                                                                                    
          hoké·t hakít úc šakitólšn̥st                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           án šakitólšn̥st                                                          
                                                                                    
            ahinákka úc šakiwíln̥st                                                 
                                                                                    
          itiyílš tanúk šakiwíln̥st                                                 
                                                                                    
           án šakitólšn̥st tanúkip yílkit mo·n                                      
                                                                                    
           hakít án kimátip né ócot án šakitólšn̥st                                 
                                                                                    
          Tottokš variations:                                                       
                                                                                    
          Line 2: cahtá·š hakít nómš išá·t šakpalpálišat                            
                                                                                    
          Line 6: hakít án kimátip né ócot án teltélšn̥st                           
                                                                                    
          Kišyuc version:                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          As for the Atakapa, the children do not have flat places on their heads.  
                                                                                    
           They did not make them flat on the heads like the Choctaw.               
                                                                                    
          The mothers customarily arranged their noses,                             
                                                                                    
           and they arranged their ears                                             
                                                                                    
            in how they customarily put these noses in order.                       
                                                                                    
          They customarily put them in order for one month.                         
                                                                                    
           They customarily unfold their ears once throughout the day,              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         263                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           They customarily unfold the inside of the ear from bottom to top.        
                                                                                    
          Tottokš version:                                                          
                                                                                    
          As for the Atakapa, the children do not have flat places on their heads.  
                                                                                    
           As for the Choctaw, they flattened the various heads of their children.  
                                                                                    
          The mothers customarily arranged their noses,                             
                                                                                    
           and they arranged their ears                                             
                                                                                    
            in how they customarily put these noses in order.                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          They customarily put them in order for one month.                         
                                                                                    
           They customarily unfold their ears once throughout the day,              
                                                                                    
           They customarily unfold the inside of their ears from bottom to top.     
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊtic noˊmc icāˊtkin paˊltit haˊ]                                
                                                                                    
          1)   yukhíti·š nómš išá·tkin páltitháh.                                   
                                                                                    
               yukhiti-iš nomš     iša·t-kin ø-pal-tit-hah.                         
                                                                                    
               Indian-FOC child    head-LOC  3STATS-be.flat-PROG(PL)-NEG            
                                                                                    
               ‘As for the Atakapa, the children do not have flat places on their heads.’
                                                                                    
               [Tsaˊxtaˊ aihināˊk icātkiñ capaˊłcitaˊ-ha]                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          2)   cáhta ahinák išá·tkin šappalšatháh                                   
                                                                                    
               cahta     ahinak    iša·t-kin šak-pal-š-at-háh                       
                                                                                    
               Choctaw   be.like   head-LOC  3PLSTATS-be.flat-CAUS-PRET-NEG.        
                                                                                    
               ‘They did not make them flat on the heads like the Choctaw.’         
                                                                                    
               [Hukēˊt hakīˊt uˊts cakitoˊlcĕnst aˊn cakitoˊlcĕnst ahenaˊka uˊts cakiwiˊlĕnst]
                                                                                    
          3)   hoké·t hakít úc šakitólšn̥st án šakitólšn̥st ahinákka úc šakiwíln̥st 
                                                                                    
               hoke·t hakit uc šak-itolš-n̥st     an   šak-itolš-n̥st               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         264                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               mother their nose 3PLO-arrange-CUSTOM ear 3PLO-arrange-CUSTOM        
                                                                                    
               ahinakka  uc   šak-iwil-n̥st                                         
                                                                                    
               like      nose 3PLO-put.in.order-CUSTOM                              
                                                                                    
               ‘The mothers customarily arranged their noses, and they arranged their ears
                                                                                    
               like they customarily put their noses in order.’                     
                                                                                    
               [Itiyiˊlc tanuˊk cakiwiˊlĕnst]                                       
                                                                                    
          4)   itiyílš tanúk šakiwílnst                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               itiyilš   tanuk     šak-iwil-n̥st                                    
                                                                                    
               month     one       3PLO-put.in.order-CUSTOM                         
                                                                                    
               ‘They customarily put them in order for one month.’                  
                                                                                    
               [An cakteˊlcĕnst tanuˊkip yiˊlkit mōn]                               
                                                                                    
          5)   án šaktélšn̥st tanúkip yílkit mo·n                                   
                                                                                    
               an   šak-telš-n̥st       tanuk-ip  ø-yil-kit           mo·n          
                                                                                    
               ear  3PLO-unfold-CUSTOM  one-INESS 3STATS-be.day-PROG(SG) all        
                                                                                    
               ‘They customarily unfold their ears once throughout the day.’        
                                                                                    
               [hakīˊt an kimaˊtip neˊoˊts oˊt aˊn cakteˊlcnt]                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          6)   hakít án kimátip né ócot án šaktélšn̥st                              
                                                                                    
               hakit an  kimatip   ne        oc-ot     an   šak-telš-n̥st           
                                                                                    
               their ear inside    bottom    top-DIR   ear  3PLO-unfold-CUSTOM      
                                                                                    
               ‘They customarily unfold the inside of the ear from bottom to top.’  
                                                                                    
          Variations from Tottokš:                                                  
                                                                                    
               [Tsaxtaˊ hakīt noˊmc icaˊt cakpalpaˊlicat]                           
                                                                                    
          2a)  cahtá·š hakít nómš išá·t šakpalpálišat                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         265                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               cahta-iš       hakit nomš     iša·t šak-,pal,pal-iš-at               
                                                                                    
               Choctaw-FOC    their child    head 3PLO-,REDUP,be.flat-CAUS-PRET     
                                                                                    
               ‘The Choctaw flattened the various heads of their children.’         
                                                                                    
               [hakīˊt an kimaˊtip neˊoˊts oˊt aˊn telteˊlcnst]                     
                                                                                    
          6a)  hakít án kimátip né ócot án teltélšn̥st                              
                                                                                    
               hakit an  kimatip   ne        oc-ot     an                           
                                                                                    
               their ear inside    bottom    top-DIR   ear                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               tel,telš-n̥st                                                        
                                                                                    
               ,REDUP,-unfold-CUSTOM                                                
                                                                                    
               ‘They customarily unfold the inside of their ears from bottom to top.’
                                                                                    
          Commentary.                                                               
                                                                                    
          Line 1. Because the noun yukhíti is a focus form, it cannot be the possessor of nómš ‘child;
                                                                                    
          children.’ The verb pál ‘be flat’ is intransitive, so it is not a subject; the subject of the verb is
                                                                                    
          nómš. As the text is a comparion of customs between the Atakapa and Choctaw, the word
                                                                                    
          yukhítiš puts the focus first on the Atakapa, and so is best translated ‘As for the Atakapa.’
                                                                                    
          Line 2. The published form [capaˊłcitaˊ-ha] is an error on Swanton’s part. Gatschet originally
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wrote {shapá’lshitá}, and when he went back over the text with Tottokš, he wanted to make sure
                                                                                    
          that he understood that the element {á} was the negative, so he wrote {-há} in red after the word.
                                                                                    
          The phonetics of the word Kišyuc spoke seem to have been /šappál̥šətháh/; Gatschet did not
                                                                                    
          write the geminate p, the th cluster, or the word-final h, and heard the unstressed a as i, and
                                                                                    
          indicated the devoicing of l before a stop by the apostrophe before it. Totokš altered the sentence
                                                                                    
          so the subject referred to the Choctaw, and replaced the singular verb pálš, with the plural
                                                                                    
          palpáliš.                                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         266                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 2a. The sentence as published suggests that the noun cahtá, should be in the ergative case,
                                                                                    
          not the absolutive state. However, when Gatschet took the dictation, he originally wrote {Tsa’htá
                                                                                    
          shakīt} for the phrase in sandhi /cahtá·šhakít/, where the noun is in the focus form. As he never
                                                                                    
          understood the nature of the focus suffix, he went back and scratched out the {s}. However, the
                                                                                    
          change that Tottokš made to the second line makes the couplet more perfect:
                                                                                    
           yukhíti·š nómš iša·tkin páltitháh.                                       
                                                                                    
            cahtá·š hakít nómš išá·t šakpalpálišat                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           As for the Atakapa, the children do not have flat places on their heads. 
                                                                                    
            As for the Choctaw, they flattened the various heads of their children. 
                                                                                    
          Line 6a. Tottokš seems not to have liked a single-action verb referring to a plurality of actions,
                                                                                    
          so she replaced the verb télš ‘to unfold something’ with teltélš ‘to unfold numerous things.’
                                                                                    
          Kišyuc clearly felt that the third-person plural object prefix šak- was sufficient.
                                                                                    
          Text 4. Form Letter. Found on pages 154-157 of Gatschet’s field notes, this long and
                                                                                    
          linguistically interesting text is not a natural one. The first clue comes with the title. He first
                                                                                    
          gave it the title “Letter” in English, then two Atakapa titles, yúlš ‘writing’ and šakyúlš ‘writing
                                                                                    
          them,’ which he scratched out, finally choosing {Yúlsh Shakín ók} which Gatschet thought
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          meant ‘written invitation to come for a visit’ (Gatschet, 1885b:159). This title is written at the
                                                                                    
          top of page 154 in Swanton’s hand. However, cakín is a form of the verb ín ‘to ask’ with a third-
                                                                                    
          person plural object, and ók as it stands is not a gerund, so the phrase yúlš šakín ók means
                                                                                    
          ‘writing asks people and comes,’ which does not make much sense. It seems to have been a
                                                                                    
          practice of linguists in the last quarter of the 19th century, when they had difficulty eliciting a
                                                                                    
          natural text, to translate a letter into the language (cf. Biloxi; Dorsey and Swanton, 1912:22-23).
                                                                                    
          This is the first text dicated by Tottokš, which may be the reason why Gatschet used this format.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         267                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          This text divides into four sections, three of which appear to be relatively natural texts elicited by
                                                                                    
          a statement such as, ‘What would you say to someone who you wanted to come and visit?’ The
                                                                                    
          first half of the fourth section, however, looks to have been written by Gatschet, and translated
                                                                                    
          sentence by sentence, and not well-understood by Tottokš. The second half of the fourth section,
                                                                                    
          however, does appear less artificial.                                     
                                                                                    
          Scene I: Inquiries                                                        
                                                                                    
          yíl páhw n̥yáwta n̥ókne                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           aŋicón itólšo n̥ké·tne yukít áŋkin                                       
                                                                                    
          yúlš hióy,                                                                
                                                                                    
           ók núhc á?                                                               
                                                                                    
           n̥tánat n̥háh?                                                           
                                                                                    
          hatnáhka máŋ ké·t n̥ úhc á?                                               
                                                                                    
          I am to wait seven days for you to come,                                  
                                                                                    
           I have prepared a room for you to stay in at our house.                  
                                                                                    
          Send a writing to me!                                                     
                                                                                    
           Are you able to come?                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           Or are you not?                                                          
                                                                                    
            How long are you able to stay?                                          
                                                                                    
          Scene II: Visit plans                                                     
                                                                                    
          yíl mo·n táy ó hoktiwé·išwáŋehe                                           
                                                                                    
           n̥tán nešpaliconkin tóhiya                                               
                                                                                    
            lóhkin tiwšakn̥onš išwáŋehe                                             
                                                                                    
               n̥tán tukémš išwáŋehe                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         268                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          yáŋ tíwya náwohóh išyá·mšne                                               
                                                                                    
           áyip kunán kathópš camakúc šokmó·n išyá·mšehe                            
                                                                                    
            kicóŋškin iššakwáktikit

              yáhhatpénicel                                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          téŋš tanstálik išaŋkámšne                                                 
                                                                                    
           šoktól išké·tne                                                          
                                                                                    
          All day we might go about together beside the river,                      
                                                                                    
           or get in a buggy                                                        
                                                                                    
            and go visit people on the prairie,                                     
                                                                                    
            or we might go about rowing in a boat.                                  
                                                                                    
          We are to go there and gather chinquapins;                                
                                                                                    
           we may gather everything, duck potatoes, waterlilies, and crawfish.      
                                                                                    
            We will roast them in the fire.                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          We will catch with a fishing line catfish and perch, and we will catch trout
                                                                                    
           We will bring them to our home,                                          
                                                                                    
            fry many,                                                               
                                                                                    
            and eat enough.                                                         
                                                                                    
          We are to play cards in the evening,                                      
                                                                                    
           and we should have luck.                                                 
                                                                                    
          Scene III: Village Gossip                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         269

          We are not having a dance in our village this month.                      
                                                                                    
           The Indian, Išattokš, the old chief, died.

           A strong cold had her.

              he sucked out the blood.

          28)  n̥tól á n̥hatnáh á                                                   
                                                                                    
               n̥-tol         a    n̥-hatnah      a                                 
                                                                                    
               2SSTATS-be.good Q   2SSTATS-be.how Q                                 
                                                                                    
               ‘Are you well? How are you?’                                         
                                                                                    
          Commentary. Line 1. The word páhw ‘seven’ is written {pá’h} and {pá’hĕ} by Gatschet; only
                                                                                    
          the form páhw-ip ‘seven times’ {pá’hwip} reveals the correct phonemic form. It seems that w in
                                                                                    
          word-final position after a consonant was devoiced, thus pronounced /pahw̥ /. The cognate form
                                                                                    
          in Hiyékiti also seems to have had the form /pahw̥ /, based on the transcription [paghou].
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 2. The form [otōˊlco] is a miswriting or mishearing of itólšo, which with vowel weaking
                                                                                    
          would have been pronounced /ətólšʌ/.                                      
                                                                                    
           The gerund nké·tne ‘for you to stay,’ expresses purpose. The gerund expresses purpose in the
                                                                                    
          texts rather than the participle.                                         
                                                                                    
          Line 3. Note the gapped construction with n̥háh á ‘are you not?’ standing in for ók n̥áwš á ‘are
                                                                                    
          you not able to come?’                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         279                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 5. The element tukémš is a locative compound meaning ‘row in a boat.’ It is comparable to
                                                                                    
          the compound nuŋké·t ‘to live in a village.’                              
                                                                                    
          Line 8. Here the aorist mode is used describing a situation in the future, since there is no focus
                                                                                    
          on the temporal aspects of the action.                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 9. The first gerund in this line, išaŋkámšne, expresses purpose, ‘we are to play.’ The
                                                                                    
          second gerund išké·tne, expresses intention, ‘we are to have.’ In an interlinear note, Gatschet
                                                                                    
          points out that Tottokš insisted that the gerund ké·tne does not come from ké·t ‘to sit.’ Thus we
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          have to accept that the verb ké ‘to have’ has an irregular gerund, ké·tne, as well as a regular
                                                                                    
          gerund kéne.                                                              
                                                                                    
          Line 10. The phrase púm išháhš was incorrectly glossed in Gatschet’s fieldnotes as ‘we have a
                                                                                    
          dance,’ and this error was carried on by Swanton into the publication. It is clearly ‘we do not
                                                                                    
          have a dance,’ and with the death of the chief mentioned afterwards, it makes sense that a
                                                                                    
          planned dance would have been cancelled.                                  
                                                                                    
          Line 13. This line acts as a supplement to Text 7 which discusses traditional Atakapa medical
                                                                                    
          practices. The fact that traditional Atakapa doctors treated illness by sucking out blood would
                                                                                    
          otherwise be unknown.                                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Lines 15-20. These sentences do not make good sense and are not well-coordinated. Gatschet
                                                                                    
          seems to have prepared a series of sentences, and asked for their translation, and Tottokš did her
                                                                                    
          best.                                                                     
                                                                                    
          Lines 15-16. These sentences are ungrammatical. They are translated in the publication “Did
                                                                                    
          you find the horses you lost three weeks ago?” However, the Atakapa consists of three sentences:
                                                                                    
          ‘You find your horses,’ ‘You lost them,’ ‘It happened three weeks ago.’ There is no interrogative
                                                                                    
          element, and there is no coordination between the three sentences. The sentence in the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         280                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          translation clearly was offered to Tottokš to put into Atakapa, and the tacit relative clause
                                                                                    
          confused her, given that the syntax of relative clause equivalents in Atakapa is very different
                                                                                    
          than in English. Based on other, grammatical, sentences, the possible translation of the English
                                                                                    
          would be:                                                                 
                                                                                    
           nakít canúk yílhiwéw cikát lá·t nakšakáłišne šakwinétem á?               
                                                                                    
           nakit canuk yílhiwew cikat la·t nak-šak-ałiš-ne  šak-wine-tem            
                                                                                    
           your horse week    ago  three 2PLSTATS-3PLO-lose-GER 3PLO-find-2PLS      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           a                                                                        
                                                                                    
           Q                                                                        
                                                                                    
           ‘Did you find the horses that you lost three weeks ago?’                 
                                                                                    
          Line 18. This is a relative clause equivalent. A free translation is not provided in the
                                                                                    
          publication. The English original seems to have been ‘That is all you have to do.’ The noun iyáŋ
                                                                                    
          ‘there’ is used in the ergative case as a kind of resumptive pronoun, mó·n is verbalized as ‘to be
                                                                                    
          all,’ and then n̥káne is the relative clause equivalent, ‘that you do.’ It is difficult to assess how
                                                                                    
          grammatical this sentence is, but it seems much closer to what it should be than lines 15 and 16.
                                                                                    
          Line 19. In the publication, this line is translated as a unit with line 17, completely ignoring line
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          18. The two sentences are not congruent, as line 17 is in the aorist mode and line 19 is in the
                                                                                    
          preterite mode. It is possible to conceive of this sentence referring to a present tense if the
                                                                                    
          actions are conceived as being resultative, that is if the unnamed actors have been successful in
                                                                                    
          completing their actions of finding, driving, and bringing.               
                                                                                    
          Line 20. This sentence about a thief is a non sequitur. Line 15 mentions the horses as having
                                                                                    
          been lost, not stolen.                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         281                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 21. The fact that this entire text is highly artificial is brought out in this sentence: ‘Kiletkiš
                                                                                    
          got married two weeks ago, and is to have a baby soon.’ Kiletkiš is noted as being the mother of
                                                                                    
          Tottokš, who at the time she worked with Gatschet was 55 years old. Her mother, had she been
                                                                                    
          living, would have been over 70, and not about to have any babies.        
                                                                                    
          Line 22. The English original of this sentence seems to have been “John will marry Annie,” and
                                                                                    
          given what Gatschet wrote in his field notes, it gave Tottokš and himself a bit of trouble. Part of
                                                                                    
          the problem is that Atakapa has two verbs ‘to marry,’ yilké and yilwáy, the first being intransitive
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          and the second being transitive. Gatschet seems to have thought that the first meant ‘for a
                                                                                    
          woman to get married,’ and the second, ‘for a man to get married,’ based on his interlinear
                                                                                    
          glosses with Latin nubere and ducere. The sentence first started out as {Hāsh Annie yilkétikit}
                                                                                    
          then Gatschet drew a line through yilkétikit and replaced it with {yilwáitikit John}, then realizing
                                                                                    
          that yilway, as he assumed, meant for a man to get married, scratched out John, and inserted it
                                                                                    
          between Hāsh and Annie, so that it meant ‘He, John, will marry Annie,’ not ‘She, Annie, will
                                                                                    
          marry John,’ as it originally did. Further examples show that if the person to whom one is
                                                                                    
          getting married must be expressed after using yilké, then the noun referring to that person is put
                                                                                    
          in the directional case.                                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 24. The English original of this sentence was probably, ‘The boys’ trousers are full of dirt
                                                                                    
          and holes.’ The Atakapa devised the word šok-hat-ké ‘the thing one has on oneself,’ to cover the
                                                                                    
          concept of Western clothing, but they did not make the gender distinctions so important to
                                                                                    
          English speakers (blouse/shirt; dress/trousers, etc.) In addition, the Atakapa word nómš ‘child’
                                                                                    
          does not make gender distinctions. Thus, in translating the sentence Tottokš had to say “The
                                                                                    
          male children, their male clothing.” Furthermore, at first, she started to translate the rest of the
                                                                                    
          sentence with {lú ntál} lún̥ tál ‘mud and holes,’ and then switched to the more Atakapa
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         282                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          expression lúšim tálšim ‘they are nothing but mud and nothing but holes,’ after which Gatschet
                                                                                    
          scratched out {lú ntál}.                                                  
                                                                                    
          Line 26. This sentence is an aorist expressing past time, as shown by the adverb tém,
                                                                                    
          ‘yesterday.’                                                              
                                                                                    
          Line 27. The gerund here is acting as an adverbial modifier: pélháh ‘it is not far’ canúkkin
                                                                                    
          itéwókne ‘coming riding on a horse.’                                      
                                                                                    
          Text 5. Biographical sketch of Kišmok. With this text, found on pages 161-163 of Gatschet’s
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          field notes, we return to natural texts. It talks about Kišmok, the cousin of Tottokš and mother of
                                                                                    
          Kišyuc. Gatschet entitled it “Biographic Notice of Ponponne.” Ponponne was the French
                                                                                    
          nickname of Kišmok (probably bonbon ‘sugar-plum,’ very likely an ironic reference to her sharp
                                                                                    
          personality). As Ponponne she appears in the 1870 United States census, living on Lake Prien,
                                                                                    
          with her daughter Louison and grandchildren Adele and Celanie13 and Celanie’s husband
                                                                                    
          Narcisse14 and their two sons. If the census records are anywhere near correct, Kišmok was born
                                                                                    
          around 1805.                                                              
                                                                                    
          wé·t kiš mók                                                              
                                                                                    
           kic mókn̥ <Ponponne> yóŋšulat                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          tákapo kìc yukhíti núŋkin ké·tn̥tat                                       
                                                                                    
           yukhíti túl tólpot inó                                                   
                                                                                    
            túl téw iyéhicon                                                        
                                                                                    
          iyáŋ ké·tn̥ tat yukíts túl maynóy išicyúckin                              
                                                                                    
          há·š hipá háhšat hiúhc atkákiya                                           
                                                                                    
           néšpalpaláŋkin nuŋkè·tn̥tat                                              
                                                                                    
          ikunyúc šaktikáw                                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         283                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           šokká hiwéw šityáŋ líliya šokšakyíkšya                                   
                                                                                    
            šokwákn̥ á·l payókya                                                    
                                                                                    
              pílkin tihtšokšílkit
