           yukhíti šakhimo·šne·kinháh                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         284                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          kištó·t ha níl                                                            
                                                                                    
           yoyót ha iškišíl                                                         
                                                                                    
            háhuwi·t káwat á núŋkin                                                 
                                                                                    
             šoké·š hoktanúk šakkáat                                                
                                                                                    
              kiwílš šakhimo·šne·kin himó·šulat.                                    
                                                                                    
          My cousin was a short woman;                                              
                                                                                    
           they called her Short-Woman and Ponponne.                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          She was an Atakapa woman, and she used to live in an Indian village       
                                                                                    
           on the east side of Lake Prien;                                          
                                                                                    
           Lake Charles is smaller.                                                 
                                                                                    
          There she used to dwell while we grew up on the other side of the lake.   
                                                                                    
          She did not have a husband from the time I knew her,                      
                                                                                    
           She lived in the village in a split-plank house.                         
                                                                                    
          She looks like one of the young people.                                   
                                                                                    
           She works hard;                                                          
                                                                                    
             she softens green Spanish moss, and she sells,                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
              and brings back bread and meat,                                       
                                                                                    
              and she keeps on lying on the bed sewing.                             
                                                                                    
          She did not dress herself like an Indian.                                 
                                                                                    
          She made different makes of baskets and sold them.                        
                                                                                    
          She was a short woman and an ill-tempered one;                            
                                                                                    
           she got drunk and wanted to go about beating people and cursing them,    
                                                                                    
          She did not have cows or horses.                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         285                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           She had three children, two men and one woman.                           
                                                                                    
            Her older son’s name is Toussaint.                                      
                                                                                    
              Her younger son’s name is Ursin; his Indian name is Kona·š.           
                                                                                    
               Her daughter’s name is Louison;                                      
                                                                                    
               her Indian names are Yoyot and Kišyuc.                               
                                                                                    
          She was able to tell her children and her grandchildren many things;      
                                                                                    
           the old things that people did long ago.                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
             She sang songs to them.                                                
                                                                                    
          She died fourteen years ago;                                              
                                                                                    
           she was about sixty years old.                                           
                                                                                    
            She died of yellow fever,                                               
                                                                                    
              and they buried her in her village.                                   
                                                                                    
               She alone lies buried there.                                         
                                                                                    
               She is not in the Indian cemetery.                                   
                                                                                    
          Kišto·t, her grandchild,                                                  
                                                                                    
           Yoyot’s daughter,                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            had died previously in this town.                                       
                                                                                    
              They had different illnesses.                                         
                                                                                    
              They buried her in the white people’s cemetery.

               ‘They buried her in the white people’s cemetery.’                    
                                                                                    
          Commentary. This text was analyzed and translated by Swanton in his sketch of Atakapa
                                                                                    
          grammar (1929:147), and retranslated for the 1932 publication. Occasionally in collecting a text,
                                                                                    
          Gatschet would write words and phrases out of proper order, and then, when he went back over it
                                                                                    
          with his consultant, would make marginal notes about how the phrase actually should be ordered,
                                                                                    
          with subscript numbers or notes. While Swanton was usually very careful about following
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Gatschet’s notes regarding proper word order, he did not do so in this text, which changed the
                                                                                    
          meaning of part of it radically. This is most evident in lines 16-18.     
                                                                                    
          Lines 16-18 as published:                                                 
                                                                                    
          šók héw šakwánc úhcat                                                     
                                                                                    
          icá·k šok wašwášiŋ kúltan káulat ká                                       
                                                                                    
          ha šaknómšya ha šakníl šakyókat yokón hakítot                             
                                                                                    
          Lines 16-18 as directed by Gatschet to be ordered:                        
                                                                                    
          ha šaknómšya ha šakníl šók héw šakwánc úhcat                              
                                                                                    
          icá·k šok wašwášiŋ kúltan káulat ká                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          yokón šakyókat hakítot                                                    
                                                                                    
          Translations:                                                             
                                                                                    
          Swanton (1929):                                                           
                                                                                    
               She could tell many things that men had done long ago.               
                                                                                    
               About them she sang songs to her children and grandchildren.         
                                                                                    
          Swanton (1932):                                                           
                                                                                    
               She could tell to her children and her grandchildren many things about what
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         294                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                happened in olden times.                                            
                                                                                    
               She sang songs to them.                                              
                                                                                    
          Based on Gatschet:                                                        
                                                                                    
               She was able to tell her children and her grandchildren many things, 
                                                                                    
               the old things that people did long ago.                             
                                                                                    
               She sang songs to them.                                              
                                                                                    
          Swanton’s 1932 translation is closer to what Gatschet had intended; however, Swanton never re-
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          edited the text as Gatschet had directed.                                 
                                                                                    
          Line 1. Even though Kišmok was a first cousin to Tottokš; here she is called wé·t, or ‘woman’s
                                                                                    
          sister.’ This goes along with the statement in Text 1 that cousins were considered to be brothers
                                                                                    
          and sisters. The daughter of Kišmok, Kišyuc, Gatschet’s other consultant, although a first cousin
                                                                                    
          once removed of Tottokš, was older than she, suggesting that the unnamed mother of Kišmok
                                                                                    
          was the eldest daughter of Šukuhuy (see Text 6).                          
                                                                                    
          Line 3. The focus form of the pronoun ‘we’ yukíts, is used here, as the focus of the narrative
                                                                                    
          switches from Kišmok to the family of Tottokš.                            
                                                                                    
          Lines 6-7. The mode of the narrative switches in these two lines from the preterite to the aorist.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The stylistic purpose seems to be to make the narrative more vivid, as Tottokš describes her
                                                                                    
          cousin as if she were still living.                                       
                                                                                    
          Line 7. There are several mishearings in this line. The first is [cithaˊñ] for ‘Spanish moss.’ This
                                                                                    
          is actually šit yáŋ ‘green Spanish moss.’ The noun šit is a mass noun and requires a plural
                                                                                    
          adjective (eg. šit mélme·l ‘black Spanish moss’). However, the verb yáŋ ‘to be green’ explicitly
                                                                                    
          lacks a plural form. The Indians in Louisiana had a technology for spinning and weaving
                                                                                    
          Spanish moss, which was used to make saddle blankets and mattresses among other items. The
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         295                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          next mishearing is [liˊl] líl ‘to be soft’ for líli, ‘to make something soft.’ The final i of the verb
                                                                                    
          seems to have been missed, given that it was followed by the clitic =ya ‘and.’
                                                                                    
           The published form [ti(xt)] was originally written {tíu tí} and when Gatschet went back over
                                                                                    
          the text, he scratched out the {tíu} and added {‘ht} in parentheses after {tí} to show that he had
                                                                                    
          missed hearing it. His parentheses partially covered the accent mark, so in the publication
                                                                                    
          [ti(xt)] was printed without its accent, and not as tíht as Gatschet intended.
                                                                                    
           Finally, the form [coxkciˊwkit] shows an example of preaspiration in the prefix šok- and the
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          mishearing of the l in the verb sokšíl, ‘to be sewing,’ as w.             
                                                                                    
          Line 9. The word hoktanúk ‘to be different’ is derived from the numeral tanúk ‘one,’ verbalized
                                                                                    
          and with the reciprocal prefix hok- ‘it is alone is regards to others.’ The verb šokšakyíkš ‘to sell’
                                                                                    
          is irregularly derived from šakyíkš ‘to buy,’ and ultimately from yíkš ‘to pay someone
                                                                                    
          something.’                                                               
                                                                                    
          Line 10. There is a nice example here of a complex compound desiderative with omitted
                                                                                    
          inflection in the coordinate verb. Examples such as these suggest that at the time Gatschet did
                                                                                    
          his field work, Atakapa was not yet beginning to show signs of language death.
                                                                                    
          Line 11. By the late 19th century, the Atakapa had adopted animal husbandry, along with
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          agriculture (Post, 1962:241-42). If they were at all like the Koasati of the same period, horses
                                                                                    
          and cattle were an indication of wealth. This being so, Tottokš suggests that her cousin was not a
                                                                                    
          wealthy woman.                                                            
                                                                                    
          Line 15. This line puts Gatschet’s first consultant, Kišyuc, in her family context. It is interesting
                                                                                    
          that she had more than one Indian name.                                   
                                                                                    
          Lines 16-18; 16a-18a. The mis-ordering of these sentences by Swanton is discussed at the
                                                                                    
          beginning of the commentary on this text.                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         296                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 19. There is a slight meaning difference between wáši ‘to be old,’ and wášiŋ ‘to be so old.’
                                                                                    
          Note the forms wášwaši ‘to be old, plural subject’ and wášinnakwášiŋ ‘to become very old.’
                                                                                    
          Line 20. In taking down the dictation, Gatschet asked if, instead of núŋkin ‘in (her) village,’ if
                                                                                    
          one could say kánkin ín (her) home,’ and was given a definite no. Atakapa makes a definite
                                                                                    
          distinction between áŋ ‘house, the building,’ and kán ‘the concept of home,’ which is not so
                                                                                    
          clearly made in English.                                                  
                                                                                    
          Line 21. Here there is a verbalization of a noun phrase in the locative case: yukhíti
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          šakhimo·šné·kinháh ‘she is not in the Indian cemetery.’                   
                                                                                    
          Line 22. This is the only textual reference to any of the children of Kišyuc; however on page 45
                                                                                    
          of Gatschet’s field notes, the name of another daughter, Kiškicaw, and the name of her son,
                                                                                    
          Nolkop, are given.                                                        
                                                                                    
          Line 23. Even though the verb hoktanúk appeared previously in line 9 with the meaning
                                                                                    
          ‘different,’ here Gatschet mistranslated it as ‘the same.’ Actually, ‘to be the same’ is expressed
                                                                                    
          by the verb hoktikáw from the verb tikáw ‘to be similar’ and the reciprocal prefix hok-. Perhaps
                                                                                    
          Tottokš meant to say hoktikáw, but said hoktanúk by accident.             
                                                                                    
          Text 6. Notes on the family of Tottokš. This is another biographical text, one in which Tottokš
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          talks about her grandfather, mother, and stepfather. It is found on pages 166-168 of Gatschet’s
                                                                                    
          field notes. Gatschet entitled it “Chief Shukuhuˊ-i and Cyprien” although it is as much about her
                                                                                    
          mother Kiletkiš (here unnamed) as it is about the two men. Šukuhuy17, grandfather of Tottokš,
                                                                                    
          died about 1854, while her step-father Cyprien died during the Civil War in 1864.
                                                                                    
          wi kulšá šúkuhuy yukhíti šokóyat tákapa óci                               
                                                                                    
          inkilíš táy óc núŋ ké·tn̥ tat túl téw óci                                 
                                                                                    
          hálkin cípšat yukhíti túlot                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         297                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           iyáŋ wášinnakwášiŋ                                                       
                                                                                    
          hináka ilú wošpé lá·t atkáki káwat                                        
                                                                                    
           hiláy tanúk kéat ha é·ŋ <Mary Ann>                                       
                                                                                    
            iškišíl nít kéat hiyó·l háhšat                                          
                                                                                    
          wi oké·t hicónot kinéat                                                   
                                                                                    
           ha ité·t hoktiwé·tóhulat tik nómš lá·t kéat                              
                                                                                    
          wi kulša káwkin ya há·š yilké                                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           túl maynóy cípšatya išicyúcišat                                          
                                                                                    
          yukít ité·tpél <Cyprien> iša·k tólat yukít nómšot                         
                                                                                    
           išyucyucya tanúkip šoháh išpácháhat                                      
                                                                                    
            wí·š wi ité·t hiyanyánháhšat                                            
                                                                                    
              paytólušat wíot                                                       
                                                                                    
          hiyúckin há·š okwáŋšot tíšatya káwat                                      
                                                                                    
           wi oké·t máŋ iláŋwáŋn̥tat                                                
                                                                                    
            tikhúkóhat káwháhštán tíš áwšat                                         
                                                                                    
          tanukip tíšat tikpáyat                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           kawkáw kášik iših áwšat                                                  
                                                                                    
          yukít nómšsim túhcel yukít nuk                                            
                                                                                    
           ití tanúkip yíl tutnen ó·l paymóhat                                      
                                                                                    
            yukíts iššaccíškišat yukít oké·t húne                                   
                                                                                    
              payákš tíškóhat yukíts tíšne išóŋšat                                  
                                                                                    
               ké·tne námcel tíšháhšne                                              
                                                                                    
          My grandfather, Šukuhuy, was an Indian chief over the Atakapa.            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         298                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          He used to live in a village right on English Bayou, over Lake Charles.   
                                                                                    
          Afterwards he moved to Lake Prien.                                        
                                                                                    
           There he grew to be very old.                                            
                                                                                    
            He died about thirty years ago.                                         
                                                                                    
          He had one wife; her name was Mary Ann                                    
                                                                                    
           He had five daughters; he had no sons.                                   
                                                                                    
            My mother was next to youngest.                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
              She and her father dwelt together until she had three children.       
                                                                                    
          When my grandfather died,                                                 
                                                                                    
           then she married and moved to the other side of the lake, and raised us. 
                                                                                    
          Our step-father Cyprien was a good man to us children.                    
                                                                                    
           He raised us and never whipped us even once.                             
                                                                                    
            I did not fear my father.                                               
                                                                                    
            He was generous to me.                                                  
                                                                                    
          When I was grown, he went to the war and died.                            
                                                                                    
           My mother kept on going about mourning for a long time.                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            She wanted to go and see him before he died, but she was unable to go.  
                                                                                    
              One time she went; she went and returned.                             
                                                                                    
              Because of the high water she was not able to cross.                  
                                                                                    
          It was nothing but us children; we stayed there all by ourselves.         
                                                                                    
           One night, just before daybreak, she returned back.                      
                                                                                    
            We were glad to see our mother.                                         
                                                                                    
              She wanted to go back.                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         299                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               We did not want her to go.                                           
                                                                                    
               We begged her to stay and not to go.

                                         301                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [ha itēˊt hoktiwē toˊhulat tik noˊmc lāt keˊat]                      
                                                                                    
          9)   ha ité·t hoktiwé·tóhulat tik nómš lá·t kéat                          
                                                                                    
               ha   ité·t hoktiwe·-toh-ul-at      tik  nomš      la·t

          10)  wi kulšá káwkinya ha·š yílke túl maynóy cípšatya išicyúcišat         
                                                                                    
               wi   kulša     ø-kaw-kin=ya        ha-iš     yilke     tul           
                                                                                    
               my   grandfather 3STATS-die-WHEN=and she-FOC marry     lake

               [Tik’huˊ koˊhat kaˊ-u hāˊxc taˊn, tiˊc aˊ-ucat]                      
                                                                                    
          17)  tikhúkóhat káwháhštán tíš áwšat                                      
                                                                                    
               tik-ø-hu-koh-at          ø-kaw-hahš-tan tiš                          
                                                                                    
               GO.AND-3STATS-see-DESID-PRET 3STATS-die-NEG-ASP go

               water     high-ERG.INSTR cross 3STATS-not.be.able-PRET               
                                                                                    
               ‘Because of the high water she was not able to cross.’               
                                                                                    
               [Yukiˊt noˊmcsĕm tuhtsĕl yukiˊt nuk]                                 
                                                                                    
          20)  yukít nómšsim tóhcel yukít nuk                                       
                                                                                    
               yukit nomš-šim      toh-cel        yukit nuk                         
                                                                                    
               we   child-NOTHING.BUT stay(pl)-1PLS we self                         
                                                                                    
               ‘It was nothing but us children; we stayed there all by ourselves.’

               [Itiˊ tanuˊkip yiˊl tūt nēn ōˊl paˊi moˊhat]
