               ‘She wanted to go back.’                                             
                                                                                    
               [Yukīˊts tiˊcne icōˊñcat]                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          24)  yukits tišne išoŋšat                                                 
                                                                                    
               yukit-š   tiš-ne    iš-oŋš-at                                        
                                                                                    
               we-FOC    go(sg)-GER 1PLSTATS-not.want-PRET                          
                                                                                    
               ‘We did not want her to go.’                                         
                                                                                    
               [Kēˊtne naˊmtsel tiˊc hāˊxcne]                                       
                                                                                    
          25)  ke·tne namcel tišhahšne                                              
                                                                                    
               ke·t-ne   nam-cel   tiš-hahš-ne                                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         305                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               stay-GER  beg-1PLS  go(sg)-NEG-GER                                   
                                                                                    
               ‘We begged her to stay and not to go.’                               
                                                                                    
          Commentary.                                                               
                                                                                    
          Line 2-3. The movement of Šukuhuy from above Lake Charles to Lake Prien below it mirrors
                                                                                    
          the contraction of the Atakapa population. Yukhíti-speakers formerly had villages near all the
                                                                                    
          lakes along the Calcasieu River, but as their population fell, their villages were reduced to one on
                                                                                    
          either side of Lake Prien, the village of Ló on the west side of the lake, and the village of Túl
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Núŋ on the east side of the lake.                                         
                                                                                    
          Line 6. It is of interest that Tottokš only gives her grandmother’s English name, not her Atakapa
                                                                                    
          one. Among the Koasati upstream from the Atakapa, Indian names were linked to a person’s
                                                                                    
          soul, so they were not used lightly, rather nicknames and English or French names were
                                                                                    
          commonly used.                                                            
                                                                                    
          Line 8. Tottokš never mentions her mother’s name or her father’s or the names of any of her
                                                                                    
          aunts. Although Gatschet remarks in his field notes that there was no ban against naming the
                                                                                    
          dead, there still may have been a taboo against naming one’s deceased parents. She does name
                                                                                    
          her deceased step-father, but he was not a relative by blood. She does not name her siblings, but
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          is seems likely that Rosemond (Teet) Verdine, Swanton’s consultant, and Eliza Verdine were her
                                                                                    
          brother and sister.                                                       
                                                                                    
          Line 10. In this text Tottokš uses the focus forms of pronouns in order to change emphasis from
                                                                                    
          one thread of the narrative to another.                                   
                                                                                    
          Line 13. Here, Tottokš calls Cyprien her father, which suggests that her natural father had died
                                                                                    
          when she was very young.                                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         306                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 15. The verb root yúc is both transitive and intransitive. Here, in the subordinate mode, it
                                                                                    
          is intransitive: ‘when I grew up.’ Above in line 12, the root, reduplicated to mark a plural object,
                                                                                    
          is transitive: ‘he raised us.’                                            
                                                                                    
           The war that her step-father went to was the Civil War. It is likely that he fought on the
                                                                                    
          Confederate side, like the majority of Louisiana residents. In this sentence because his going and
                                                                                    
          his dying are in sequence the two verbs are joined by the clitic =ya.     
                                                                                    
          Line 16. Here the actions of the mother, mourning and going about, are occurring at the same
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          time. Therefore the two verbs iláŋ and wáŋ are compounded.                
                                                                                    
          Line 17. Gatschet notes that Cyprien was in Alexandria, Louisiana, which was back in
                                                                                    
          Confederate hands in 1864 after a year of Union occupation during the Civil War.
                                                                                    
          Line 20. The deverbal suffix –šim ‘to be nothing but a noun,’ is here attached to the noun nómš
                                                                                    
          ‘child,’ to indicate that after the mother had left, the children were left alone to take care of
                                                                                    
          themselves. Their isolation is further emphasized by the use of the pronoun yukít nuk ‘we
                                                                                    
          ourselves.’                                                               
                                                                                    
          Lines 24-25. Here are examples of gerunds being used as infinitives, as well as the only example
                                                                                    
          of a negative infinitive, tišháhšne ‘not to go.’                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Text 7. Traditional treatment of disease. Found on page 170 of Gatschet’s field notes, this
                                                                                    
          short ethnographic text deals with traditional Atakapa medical practices. He did not give it a
                                                                                    
          title, so Swanton entitled it “Treatment of the Sick.” This entire text suggests that to the Atakapa,
                                                                                    
          illness had a supernatural, not a natural, origin, and was brought about by the malevolence of
                                                                                    
          other people.                                                             
                                                                                    
          šá šoké·š hiwéw káuln̥                                                    
                                                                                    
           tákapa išá·k hatwíwat šáik šoktéy hacehéš míšulat                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         307                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ití tanúk hokinúlulat ha áŋkin                                            
                                                                                    
           šoká·kulat ití máŋ mo·n                                                  
                                                                                    
            šíŋšne šóŋya nákšulat                                                   
                                                                                    
          iláŋšwánkin yókya                                                         
                                                                                    
           yukhíti ipšók hálkin móhat                                               
                                                                                    
            okhúya péniatya yíkšat                                                  
                                                                                    
          tannéw šakhináwškóhšháhulat                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           ikunyúcip ináw šaknáwšulháh                                              
                                                                                    
          iša·k šoké·š káwkin                                                       
                                                                                    
           wíwulat šok hacehéš hiwéw káat                                           
                                                                                    
          When some people became very sick                                         
                                                                                    
           the Atakapa people believed that some people had given them bad medicine.
                                                                                    
          One night they met each other at his/her house.                           
                                                                                    
          They did ceremonial dancing all night long.                               
                                                                                    
           They rattled gourd rattles and played musical instruments.               
                                                                                    
          As they went around grieving, they sang,                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           and afterwards the Indian doctor was going about and arriving.           
                                                                                    
            He came to see him/her and cured him/her, and he/she paid him           
                                                                                    
          They did not want other people to enter,                                  
                                                                                    
           They did not allow for young people to enter.                            
                                                                                    
          After someone sick died,                                                  
                                                                                    
           they believed that something very bad had done it.                       
                                                                                    
               [Caˊ cok’hĕc hiweˊ-u kaˊ-ulĕn]                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         308                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          1)   šá šoké·š hiwéw káuln̥                                               
                                                                                    
               ša        šoke·š    hiwew     ka-ul-n̥                               
                                                                                    
               someone   be.sick   be.strong do-3PLS-IF                             
                                                                                    
               ‘When people became very sick…                                       
                                                                                    
               [Taˊkapo icāˊk hatwiˊvat, caˊ-ik cukteˊ-i hatse-eˊc miˊculat]        
                                                                                    
          2)   tákapa išá·k hatwíwat šáik šoktéy hacehéš míšulat                    
                                                                                    
               takapa    iša·k     hat-wiw-at     ša-ik               šoktey        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Atakapa   people    REFLEX-believe-PRET someone-ERG.AGNT             
                                                                                    
               medicine                                                             
                                                                                    
               haceheš   ø-miš-ul-at                                                
                                                                                    
               bad(sg)   3OBJ-give-3PLS-PRET                                        
                                                                                    
               ‘the Atakapa people believed that some people had given them bad medicine.’
                                                                                    
               [Itiˊ tanuˊk okinuˊlulat haˊ aˊñkin]                                 
                                                                                    
          3)   ití tanúk hokinúlulat ha áŋkin                                       
                                                                                    
               ití  tanuk hok-inul-ul-at     ha   aŋ-kin                            
                                                                                    
               night one RECIP-met-3PLS-PRET his house-LOC                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘One night they met each other at his/her house.’                    
                                                                                    
               [cukaˊulat itiˊ maˊñ mōn]                                            
                                                                                    
          4)   šoká·kulat ití máŋ mo·n                                              
                                                                                    
               šoka·k-ul-at        ití  maŋ  mo·n                                   
                                                                                    
               ceremonial.dance-3PLS-PRET night long all                            
                                                                                    
               ‘They did ceremonial dancing all night long.’                        
                                                                                    
               [ciˊñcne cōˊñxia yaˊ naˊxkculat]                                     
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         309                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          5)   šíŋšne šóŋya nákšulat                                                
                                                                                    
               šiŋšne    šoŋ=ya    nakš-ul-at                                       
                                                                                    
               gourd.rattle rattle=and play.instrument-3PLS-PRET                    
                                                                                    
               ‘They rattled gourd rattles and played musical instruments.’         
                                                                                    
               [Iˊñc-waˊnkin yoˊk yaˊ Yuk’hiˊti ipcōˊk haˊl(kin) moˊk waˊnkit]      
                                                                                    
          6)   iláŋšwáŋkin yókya yukhíti ipšók hálkin mókwáŋhki                     
                                                                                    
               ilaŋš-waŋ-kin       yok=ya    yukhiti   ipšok     halkin             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               grieve-go.about-WHEN sing=and Indian    doctor    afterwards         
                                                                                    
               mok-waŋ-kit                                                          
                                                                                    
               arrive(sg)-go.about-PRET-PROG(SG)                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘As they went around grieving, they sang, and afterwards the Indian doctor
                                                                                    
               was going about and arriving.’                                       
                                                                                    
               [ok hūˊya peˊneat yaˊ yīˊkcat]                                       
                                                                                    
          7)   okhúya péniatya yíkšat                                               
                                                                                    
               ok-hu=ya            peni-at=ya     yikš-at                           
                                                                                    
               COME.AND-see=and    cure-PRET=and  pay-PRET                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘He came to see him/her and cured him/her, and he/she paid him.’     
                                                                                    
               [Taneˊ-u cakinaˊ-uc koˊxcaˊhulat]                                    
                                                                                    
          8)   tannéw šakináwškóhšháhulat                                           
                                                                                    
               tan-hew        šak-inawš-kohš-hah-ul-at                              
                                                                                    
               other-PL       3PLO-enter-DESID-NEG-3PLS-PRET                        
                                                                                    
               ‘They did not want other people to enter.’                           
                                                                                    
               [Ikunyuˊtsip inaˊ-u caknaˊ-ucul haˊ]                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         310                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          9)   ikunyúcip ináwš šaknáwšulháh                                         
                                                                                    
               ikunyuc-ip inawš šak-nawš-ul-hah                                     
                                                                                    
               youth-INESS enter 3PLO-allow-3PLS-NEG                                
                                                                                    
               ‘They do/did not allow for young people to enter’                    
                                                                                    
               [Icāk cuk’hēˊc kaˊ-ukin vīˊwulat cok hatse-eˊc hiweˊ-u kaˊ-at]       
                                                                                    
          10)  iša·k šoké·š káwkin wíwulat šok hacehéš hiwéw káat                   
                                                                                    
               iša·k šoke·š kaw-kin wiw-u-lat     šok  haceheš hiwew                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               person sick die-WHEN believe-3PLS-PRET thing bad very                
                                                                                    
               ka-at                                                                
                                                                                    
               do-PRET                                                              
                                                                                    
               ‘After someone sick died, they believed that something very bad had done it.’
                                                                                    
          Commentary.                                                               
                                                                                    
          Line 1. The verb ‘to be sick’ is written in this text [cok’hēˊc] and [cuk’hēˊc], but recordings of
                                                                                    
          this word elsewhere indicate that the form is actually šoké·š without the /kh/ consonant cluster.
                                                                                    
          As Atakapa stops seem to have been fortis and aspirated, it seems that Gatschet was
                                                                                    
          overdifferentiating, and recording the non-phonemic feature of aspiration.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           The translation of this line is a bit misleading. Literally translated it says, “When strong
                                                                                    
          illnesses did people…” This reflects the idea as expressed further on in this text that illness was
                                                                                    
          the result of sorcery, not natural causes.”                               
                                                                                    
          Line 2. The mention of an indefinite “someone” may refer to the people described in the
                                                                                    
          vocabulary as ‘witch’ and ‘sorceror:’ kíš yón and išá·k yón.              
                                                                                    
           In this sentence, the complement clause of the verb hatwíwat ‘believed’ is an independent
                                                                                    
          sentence.                                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         311                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 4. The phrase ití máŋ mo·n ‘all night long,’ literally ‘night long all,’ although it seems to be
                                                                                    
          a calque on English, is rather an independent, parallel development, as the last speakers of
                                                                                    
          Atakapa were more conversant in French than in English, and the French idiom, tout la nuit is
                                                                                    
          rather different in structure.                                            
                                                                                    
          Line 5. In the form of this line in the field notes and publication, the clitic =ya, already affixed
                                                                                    
          to the verb šóŋ ‘to rattle,’ is repeated by Gatschet as an independent word because he had
                                                                                    
          internalized the idea that since the clitic meant ‘and,’ it was an independent word, as in English
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          or German.                                                                
                                                                                    
          Line 6. Swanton interpreted the otherwise meaningless string [Iˊñc], which is what appears in
                                                                                    
          the field notes, as the verb iláŋš ‘mourn’ based on Gatschet’s interlinear gloss.
                                                                                    
           In the original field notes, the phrase {mók wankit} has noted beneath it {or (móhat)}. This
                                                                                    
          latter has been lined out and further below is written {or móhat without wánkit}. This latter note
                                                                                    
          was included in the publication; móhat means ‘he arrived.’                
                                                                                    
           Further information about the practices of the traditional Atakapa doctor is given in lines 12-13
                                                                                    
          of Text 4, above, the most important being that the traditional doctor sucked out the illness. This
                                                                                    
          is also reflected in the phrase recorded in the corpus: šoktéy hacehéš hólš ‘to bewitch someone,’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          literally, ‘to put bad medicine into someone.’                            
                                                                                    
          Line 7. Usually verbs joined with the enclitic =ya have any aspectual suffixes attached to the
                                                                                    
          final verb of the sentence. Here, instead, okhúya péniatya yíkšat shows =ya occurring after the
                                                                                    
          preterite suffix -at. This is due to the fact that the verbs péni ‘cure’ and yíkš ‘pay’ have different
                                                                                    
          subjects.                                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         312                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 8. The verb in this sentence gives an example of the negative desiderative. There seems to
                                                                                    
          be a subtle semantic difference between the negative desiderative and the independent verb óŋš
                                                                                    
          ‘not to want.’                                                            
                                                                                    
            Traditional doctors in the Southeast did not like to have people observe them as they did their
                                                                                    
          curing. This attitude is parodied in the Koasati tale ‘Vulture “Cures” Rabbit’ (Kimball 2010:42-
                                                                                    
          46).                                                                      
                                                                                    
          Line 9. The inessive case is used on the noun in this sentence to mark it as peripheral, thus
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          avoiding a relative clause equivalent. This line is in the aorist mode, rather than the preterite, but
                                                                                    
          it can still be understood as referring to past time.                     
                                                                                    
          Text 8. Traditional burial practices. This is a further ethnographic text about burial customs.
                                                                                    
          It is found on pages 171-173 of Gatschet’s field notes. He entitled it “Himōˊc; Burial.” The text
                                                                                    
          falls into six sections, in which the third, fourth, and fifth sections can be understood as sub-
                                                                                    
          sections of the second section.                                           
                                                                                    
          I. Preparations for Burial                                                
                                                                                    
          tákapa kícn̥ išá·k káwkin                                                 
                                                                                    
           í·t uckáwšulat okyúlikya okká óci                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          wíwulat iša·k šappíhk tannéw okšakhúulat                                  
                                                                                    
           áŋkin tíhkin okhúulat                                                    
                                                                                    
          káy aŋkát páyš náwulat ha áŋkin                                           
                                                                                    
           káw atkáki tík himó·štin né·támkinya                                     
                                                                                    
            hál yílkin himó·šulat                                                   
                                                                                    
            šoká·kháŋštin himó·šulat                                                
                                                                                    
          yukhíti mó·ni hokinúlulat himó·šot tíwne                                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         313                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           káw ókkin tókšya himó·šulat                                              
                                                                                    
          When an Atakapa woman or man died,                                        
                                                                                    
           they covered the face with a handkerchief and put a cloth on top.        
                                                                                    
          They believed that other dead people came to see them;                    
                                                                                    
           they came to see him/her while he/she was lying in the house.            
                                                                                    
          And then they left the door open in his/her house                         
                                                                                    
           from the time of his death until they went to bury him/her in a grave,   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            and on the next day, they buried him/her;                               
                                                                                    
            when they had finished the ceremonial dance, they buried him/her.       
                                                                                    
          All the Indians gathered together to go to the burial.                    
                                                                                    
           They wrapped the dead person in cloth and buried him/her.                
                                                                                    
          II. Burial Goods.                                                         
                                                                                    
           ha cokmó·n hoktiwé·himó·šulat                                            
                                                                                    
            hatakinén                                                               
                                                                                    
              cikƛúkšne.                                                            
                                                                                    
               wošké                                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                ánhatkómš                                                           
                                                                                    
           kucnán túik ká                                                           
                                                                                    
            škóp                                                                    
                                                                                    
              náw                                                                   
                                                                                    
               šokhatké mo·n                                                        
                                                                                    
                 laklákš.                                                           
                                                                                    
          They buried his/her possessions with him/her:                             
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         314                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            Beads,                                                                  
                                                                                    
              pipe,                                                                 
                                                                                    
               rings,                                                               
                                                                                    
               earrings,                                                            
                                                                                    
             knife made of cane,                                                    
                                                                                    
              cups,                                                                 
                                                                                    
               moccasins,                                                           
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                all clothing,                                                       
                                                                                    
                and money.                                                          
                                                                                    
          III. Burial Goods for a Warrior                                           
                                                                                    
          išá·k hokwáŋš wáŋn̥                                                       
                                                                                    
           ha šokhokinácne mo·n hoktiwé·himó·šulat                                  
                                                                                    
          té                                                                        
                                                                                    
           tík                                                                      
                                                                                    
            yapúhne                                                                 
                                                                                    
              kucne                                                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          tikšakkáwne                                                               
                                                                                    
           pém                                                                      
                                                                                    
            hatpáˑšne                                                               
                                                                                    
          If a person went to war,                                                  
                                                                                    
           they buried all his weapons together with him:                           
                                                                                    
            bow,                                                                    
                                                                                    
              arrows,                                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         315                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               spears,                                                              
                                                                                    
               and knife,                                                           
                                                                                    
           quiver,                                                                  
                                                                                    
            gun,                                                                    
                                                                                    
            and armour.                                                             
                                                                                    
          IV. Burial Goods for a Chief                                              
                                                                                    
          yá iša·k káw yukhíti šokóyn̥                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           ha canúk                                                                 
                                                                                    
            nešipáhš                                                                
                                                                                    
              iša·thatyínsne laklákškópik ká hoktiwé·himó·šulat                     
                                                                                    
          If that dead person were an Indian chief,                                 
                                                                                    
           they buried him together with his horse,                                 
                                                                                    
            saddle,                                                                 
                                                                                    
            and crown made of silver.                                               
                                                                                    
          V. The Finding of a Buried Crown                                          
                                                                                    
          iša·thatyínsne laklákškópik ká winéulat                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           yukhíti túl hikí inó                                                     
                                                                                    
            né· húy                                                                 
                                                                                    
            iwálkin                                                                 
                                                                                    
             núl támtin                                                             
                                                                                    
             neškiŋštamšáŋ núl kátin                                                
                                                                                    
               payákš himó·šulat                                                    
                                                                                    
          čákta yukhíti iša·thatyínsne hináka šakkéat                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         316                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          They found a crown made of silver                                         
                                                                                    
           on the west side of Lake Prien,                                          
                                                                                    
            under the ground                                                        
                                                                                    
            in the shells,                                                          
                                                                                    
              when they were digging a foundation,                                  
                                                                                    
              when they were making the sawmill’s foundation,                       
                                                                                    
               and they buried it again.                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The Choctaw Indians had crowns in the same way.                           
                                                                                    
          VI. Rites after Burial                                                    
                                                                                    
          iša·k káw áŋ láwiulat                                                     
                                                                                    
           ha šakišá·k cípšulat hiwéwš tanúk máy                                    
                                                                                    
          himó·šmak máŋ kaya                                                        
                                                                                    
           hó·p náwulat šó hitáhne                                                  
                                                                                    
          icà·k šappíhk šakláwiulháhat                                              
                                                                                    
          They burned the dead person’s house.                                      
                                                                                    
           His relatives moved a mile away.                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
            They made a long grave-mound,                                           
                                                                                    
            and left a hole for the soul to come out.                               
                                                                                    
          They did not burn the dead.                                               
                                                                                    
               [Taˊkapa kic n icāˊk kaˊ-ukin īt utskaˊ-uculat okyuˊlik yaˊ okaˊ oˊtsi]

               under the ground in the shells, when they were digging a foundation, when they
                                                                                    
               were making the sawmill’s foundation, and they buried it again.’     
                                                                                    
               [Tchaˊkta yuk’hiti icāˊt atyiˊnsne hinaˊka cakeˊat]
