          21)  išà·k šappíhk šakláwiulháhat                                         
                                                                                    
               iša·k     šak-pihk-ø     šak-lawi-ul-hah-at                          
                                                                                    
               person    PL-die(pl)-ABS 3PLOBJ-BURN-3PLS-NEG-PRET                   
                                                                                    
               ‘They did not burn the dead.’                                        
                                                                                    
          Commentary.                                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         323                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 1. When dictating the text, Tottokš first said okkáik ‘with a cloth, and then changed it to
                                                                                    
          okyúlik ‘with a handkerchief.’ The presence of the clitic =ya¸which joins sentences, suggests that
                                                                                    
          the final words of the sentence okká óci ‘a cloth on top’ are understood to be followed by a verb
                                                                                    
          such as uckáwš.                                                           
                                                                                    
          Line 4. This statement that the Atakapa buried a corpse the day after death indicates that the
                                                                                    
          story of Palnal in Text 1, who was beaten by his wives and died, does not describe him as lying
                                                                                    
          dead on the ground three or four days, but rather as surviving that length of time before dying.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 6. Tottokš originally began the sentence with mó·ni ‘everyone,’ but on further review with
                                                                                    
          Gatschet changed it to yukhíti mó·ni, ‘all the Indians.’                  
                                                                                    
           Here the gerund tíwne ‘to go (pl)’ is used to express purpose: ‘in order to go.’
                                                                                    
          Line 9. In his field notes, after the word kucnán ‘knife,’ Gatschet wrote parenthetically (Had
                                                                                    
          they any?), presumably thinking that traditional Atakapa technology was without metal. When
                                                                                    
          Tottokš went over the text again with him, she added the words túik ká ‘made of cane,’ to satisfy
                                                                                    
          his concern.                                                              
                                                                                    
          Line 10. For no clear reason, Gatschet altered the words Tottokš said here from hakít škóp ‘their
                                                                                    
          cups’ simply to škóp ‘cups.’                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 15. Gatschet mis-heard the word for ‘crown,’ writing {ishāt atyĕne} for išá·t hatyíncne.
                                                                                    
          When Tottokš repeated the word again in the next line, Gatschet abbreviated it as {ishāt
                                                                                    
          aty.lik.ka}, rather than writing out iša·thatyíncne laklákškópik ká ‘a crown made of silver.’ In
                                                                                    
          line 17, when she repeated the word again, Gatschet transcribed it more accurately as {ishāt
                                                                                    
          atyínsne]. When Swanton edited the text for publication, however, he used the erroneous first
                                                                                    
          transcription rather than the second one.                                 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         324                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           Before becoming integrated into the money economy, the Atakapa, Choctaw, and Koasati in
                                                                                    
          Louisiana used to take any silver coins they received and hammer them into jewelry. The
                                                                                    
          National Museum of the American Indian has an example of a silver crown such as is described
                                                                                    
          in the text that was collected from the Choctaw in 1908 (NMAI 1/9081), and another that was
                                                                                    
          collected from the Koasati in the same year (NMAI 1/8623).                
                                                                                    
          Line 16. This sentence is an elaborate piece of verbal art, and gives a hint of what Atakapa oral
                                                                                    
          literature must have been like. It is composed of interlocking phrases, with the first and last
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          phrases framing the rest; the locative phrases are paired, with the second divided in two, and
                                                                                    
          further actions also arranged in a pair. A visual arrangement of the translation is as follows:
                                                                                    
           They found a crown made of silver                                        
                                                                                    
            on the west side of Lake Prien,                                         
                                                                                    
              under the ground                                                      
                                                                                    
              in the shells                                                         
                                                                                    
               when they were digging a foundation,                                 
                                                                                    
               when they were making the sawmill’s foundation,                      
                                                                                    
           and they buried it again.                                                
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          It is interesting that the men who found the crown reburied it, rather than keeping it as a
                                                                                    
          curiousity or for melting down and selling. Presumably an Indian on the work gang told the rest
                                                                                    
          that it was part of a burial, and the men felt that reburial was appropriate.
                                                                                    
          Text 9. A fight among Black people in Lake Charles. This short text, found on pages 174-
                                                                                    
          175 of Gatschet’s field notes, is an anecdote about a fight among Black people in Lake Charles.
                                                                                    
          Gatschet himself gave it a title that we would find shocking nowadays; Swanton very wisely
                                                                                    
          toned it down for publication and entitled it “A Fight Among Negroes at Lake Charles.” The
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         325                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          story is told with a dry sense of humor, as the action as described is quite over the top, using two
                                                                                    
          examples of reduplication with infixation to heighten the drama, yet despite everything no one
                                                                                    
          seems to have been injured or killed. This text contains the only example of quoted speech in the
                                                                                    
          corpus, so it is linguistically quite interesting.                        
                                                                                    
          samtí ití kúšmelmé·l púm káulat                                           
                                                                                    
          wáŋpúmya                                                                  
                                                                                    
           kíš tanúk tánot iká·t pákat                                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          ya kíš tánik wáncat,                                                      
                                                                                    
           “wi iká·t n̥pákat,”                                                      
                                                                                    
            “hihaccíškišháh,” wánat                                                 
                                                                                    
              hikáwškin kát óc pákat                                                
                                                                                    
          ya šakyól hakítiš hokináculat                                             
                                                                                    
           kicá·k šappíhkya pámhokpámišulatya hokpé·mulat                           
                                                                                    
            tannéw túlkin pákšakpákišat                                             
                                                                                    
              tannéw kawkáwkin púmhikhólšulat                                       
                                                                                    
          wáŋhokpé·mkin iša·kšakkoŋne okšakyálat                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           iša·k ní·t áŋlá·kkin šakináwšulat                                        
                                                                                    
            kéš lá·t šakináwšulat                                                   
                                                                                    
              yilhiwéw pétik yíl šakhitáwšulat                                      
                                                                                    
                tanúk eykún tan áŋlá·kkin tíht                                      
                                                                                    
          On Saturday night, the black people held a dance.                         
                                                                                    
          They were going about dancing,                                            
                                                                                    
           and one woman stepped on the foot of another.                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         326                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          The other woman said to her,                                              
                                                                                    
           “You stepped on my foot.                                                 
                                                                                    
             I am not pleased,” she said.                                           
                                                                                    
              When she finished, she hit her on the mouth.                          
                                                                                    
          As for those men, they fought.                                            
                                                                                    
           They were drunk, and they really beat each other up.                     
                                                                                    
            They threw some of them again and again into the lake.                  
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
              Others went and jumped into the water.                                
                                                                                    
          When they were going about shooting at each other, the sheriff came and arrested them.
                                                                                    
           They put five men in jail.                                               
                                                                                    
            They put three women in.                                                
                                                                                    
              On Monday, they let them out.                                         
                                                                                    
               One is still now in jail.                                            
                                                                                    
               [Samtiˊ hitiˊ kuˊcmelmēl puˊm kaˊ-ulat]                              
                                                                                    
          1)   samtí ití kúšmélme·l púm káulat                                      
                                                                                    
               samti     ití  kuš,mel,me·l   pum  ka-ul-at                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Saturday  night black.person,PL, dance make-3PLS-PRET                
                                                                                    
               ‘On Saturday night, the black people held a dance.’                  
                                                                                    
               [Wāˊñ-pum yaˊ kic tanuˊk taˊxn ut ikāt paˊkĕt]                       
                                                                                    
          2)   wáŋpúmya kíš tanúk tánot iká·t pákat                                 
                                                                                    
               waŋ-pum=ya     kiš       tanuk tan-ot   hika·t pak-ø-at              
                                                                                    
               go.about-dance=and woman one  another-DIR foot step.on-3SS-PRET      
                                                                                    
               ‘They were going about dancing, and one woman stepped on the foot of another.’
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         327                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [ya kiˊc taˊxnik waˊntsăt]                                           
                                                                                    
          3)   ya kíš tánik wáncat                                                  
                                                                                    
               ya   kiš       tan-ik         wanc-ø-at                              
                                                                                    
               that woman     other-ERG.AGNT say.to-3SS-PRET                        
                                                                                    
               ‘The other woman said to her,’                                       
                                                                                    
               [wi ikāt ‘npaˊkat]                                                   
                                                                                    
          4)   wi hiká·t n̥pákat                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               wi   hika·t n̥-pak-at                                                
                                                                                    
               my   foot 2SSTATS-step.on-PRET                                       
                                                                                    
               ‘You stepped on my foot.’                                            
                                                                                    
               [Atsiˊc-kic haˊ waˊnat]                                              
                                                                                    
          5)   “hihaccíškišháh,” wánat                                              
                                                                                    
               hi-hat-ciškiš-hah        wan-ø-at                                    
                                                                                    
               1SSTATS-REFLEX-be.pleased-NEG say-3SS-PRET                           
                                                                                    
               ‘“I am not pleased,” she said.’                                      
                                                                                    
               [Hikaˊ-uckin kaˊt oˊts paˊkĕt]                                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          6)   hikáwškin kát óc pákat                                               
                                                                                    
               ø-hikawš-kin   kat       oc   pak-ø-at                               
                                                                                    
               3STATS-finish-WHEN mouth on   hit-3SS-PRET                           
                                                                                    
               ‘When she finished, she hit her on the mouth.’                       
                                                                                    
               [Ya cukiōˊl hak’hiˊtic ok-inaˊtsûlat]                                
                                                                                    
          7)   ya šakyól hakítiš hokináculat                                        
                                                                                    
               ya   šakyol    hakit-iš  hok-inac-ul-at                              
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         328                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               that man       they-FOC  RECIP-fight-3PL-PRET                        
                                                                                    
               ‘As for those men, they fought each other.’                          
                                                                                    
               [Kitsāˊk cakpiˊx̥ k yaˊ paˊm (h)okpaˊmiculat]                        
                                                                                    
          8)   kicá·k šakpíhkya pámhokpámišulat                                     
                                                                                    
               kica·k    šak-pihk=ya         pam,hok,pam-iš-ul-at                   
                                                                                    
               whiskey   3PLSTATS-be.dead(pl)=and REDUP,RECIP,beat-CAUS-3PLS-PRET   
                                                                                    
               ‘They were drunk, and they really beat each other up.’               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [Taneˊ-u tuˊlkin paˊk-cakpaˊkicat]                                   
                                                                                    
          9)   tannéw túlkin pákšakpákišat                                          
                                                                                    
               tan-hew   tul-kin   pak,šak,pakiš-ø-at                               
                                                                                    
               some-PL   lake-LOC  REDUP,3PLOBJ,throw.in.- 3SS-PRET                 
                                                                                    
               ‘They threw some of them again and again into the lake.              
                                                                                    
               [Taneˊ-u kaukauˊkin puˊm-hik’huˊlculat]                              
                                                                                    
          10)  tannéw kawkáwkin púmhikihólšulat                                     
                                                                                    
               tan-hew   kawkaw-kin pum-hik-holš-ul-at                              
                                                                                    
               some-PL   water-LOC jump-LOC-put.in-3PLS-PRET                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘Others went and jumped into the water.’                             
                                                                                    
               [Wāˊñ-hokpēˊmkin iˊcāk cakōˊñne okcĕkiaˊlat]                         
                                                                                    
          11)  wáŋhokpé·mkin iša·kšakkóŋne okšakyálat                               
                                                                                    
               waŋ-hok-ø-pe·m-kin       iša·k-šak-koŋ-ne                            
                                                                                    
               go.about-RECIP-3STATS-shoot-WHEN person-3PLO-sieze(sg)-GER           
                                                                                    
               ok-šak-yal-ø-at                                                      
                                                                                    
               COME.AND-3PLO-sieze(pl)- 3SS-PRET                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         329                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘When they were going about shooting at each other, the sheriff came and
                                                                                    
               arrested them.’                                                      
                                                                                    
               [Iˊcāk nīt aˊñlaˊkin cakinaˊ-uculat]                                 
                                                                                    
          12)  iša·k ní·t áŋlá·kkin šakináwšulat                                    
                                                                                    
               iša·k ni·t aŋla·k-kin šak-inawš-ul-at                                
                                                                                    
               man  five jail-LOC  3PLO-put.in-3PLS-PRET                            
                                                                                    
               ‘They put five men in jail.’                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               [kec lāt cakinaˊ-uculat]                                             
                                                                                    
          13)  kéš lá·t šakináwšulat                                                
                                                                                    
               keš       la·t šak-inawš-ul-at                                       
                                                                                    
               woman(pl) three 3PLO-put.in-3PLS-PRET                                
                                                                                    
               ‘They put three women in.’                                           
                                                                                    
               [Yil hiweˊ-u pētik yiˊl cakitaˊ-uculat]                              
                                                                                    
          14)  yilhiwéw pétik yíl šakhitáwšulat                                     
                                                                                    
               yilhiwew  petik     yil  šak-hitawš-ul-at                            
                                                                                    
               Sunday    (?)       day  3PLO-let.out-3PLS-PRET                      
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               ‘On Monday, they let them out.’                                      
                                                                                    
               [tanuˊk ik’huˊntan aˊñlaˊkin tiˊ(xt)]                                
                                                                                    
          15)  tanúk eykún tan áŋlá·kkin tíht                                       
                                                                                    
               tanuk eykun tan aŋla·k-kin tiht                                      
                                                                                    
               one  now  still jail-LOC lie(sg)                                     
                                                                                    
               ‘One is now still in jail.’                                          
                                                                                    
          Commentary.                                                               
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         330                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 1. The word samtí is the Atakapa pronunciation of the French Samedi. Atakapa names of
                                                                                    
          only two of the days of the week are recorded: yílhiwéw ‘Sunday,’ and yílhiwéw pétik yíl
                                                                                    
          ‘Monday.’ Given that in other Southeastern languages the names of the days of the week are
                                                                                    
          based on ordinal numbers, and Atakapa lacks ordinal numbers, it is likely that the other names of
                                                                                    
          the days of the week were borrowed from French.                           
                                                                                    
          Line 2. Here, the word tánot ‘to the other’ is preaspirated: /táhnut/. Note that preaspiration does
                                                                                    
          not occur in the word tannéw ‘others’ in line 9, the process being blocked by the geminate
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          consonant.                                                                
                                                                                    
          Line 3. The verb wánc is a ditransitive verb meaning ‘to say something to someone,’ with one
                                                                                    
          object being the quoted speech, and the other being the person spoken to. This verb can also
                                                                                    
          mean ‘to tell a story to someone.’ Further on in line 5, the simple transitive verb wán is used just
                                                                                    
          to quote speech.                                                          
                                                                                    
          Line 5. Gatschet missed the initial syllable hi in the word hihaccíškišháh, but recorded it
                                                                                    
          correctly elsewhere in his field notes. The translation in the publication, “I don’t care,” is not
                                                                                    
          correct and gives the impression that such is what the second woman said, not the real situation
                                                                                    
          in which the first woman is still speaking. The second woman never says anything at all, simply
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          gets slapped on the mouth.                                                
                                                                                    
          Line 7. Because the focus of the narrative now shifts from the quarrel between the two women
                                                                                    
          to the general melee that broke out, the pronoun hakít ‘they’ appears in its focus form hakítiš.
                                                                                    
          Line 8. Here and in the following line the verbs containing reduplication with infixation add
                                                                                    
          liveliness to the narrative.                                              
                                                                                    
          Line 9-10. Note the syntactic construction “some…others…” tannéw…tannéw…. 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         331                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Line 10. The verb complex pumhikhólš is difficult. It is comprised of pum ‘to jump’ and hólš
                                                                                    
          ‘to put in,’ but the element –hik- is without explanation. The only other occurrence in the corpus
                                                                                    
          is Gatschet (1885a:215), where <John> pumhikhólkit given as the translation of the phrase ‘John
                                                                                    
          is jumping,’ and like many of the elicited terms therein lacks any contextual clues. It is just
                                                                                    
          possible that hik- is a locative verbal prefix meaning ‘action performed in water;’ certainly such
                                                                                    
          a locative verbal prefix occurs in other Southeastern languages, and the word hikú ‘soup; broth’
                                                                                    
          is suggestive, as are the verbs ikumákš ‘to dive into water;’ mákš ‘to dive,’ and ikháwš ‘to drown
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          something,’ which would be derived from hik- + káw ‘to die.’              
                                                                                    
          Line 14. The word pétik in the compound term for ‘Monday’ is derived using the ergative case
                                                                                    
          from the unique verb pét, ‘to be completed, to be over.’ Thus yilhiwéw pétik yíl means literally,
                                                                                    
          ‘the day with Sunday being over.’                                         
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          Notes                                                                     
                                                                                    
          1. Swanton added the m in parentheses, and gave the variant móyum in the dictionary for
                                                                                    
          ‘pumpkin.’ This word appears only as móyu in Gatschet’s text, and appears nowhere else in the
                                                                                    
          field notes. Swanton does not give his source for the variant.            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          2. Originally the line read {céc pû́m wacwáci pû́m cakéat púmulat} šé·š púm wašwáši púm
                                                                                    
          šakkéat púmulat ‘They had and danced the infants’ dance and the elders’ dance,’ but Gatschet
                                                                                    
          struck out šakkéat in red when he went over the text with Tottokš.        
                                                                                    
          3. In the field notes a faint k is scratched out in red here.             
                                                                                    
          4. Gatschet made an interlinear note here: “P.(Palnal) on middle lake, a great fornicator = had 3
                                                                                    
          wives.”                                                                   
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         332                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          5. In the field notes Gatschet added a macron over the last a, and separated the last syllable from
                                                                                    
          the word with red lines, mistaking that for the verb tá·t, which is actually found in the first
                                                                                    
          syllable.                                                                 
                                                                                    
          6. The clitic =n̥ was inserted later in red ink.                          
                                                                                    
          7. The gloss is given as the plural of šokiyáhi, šokšakiyáhi, meaing ‘they are ahead in regard to
                                                                                    
          things.’                                                                  
                                                                                    
          8. The verb iyáhi is confused in the dictionary portion of the publication with the verbs iyé ‘to be
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          more,’and iyáw ‘to hoist,’ and the verbal prefix hiyik- ‘upward motion.’ Frequent confusions of
                                                                                    
          this nature limit the usefulness of the dictionary.                       
                                                                                    
          9. On page 51 of Gatschet’s field notes her French name is so given.      
                                                                                    
          10. Gatschet seem to have inserted the i thinking that the first part of the word was the same as
                                                                                    
          the word for person, išá·k. He does not seem to have recognized the nominal plural prefix šak-.
                                                                                    
          11. There are a number of examples in Gatschet’s field notes of him writing t for k, and later
                                                                                    
          going back and correcting it.                                             
                                                                                    
          12. The phonetic sequence /mst/ must reflect the actual pronunciation /mn̥st/, as the consonant
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          cluster /mš/ is very frequent and never changes, while /n̥š/ always surfaces phonetically as /n̥s/.
                                                                                    
          13. Adele’s Indian name was probably Kišto·t, and Celanie’s name Kiškicaw.
                                                                                    
          14. Narcisse was also known as Narcisse Lo according to Gatschet’s notes; he was the grandson
                                                                                    
          of Chief Lo and his wife Fanchonette.                                     
                                                                                    
          15. This indicates that Kišmok lived in the village of Túl Núŋ, and that Tottokš was raised in the
                                                                                    
          village of Ló.                                                            
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         333                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          16. The translation ‘End Lake’ found in the publication is not true to the Atakapa, where téw
                                                                                    
          means ‘upstream.’ Lake Charles is upstream of Lake Prien, where the last Atakapa villages
                                                                                    
          stood.                                                                    
                                                                                    
          17. He is also mentioned in a manuscript note by Swanton (1907a:141) “Villages at Little Lake
                                                                                    
          [Lake Prien], Lake Charles & English Bayou. cakuhúi [Šukuhúy] Indian name of Eliza’s
                                                                                    
          father.” Eliza must be the English name of one of the five daughters of Šukuhúy. She is perhaps
                                                                                    
          the Eliza Verdaine, cousin of Tottokš, mentioned by Gatchet in his field notes (1885b:148)
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          18. Although this description is somewhat elliptical, it suggests that Kiletkiš, mother of Tottokš,
                                                                                    
          lived with her father Šukuhuy in the village of Ló, and then moved across the lake to the village
                                                                                    
          of Túl Núŋ after her father and first husband died and she had married Cyprien. It also helps
                                                                                    
          explain how Tottokš knew Kišmok (Text 5) so well, as the latter was already living in Túl Núŋ.

                            YUKHÍTI – ENGLISH VOCABULARY                            
                                                                                    
               The vocabulary contains all the words found in Gatschet and Swanton (1932), as well as
                                                                                    
          those few words missed by Swanton, but to be found in the original field notes of Albert S.
                                                                                    
          Gatschet. The words are given in their phonemically reconstructed forms, including vowel
                                                                                    
          length and accentuation. In the alphabetical order, long vowels precede short vowels, the
                                                                                    
          phoneme n̥ is placed after n, and the phoneme ƛ and its variant ł are placed after t. The sounds s
                                                                                    
          and š are alphabetized together.                                          
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               A compound word is listed under the basic elements that make up the compound. In
                                                                                    
          cases where an element does not occur as an independent word, it is listed without a definition.
                                                                                    
          A few words are reconstructed whenever they can clearly be derived from an attested word.
                                                                                    
          These reconstructed words are marked with an asterisk.                    
                                                                                    
               The following are the abbreviations used:                            
                                                                                    
          adj       adjective                                                       
                                                                                    
          adv       adverb                                                          
                                                                                    
          deic      deictic                                                         
                                                                                    
          intj      interjection                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          iv        intransitive verb                                               
                                                                                    
          n         noun                                                            
                                                                                    
          pp        postposition                                                    
                                                                                    
          pro       pronoun                                                         
                                                                                    
          sv        stative verb                                                    
                                                                                    
          tv        transitive verb                                                 
                                                                                    
          var       variant form                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         340

           šokónyúl n spotted ox (pl. šokónyúlyul)                                  
                                                                                    
           yúl máŋš n line (pl yúl máŋmaŋš)                                         
                                                                                    
          A note on the semantics of certain words. While the glosses provided for the majority of
                                                                                    
          Atakapa words by Gatschet are straightforward, there are a handful of words whose meanings
                                                                                    
          seem not to have been correctly recorded. The word nohámš áyip ‘gallinule’ which is glossed
                                                                                    
          ‘turkey’ has been noted above in the discussion of Text 1 in Chapter 5. The word míŋ áŋšakhópš
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          is glossed ‘bumblebee;’ however, the literal meaning ‘bee making holes in the house’ suggests
                                                                                    
          rather the carpenter bee. The animal name šá·kš is glossed only by the Louisiana French pichou
                                                                                    
          (itself a loan from Micmac), which usually means ‘bobcat.’ However, niyá·l is given as wildcat
                                                                                    
          (a direct translation of Louisiana French chat sauvage ‘bobcat’) and in one place in the field
                                                                                    
          notes it is asserted that the niyá·l is not a pichou. In a third place ša·kš (written shā́ks) is glossed
                                                                                    
          ‘fox.’ The latter meaning is the one accepted here, even though ‘fox’ is reynard in Louisiana
                                                                                    
          French, not pichou, and there is no attested word for ‘cougar,’ which would otherwise seem the
                                                                                    
          most likely candidate.                                                    
                                                                                    
               The tribal name nóhay is glossed ‘Alabama Indian’ by Gatschet, but then the loan word
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          alpamú is also glossed ‘Alabama Indian.’ The Koasati were the principal Indian group who lived
                                                                                    
          near the Atakapa, and there are clear connections between the two peoples. Although there were
                                                                                    
          always a few Alabama living among the Koasati, they did not form a major portion of the
                                                                                    
          population. Thus given the presence of the loan word alpamú, it is most likely that nóhay refers
                                                                                    
          to the Koasati. Gatschet gives a tentative etymology of nóhay as náwóh áy ‘chinquapin swamp
                                                                                    
          person.’ Given that the chinquapin is a tree that grows on hills or sandy ridges, never in swamps,
                                                                                    
          one must reject this as a false etymology, and accept that the word has no known source.
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         455                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
               Another word of uncertain meaning is ištóhš glossed by Gatschet as ‘mulatto.’ A special
                                                                                    
          term for a person of mixed race is highly suspect, given that the Atakapa lacked the American
                                                                                    
          preoccupation with race. The nearby Koasati never developed a term for ‘mulatto’ in their
                                                                                    
          language, so it would be highly unlikely that the Atakapa did. It seems more likely that ištóhš is
                                                                                    
          a tribal name that Gatschet completely misunderstood.                     
                                                                                    
               Finally, the word kíwilš for ‘Frenchman, white man, master,’ is derived by Swanton from
                                                                                    
          the French word creole, which in French specifically refers to a person of Old-World origin
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          regardless of race. The semantics of the Yukhíti word have been influenced by Chitimacha,
                                                                                    
          where the word ka·nuš means ‘white person; master.’(Swadesh 1950b:78) and ka·nuš niki (‘real’)
                                                                                    
          means ‘Frenchman.’ The latter form is strikingly similar to Tonkawa ka·nos ‘Mexican’
                                                                                    
          (Hoijer:1949:38) and Caddo ka·nuš ‘Frenchman’ (Chafe,2018:233), and suggests a wide-spread
                                                                                    
          loan. In most other Southeastern languages, the word for ‘Frenchman’ is a borrowing from
                                                                                    
          français (e.g. Koasati falancí). It seems unlikely that the Atakapa form is a borrowing from
                                                                                    
          French, as creole should give something like *kiliyó·l not the attested kíwilš. Therefore, the
                                                                                    
          source of the word cannot be determined at present.                       
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                         456                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          APPENDIX I                                                                
                                                                                    
                          Hiyékiti (Eastern Atakapa) - English Vocabulary           
                                                                                    
           The source for the vocabulary is Manuscript 1627, National Anthropological Archives,
                                                                                    
          Smithsonian Institution, pp. 195-201. This is a 19th century copy of the vocabulary collected by
                                                                                    
          Martin Duralde 23 April 1802. The reconstructed word and definition is followed by the original
                                                                                    
          manuscript form. Phrases and sentences are placed in italics under the headword; Hiyékiti words
                                                                                    
          remembered by Kišyuc have her forms marked by (K.). Forms with G. in square brackets are
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
          lacking in the manuscript and are taken from Gallatin (1836). The forms found in Gallatin have to
                                                                                    
          be used with extreme caution, as there are many letter substitutions (w for u or ou), misreadings (t
                                                                                    
          for l, h for k) and letter turnings (n for u, u for n). A very few forms found in Gallatin and not in
                                                                                    
          the manuscript are so garbled that the phonetic form cannot be reconstructed.
                                                                                    
                                          A                                         
                                                                                    
          a pro he; she (a)                                                         
                                                                                    
           a okhe ‘He came.’ (a okghè)
